cards.title: Cricothyroid Membrane
secs: The cricothyroid membrane sits in the anterior midline of the neck, bounded by the thyroid cartilage above and the cricoid cartilage below — the spot you palpate for emergency airway access. It is made up of a thick, triangular central portion (the median cricothyroid ligament) plus two thinner lateral ligaments. It measures roughly 9 mm tall by 30 mm wide and lies about 1–1.5 cm below the thyroid notch (the “Adam’s apple”). The vocal cords lie ~1 cm above the membrane, and the small cricothyroid arteries cross its UPPER third — so you incise the LOWER third to avoid injuring either.

It is the entry point for emergency front-of-neck airway access when you Can’t Intubate and Can’t Oxygenate (the CICO situation). In adults, the Difficult Airway Society (DAS) recommends a scalpel–bougie–tube cricothyrotomy; it is faster, more superficial, and crosses fewer vessels than a formal tracheostomy. The same membrane is used for needle cricothyrotomy with jet ventilation, for retrograde intubation, and for the transtracheal block that anesthetizes the airway below the cords. In children younger than ~6 years the surgical approach is contraindicated (the cricoid ring is the narrowest part of the pediatric airway and is prone to stenosis) — use a needle technique with jet ventilation instead.
*Incise the lower third — cords are ~1 cm above and the cricothyroid arteries cross the upper portion.*

cards.title: Internal & External Jugular Veins
secs: The internal jugular vein (IJV) is the continuation of the sigmoid venous sinus and runs within the carotid sheath, just lateral to the carotid artery. Its surface landmark is the apex of the triangle formed by the sternal and clavicular heads of the sternocleidomastoid (SCM) muscle. The external jugular vein (EJV) runs more superficially, crossing the SCM at an angle; its valves and tortuous course make it harder to thread a catheter centrally. The RIGHT IJV is the preferred cannulation site: it offers a straight, valveless path to the superior vena cava (SVC), the pleural dome is lower on the right, and it avoids the thoracic duct (which is left-sided).

It is the primary site for ultrasound-guided central venous access. Placing the patient head-down (Trendelenburg) distends the vein, making it an easier target and lowering the risk of venous air embolism during insertion. Recognized complications include carotid artery puncture, pneumothorax, air embolism, arrhythmias from guidewire irritation, and — on the left — thoracic duct injury.
*Right IJV preferred (direct to SVC, no thoracic duct, lower pleural dome); always use ultrasound + Trendelenburg.*

cards.title: Thoracic Duct
secs: The thoracic duct is the body’s largest lymphatic vessel; it begins at a dilated sac called the cisterna chyli around L1–L2 and ascends into the chest through the aortic hiatus. It crosses to the left side of the body around T4–T5 and empties at the LEFT venous angle, where the left IJV meets the left subclavian vein. It drains lymph from the entire body EXCEPT the right upper quadrant (the right head/neck, right arm, and right chest), which is served by the separate right lymphatic duct.

It is vulnerable during LEFT-sided IJV or subclavian cannulation, left neck dissection, and esophagectomy. Injuring it causes a chylothorax — a milky, triglyceride-rich pleural effusion that drains fat, protein, and lymphocytes, leading to nutritional and immune loss. Avoiding this injury is one more reason the RIGHT side is favored for central lines and many neck procedures.
*Left-sided structure draining into the left venous angle; injury causes chylothorax — another point for right-sided lines.*

cards.title: Carotid & Vertebral Arteries
secs: The common carotid artery divides at about the C4 level (the upper border of the thyroid cartilage) into the internal and external carotid arteries. Two reflex sensors sit at that bifurcation: the carotid SINUS (a baroreceptor sensing blood pressure) and the carotid BODY (a chemoreceptor sensing O₂, CO₂, and pH), both relaying through the glossopharyngeal nerve (cranial nerve IX). The internal carotid gives off no branches in the neck and supplies the anterior cerebral circulation. The vertebral arteries are the first branch of the subclavian artery; they ascend through the transverse foramina of C6 up to C1 and then join to form the basilar artery.

Accidental carotid puncture is a recognized complication of IJV cannulation, and carotid endarterectomy (surgical plaque removal) is an important surgical context. Pressing on or manipulating the carotid sinus triggers a baroreceptor reflex — bradycardia and hypotension. Injecting even a tiny volume of local anesthetic (LA) directly into a vertebral or carotid artery during a neck block can cause an immediate seizure, because the drug reaches the brain directly.
*Bifurcation at C4; vertebral artery runs in the transverse foramina — small intra-arterial LA injection causes instant seizures.*

cards.title: Stellate Ganglion
secs: The stellate (cervicothoracic) ganglion is formed by the fusion of the inferior cervical and first thoracic sympathetic ganglia. It lies in front of the C7 transverse process and the neck of the first rib, resting on the longus colli muscle and close to the vertebral artery. The block is deliberately performed one level higher, at C6 (Chassaignac’s tubercle), to keep the needle away from the vertebral artery and the pleura.

Indications include upper-limb complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS), vascular insufficiency such as Raynaud’s, refractory angina or ventricular arrhythmias, and acute herpes-zoster pain of the head, neck, or arm. A successful sympathetic block produces Horner’s syndrome — ptosis (drooping eyelid), miosis (small pupil), anhidrosis (no sweating), and enophthalmos — along with a warm, dry hand. Complications include vertebral-artery injection (seizure), recurrent laryngeal nerve block (hoarseness), phrenic nerve block (diaphragm weakness), and pneumothorax.
*Horner’s syndrome confirms the block; approach at C6/Chassaignac’s tubercle to avoid the vertebral artery.*

head: Sign
Finding
tables.note: Blocked at C6 (Chassaignac tubercle); also blocks upper-limb sympathetics.

cards.title: Cervical Spine Landmarks (Vertebra Prominens, Chassaignac’s Tubercle)
secs: The vertebra prominens is the C7 spinous process — the most prominent bump you can feel at the base of the neck, used as a starting point to count vertebral levels. Chassaignac’s tubercle is the anterior tubercle of the C6 transverse process; it lies at the same level as the cricoid cartilage. Useful surface correlations: the hyoid bone ≈ C3, the thyroid-cartilage notch ≈ C4, the carotid bifurcation ≈ C4, and the cricoid cartilage ≈ C6.

Chassaignac’s tubercle (C6) is the landmark for the stellate ganglion block and for applying carotid compression. The vertebra prominens (C7) anchors surface counting for neuraxial and other regional landmarks.
*C6 = cricoid = Chassaignac’s tubercle = stellate ganglion block level; C7 = vertebra prominens.*

cards.title: Hyoid Bone
secs: The hyoid is a U-shaped bone at the C3 level and is unique in that it does not directly articulate with any other bone — it is suspended by muscles and ligaments. It anchors the tongue and suspends the larynx beneath it. Its greater cornu (horn) is the landmark used for the superior laryngeal nerve (SLN) block.

The internal branch of the SLN pierces the thyrohyoid membrane just below the greater cornu of the hyoid — the point where local anesthetic is deposited. Blocking the SLN anesthetizes the supraglottic mucosa (the airway above the vocal cords), a key step when preparing a patient for awake fiberoptic intubation.
*Greater cornu of the hyoid = target for the SLN block; provides supraglottic anesthesia for awake intubation.*

cards.title: Superficial Cervical Plexus
secs: The cervical plexus is formed by the anterior rami of C1–C4, with its sensory (cutaneous) branches arising from C2–C4. These branches surface at the posterior border of the SCM, at its midpoint — a spot called Erb’s point (the “nerve point of the neck”). There are four terminal cutaneous branches: the lesser occipital, great auricular, transverse cervical, and supraclavicular nerves.

A single injection at the SCM midpoint anesthetizes the skin of the neck, the angle of the jaw, the ear, and the shoulder. It is used for awake carotid endarterectomy (CEA), thyroid and other neck surgery, and — combined with other blocks — clavicle surgery. It is safer than the deep cervical plexus block, which risks blocking the phrenic nerve, hitting the vertebral artery, or affecting the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
*Block at Erb’s point (posterior SCM midpoint); four cutaneous branches; workhorse for awake carotid endarterectomy.*

cards.title: Caudal Space
secs: The caudal space is reached through the sacral hiatus (the gap left by the unfused S4–S5 laminae), which is flanked by the two bony sacral cornua and covered by the sacrococcygeal ligament. It is simply the most caudal (lowest) part of the epidural space. The dural sac ends around S2 in adults but sits lower (~S3) in infants — so the risk of an accidental dural puncture is higher in young children.

The single-shot caudal is the most common pediatric regional technique, used for surgery below the umbilicus (circumcision, inguinal hernia repair, hypospadias). Armitage volumes with 0.25% bupivacaine guide the spread: 0.5 mL/kg for a sacral level, 1 mL/kg for a thoracolumbar level, and 1.25 mL/kg for a mid-thoracic level; clonidine is a common additive to prolong the block. You feel a characteristic “pop” as the needle passes through the sacrococcygeal ligament; always aspirate to rule out blood or cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) before injecting.
*Pediatric workhorse via the sacral hiatus/cornua; the dural sac sits lower in infants — remember the Armitage volumes.*

cards.title: Dermatome Anatomy (Sensory & Motor)
secs: A dermatome is the strip of skin supplied by a single spinal nerve root — the basis for checking how high a neuraxial (spinal or epidural) block has spread. Upper-limb checkpoints: C6 = thumb, C7 = middle finger, C8 = little finger, T1 = medial forearm. Trunk checkpoints: T4 = nipple, T6 = xiphoid, T10 = umbilicus, L1 = inguinal crease, L3 = knee, S2–S4 = perineum.

Sensory levels confirm a block is adequate: a T4 level is needed for cesarean delivery, and roughly T6–T10 for lower-abdominal surgery. The sympathetic block extends about 2–6 dermatomes ABOVE the sensory level, while the motor block sits a few levels below it — which is why hypotension can occur even when the sensory level looks modest.
*Memorize T4 nipple, T6 xiphoid, T10 umbilicus, L1 inguinal — and that cesarean needs a T4 sensory level.*

cards.title: Doppler Ultrasound for Central Venous Access
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Anatomy › Radiologic & Ultrasound Anatomy (I.A.1.b)
secs: Real-time 2D (two-dimensional) ultrasound is now the standard of care for central venous cannulation, especially of the IJV. Telling vein from artery: a vein is compressible, thin-walled, and non-pulsatile and enlarges with a Valsalva maneuver or Trendelenburg; an artery is pulsatile, thick-walled, and non-compressible. The IJV usually lies anterolateral to the carotid artery at the apex of the SCM. A short-axis (out-of-plane) view is easier for beginners, whereas a long-axis (in-plane) view follows the needle tip better.

Color or spectral Doppler distinguishes arterial from venous flow and confirms the vessel is patent rather than thrombosed. Ultrasound reduces carotid puncture, pneumothorax, and the number of needle passes, and reveals anatomic variation before you puncture.
*Vein = compressible & non-pulsatile; right IJV anterolateral to carotid; ultrasound lowers carotid puncture and pneumothorax.*

cards.title: Upper Extremity (Bones, Vasculature, Innervation)
secs: The brachial plexus is organized Roots (C5–T1) → Trunks → Divisions → Cords → Branches (mnemonic: “Randy Travis Drinks Cold Beer”). Its five terminal nerves are the musculocutaneous, axillary, radial, median, and ulnar. Block site determines coverage: interscalene targets the roots/trunks (shoulder surgery, but spares the ulnar/C8–T1), supraclavicular the trunks/divisions, infraclavicular the cords, and axillary the terminal branches (but spares the musculocutaneous). Arterial supply runs subclavian → axillary → brachial → radial and ulnar arteries, with the ulnar/palmar arches providing collateral flow to the hand.

Positioning matters: the ulnar nerve at the elbow is the most common perioperative nerve injury, and keeping arm abduction under 90° protects the plexus from stretch. The radial artery is the preferred arterial-line site; an Allen’s test first confirms the ulnar artery can supply the hand if the radial occludes.
*Roots–Trunks–Divisions–Cords–Branches; interscalene spares the ulnar (C8–T1), axillary spares the musculocutaneous; ulnar = #1 positioning neuropathy.*

cards.title: Lower Extremity (Bones, Vasculature, Innervation)
secs: The lumbar plexus (L1–L4) gives the femoral (L2–4), obturator (L2–4), and lateral femoral cutaneous (L2–3) nerves; the saphenous nerve is the femoral’s terminal sensory branch to the medial leg. The sacral plexus (L4–S4) gives the sciatic nerve (L4–S3), which divides into the tibial and common peroneal (fibular) nerves, plus the posterior femoral cutaneous nerve. A complete ankle block anesthetizes 5 nerves: posterior tibial, deep peroneal, superficial peroneal, sural, and saphenous. At the groin, the femoral structures run in the order NAVEL from lateral to medial: Nerve, Artery, Vein, Empty space, Lymphatics.

For the knee, the femoral nerve (with its adductor-canal/saphenous branch) covers the anterior knee, while the sciatic covers the posterior knee. An adductor-canal block preserves quadriceps strength, so it is favored for early walking after total knee replacement.
*Lumbar plexus (femoral, obturator, LFCN) + sacral plexus (sciatic); ankle block = 5 nerves; adductor canal spares the quads.*

cards.title: Trunk (Intercostal & Abdominal Wall)
secs: The intercostal nerves are the anterior rami of T1–T11 (T12 is the subcostal nerve); each runs in the costal groove just below its rib, in the order VAN — vein, artery, then nerve, from top to bottom. The abdominal wall is supplied by the thoracoabdominal nerves, T6–L1. A transversus abdominis plane (TAP) block deposits local anesthetic in the plane between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles, covering T10–L1. A rectus sheath block covers midline incisions by reaching the T9–T11 anterior cutaneous branches.

The intercostal block has the HIGHEST systemic local-anesthetic absorption of any regional block, giving high plasma levels and the greatest risk of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST), plus a risk of pneumothorax. TAP and rectus sheath blocks provide somatic (abdominal-wall) analgesia but do not cover visceral pain.
*Intercostal bundle order is VAN, inferior to the rib; intercostal block = highest LA absorption; TAP plane = internal oblique ↔ transversus abdominis.*

cards.title: Spinal, Epidural, Caudal & Combined Spinal-Epidural
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Regional Anesthesia (I.B.2)
secs: A spinal (intrathecal) injects a small dose into the CSF below the conus medullaris (insert at or below L3–L4 in adults, since the conus ends around L1); it is rapid, dense, reliable, and single-shot. An epidural places a larger volume into the epidural (potential) space; it is segmental, titratable, and catheter-based, with a slower onset, and is identified by loss of resistance. A caudal is an epidural placed through the sacral hiatus (the pediatric workhorse), and a combined spinal-epidural gives a rapid spinal onset plus an epidural catheter for prolongation.

Absolute contraindications are patient refusal, infection at the site, raised intracranial pressure, severe coagulopathy or anticoagulation, and severe hypovolemia. Pencil-point needles (Whitacre, Sprotte) cause fewer post-dural-puncture headaches than cutting (Quincke) needles.
Spinal below the conus (insert ≤ L3–L4; conus ~L1); pencil-point needles ↓ PDPH Spinal = small intrathecal dose; epidural = larger titratable catheter

*A spinal is a small intrathecal dose below the conus (fast and dense), an epidural is a larger titratable catheter, and a CSE combines both; pencil-point needles reduce post-dural-puncture headache, and coagulation contraindications must be respected.*

head: Feature
Spinal
Epidural
tables.note: Spinal injected below L2 to avoid the conus.

cards.title: Factors Influencing Onset, Duration & Termination (Regional)
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Regional Anesthesia (I.B.2)
secs: Spinal block height depends mainly on baricity and patient position, on the DOSE (the most important factor), and on CSF volume — much less on the volume or concentration injected, or on barbotage. Hyperbaric solutions sink to the dependent dermatomes, hypobaric solutions rise, and isobaric solutions stay where they are deposited. For the local anesthetic itself, onset tracks the pKa, potency tracks lipid solubility, and duration tracks protein binding; additives (epinephrine, opioids, alpha-2 agonists) prolong and intensify the block.

The block terminates by vascular absorption and redistribution — not by local metabolism — and epinephrine prolongs it by slowing that absorption. Higher blocks produce more sympathetic blockade, and so more hypotension and bradycardia.
Spinal spread ~ baricity + position + DOSE (dose most important) Onset ~ pKa; potency ~ lipid solubility; duration ~ protein binding

*Spinal spread is driven mainly by dose, baricity, and position; local-anesthetic onset tracks pKa, potency lipid solubility, and duration protein binding; additives prolong the block, which ends by vascular absorption.*

head: Property
Determined by
tables.note: Bicarbonate speeds onset; epinephrine prolongs & ↓ systemic uptake.

cards.title: Epidural Test Dose & Systemic Toxicity
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Regional Anesthesia (I.B.2)
secs: The epidural test dose is given to detect an inadvertent intravascular or intrathecal catheter before the full dose goes in. The classic test dose is 3 mL of 1.5% lidocaine with 1:200,000 epinephrine (15 mcg of epinephrine). The markers are: intravascular placement → a heart-rate rise of at least 20 bpm and/or a systolic-pressure rise within about a minute; intrathecal placement → a rapid, dense spinal block.

Beta-blockade, labor, and general anesthesia blunt the epinephrine heart-rate response, giving false negatives. Always aspirate first, dose incrementally, and stay alert for LAST even after a negative test dose.
Test dose: 3 mL 1.5% lidocaine + 15 mcg epinephrine (1:200,000) Intravascular: HR ↑ ≥ 20 bpm;  intrathecal: rapid spinal block

*The epidural test dose (3 mL of lidocaine plus 15 mcg of epinephrine) screens for intravascular placement (heart rate up ≥ 20 bpm) or intrathecal placement (a rapid spinal); aspirate and dose incrementally regardless.*

cards.title: ASRA Anticoagulation Guidelines (Neuraxial)
secs: A neuraxial or deep block risks an epidural hematoma, so follow the ASRA hold-and-restart intervals. Prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) is held 12 hours beforehand and therapeutic LMWH 24 hours; intravenous heparin is held 4–6 hours with a normal aPTT. Clopidogrel is held 5–7 days, ticagrelor 5–7 days, and warfarin until the INR is 1.5 or less; the direct oral anticoagulants are held about 72 hours (longer with renal impairment); aspirin or NSAIDs alone do NOT add risk.

Remove catheters only when the anticoagulant effect is minimal, and redose after an appropriate delay. Watch for an epidural hematoma (new back pain, a motor or sensory deficit, bowel or bladder change) — it needs urgent MRI and decompression (ideally within about 8 hours).
Prophylactic LMWH 12 h / therapeutic 24 h; IV heparin 4–6 h (normal aPTT) Clopidogrel 5–7 d; warfarin INR ≤ 1.5; DOACs ~72 h; aspirin OK alone
*Follow the ASRA hold times (LMWH 12/24 hours, clopidogrel 5–7 days, DOACs ~72 hours, warfarin INR ≤ 1.5; aspirin alone is fine), and watch for an epidural hematoma that needs urgent decompression.*

head: Agent
Hold before
Restart after
tables.note: Times lengthen with renal impairment; verify current ASRA guidance.

cards.title: Physiologic Effects of Neuraxial Block
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Regional Anesthesia (I.B.2)
secs: The sympathetic block extends about 2–6 dermatomes above the sensory level, causing vasodilation and hypotension (treat with fluids, phenylephrine or ephedrine, and leg elevation). A high block (above T4) reaches the cardiac accelerator fibers (T1–T4) and causes bradycardia — which can be profound — from unopposed vagal tone. Respiration is usually preserved until a very high block, since the phrenic nerve (C3–C5) is spared until that point.

Other effects include urinary retention, nausea (from hypotension and gut hyperperistalsis), shivering, and — with a high block — Horner’s syndrome. Neuraxial opioids add analgesia without a sympathectomy but cause pruritus and retention, and morphine causes delayed respiratory depression.
Sympathectomy 2–6 levels above sensory; cardiac accelerators T1–T4 Hypotension (vasodilation) + bradycardia (high block)
*Neuraxial block causes a sympathectomy → hypotension, and a high block (above T4) reaches the cardiac accelerators (T1–T4) → bradycardia; treat with fluids and vasopressors.*

head: System
Effect
rows: ↓ SVR & preload → hypotension; bradycardia if T1–4
Preserved unless high block reaches intercostals/diaphragm
tables.note: High block blocks cardioaccelerators (T1–4) → bradycardia/hypotension.

cards.title: IV Regional Anesthesia (Bier Block)
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Regional Anesthesia (I.B.2)
secs: A Bier block injects intravenous local anesthetic into an exsanguinated limb that is isolated by a double-cuff tourniquet, giving anesthesia distal to the cuff for short (under 60 minutes) distal-extremity procedures. Use preservative-free lidocaine — NOT bupivacaine, which is cardiotoxic if it is released into the circulation. Keep the tourniquet inflated for at least 20–30 minutes and never release it early, or a sudden bolus of local anesthetic enters the circulation (LAST).
Tourniquet pain limits the duration; the double cuff lets you inflate the distal cuff over already-anesthetized skin. The block wears off quickly once deflated, so have LAST resources ready.
Preservative-free lidocaine (no bupivacaine); tourniquet up ≥ 20–30 min
*A Bier block is intravenous lidocaine in a tourniquet-isolated limb for short distal procedures; never use bupivacaine and never deflate the cuff before about 20–30 minutes (LAST risk).*

cards.title: Transversus Abdominis Plane (TAP) Block
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Regional Anesthesia (I.B.2)
secs: A transversus abdominis plane (TAP) block deposits local anesthetic in the plane between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles, blocking the thoracoabdominal nerves (T6–L1). It provides SOMATIC (abdominal-wall) analgesia only — it does not cover visceral pain. It is ultrasound-guided and used for laparotomy, cesarean delivery, hernia repair, and laparoscopic port sites.
Large local-anesthetic volumes mean you must watch the total dose and the LAST risk (absorption is high). Subcostal, lateral, and posterior approaches change which dermatomes are covered.
Plane = internal oblique ↔ transversus abdominis; covers T6–L1 (somatic only)
*The TAP block targets the internal-oblique/transversus plane (T6–L1) for abdominal-wall somatic analgesia only — watch the total local-anesthetic dose for LAST.*

cards.title: Brachial Plexus Blocks
secs: The interscalene block covers the shoulder and proximal arm; it spares the inferior trunk (so ULNAR sparing) and causes a phrenic-nerve block (in nearly 100%, with a hemidiaphragm paresis), so avoid it in severe respiratory compromise. The supraclavicular block (the “spinal of the arm”) covers the whole arm below the shoulder (with a pneumothorax risk); the infraclavicular covers the elbow, forearm, and hand; and the axillary covers the forearm and hand (the musculocutaneous nerve is often spared — supplement it). Know the dermatomal and terminal-nerve coverage; ultrasound improves safety.
A phrenic block with the interscalene approach means caution in respiratory disease. Horner syndrome (from stellate-ganglion spread) is common and benign.
Interscalene: shoulder (ulnar spares) + phrenic block; supraclavicular: whole arm (PTX risk)

*The interscalene block covers the shoulder (ulnar-sparing) but causes a near-universal phrenic and hemidiaphragm paresis; the supraclavicular covers the whole arm (a pneumothorax risk); and the axillary often spares the musculocutaneous nerve.*

cards.title: Lower Extremity Blocks
secs: The femoral block covers the anterior thigh and knee (and, via the saphenous nerve, the medial leg); the adductor-canal (saphenous) block gives knee analgesia while SPARING the quadriceps strength (better for early ambulation). The sciatic block (proximal or popliteal) covers the posterior thigh, leg, and foot (except the medial saphenous strip); the popliteal approach is used for foot and ankle surgery. The ankle block covers foot surgery (five nerves — the posterior tibial, deep and superficial peroneal, sural, and saphenous).
Combine a femoral or adductor block with a sciatic block for the full leg and foot. The adductor canal preserves motor function compared with the femoral block (a fall risk).
Adductor canal (saphenous) = knee analgesia, quad-sparing; popliteal sciatic = foot/ankle

*Femoral and adductor-canal blocks cover the anterior knee (the adductor canal spares the quadriceps for ambulation); the sciatic (popliteal) block covers the foot and ankle except the medial saphenous strip.*

head: Block
Covers
tables.note: Whole knee needs femoral + sciatic; saphenous covers medial leg.

cards.title: Lumbar Plexus (Psoas) Block
secs: The lumbar plexus (psoas compartment) block is a deep block covering the lumbar plexus (the femoral, lateral femoral cutaneous, and obturator nerves) for hip, anterior thigh, and knee analgesia. Its depth raises the risk of epidural or intrathecal spread, a retroperitoneal hematoma, and local anesthetic systemic toxicity (from vascular uptake) — so follow the anticoagulation guidelines. It is useful for hip surgery (combined with a sciatic block for full lower-limb coverage).
Use ultrasound or nerve stimulation, aspirate, and limit the dose (a LAST risk). Bilateral spread and hypotension can occur (from neuraxial spread).

*The lumbar plexus (psoas compartment) block covers the femoral, lateral femoral cutaneous, and obturator nerves for hip and thigh surgery — but its depth raises the risks of epidural spread, a retroperitoneal hematoma, and LAST.*

cards.title: Erector Spinae Plane Block
secs: The erector spinae plane block is an ultrasound-guided injection deep to the erector spinae muscle at a transverse process; the local anesthetic spreads toward the paravertebral space and the dorsal and ventral rami for a multi-dermatomal truncal analgesia. It is used for thoracic, breast, abdominal, and rib-fracture analgesia, and is relatively easy and safe (away from the pleura and neuraxis) compared with a paravertebral block. It is a fascial-plane block (with a volume-dependent spread), and a catheter is possible.
It has a lower-risk profile but is somewhat variable and less dense than a paravertebral block. Watch the total local-anesthetic dose (a LAST risk).

*The erector spinae plane block deposits local anesthetic at the transverse process for a multi-level truncal analgesia (thoracic, breast, abdomen, ribs) — easier and safer than a paravertebral block but volume-dependent.*

cards.title: Paravertebral Block
secs: The paravertebral block is an injection into the paravertebral space alongside the spinal nerves as they exit, giving an ipsilateral, segmental (dermatomal) sensory, motor, and sympathetic block; it is excellent for breast and thoracic surgery and rib fractures. It provides analgesia comparable to an epidural with less hypotension (because it is unilateral), but it risks a pneumothorax, a vascular puncture, and (with spread) hypotension or neuraxial spread. It can be done at single or multiple levels, and a catheter allows continuous analgesia.
It is a good unilateral alternative to a thoracic epidural. Follow the anticoagulation guidelines (it is a deep block near the neuraxis).

*The paravertebral block gives a dense unilateral segmental analgesia (breast, thoracic, rib fractures) comparable to an epidural with less hypotension — but it risks a pneumothorax and a vascular puncture.*

cards.title: Quadratus Lumborum Block
secs: The quadratus lumborum block is an ultrasound-guided injection around the quadratus lumborum muscle; the local anesthetic can spread toward the paravertebral space for a broader abdominal analgesia (T7–L1) than a TAP block. It is used for abdominal, cesarean, and hip surgery, and it covers somatic and some visceral pain (with the paravertebral spread). Several approaches (anterior, posterior, lateral) affect the spread.
It uses a larger volume and a more posterior spread than a TAP block. Watch the total local-anesthetic dose (a LAST risk).
*The quadratus lumborum block spreads toward the paravertebral space for a broader abdominal analgesia (T7–L1) than a TAP block — useful for abdominal, cesarean, and hip surgery.*

cards.title: Serratus Anterior & Intercostal Blocks
secs: The serratus anterior plane block covers the lateral chest wall (the T2–T9 lateral cutaneous branches) for breast, thoracic, and rib analgesia — it is easy, superficial, and safe. The intercostal block places local anesthetic at each rib’s neurovascular bundle (in the inferior groove) for a segmental chest- and abdominal-wall analgesia; it has the HIGHEST local-anesthetic systemic absorption of the regional techniques (rapid vascular uptake — a LAST risk) plus a pneumothorax risk. Both are useful for rib fractures and thoracotomy.
Intercostal blocks need multiple injections (one per level). Watch the cumulative local-anesthetic dose (because of the high systemic absorption).
Intercostal block: highest LA systemic absorption (LAST risk) + pneumothorax

*Serratus anterior plane blocks cover the lateral chest wall (breast, ribs) safely; intercostal blocks give segmental analgesia but have the HIGHEST local-anesthetic absorption (a LAST risk) plus a pneumothorax risk.*

cards.title: Rectus Sheath Block
secs: The rectus sheath block places local anesthetic between the rectus abdominis muscle and the posterior rectus sheath, blocking the terminal branches (T7–T11) that supply the anterior abdominal wall around the midline. It is used for midline incisions (umbilical, midline laparotomy) and umbilical hernia repair, with bilateral injections. It provides somatic (not visceral) analgesia.
Use ultrasound guidance to avoid peritoneal and vascular (epigastric) puncture. Combine it with visceral analgesia for a laparotomy.
*The rectus sheath block (between the rectus muscle and the posterior sheath) covers the midline anterior abdominal wall (T7–T11) for umbilical and midline incisions — somatic analgesia only.*

cards.title: Retrobulbar/Peribulbar; Cervical Plexus; Superior Laryngeal
secs: Retrobulbar and peribulbar blocks (for eye surgery): the retrobulbar approach risks globe perforation, a retrobulbar hemorrhage, optic-nerve injury, and brainstem anesthesia (from intrathecal spread → apnea); the peribulbar approach is safer but slower. The cervical plexus block (superficial or deep) is for carotid endarterectomy and neck surgery (C2–C4); the deep block risks a phrenic or recurrent laryngeal nerve block and a vascular or intrathecal injection. The superior laryngeal, glossopharyngeal, and transtracheal blocks anesthetize the airway for an awake intubation.
Recognize brainstem anesthesia after a retrobulbar block (apnea, contralateral effects). A deep cervical block causes phrenic block and hoarseness — so avoid doing it bilaterally.

*Retrobulbar blocks (for the eye) risk globe perforation, hemorrhage, and brainstem anesthesia and apnea (peribulbar is safer); cervical-plexus blocks serve carotid endarterectomy and neck surgery; and the airway blocks (superior laryngeal, glossopharyngeal, transtracheal) enable awake intubation.*

cards.title: Ultrasound & Nerve Stimulator Guidance; Catheters
secs: Ultrasound allows real-time visualization of the needle, the nerve, and the local-anesthetic spread, improving success and reducing complications and the volume needed; there are in-plane and out-of-plane approaches. A nerve stimulator: a motor response at a low current (about 0.2–0.5 mA) indicates needle-to-nerve proximity, while injection pain or a high opening pressure suggests an intraneural placement (which is to be avoided). Continuous catheters provide prolonged analgesia — secure them and manage the infusion (monitoring for LAST and catheter migration).
Ultrasound is now the standard, combined with stimulation in some cases. Avoid an intraneural or intravascular injection (aspirate and watch the pressure).
Nerve stimulator: motor twitch at ~0.2–0.5 mA = proximity; < 0.2 mA suggests intraneural

*Ultrasound guidance (real-time visualization of the needle and spread) improves block success and safety; with nerve stimulation a twitch at about 0.2–0.5 mA indicates proximity (a twitch below about 0.2 mA suggests an intraneural position — withdraw).*

cards.title: Sensory Innervation of the Airway
secs: Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX): posterior 1/3 of tongue, vallecula, oropharynx, and tonsillar pillars — blocked by local anesthetic at the palatoglossal arch (base of the anterior tonsillar pillar). Nasopharynx and anterior nasal cavity: maxillary division of trigeminal (V2, via sphenopalatine ganglion); anterior nose also anterior ethmoidal nerve (V1). Larynx ABOVE the vocal cords (incl. superior epiglottis): internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve (SLN, from vagus). Larynx BELOW the cords and trachea: recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN, from vagus).

This map underlies awake fiberoptic intubation: glossopharyngeal block abolishes the gag reflex, SLN block anesthetizes the supraglottis, and transtracheal injection or nebulized local anesthetic covers the RLN territory. The RLN is also the MOTOR supply to all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except the cricothyroid (external SLN); bilateral RLN injury can cause airway obstruction from unopposed adduction.
IX = tongue base/vallecula; SLN internal = above cords; RLN = below cords; V2 = nasopharynx.

cards.title: Sciatic Nerve — Sensory Innervation of the Lower Limb
secs: The SCIATIC NERVE (roots L4-S3) is the largest nerve in the body and, via its terminal branches the TIBIAL and COMMON PERONEAL (fibular) nerves, supplies sensation to the POSTERIOR THIGH and essentially the ENTIRE LEG AND FOOT BELOW THE KNEE. The one major exception is a medial strip of the leg and medial foot (down to the medial malleolus/great toe) supplied by the SAPHENOUS NERVE, the terminal cutaneous branch of the femoral nerve (lumbar plexus, L3-L4). Thus a complete foot/ankle block or reliable analgesia below the knee requires blocking BOTH the sciatic and the saphenous nerves.

This anatomy explains why a popliteal sciatic block alone leaves a strip of medial sensation intact — for foot and ankle surgery you must add a saphenous (adductor-canal) block. The sciatic also carries motor fibers to the hamstrings and all muscles below the knee, so a dense block produces foot drop and impaired plantarflexion/dorsiflexion. Anterior thigh sensation is femoral, and the medial thigh is obturator — neither is sciatic.
Sciatic covers the whole leg/foot below the knee EXCEPT the medial strip, which is saphenous (femoral) — block both for the foot.

cards.title: Meralgia Paresthetica — Lateral Femoral Cutaneous Neuropathy
secs: MERALGIA PARESTHETICA is an isolated MONONEUROPATHY of the LATERAL FEMORAL CUTANEOUS NERVE (LFCN, purely SENSORY, roots L2-L3), producing DYSESTHESIA, burning, and numbness over the ANTEROLATERAL THIGH. It is typically an ENTRAPMENT/compression neuropathy where the nerve passes under or through the INGUINAL LIGAMENT near the anterior superior iliac spine. There is NO motor deficit because the nerve is purely sensory.

Common contributors include obesity, pregnancy, tight belts/clothing, diabetes, and — relevant to anesthesia/surgery — PRONE POSITIONING and iliac crest bone-graft harvest, which can compress or injure the LFCN. Recognizing the purely sensory, well-demarcated anterolateral thigh distribution distinguishes it from a femoral neuropathy (which also causes quadriceps weakness and lost patellar reflex). Most cases resolve with removing compression and weight loss; refractory cases may respond to LFCN block.
Meralgia paresthetica = compression of the purely sensory lateral femoral cutaneous nerve at the inguinal ligament → anterolateral thigh dysesthesia, no weakness.

cards.title: Mandibular Division of the Trigeminal Nerve (V3)
secs: The trigeminal nerve (CN V) has three divisions. The ophthalmic (V1) and maxillary (V2) divisions are PURELY SENSORY, while the MANDIBULAR division (V3) is the ONLY division carrying both SENSORY and MOTOR fibers. V3 motor fibers supply the MUSCLES OF MASTICATION (masseter, temporalis, medial and lateral pterygoids) plus tensor tympani, tensor veli palatini, mylohyoid, and the anterior belly of digastric. Its sensory territory includes the lower face and jaw, the lower teeth, and general (not taste) sensation to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue via the lingual nerve. The divisions exit the skull separately: V1 through the superior orbital fissure, V2 through foramen rotundum, and V3 through FORAMEN OVALE (see figures).

V3 anatomy underlies inferior alveolar and lingual nerve blocks for awake airway anesthesia and dental procedures, and it is a common site in trigeminal neuralgia. Motor function is tested by jaw clench and lateral movement; with unilateral pterygoid weakness the jaw DEVIATES TOWARD the weak (lesioned) side.
V3 (mandibular) is the only trigeminal division carrying both sensory and motor fibers, supplying the muscles of mastication and exiting the skull through foramen ovale.

noteimg.cap: Trigeminal nerve divisions (V1, V2, V3) and their sensory distributions over the face.
Branches of the mandibular division (V3), including motor branches to the muscles of mastication.

cards.title: Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Injury
secs: The RECURRENT LARYNGEAL NERVE (a branch of the vagus, CN X) provides motor innervation to ALL intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT the cricothyroid (supplied by the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve) and sensation BELOW the vocal cords. The POSTERIOR CRICOARYTENOID is the only ABDUCTOR of the vocal cords. UNILATERAL RLN injury leaves the affected cord in a paramedian position and causes HOARSENESS, partially compensated by the opposite cord. ACUTE BILATERAL RLN injury leaves both cords near the midline with unopposed adduction, producing AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION and STRIDOR that may require reintubation.

The RLN is at risk in thyroid and parathyroid surgery, anterior cervical spine, carotid, and mediastinal procedures; the LEFT RLN has a longer intrathoracic course (looping under the aortic arch) and is more vulnerable. Combined superior and recurrent laryngeal nerve injury leaves the cords flaccid in the midposition, causing aphonia.
Unilateral RLN injury causes hoarseness from a paramedian cord, while acute bilateral RLN injury causes airway obstruction and stridor, because the posterior cricoarytenoid is the sole abductor.

cards.title: Difficult Airway Assessment; Mallampati
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Airway Management (I.B.3.c)
secs: The Mallampati score (taken seated, mouth open, tongue out) is: I — soft palate, uvula, and pillars visible; II — uvula visible; III — only the base of the uvula; IV — only the hard palate; classes III and IV predict a difficult airway. Combine it with the thyromental distance (under 6 cm), mouth opening (under 3 cm), neck extension, and jaw protrusion (the upper-lip-bite test). No single test is reliable on its own — use several predictors plus the patient’s prior records.

Distinguish difficult mask ventilation, difficult intubation, difficult supraglottic placement, and difficult front-of-neck access. Anticipation drives the plan: awake versus asleep, the choice of device, and the backup.
Mallampati III–IV + thyromental < 6 cm + limited 3-3-2 predict difficulty

*Mallampati III–IV (only the base of the uvula or just the hard palate) plus a thyromental distance under 6 cm and a limited 3-3-2 predict a difficult airway — no single test suffices, so combine them.*

head: Class
Visible
tables.note: LEMON: Look, Evaluate 3-3-2, Mallampati, Obstruction, Neck mobility.

cards.title: Difficult Airway Predictors (MOANS / LEMON)
secs: Predict each difficulty separately: mask ventilation (MOANS), laryngoscopy/intubation (LEMON), supraglottic placement (RODS), and front-of-neck access (SHORT). No single test suffices — combine them and anticipate so you can choose awake versus asleep.

Independent predictors of difficult mask ventilation: age over 55, BMI over 26, a beard, edentulous, snoring/OSA, and Mallampati III–IV; impossible mask ventilation adds neck radiation, male sex, and a thick neck. When several coexist, plan an awake or video-assisted technique and ready a backup and front-of-neck plan.
*Screen every airway for difficult MASK ventilation (MOANS) and difficult INTUBATION (LEMON); when multiple predictors coexist, secure the airway awake with spontaneous ventilation preserved.*

head: Predictor
tables.note: 3-3-2: 3 fingers mouth opening, 3 hyoid–chin, 2 thyroid–hyoid.

cards.title: ASA Difficult Airway Algorithm
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Airway Management (I.B.3.c)
secs: When difficulty is anticipated, consider an AWAKE intubation (which preserves spontaneous ventilation) and optimize the plan and backup before inducing. When failure is unanticipated, optimize mask or supraglottic ventilation and LIMIT intubation attempts (no more than 3, plus one by an expert) to avoid trauma and a cannot-intubate-cannot-oxygenate situation. In a “cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate” (CICO) emergency, move immediately to front-of-neck access (cricothyrotomy).
Always have plans A, B, and C, call for help early, and use cognitive aids. Wake the patient when feasible if ventilation is adequate but intubation has failed.
Anticipated → awake technique;  CICO → front-of-neck access;  limit attempts (≤ 3+1)

*For anticipated difficulty, intubate awake; for unanticipated failure, limit attempts while continuing to oxygenate with a mask or supraglottic device, and move straight to front-of-neck access in a CICO emergency.*

head: Step
Action
rows: Attempt intubation — LIMIT to ≤ 3 (+1 by an expert)
Non-emergency: SGA, video, adjuncts → if fail, WAKE / postpone
Call for help early; confirm with capnography; keep oxygenating
tables.note: Always have plans A/B/C; wake the patient whenever ventilation is adequate.

cards.title: Video Laryngoscopy, Fiberoptic & Alternative Devices
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Airway Management (I.B.3.c)
secs: Video laryngoscopy improves the view of the glottis and the first-pass success rate (helpful with limited neck mobility or an anterior larynx); the hyperangulated blades usually need a rigid stylet to direct the tube. Flexible fiberoptic intubation is the gold standard for an anticipated difficult airway and for awake intubation (an unstable cervical spine, severe mouth-opening restriction). Useful adjuncts include the bougie, optical stylets, an intubating LMA used as a conduit, and retrograde intubation.

An awake fiberoptic intubation needs good topicalization (superior laryngeal, glossopharyngeal, and transtracheal blocks) plus light sedation. Choose the device by the pathology, the urgency, and the operator’s skill.

*Video laryngoscopy boosts first-pass success, while flexible fiberoptic is the gold standard for the anticipated or awake difficult airway; know the adjuncts (bougie, intubating LMA, retrograde intubation).*

cards.title: LMA & Supraglottic Devices
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Airway Management (I.B.3.c)
secs: Supraglottic airways sit above the glottis in the hypopharynx, giving a hands-free airway without laryngoscopy. Second-generation devices (ProSeal/Supreme, i-gel) add a gastric drain port (some aspiration protection) and achieve higher seal pressures. Their roles are routine ventilation in fasted patients, difficult-airway rescue, and use as an intubation conduit.
Cautions: a full stomach or aspiration risk, low pulmonary compliance, high airway pressures, and limited mouth opening. Complications include malposition, leak, sore throat, and rare nerve injury.
*Supraglottic airways sit above the glottis for hands-free ventilation, and second-generation devices add a gastric port; avoid them with aspiration risk, low compliance, or high airway pressures.*

head: Size
Patient weight
tables.note: Cuff inflation volume rises with size; confirm leak < 20 cmH2O.

cards.title: Cricothyroidotomy / Transtracheal Jet Ventilation
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Airway Management (I.B.3.c)
secs: Emergency front-of-neck access goes through the cricothyroid membrane in a cannot-intubate-cannot-oxygenate (CICO) situation. A surgical (scalpel–bougie–tube) cricothyrotomy is first-line in adults, while a needle cricothyrotomy with jet ventilation is a temporizing or pediatric alternative. Transtracheal jet ventilation needs a high-pressure source (around 50 psi) and a PATENT upper airway for exhalation, or barotrauma and breath-stacking result.

A surgical cricothyrotomy is avoided in children under about 6 years (use a needle technique — the cricoid is the narrowest part and stenosis is a risk). Convert to a definitive airway as soon as it is feasible.
Surgical (scalpel–bougie–tube) in adults; jet ventilation ~50 psi needs upper-airway patency
*In a CICO emergency, perform a cricothyrotomy through the cricothyroid membrane (scalpel–bougie–tube in adults); jet ventilation needs about 50 psi and a patent upper airway for exhalation.*

cards.title: Double-Lumen Tubes & Bronchial Blockers
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Airway Management (I.B.3.c)
secs: Lung isolation allows one-lung ventilation for thoracic surgery, protects the other lung from soiling or bleeding, and permits differential ventilation. Left-sided double-lumen tubes are the most common choice (the left mainstem gives a greater margin of safety); confirm placement with fiberoptic bronchoscopy. Bronchial blockers suit a difficult airway, an in-situ single-lumen tube, and children, but they collapse the lung more slowly and are easier to dislodge.

Size double-lumen tubes by height and sex; complications include malposition, airway trauma, and hypoxemia during one-lung ventilation. For one-lung-ventilation hypoxemia: add CPAP to the non-dependent lung, PEEP to the dependent lung, recruit, and recheck the tube position.
Left DLT default (greater margin of safety); confirm by fiberoptic

*Lung isolation uses a (usually left) double-lumen tube confirmed by fiberoptic, or a bronchial blocker; for one-lung-ventilation hypoxemia add non-dependent CPAP and dependent PEEP and recheck the position.*

head: Sex / height
Size
tables.note: Left DLT preferred (margin of safety); confirm with fiberoptic.

cards.title: Endotracheal Tubes, Cuff Design & Laser-Safe Tubes
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Airway Management (I.B.3.c)
secs: High-volume, low-pressure cuffs reduce mucosal ischemia; keep the cuff pressure under about 30 cmH₂O (the capillary perfusion pressure) to avoid tracheal injury. Nitrous oxide diffuses into an air-filled cuff and raises its pressure over time, so monitor and adjust it. Laser-safe (metal-wrapped) tubes resist ignition during airway laser surgery; fill the cuff with saline (with or without methylene blue) and minimize the FiO₂.

Specialized tubes include reinforced (anti-kink), RAE (preformed), and microlaryngeal tubes. Airway-fire prevention rests on a low FiO₂, no nitrous oxide, a laser-resistant tube, and team communication.
Cuff pressure < ~30 cmH2O; laser surgery → laser-safe tube, saline-filled cuff, low FiO2
*Keep cuff pressure under about 30 cmH₂O (nitrous oxide raises it over time); for airway laser surgery use a laser-safe tube with a saline-filled cuff and the lowest possible FiO₂ to prevent fire.*

cards.title: Types of Jet Ventilation: Supraglottic, Translaryngeal, Transtracheal
secs: JET VENTILATION delivers high-pressure oxygen through a narrow cannula, entraining room air by the VENTURI effect and relying on PASSIVE exhalation. It is used for shared-airway (ENT/laryngeal) surgery and as a rescue technique in the CAN'T INTUBATE, CAN'T OXYGENATE (CICO) emergency. Three delivery routes: SUPRAGLOTTIC — jet aimed from above the cords (e.g., attached to a suspension laryngoscope), giving the surgeon an unobstructed, tube-free field but poor airway protection and a risk of gastric insufflation. TRANSLARYNGEAL — a small catheter passed through the glottis into the trachea (e.g., alongside the surgical field), offering better distal delivery but the cords must remain patent for exhalation. TRANSTRACHEAL — a cannula placed percutaneously through the CRICOTHYROID MEMBRANE, the classic emergency route when the upper airway is obstructed.

The shared, feared complication of ALL jet techniques is BAROTRAUMA / TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX from breath STACKING when exhalation is impeded — the upper airway MUST remain patent for passive egress, so jetting into an OBSTRUCTED airway is dangerous. Supraglottic offers the best surgical exposure but the worst airway control and greatest gastric-insufflation/aspiration risk. Transtracheal (percutaneous transtracheal jet ventilation, PTJV) is the go-to rescue in CICO because it bypasses supraglottic obstruction, but carries risks of misplacement, subcutaneous emphysema, bleeding, and barotrauma if outflow is blocked. Capnography is unreliable during jetting, and delivered tidal volume/oxygenation depend on driving pressure, inspiratory time, and airway patency rather than a set volume. Adequate expiratory time and a low respiratory rate limit air trapping.

Typical PTJV driving pressure ~ 15-50 psi (wall O2 ~ 50 psi); use a large-bore (14-16 G) catheter for transtracheal rescue; keep inspiratory time short (~1 sec) with a long expiratory phase to prevent stacking.

Jet ventilation (supraglottic, translaryngeal, transtracheal) needs a PATENT upper airway for passive exhalation; transtracheal is the CICO rescue route, and the dreaded complication of all types is barotrauma/pneumothorax from breath stacking.

cards.title: Peritonsillar Abscess vs Epiglottitis
secs: Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) is the most common deep neck space infection; GROUP A beta-hemolytic Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes) is the most common cause, though infections are often polymicrobial. It typically affects adolescents and young adults with severe sore throat, TRISMUS, a muffled hot-potato voice, drooling, and uvular deviation AWAY from the abscess. Compared with epiglottitis, airway compromise from a peritonsillar abscess is LESS common because the process is supraglottic and usually unilateral.

Contrast with EPIGLOTTITIS, a true airway EMERGENCY with rapidly progressive supraglottic obstruction (classically Haemophilus influenzae type b in unvaccinated children, increasingly Streptococcus): keep the child calm, avoid airway instrumentation, and secure the airway in the OR with an inhalational induction preserving SPONTANEOUS ventilation and ENT standing by for rigid bronchoscopy or tracheostomy. For a peritonsillar abscess, trismus may limit mouth opening, and the key anesthetic hazards are abscess RUPTURE with soiling of the airway during induction; definitive care is needle aspiration or incision and drainage plus antibiotics.

*Group A Streptococcus is the most common cause of peritonsillar abscess (uvular deviation, trismus, hot-potato voice); airway compromise is LESS common than in epiglottitis, but trismus and abscess RUPTURE are the main anesthetic concerns.*

cards.title: ASA Difficult Airway Algorithm
secs: The ASA Difficult Airway Algorithm guides management when mask ventilation, supraglottic ventilation, or intubation proves difficult (see figure). Core principles: ASSESS the airway and optimize positioning and preoxygenation BEFORE induction, and when difficulty is anticipated, favor an AWAKE intubation with spontaneous ventilation preserved. If difficulty is encountered after induction, call for help, consider returning to spontaneous ventilation or awakening the patient, and above all MAINTAIN oxygenation. The critical branch point is whether oxygenation is adequate.

"Can't intubate but CAN oxygenate" is a non-emergency pathway that buys time for alternatives — video laryngoscopy, a supraglottic airway, or flexible fiberoptic intubation. "CAN'T intubate, CAN'T oxygenate" (CICO) is an EMERGENCY: place a supraglottic airway, and if that fails proceed without delay to emergency invasive front-of-neck airway (cricothyrotomy). Limit intubation attempts (about 3, plus one by a more experienced operator) to avoid trauma and the can't-ventilate spiral; use apneic oxygenation to extend safe apnea time; and pre-formulate and communicate a plan A/B/C with the difficult-airway cart ready. Extubation of the difficult airway also needs a strategy, such as an airway exchange catheter.

*When a difficult airway is anticipated, intubate AWAKE with spontaneous ventilation; the algorithm's crux is OXYGENATION — can't-intubate-can't-oxygenate demands a supraglottic airway and, if that fails, emergency front-of-neck cricothyrotomy.*

cards.title: Cormack-Lehane Laryngeal View Grading
secs: The Cormack-Lehane (CL) system grades the view of the glottis obtained DURING direct laryngoscopy (see figure). Grade 1: most of the glottis and vocal cords are visible. Grade 2: only the posterior glottis is seen — 2a shows part of the cords, 2b shows only the arytenoids or the epiglottis with no cords. Grade 3: only the EPIGLOTTIS is visible with no glottis (3a the epiglottis can be lifted, 3b it is adherent to the pharynx). Grade 4: neither glottis nor epiglottis is seen, only the soft palate. Higher grades (3-4) predict a DIFFICULT intubation.

Unlike the Mallampati score — a PREOPERATIVE bedside predictor graded I-IV on mouth opening — the Cormack-Lehane grade describes the ACTUAL laryngoscopic view, so it is assigned only at the time of laryngoscopy. Grades 3-4 correlate with difficult or failed intubation; optimize the view with external laryngeal manipulation (BURP: Backward-Upward-Rightward Pressure), better sniffing-position alignment, a bougie, or video laryngoscopy. The POGO score (Percentage Of Glottic Opening) is an alternative way to describe the view.

*Cormack-Lehane grades the DIRECT laryngoscopy view (1 = full cords, 4 = not even epiglottis); grades 3-4 signal a difficult airway — improve it with BURP, a bougie, or video laryngoscopy. Mallampati predicts; Cormack-Lehane describes.*

cards.title: Pharmacokinetics & Pharmacodynamics
secs: Pharmacokinetics (PK) is what the body does to the drug — absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME). Pharmacodynamics (PD) is what the drug does to the body — receptor binding, the dose–response relationship, efficacy, and potency. The core PK parameters are the volume of distribution (Vd), clearance (Cl), and half-life (t½).

The loading dose depends on the volume of distribution, while the maintenance (infusion) rate depends on clearance. Key PD ideas include the ED50, the therapeutic index, agonist versus antagonist, and the distinction between efficacy and potency.
Vd = dose / plasma concentration;  t-half = 0.693 × Vd / Cl Loading dose = Vd × target conc.;  infusion rate = Cl × target conc.
*PK is ADME (Vd, Cl, t½) and PD is the drug’s effect; set the loading dose by the volume of distribution and the maintenance rate by clearance (t½ = 0.693·Vd/Cl).*

cards.title: Protein Binding, pKa & Ionization
secs: Only the unbound (free) fraction of a drug is pharmacologically active and able to cross cell membranes. The pKa is the pH at which a drug is 50% ionized; the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation sets the ratio of ionized to unionized drug at any pH. The unionized (lipophilic) form crosses membranes, so a local anesthetic acts faster when more of it is unionized (its pKa is closer to body pH).

Acidic drugs bind mainly to albumin and basic drugs to alpha-1-acid glycoprotein; binding shifts in uremia, pregnancy, and burns, changing the free fraction. Ion trapping makes weak bases concentrate in acidic compartments — local anesthetic in an acidotic fetus, or drugs in gastric fluid.
pKa = pH at 50% ionization (Henderson-Hasselbalch) Unionized fraction crosses membranes; pKa near pH → faster onset
*Only free, unionized drug is active and crosses membranes; the pKa is the pH of 50% ionization, and ion trapping concentrates weak bases in acidic compartments such as the acidotic fetus.*

cards.title: Compartment Models & Exponential Decay
secs: Most intravenous anesthetics fit a two- or three-compartment model: a rapid distribution phase (alpha) followed by slower elimination (beta). After a bolus, the plasma concentration falls first by REDISTRIBUTION into peripheral tissues, and only later by elimination. Each phase is exponential, described by its own rate constant and half-life.
It is redistribution — not metabolism — that ends the effect of a single bolus of thiopental or propofol. Repeated doses saturate the peripheral compartments, so the effect becomes prolonged.
Multi-compartment: distribution (alpha) then elimination (beta) exponentials Single-bolus offset = redistribution, not metabolism
*Intravenous drugs follow multi-compartment exponential kinetics; a single bolus wears off by REDISTRIBUTION rather than metabolism, so repeated dosing accumulates and prolongs the effect.*

cards.title: Pharmacokinetics of Neuraxial Drug Administration
secs: How a neuraxial drug behaves depends on its lipophilicity: lipophilic opioids (fentanyl, sufentanil) act quickly, over a few segments, and only briefly. Hydrophilic opioids (morphine) linger and spread rostrally (headward) in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), giving a delayed, prolonged effect and a risk of late respiratory depression. Intrathecal (spinal) doses are far smaller than epidural doses, because there is no dura to cross.

Intrathecal morphine can cause delayed respiratory depression peaking around 6–12 hours later, as it spreads rostrally in the CSF. Epidural drug is also absorbed systemically into the bloodstream, and baricity plus patient position determine how a spinal dose spreads.
Lipophilic (fentanyl) → fast, segmental, short Hydrophilic (morphine) → rostral CSF spread, delayed/prolonged
*Lipophilic neuraxial opioids act fast and segmentally; hydrophilic morphine spreads rostrally in the CSF, giving a delayed, prolonged effect and the risk of late respiratory depression.*

cards.title: Tolerance & Tachyphylaxis
secs: Tolerance is a reduced effect after repeated or prolonged exposure, so higher doses are needed; it develops over days to weeks (for example, with opioids). Tachyphylaxis is a rapid form of tolerance that appears after just a few doses over a short time (for example, ephedrine or hydralazine). The mechanisms include receptor downregulation or desensitization, or depletion of a needed mediator.
Ephedrine’s tachyphylaxis comes from depleting the presynaptic norepinephrine it relies on as an indirect-acting drug. Opioid tolerance and physical dependence are distinct from addiction.
*Tolerance develops gradually (as with opioids), while tachyphylaxis appears rapidly over a few doses (ephedrine, via norepinephrine depletion).*

cards.title: Context-Sensitive Half-Time
secs: The context-sensitive half-time (CSHT) is the time for the plasma concentration to fall by 50% AFTER an infusion is stopped, and it depends on how long the infusion ran (the “context”). It lengthens with infusion duration because the peripheral compartments fill up and then diffuse drug back into the plasma. Remifentanil’s CSHT stays short and flat (~3–4 minutes) no matter how long it runs (it’s cleared by ester hydrolysis), whereas fentanyl’s climbs steeply with a prolonged infusion.
CSHT guides drug choice when you give a long infusion and need a rapid offset. The elimination half-life is a poor predictor of recovery after an infusion — the CSHT is the better measure.
CSHT = time for 50% plasma drop after stopping an infusion (depends on duration) Remifentanil CSHT ~3–4 min (constant); fentanyl climbs with duration
*The context-sensitive half-time is the time to a 50% drop after stopping an infusion, and it grows with infusion duration; remifentanil stays ~3–4 minutes while fentanyl climbs steeply.*

head: Drug
After long infusion
tables.note: Time for 50% ↓ after stopping; lengthens with infusion duration.

cards.title: Biotransformation; Renal & Hepatic Disease
secs: Phase I reactions (oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis — mostly via the cytochrome P450 system) and Phase II reactions (conjugation, such as glucuronidation) make drugs more water-soluble so they can be excreted. Hepatic clearance depends either on liver blood flow (for high-extraction drugs) or on enzyme capacity (for low-extraction drugs). Renal failure prolongs renally cleared drugs and their active metabolites — morphine to M6G, meperidine to normeperidine.
In organ failure, prefer drugs with organ-independent metabolism: cisatracurium (Hofmann elimination) and remifentanil (plasma esterases).
Phase I (P450) then Phase II (conjugation) → water-soluble excretion Organ-independent: cisatracurium (Hofmann), remifentanil (esterases)
*Phase I (P450) and Phase II conjugation prepare drugs for excretion; in organ failure choose Hofmann- or ester-cleared agents, and remember renal failure accumulates M6G and normeperidine.*

head: Phase
Reactions
Result
tables.note: Age, hepatic, and renal disease slow clearance.

cards.title: Enzyme Induction & Inhibition
secs: Enzyme inducers (chronic alcohol, phenytoin, carbamazepine, rifampin, St. John’s wort) increase P450 activity, speeding metabolism and lowering drug levels. Enzyme inhibitors (cimetidine, macrolide antibiotics, grapefruit juice, azole antifungals) slow metabolism, raising drug levels toward toxicity. Induction takes days to weeks (new enzyme must be synthesized), whereas inhibition is often immediate.
Chronic alcohol use raises anesthetic requirements (induction plus tolerance), while acute intoxication lowers them. These interactions matter especially for warfarin, opioids, and benzodiazepines.
Inducers ↑ metabolism (slow onset, days);  inhibitors ↓ metabolism (fast)
*Inducers (phenytoin, rifampin, chronic alcohol) speed metabolism over days, while inhibitors (cimetidine, macrolides, grapefruit) raise drug levels quickly.*

cards.title: Alternative & Herbal Medicines (Perioperative)
secs: The “G” herbs raise bleeding risk: garlic, ginkgo, ginger, and ginseng (and fish oil). Ginseng can also lower blood glucose and interact with warfarin, and St. John’s wort induces P450 while adding a serotonin-syndrome risk. Ephedra (ma huang) causes hypertension and arrhythmias, while kava and valerian potentiate GABA and sedatives and can prolong anesthetic effect.
The ASA suggests stopping many herbal products about 1–2 weeks before surgery. Ask specifically about supplements, since patients often don’t volunteer them.
Bleeding “G”s: garlic, ginkgo, ginger, ginseng (+ fish oil) St. John’s wort = P450 inducer + serotonin-syndrome risk
*The “G” herbs and fish oil increase bleeding, St. John’s wort induces P450 (and risks serotonin syndrome), and kava/valerian prolong sedation — stop most herbals about 1–2 weeks before surgery.*

head: Herb
Effect
tables.note: Stop most herbals ~1–2 weeks before surgery.

cards.title: Drug Reactions (Anaphylaxis, Idiosyncratic)
secs: Anaphylaxis is an IgE-mediated (Type I) hypersensitivity in which mast cells and basophils release histamine and tryptase; anaphylactoid reactions are not IgE-mediated but look clinically identical. The leading perioperative triggers are neuromuscular blockers (rocuronium, succinylcholine), antibiotics (beta-lactams), chlorhexidine, and latex. Under anesthesia the signs are cardiovascular collapse (often the first sign), bronchospasm, high airway pressures, flushing, and urticaria.

The first-line treatment is EPINEPHRINE, plus fluids and removal of the trigger, while securing the airway and supporting the circulation. Confirm later with a serum tryptase level (it peaks ~1–2 hours afterward) and refer the patient for allergy testing.
IgE (Type I) anaphylaxis vs non-IgE anaphylactoid (clinically similar) Treat with epinephrine; confirm with serum tryptase

*Anaphylaxis (IgE) and anaphylactoid reactions (non-IgE) look alike; neuromuscular blockers, antibiotics, chlorhexidine, and latex are the top triggers — treat with epinephrine and confirm with serum tryptase.*

head: Step
Detail
tables.note: Top triggers: NMBs (roc, succ), antibiotics, latex, chlorhexidine. Send tryptase.

cards.title: Volatile Anesthetics: Mechanism of Action
secs: Volatile anesthetics enhance inhibitory channels (GABA-A and glycine) and inhibit excitatory ones (NMDA and neuronal nicotinic), and they also activate two-pore potassium channels. The Meyer–Overton correlation links lipid solubility (the oil:gas coefficient) to potency (MAC), implying the drugs act at a hydrophobic site. Nitrous oxide and xenon work mainly by blocking NMDA receptors rather than through GABA.
The immobility volatiles produce is largely a SPINAL CORD effect, whereas amnesia and unconsciousness are supraspinal (brain) effects.
↑ GABA-A & glycine;  ↓ NMDA & nicotinic;  Meyer-Overton: lipid solubility ∝ potency
*Volatiles potentiate GABA-A and glycine while inhibiting NMDA and nicotinic receptors; potency tracks lipid solubility (Meyer–Overton), and the immobility they cause is a spinal-cord effect.*

cards.title: Volatile Anesthetics: Systemic Effects
secs: On the brain, volatiles lower the cerebral metabolic rate (CMRO₂) dose-dependently yet cause cerebral VASODILATION, raising cerebral blood flow (CBF) and intracranial pressure (ICP) — an uncoupling — and above 1 MAC they abolish autoregulation. On the heart and vessels, they dose-dependently lower systemic vascular resistance and depress the myocardium, lowering mean arterial pressure. On breathing, they reduce tidal volume, raise the rate, blunt the ventilatory response to CO₂ and hypoxia, and cause bronchodilation.

Halothane sensitizes the myocardium to catecholamines (arrhythmias), while sevoflurane is the least pungent and so best for an inhalational induction. All volatiles can trigger malignant hyperthermia and relax the uterus in a dose-dependent way.
↑ CBF/ICP with ↓ CMRO2 (uncoupling); dose-dependent ↓ MAP and respiratory depression
*Volatiles raise CBF and ICP while lowering CMRO₂ (uncoupling), depress the heart and breathing dose-dependently, relax the uterus, and can trigger malignant hyperthermia.*

head: System
Effect
rows: ↓ BP (↓ SVR), ↓ CO; iso/des can ↑ HR
Uterine relaxation; trigger MH; sevo → Compound A

cards.title: Minimum Alveolar Concentration (MAC)
secs: MAC (minimum alveolar concentration) is the alveolar concentration that prevents movement to a standard surgical stimulus in 50% of patients — an ED50, and inversely related to potency. MAC-awake is about 0.3–0.4 MAC, and MAC-BAR (which blocks the autonomic response) is about 1.3 MAC. MAC values are ADDITIVE — 0.5 MAC of nitrous oxide plus 0.5 MAC of sevoflurane together act as about 1 MAC.

MAC is RAISED by infancy (it peaks around 6 months), hyperthermia, chronic alcohol use, stimulants, red hair, and hypernatremia. MAC is LOWERED by old age, hypothermia, pregnancy, acute alcohol, opioids and sedatives, lithium, and alpha-2 agonists.
MAC values: N2O 104%, des ~6%, sevo ~2%, iso ~1.2%, halothane ~0.75% MAC-awake ~0.3–0.4 MAC; MAC-BAR ~1.3 MAC; MACs are additive
*MAC is the ED50 for immobility (inversely related to potency) and is additive; it’s raised by young age, hyperthermia, and alcohol, and lowered by old age, opioids, pregnancy, and hypothermia.*

tables.cap: MAC of Common Volatiles (40 yr, 1 atm)
head: Agent
MAC %
tables.note: MAC ↓ ~6%/decade after 40; additive; ↓ by opioids, α2-agonists, hypothermia, pregnancy.

cards.title: Volatile Biotransformation & Toxicity
secs: The fraction metabolized differs widely: halothane ~20% (the most) > sevoflurane ~5% > isoflurane ~0.2% > desflurane ~0.02% (the least). Halothane hepatitis is an immune-mediated hepatotoxicity, triggered when an oxidative metabolite haptenizes liver proteins (trifluoroacetyl adducts). Sevoflurane produces inorganic fluoride and Compound A (with a dry absorbent); the historic agent methoxyflurane caused fluoride-mediated kidney injury.
The agents metabolized least (desflurane, isoflurane) carry the least risk of organ toxicity. All volatiles can trigger malignant hyperthermia.
*Halothane is metabolized the most (and causes immune hepatitis) and desflurane the least; sevoflurane yields fluoride and Compound A — and every volatile can trigger malignant hyperthermia.*

head: Agent
Metabolized
Toxicity
tables.note: More metabolism → more organ toxicity.

cards.title: Environmental Impact of Anesthetic Gases
secs: Volatile agents and nitrous oxide are greenhouse gases; DESFLURANE has by far the highest global-warming potential, followed by isoflurane, with sevoflurane lower. Nitrous oxide is also ozone-depleting and persists in the atmosphere for a very long time. Mitigation includes low fresh-gas flows, limiting desflurane and nitrous oxide, choosing total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA) or regional where appropriate, and using capture/recycling systems.
Reducing this footprint (“green anesthesia”) is a growing focus of practice and quality improvement.
Global-warming potential: desflurane >> isoflurane > sevoflurane; N2O also ozone-depleting
*Desflurane has the highest global-warming potential and nitrous oxide depletes ozone; reduce fresh-gas flows, limit desflurane and nitrous oxide, and consider TIVA or regional techniques.*

cards.title: Opioids
secs: Opioids act at mu, kappa, and delta G-protein-coupled receptors, reducing neurotransmitter release presynaptically and hyperpolarizing neurons postsynaptically, giving both spinal and supraspinal analgesia. Their effects are analgesia, dose-dependent respiratory depression (a blunted response to CO₂ — the cause of overdose death), miosis, bradycardia, sedation, nausea, constipation (which never develops tolerance), pruritus, and chest-wall rigidity after rapid high-dose fentanyl. Metabolism pearls: remifentanil is broken down by plasma esterases (ultra-short acting), morphine forms the active metabolite M6G (which accumulates in renal failure), and meperidine forms normeperidine (which can cause seizures).

Naloxone reverses opioids but is short-acting, so watch for re-narcotization; avoid meperidine with monoamine-oxidase inhibitors (serotonin syndrome). Neuraxial morphine causes delayed respiratory depression as it spreads rostrally in the CSF. Remifentanil’s ester hydrolysis is mature even in neonates, so its clearance is as fast as (or faster than) in older children — unlike opioids that depend on immature neonatal hepatic metabolism.
mu/kappa/delta GPCRs; respiratory depression via blunted CO2 response Remifentanil → esterases; morphine → M6G (renal); meperidine → normeperidine (seizures)
*Opioids act at mu, kappa, and delta receptors; their lethal effect is respiratory depression from a blunted CO₂ response, and M6G and normeperidine accumulate in renal failure.*

tables.cap: Opioid Equianalgesic Doses (≈ 10 mg IV morphine)
head: Opioid
IV
PO
tables.note: Incomplete cross-tolerance → reduce new opioid 25–50%.

cards.title: Barbiturates
secs: Barbiturates enhance GABA-A by prolonging the DURATION of chloride-channel opening, and at high doses they open the channel directly. Thiopental has a rapid onset and offset (by redistribution) and lowers CMRO₂, CBF, and ICP (useful for cerebral protection and burst suppression), but it is a potent venodilator and myocardial depressant. It causes dose-dependent respiratory depression and provides NO analgesia (and can even be hyperalgesic at low doses).

Barbiturates are contraindicated in porphyria, because they induce ALA synthase and can precipitate an acute attack. Intra-arterial injection causes severe vasospasm and tissue necrosis, and the dose should be reduced in hypovolemia.
GABA-A: ↑ DURATION of chloride-channel opening;  ↓ CMRO2/CBF/ICP Contraindicated in acute intermittent porphyria
*Barbiturates prolong GABA-A channel opening, lower CMRO₂/CBF/ICP (cerebral protection), wear off by redistribution, provide no analgesia, and are contraindicated in porphyria.*

cards.title: Propofol
secs: Propofol is a GABA-A agonist (it increases the FREQUENCY of channel opening) with a rapid onset, quick redistribution, and fast clearance — making it ideal for TIVA. It lowers CMRO₂, CBF, and ICP and is antiemetic, but it is a potent vasodilator and myocardial depressant (causing hypotension), a respiratory depressant, and painful on injection. It is formulated in a lipid emulsion (egg lecithin and soybean oil) that supports bacterial growth, so strict asepsis and discard timing matter.

Propofol infusion syndrome — from a prolonged, high-dose infusion — causes metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, and cardiac failure (especially in the critically ill and in children). It reduces but does not abolish postoperative nausea and vomiting, and it provides no analgesia.
GABA-A (↑ opening FREQUENCY);  ↓ CMRO2/CBF/ICP;  antiemetic PRIS: prolonged high-dose → acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac failure
*Propofol is a fast-acting GABA-A agonist that is antiemetic and lowers ICP but causes hypotension; beware propofol infusion syndrome with prolonged high-dose infusions.*

head: Agent
Dose
Hemodynamics
tables.note: Etomidate for unstable; ketamine bronchodilates; propofol is antiemetic.

cards.title: Etomidate
secs: Etomidate is a GABA-A agonist notable for its HEMODYNAMIC STABILITY (minimal myocardial depression), which makes it a favorite for unstable or cardiac patients. It lowers CMRO₂, CBF, and ICP while maintaining cerebral perfusion pressure. It suppresses the adrenal cortex (by inhibiting 11-beta-hydroxylase) even after a single induction dose, and it causes myoclonus, injection pain, and a high rate of postoperative nausea.

Avoid etomidate infusions because of the adrenal suppression, and use caution in sepsis (where its outcome impact is debated). It provides no analgesia and is useful for electroconvulsive therapy and for rapid-sequence induction in shock.
GABA-A; hemodynamically stable;  inhibits 11-beta-hydroxylase → adrenal suppression
*Etomidate is the hemodynamically stable induction agent (good for an unstable heart) but it suppresses adrenal cortisol even after a single dose and causes myoclonus.*

cards.title: Benzodiazepines & Antagonism
secs: Benzodiazepines enhance GABA-A by increasing the FREQUENCY of chloride-channel opening, producing anxiolysis, anterograde amnesia, sedation, anticonvulsant action, and muscle relaxation, with minimal analgesia. Midazolam is water-soluble and short-acting (and P450-metabolized), while diazepam and lorazepam are longer-acting. On their own they have a ceiling on respiratory depression, but combined with opioids the respiratory depression is SYNERGISTIC.

Flumazenil is a competitive antagonist that reverses them, but it is short-acting (risking re-sedation) and can precipitate seizures in chronic users or in tricyclic-antidepressant overdose. They are used for premedication, procedural sedation, and status epilepticus.
GABA-A: ↑ FREQUENCY of channel opening (barbiturates ↑ DURATION) Flumazenil = competitive antagonist (short-acting; seizure risk in dependence)

*Benzodiazepines raise the FREQUENCY of GABA-A channel opening (barbiturates raise the duration); they synergize with opioids for respiratory depression, and flumazenil reverses them but risks re-sedation and seizures.*

cards.title: Ketamine
secs: Ketamine is an NMDA-receptor antagonist that produces dissociative anesthesia, and it also acts at opioid and monoaminergic sites. It is a potent analgesic and bronchodilator and relatively preserves airway reflexes and respiratory drive. As an indirect sympathomimetic it raises heart rate, blood pressure, and cardiac output (useful in hypovolemia or shock), though it is a direct myocardial depressant when catecholamine stores are already depleted.

It raises CBF, CMRO₂, and ICP (the classic caution, though now debated), increases secretions (give an antisialagogue), and causes emergence delirium (mitigated with a benzodiazepine). Uses include induction in shock or asthma, sub-anesthetic analgesia and opioid sparing, procedural sedation, and chronic pain or depression.
NMDA antagonist; indirect sympathomimetic (↑ HR/BP/CO) ↑ CBF/CMRO2/ICP; bronchodilator; preserves airway/respiration

*Ketamine is an NMDA antagonist giving dissociative anesthesia with analgesia and bronchodilation; it’s sympathomimetic (good in shock or asthma) but raises ICP and causes secretions and emergence delirium.*

head: System
Effect
tables.note: Useful in shock/asthma; give an antisialagogue + benzodiazepine.

cards.title: Dexmedetomidine
secs: Dexmedetomidine is a highly selective central alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that acts at the locus coeruleus to produce sedation, anxiolysis, analgesia, and sympatholysis. It gives an arousable, “cooperative” sedation with very little respiratory depression — useful for awake fiberoptic intubation, ICU sedation, and procedural sedation. It causes dose-dependent bradycardia and hypotension, although a rapid loading bolus can produce a transient hypertension (from peripheral alpha-2B vasoconstriction).
It spares opioids and other anesthetics and reduces emergence delirium and shivering. It does not give reliable amnesia, and it should be avoided in heart block or severe bradycardia.
Selective alpha-2 agonist; sedation/analgesia with minimal respiratory depression Bradycardia + hypotension (transient HTN with a bolus)

*Dexmedetomidine is a selective alpha-2 agonist giving arousable sedation and analgesia with little respiratory depression; expect bradycardia and hypotension, with transient hypertension after a rapid bolus.*

head: Property
Detail
tables.note: “Cooperative sedation”; good for awake fiberoptic; no reliable amnesia.

cards.title: Local Anesthetics: Mechanism & Uptake
secs: Local anesthetics block voltage-gated sodium channels from INSIDE the cell, binding the open/inactivated state to stop depolarization and nerve conduction. The drug must be unionized to cross the membrane and then ionized to bind inside, so onset is faster when more of it is unionized (a pKa nearer physiologic pH).
