Aminoesters (one “i”: procaine, chloroprocaine, tetracaine) are broken down by plasma pseudocholinesterase to PABA, which can cause allergy, whereas aminoamides (two “i”s: lidocaine, bupivacaine, ropivacaine) are metabolized hepatically and rarely cause allergy.

Potency tracks lipid solubility, duration tracks protein binding, and onset tracks pKa. A differential block develops (autonomic blocked before sensory before motor), and adding epinephrine prolongs the block and reduces systemic absorption.

Block Na+ channels (inactivated state) from inside the cell Potency ~ lipid solubility; duration ~ protein binding; onset ~ pKa Esters (1 “i”, pseudocholinesterase, PABA allergy) vs amides (2 “i”s, hepatic)

*Local anesthetics block sodium channels from inside the cell; potency tracks lipid solubility, duration protein binding, and onset pKa — count the “i”s to separate esters (PABA allergy) from amides.*

head: Agent
Plain
+ Epi
tables.note: Amides (2 i’s) → hepatic; esters → plasma cholinesterase.

cards.title: Cauda Equina Syndrome & Transient Neurologic Symptoms
secs: Cauda equina syndrome (CES) is a serious neurologic injury: bowel and bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, and lower-extremity weakness. It is classically linked to continuous spinal microcatheters combined with hyperbaric 5% lidocaine, where pooling and maldistribution of the drug cause neurotoxicity. Transient neurologic symptoms (TNS) are back, buttock, and leg pain after a spinal that resolve within days; the risk is highest with spinal LIDOCAINE, the lithotomy position, and early ambulation — and it is NOT a structural injury.

Reduce the risk by avoiding high-concentration intrathecal lidocaine and ensuring the drug distributes well. CES needs urgent neurosurgical evaluation, whereas TNS is self-limited and treated with NSAIDs.

*Cauda equina syndrome (saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder loss) is linked to 5% lidocaine plus spinal microcatheters; transient neurologic symptoms are short-lived pain after spinal lidocaine and are not a structural injury.*

cards.title: Methemoglobinemia
secs: Methemoglobinemia is the oxidation of hemoglobin iron from the ferrous (Fe²⁺) to the ferric (Fe³⁺) state, which cannot bind oxygen and left-shifts the remaining hemoglobin — producing a functional anemia and tissue hypoxia. Anesthetic triggers include benzocaine (topical sprays), prilocaine (and EMLA cream in neonates), dapsone, and nitrates. It presents as cyanosis that does not improve with oxygen, chocolate-brown blood, and an SpO₂ stuck near 85% regardless of the true saturation (a “saturation gap”, with a normal PaO₂). Congenital methemoglobinemia (NADH–cytochrome-b5 reductase deficiency or hemoglobin M) leaves patients chronically predisposed — avoid oxidizing drugs entirely (prilocaine, benzocaine, dapsone, nitrates).
Diagnose it with co-oximetry, and treat with methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg IV. Avoid methylene blue in G6PD deficiency (it can cause hemolysis) — use ascorbic acid or exchange transfusion instead.
Hgb Fe2+ → Fe3+ (cannot carry O2); SpO2 plateaus ~85% Treat: methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg IV (avoid in G6PD deficiency)

*Methemoglobinemia (benzocaine, prilocaine) causes oxygen-refractory cyanosis with the SpO₂ stuck near 85%; confirm with co-oximetry and treat with methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg — but not in G6PD deficiency.*

cards.title: Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST)
secs: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) follows high systemic local-anesthetic plasma levels and produces progressive central-nervous-system then cardiovascular toxicity. The CNS prodrome is perioral numbness, tinnitus, a metallic taste, and agitation, progressing to seizures and then CNS depression or coma. Cardiovascular toxicity comes later and is more ominous: hypotension, conduction block, arrhythmias, and arrest — and BUPIVACAINE is the most cardiotoxic agent because it binds sodium channels “fast-in, slow-out”.

The risk is highest with an intravascular injection or a high-absorption block (intercostal > caudal > epidural > brachial plexus). Prevent it by aspirating, using a test dose, injecting incrementally, using ultrasound, and giving the lowest effective dose.
CNS first (tinnitus, perioral numbness, seizures) → then CV collapse Bupivacaine = most cardiotoxic (fast-in, slow-out Na-channel binding)
*LAST is CNS first (tinnitus, perioral numbness, seizures) then cardiovascular collapse; bupivacaine is the most cardiotoxic agent — prevent it with aspiration, a test dose, and incremental dosing.*

head: Stage
Features
tables.note: Bupivacaine most cardiotoxic; treat with 20% lipid emulsion + ACLS.

cards.title: ASRA LAST Management Checklist
secs: At the first sign of LAST, stop injecting, call for help and the lipid kit, and manage the airway with 100% oxygen (hypoxia and acidosis worsen the toxicity). Control seizures with a benzodiazepine, avoiding large propofol doses if the patient is hemodynamically unstable. Give 20% lipid emulsion early; for cardiac arrest use SMALL epinephrine doses (≤ 1 mcg/kg) and AVOID vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and additional local anesthetics.

Prolonged CPR may be needed because bupivacaine is “slow-out”, and refractory arrest may warrant cardiopulmonary bypass or ECMO. Monitor for at least 4–6 hours after a significant event, since toxicity can recur as the drug redistributes.
Airway/O2 + benzodiazepine for seizures + 20% lipid emulsion Reduced epinephrine (≤ 1 mcg/kg); AVOID vasopressin, CCB, beta-blockers, lidocaine

*Manage LAST with airway/oxygen, a benzodiazepine for seizures, early lipid emulsion, and REDUCED epinephrine while avoiding vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, and lidocaine — and be ready for prolonged resuscitation.*

cards.title: IV Lipid Emulsion for LAST
secs: 20% lipid emulsion works as a “lipid sink” that sequesters lipophilic local anesthetic out of the tissues, plus it has metabolic and cardiotonic effects. Give a bolus of 1.5 mL/kg (lean body weight) over about a minute, then start an infusion of 0.25 mL/kg/min. If the patient stays unstable, re-bolus once or twice and/or double the infusion to 0.5 mL/kg/min.
The approximate upper limit is about 12 mL/kg total. Continue the infusion for at least 10 minutes after the circulation has stabilized.
Bolus 1.5 mL/kg (LBW) of 20% lipid, then 0.25 mL/kg/min Re-bolus / double infusion (0.5 mL/kg/min) if unstable; max ~12 mL/kg
*For LAST, give 20% lipid emulsion as a 1.5 mL/kg bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min, escalating (re-bolus or 0.5 mL/kg/min) if the patient stays unstable, up to about 12 mL/kg total.*

cards.title: Depolarizing vs Non-depolarizing Blockade
secs: Succinylcholine (depolarizing) activates nicotinic acetylcholine receptors to cause sustained depolarization — fasciculations followed by flaccid paralysis — with a fast onset (~30–60 s) and short duration (~5–10 min, ended by plasma pseudocholinesterase); it shows NO fade and NO post-tetanic potentiation. Non-depolarizers (rocuronium, vecuronium, cisatracurium) are competitive antagonists with a slower onset and longer duration, and they DO show fade and post-tetanic potentiation. Succinylcholine’s adverse effects include hyperkalemia (exaggerated with burns, denervation, or immobility, where extrajunctional receptors are upregulated), bradycardia, myalgias, raised intracranial/intraocular/intragastric pressure, malignant-hyperthermia triggering, and masseter spasm.

A phase II block (behaving like a non-depolarizing block) develops with high or repeated succinylcholine dosing. Avoid succinylcholine where receptors are upregulated: burns more than 24–48 hours old, spinal cord injury, prolonged immobilization, and the muscular dystrophies.
Sux: depolarizing, ~30–60 s onset, ~5–10 min; no fade / no post-tetanic potentiation Non-depolarizing: competitive, fade + post-tetanic potentiation

*Succinylcholine depolarizes (fast, short, no fade, with malignant-hyperthermia and hyperkalemia risk); non-depolarizers block competitively with fade and post-tetanic potentiation — avoid succinylcholine where receptors are upregulated.*

head: Drug
Dose
Onset
Dur
Clearance
tables.note: Doses mg/kg; durations min. Sugammadex reverses roc/vec.

cards.title: Abnormal Responses, Prolongation & Synergism
secs: Pseudocholinesterase (butyrylcholinesterase) deficiency prolongs the paralysis from succinylcholine (and mivacurium); the dibucaine number grades it (around 80 is normal, around 20 is a homozygous atypical enzyme). A non-depolarizing block is prolonged by hypothermia, acidosis, hypokalemia, hypermagnesemia, aminoglycoside antibiotics, and the volatile anesthetics. Combining a steroidal with a benzylisoquinolinium non-depolarizer produces synergistic blockade.

Cisatracurium and atracurium undergo Hofmann elimination (organ-independent, so useful in hepatic or renal failure); atracurium also yields laudanosine. Volatile agents deepen and prolong a non-depolarizing block, so reduce the dose.
Dibucaine number ~80 normal; ~20 homozygous atypical (prolonged sux) Potentiate non-depolarizers: volatiles, aminoglycosides, Mg, hypothermia, acidosis, hypoK

*Pseudocholinesterase deficiency (low dibucaine number) prolongs succinylcholine; volatiles, aminoglycosides, magnesium, hypothermia, and acidosis potentiate non-depolarizers — and Hofmann-eliminated agents suit organ failure.*

head: Dibucaine #
Genotype
Block duration
tables.note: Dibucaine inhibits normal enzyme ~80%; number = quality, not quantity.

cards.title: Reversal of Neuromuscular Blockade
secs: Anticholinesterases like neostigmine inhibit acetylcholinesterase, leaving more acetylcholine to outcompete a non-depolarizer; pair them with an antimuscarinic (glycopyrrolate) to block the bradycardia and secretions. Neostigmine has a CEILING — only reverse when at least 2–4 twitches are present, since it cannot reverse a deep block and excess causes weakness. Sugammadex encapsulates rocuronium and vecuronium and reverses even a DEEP block, but it does NOT reverse the benzylisoquinoliniums such as cisatracurium.
Confirm recovery with a train-of-four ratio of at least 0.9 to avoid residual paralysis. Sugammadex cautions: rare anaphylaxis, and it binds hormonal contraceptives, so advise backup contraception.
Neostigmine + glycopyrrolate; reverse at ≥ 2–4 twitches (has a ceiling) Sugammadex reverses deep rocuronium/vecuronium; target TOF ratio ≥ 0.9
*Neostigmine (with glycopyrrolate) needs at least 2–4 twitches and has a ceiling; sugammadex reverses even deep rocuronium or vecuronium — confirm recovery with a train-of-four ratio ≥ 0.9.*

head: Agent
Depth
Dose
tables.note: Sugammadex binds roc/vec only; neostigmine has a ceiling effect.

cards.title: Neuromuscular Blocker Drug Interactions
secs: Aminoglycoside antibiotics potentiate a non-depolarizing block by reducing acetylcholine release and its receptor effects. Magnesium potentiates the block (reduced acetylcholine release and membrane stabilization), which is relevant in preeclampsia. Chronic antiepileptics (phenytoin, carbamazepine) cause RESISTANCE to non-depolarizers (through receptor upregulation and faster metabolism), whereas acute phenytoin potentiates the block.
Lithium prolongs both depolarizing and non-depolarizing block. Calcium channel blockers and local anesthetics mildly potentiate.
Potentiate: aminoglycosides, Mg, Li, volatiles Chronic antiepileptics → RESISTANCE to non-depolarizers
*Aminoglycosides, magnesium, lithium, and volatiles potentiate neuromuscular block, while chronic antiepileptics make non-depolarizers less effective (resistance).*

head: Factor
Mechanism
tables.note: Antagonized by chronic phenytoin/carbamazepine and hypercalcemia.

cards.title: Butyrylcholinesterase (Pseudocholinesterase) Deficiency
secs: The plasma (pseudo)cholinesterase metabolizes succinylcholine and mivacurium, so a deficiency (genetic or acquired) causes a prolonged paralysis and apnea. The inherited variants are dibucaine-resistant, and the DIBUCAINE NUMBER quantifies the enzyme quality (a normal value of about 80 means it inhibits 80%; a homozygous atypical enzyme is about 20). The acquired causes are liver disease, pregnancy, malnutrition, and certain drugs.
Management is supportive — continue sedation and ventilation until the block resolves (over hours). The dibucaine number reflects the enzyme QUALITY, not the quantity.
Dibucaine number: normal ~80, homozygous atypical ~20; prolonged sux/mivacurium apnea

*Pseudocholinesterase deficiency prolongs succinylcholine and mivacurium paralysis; the dibucaine number gauges the enzyme quality (a normal value of about 80, a homozygous atypical of about 20) — and management is sedation and ventilation until it wears off.*

cards.title: Prolonged QT Syndrome
secs: A congenital or acquired QT prolongation raises the risk of torsades de pointes and sudden death; the thresholds are a QTc over about 470 ms (men) or 480 ms (women), with over 500 ms being high-risk. Avoid QT-prolonging drugs (droperidol, ondansetron, antipsychotics, methadone, some antibiotics) and correct the electrolytes (potassium, magnesium, calcium); blunt sympathetic surges (a smooth induction and emergence). Continue beta-blockers (in congenital long-QT syndrome), have magnesium and a defibrillator ready, and treat torsades with magnesium.
Volatiles can modestly prolong the QT, so minimize additional QT-prolonging agents. Have a torsades plan (magnesium, overdrive pacing, defibrillation).
QTc high risk > 500 ms; torsades → magnesium; avoid QT-prolonging drugs + correct K/Mg
*In long-QT syndrome, avoid QT-prolonging drugs and electrolyte derangement, blunt sympathetic surges, continue beta-blockers, and treat torsades with magnesium (a QTc over 500 ms is high-risk).*

cards.title: Genetic Factors in Dose-Response; Rapid Metabolizers
secs: Cytochrome P450 polymorphisms alter drug metabolism: CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizers convert codeine to morphine quickly (causing toxicity — so avoid codeine in children and breastfeeding mothers), while poor metabolizers get no analgesia. This variability affects many drugs (warfarin, the activation of clopidogrel, beta-blockers), and pharmacogenetics can guide dosing. The “rapid/ultrarapid” versus “poor” metabolizer phenotypes explain unexpected drug responses.

Codeine and tramadol are prodrugs activated by CYP2D6 (with a variable effect and safety). Consider genetic variation when responses are atypical. This is the basis of the boxed-warning contraindication to codeine after pediatric tonsillectomy or adenoidectomy (especially with OSA).

*P450 polymorphisms drive dose-response variability — CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizers convert codeine to morphine dangerously fast (avoid it in children and breastfeeding) while poor metabolizers get no effect.*

cards.title: Agents Affecting Neuromuscular Blockade Duration
secs: ACUTE anticonvulsant administration POTENTIATES (prolongs) non-depolarizing NMB; CHRONIC use ATTENUATES it (resistance) via hepatic enzyme induction and up-regulation of acetylcholine receptors. Other POTENTIATORS (see table): inhaled anesthetics (desflurane > sevoflurane > isoflurane > nitrous oxide), aminoglycoside/clindamycin/polymyxin/tetracycline antibiotics, local anesthetics, lithium, and magnesium. Combining two non-depolarizers is additive if same class, synergistic if different class.

Chronic epileptics on phenytoin/carbamazepine often need HIGHER non-depolarizer doses and more frequent redosing — titrate to train-of-four rather than fixed dosing. Watch for prolonged blockade when aminoglycosides, magnesium (e.g. preeclampsia), or deep volatile anesthesia are also present. Hypothermia, respiratory acidosis, hypokalemia, and hypermagnesemia likewise prolong blockade.
Acute anticonvulsants + volatiles + aminoglycosides + Mg/Li prolong NMB; CHRONIC anticonvulsants cause resistance.

noteimg.cap: Agents affecting duration of non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockade.

cards.title: Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor & Neuromuscular Blockers
secs: The mature (adult) postjunctional nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) is a LIGAND-GATED cation channel (mainly Na+ in, K+ out) built from 5 subunits: 2 alpha, 1 beta, 1 delta, and 1 epsilon. Each of the TWO alpha subunits must bind an acetylcholine molecule for the channel to open — this is why competition at just one alpha site prevents opening. NON-depolarizing agents (rocuronium, vecuronium, cisatracurium) are COMPETITIVE ANTAGONISTS that bind the alpha subunits without opening the channel. Succinylcholine is a depolarizing AGONIST that binds and opens the channel, causing persistent depolarization and fasciculations followed by flaccid block.

Because it is competitive, non-depolarizing block is surmountable: raising synaptic ACh with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (neostigmine) reverses it, and reversal must be paired with an antimuscarinic (glycopyrrolate) to block muscarinic side effects. Sugammadex reverses aminosteroid agents (rocuronium, vecuronium) by ENCAPSULATION instead. The FETAL/immature receptor substitutes a gamma subunit for epsilon and appears with denervation, burns, prolonged immobility, and upper motor neuron injury — its proliferation causes exaggerated, potentially lethal HYPERKALEMIA with succinylcholine and resistance to non-depolarizers. Extrajunctional immature receptors have longer open times and a lower conductance.

5 subunits (2 alpha, 1 beta, 1 delta, 1 epsilon in adults; gamma replaces epsilon in immature receptors). BOTH alpha sites must be occupied by ACh to open. Rocuronium intubating dose ~0.6-1.2 mg/kg; sugammadex ~2-16 mg/kg by depth of block.
Adult nAChR = 5 subunits with 2 alpha sites that BOTH need ACh to open; rocuronium is a competitive antagonist, succinylcholine an agonist.

cards.title: Vecuronium — Hepatic Metabolism & the Active 3-OH Metabolite
secs: VECURONIUM is an intermediate-acting aminosteroid nondepolarizing NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKER. It is eliminated primarily by the LIVER (biliary excretion) with a smaller renal component. It is partially DEACETYLATED in the liver to 3-DESACETYLVECURONIUM (3-hydroxyvecuronium), a metabolite that retains roughly 80% of the neuromuscular-blocking POTENCY of the parent drug. This active metabolite is renally cleared.

Because both vecuronium and its potent active metabolite depend on hepatic and renal clearance, effects are PROLONGED in HEPATIC or RENAL failure and in critically ill patients receiving prolonged infusions — accumulation of 3-desacetylvecuronium is a classic cause of persistent weakness/prolonged paralysis in the ICU. Contrast with cisatracurium/atracurium, which undergo organ-independent Hofmann elimination and are preferred when hepatorenal function is poor. Rocuronium similarly relies on hepatic elimination but has no significant active metabolite.
Vecuronium is hepatically cleared and forms 3-desacetylvecuronium, an active metabolite with ~80% potency that accumulates in renal/hepatic failure.

cards.title: Buprenorphine: Partial Mu Agonist
secs: Buprenorphine is a PARTIAL mu-opioid receptor agonist (and kappa antagonist) with very HIGH receptor affinity and slow dissociation. Because it is a partial agonist, it exhibits a CEILING EFFECT on adverse effects such as RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION, giving it a wider safety margin against fatal overdose than full agonists. Its long duration and high affinity mean it can DISPLACE full agonists from the receptor and precipitate acute WITHDRAWAL in opioid-dependent patients, and can blunt the analgesic effect of subsequently administered full agonists.

Perioperative management is challenging: high receptor occupancy limits the effect of standard opioids. Current guidance often favors CONTINUING buprenorphine through surgery and adding multimodal/regional analgesia plus higher-dose full agonists, rather than abrupt discontinuation. Reversal with naloxone is difficult because of buprenorphine's tight binding. It is frequently co-formulated with naloxone (Suboxone) to deter IV misuse.
Buprenorphine is a high-affinity partial mu agonist with a ceiling on respiratory depression; its tight binding can precipitate withdrawal and blunt the analgesia of full agonists.

cards.title: Beta-1 Adrenergic Receptor Up- and Down-Regulation
secs: Adrenergic receptor density is dynamically regulated by chronic exposure to agonist and by disease. BETA-1 receptors on cardiac myocytes are UPREGULATED by HYPERTHYROIDISM, chronic BETA-BLOCKER use, and MYOCARDIAL ISCHEMIA. They are DOWNREGULATED by sustained high circulating CATECHOLAMINES, as seen with AGING, chronic HEART FAILURE (CHF), and chronic ALBUTEROL/beta-agonist use. The general principle is that chronic ANTAGONISM or low agonist tone increases receptor number (upregulation), while chronic AGONIST excess decreases it (downregulation, via receptor internalization and desensitization).

Downregulation in chronic CHF blunts the response to beta-agonist inotropes such as DOBUTAMINE, sometimes favoring agents that bypass the beta receptor like MILRINONE (a phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor). Upregulation from chronic beta-blockade underlies rebound TACHYCARDIA, HYPERTENSION, and ischemia if the blocker is abruptly stopped, so perioperative beta-blockers should be CONTINUED. Hyperthyroid upregulation amplifies catecholamine sensitivity and the risk of thyroid storm.

Chronic antagonism or low agonist tone (beta-blockade, hyperthyroidism, ischemia) upregulates cardiac beta-1 receptors, whereas chronic catecholamine excess (aging, CHF, chronic albuterol) downregulates them, explaining beta-blocker rebound and the poor inotrope response in chronic heart failure.

cards.title: Prevention of Succinylcholine-Induced Myalgia
secs: SUCCINYLCHOLINE, a depolarizing neuromuscular blocker, causes disorganized FASCICULATIONS that are thought to contribute to postoperative MYALGIAS (myalgia incidence up to ~50%, most common in young, ambulatory patients undergoing minor surgery). The single most effective pharmacologic strategy to reduce myalgia is PRETREATMENT WITH AN NSAID (e.g., lidocaine and NSAIDs perform best in meta-analyses). The classic second-line method is a DEFASCICULATING dose of a NONDEPOLARIZING agent such as ROCURONIUM (about 10% of the intubating dose, given ~2-3 minutes prior). Counterintuitively, a meta-analysis found that INCREASING the succinylcholine dose is associated with a DECREASED incidence of myalgia.

Fasciculation intensity and myalgia are only loosely correlated, so reducing fasciculations does not guarantee less pain. Defasciculation has trade-offs: it BLUNTS fasciculations but REQUIRES a larger succinylcholine dose (roughly 1.5 mg/kg instead of 1.0 mg/kg) because the nondepolarizer antagonizes the block, prolongs onset, and can cause weakness/heavy eyelids in the awake patient. NSAID pretreatment avoids these issues by targeting the inflammatory/prostaglandin component of the myalgia. Succinylcholine's other well-known adverse effects (hyperkalemia, raised intragastric/intraocular/intracranial pressure, bradycardia, malignant hyperthermia trigger) are unaffected by these myalgia-reduction strategies.
Defasciculating rocuronium ~0.06 mg/kg (~10% of 0.6 mg/kg intubating dose) given 2-3 min before; if used, increase succinylcholine to ~1.5 mg/kg. Baseline myalgia incidence up to ~50%.

NSAID pretreatment is the MOST effective way to reduce succinylcholine myalgia; a defasciculating dose of rocuronium is second-line, and paradoxically a HIGHER succinylcholine dose lowers myalgia risk.

cards.title: Nerve Agent / Organophosphate Poisoning: Atropine & Pralidoxime
secs: Nerve agents (sarin, VX) and organophosphates irreversibly inhibit ACETYLCHOLINESTERASE, so acetylcholine accumulates at muscarinic AND nicotinic synapses. Muscarinic excess (SLUDGE / DUMBELS): salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, GI cramping, emesis, plus BRONCHORRHEA, bronchospasm, bradycardia, and miosis. Nicotinic excess: fasciculations, weakness, and eventual paralysis. CNS: seizures, coma, and central respiratory depression. Death is usually from respiratory failure (bronchorrhea + bronchospasm + muscle paralysis).

Two complementary antidotes are used. ATROPINE (antimuscarinic) reverses the MUSCARINIC and central effects, crosses the blood-brain barrier to attenuate CNS symptoms, and is titrated to DRYING OF SECRETIONS/reversal of bronchorrhea and bradycardia. PRALIDOXIME (2-PAM) REACTIVATES acetylcholinesterase by removing the organophosphate before 'AGING' makes the bond permanent - this addresses the NICOTINIC (neuromuscular) effects that atropine does not, and must be given EARLY. Add benzodiazepines for seizures. Anesthetic note: plasma cholinesterase inhibition markedly PROLONGS succinylcholine; use nondepolarizers cautiously.
Atropine 2-6 mg IV, repeated/doubled until secretions dry. Pralidoxime 1-2 g IV (given before enzyme aging). Midazolam/diazepam for seizures.

Organophosphate/nerve-agent toxicity = ACh excess from cholinesterase inhibition; atropine reverses muscarinic + CNS effects (crosses BBB), pralidoxime reactivates the enzyme for nicotinic effects if given before aging.

cards.title: Dexmedetomidine: BIS, Pharmacokinetics & Hemodynamics
secs: Dexmedetomidine is a highly selective central alpha-2 agonist (alpha2:alpha1 ~1600:1) acting at the LOCUS CERULEUS to produce sedation resembling natural NON-REM sleep (arousable), analgesia, anxiolysis, and sympatholysis with MINIMAL respiratory depression. It lowers the BIS value in a dose-dependent fashion, reflecting its sedative/hypnotic effect. It is EXTENSIVELY METABOLIZED in the LIVER (glucuronidation and CYP-mediated) with an elimination half-life of 2-3 hours, so dosing should be reduced in hepatic impairment. A key feature: MINIMAL effect on cortical/somatosensory evoked potentials, making it valuable during spinal and other neuromonitored surgery.

A rapid BOLUS/loading dose produces a BIPHASIC hemodynamic response: an initial transient INCREASE in blood pressure with reflex bradycardia from peripheral alpha-2B-mediated VASOCONSTRICTION, followed by HYPOTENSION and BRADYCARDIA from central sympatholysis (alpha-2A). Give the loading dose slowly (over ~10 min) to blunt these swings, and use caution in patients with heart block, severe bradycardia, or hypovolemia. Clinical uses: ICU sedation, awake fiberoptic intubation, opioid-sparing analgesia, blunting the sympathetic stress response, and reducing emergence delirium in children. Reliable amnesia is NOT produced.
Loading dose ~1 mcg/kg over 10 min; maintenance infusion 0.2-0.7 mcg/kg/hr; half-life 2-3 h; selectivity alpha2:alpha1 ~1600:1.

Dexmedetomidine lowers BIS, spares evoked potentials and respiration; a bolus causes transient hypertension/bradycardia (peripheral vasoconstriction) then hypotension/bradycardia (central sympatholysis).

cards.title: Pressure Measurement of Gases & Liquids
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Mechanics (I.A.2.a)
secs: Pressure is force divided by area, and is measured clinically with manometers, aneroid gauges, and electronic strain-gauge transducers. Gauge pressure is the reading relative to atmosphere — that is, absolute pressure minus atmospheric pressure. It is worth memorizing the standard ways one atmosphere is expressed (see the conversions below), since exams test unit interconversion.

Invasive (arterial-line) transducers are zeroed to atmosphere and leveled at the phlebostatic axis (roughly the level of the right atrium); placing the transducer too high or too low introduces a systematic error of about 0.74 mmHg per cm of height difference. The fidelity of an invasive pressure waveform also depends on resonance (ringing) and damping in the tubing.
P = F / A 1 atm = 760 mmHg = 101.3 kPa = 1.013 bar = 14.7 psi = 1034 cmH2O mmHg → cmH2O: ×1.36   │   kPa → mmHg: ×7.5
*Memorize the atmosphere conversions; transducers are zeroed to atmosphere and leveled at the phlebostatic axis.*

cards.title: Transducers, Regulators & Medical Gas Cylinders
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Mechanics (I.A.2.a)
secs: An oxygen E-cylinder holds ~660 L when full at ~2000 psi; because oxygen is a true gas, its contents fall in direct proportion to pressure (Boyle’s law), so the gauge can be used to calculate the volume remaining. A nitrous oxide (N₂O) E-cylinder contains liquid plus vapor, so its gauge reads a near-constant ~745 psi until almost all the liquid is gone (~75% used) — the pressure does NOT reflect how much is left. Safety systems prevent wrong connections: the Pin Index Safety System for cylinders and the Diameter Index Safety System for pipelines; in the US, oxygen is color-coded green, N₂O blue, and air yellow. A regulator steps the high cylinder pressure down to a ~45 psi working pressure, while the wall pipeline supply is ~50 psi.

You can calculate how long an O₂ cylinder will last before transport (see the equations below). Do not trust a N₂O gauge to estimate the remaining volume — weigh the cylinder instead, since only the weight tracks the liquid contents.
O2 remaining (L) = 660 × (gauge psi / 2000) O2 duration (min) = [660 × (psi/2000)] / flow (L/min) N2O E-cylinder ≈ 1590 L; pressure ~745 psi until liquid depleted
*O2 content ∝ pressure (calculable); N2O gauge stays ~745 psi until ~¾ empty; pipeline pressure is ~50 psi.*

head: Gas
Color (US)
Pressure
Volume
tables.note: N₂O is liquid: pressure stays 745 psi until liquid is gone — weigh it.

cards.title: Principles of Ultrasound
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Mechanics / Ultrasound (I.A.2.a)
secs: Diagnostic ultrasound uses frequencies of about 2–15 MHz, and sound travels through soft tissue at ≈ 1540 m/s. The central trade-off: a HIGHER frequency gives better image resolution but penetrates less deeply, while a LOWER frequency penetrates deeper but resolves less detail. Axial resolution (along the beam) is better than lateral resolution and improves with higher frequency (shorter wavelength). Reflections arise where tissues of different acoustic impedance meet; coupling gel removes the air gap that would otherwise reflect the beam at the skin.

Choose a high-frequency linear probe for superficial targets (the IJV, peripheral nerves) and a low-frequency curvilinear probe for deep structures. Be able to recognize common artifacts: acoustic shadowing, posterior (acoustic) enhancement, and reverberation.
c = f × λ   (speed = frequency × wavelength) Soft-tissue speed c ≈ 1540 m/s ↑ frequency → ↓ wavelength → ↑ axial resolution, ↓ penetration
*High frequency = high resolution but shallow penetration; axial beats lateral resolution; tissue speed is 1540 m/s.*

cards.title: Viscosity, Density & Laminar vs Turbulent Flow
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Flow & Velocity (I.A.2.b)
secs: Laminar (smooth) flow obeys Poiseuille’s law and depends on the fluid’s VISCOSITY; crucially, flow is proportional to the radius raised to the 4th power. Turbulent (chaotic) flow instead depends on DENSITY — which is why low-density heliox (a helium–oxygen mix) improves flow through a narrowed airway. The Reynolds number predicts which regime applies (roughly > 2000–4000 indicates turbulence).

Because flow scales with radius⁴, small changes in caliber dominate — this is why endotracheal-tube size, IV-catheter gauge, and airway stenoses matter so much. Heliox helps specifically in upper-airway obstruction by lowering gas density and therefore turbulence.
Poiseuille: Q = (π × ΔP × r^4) / (8 × η × L)  — laminar; flow ∝ r^4 Reynolds number: Re = (ρ × v × d) / η Laminar → viscosity-dependent; turbulent → density-dependent
*Laminar flow ∝ r^4 and viscosity (Poiseuille); turbulent flow depends on density (heliox helps); Reynolds number predicts which.*

cards.title: Flowmeters / Rotameter
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Flow & Velocity (I.A.2.b)
secs: A rotameter is a variable-orifice, constant-pressure flowmeter: the float (bobbin) rises until the upward force of gas flowing past it balances gravity. At low flow the gas behaves as if moving through a tube (laminar, viscosity-dependent); at high flow it behaves as if passing through an orifice (turbulent, density-dependent). Each flowmeter is gas-specific, calibrated to one gas’s particular viscosity and density.

Read the flow at the top of a bobbin-type float, or at the center of a ball float. The oxygen flow control is placed downstream-most (closest to the common gas outlet) so that an upstream leak is less likely to produce a hypoxic mixture.
Low flow → laminar (viscosity);  high flow → turbulent (density)
*Variable-orifice, constant-pressure, gas-specific device; the O2 knob sits downstream-most for hypoxia safety.*

cards.title: Doppler Ultrasound Principles
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Flow & Velocity (I.A.2.b)
secs: The Doppler effect is the change in frequency of ultrasound reflected off moving red blood cells, which the machine converts into velocity or flow. It is strongly angle-dependent: the signal is largest when the beam is parallel to flow (angle θ ≈ 0°) and disappears entirely at 90° (because cos 90° = 0). Applications include precordial Doppler, vascular access, esophageal-Doppler cardiac output, and fetal heart-rate monitoring.

Precordial Doppler is the most sensitive monitor for venous air embolism (VAE) short of transesophageal echocardiography (TEE). Keep the beam as parallel to flow as possible, or you will underestimate the true velocity.
Doppler shift: Δf = (2 × f0 × v × cosθ) / c Maximal signal at θ → 0°; no shift at θ = 90°
*Doppler measures velocity via a frequency shift; it is angle-dependent (best parallel, nil at 90°); precordial Doppler is most sensitive for VAE.*

cards.title: Diffusion of Gases
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Properties of Gases & Vapors (I.A.2.c)
secs: Fick’s law of diffusion: the rate is proportional to the membrane area, the partial-pressure gradient, and the gas’s solubility, and inversely proportional to membrane thickness and the square root of molecular weight. Graham’s law (a corollary): diffusion rate is inversely proportional to the square root of molecular weight, so lighter gases diffuse faster. Carbon dioxide diffuses about 20× faster than oxygen across the alveolar membrane, because it is so much more soluble.

This is why low oxygenation is largely a diffusion/shunt problem while a high CO₂ is largely a ventilation problem. Diffusion hypoxia (the Fink effect) occurs as N₂O floods out of the blood into the alveoli on emergence, diluting alveolar oxygen — give supplemental O₂ at the end of a N₂O anesthetic.
Fick: V̇gas ∝ (A × ΔP × solubility) / (thickness × √MW) Graham: rate ∝ 1 / √(molecular weight)
*Fick’s law (area, gradient, solubility, thickness, √MW); CO2 diffuses ~20× faster than O2; beware diffusion hypoxia after N2O.*

cards.title: Solubility Coefficients (Blood:Gas, Oil:Gas)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Properties of Gases & Vapors (I.A.2.c)
secs: The blood:gas partition coefficient determines the SPEED of induction and emergence — a LOWER value means a less soluble agent and therefore a FASTER onset and offset. Approximate blood:gas values: desflurane 0.42, N₂O 0.46, sevoflurane 0.65, isoflurane 1.4, halothane 2.4 (lower = faster). The oil:gas partition coefficient instead reflects POTENCY (the Meyer–Overton correlation) and is inversely related to MAC (minimum alveolar concentration).
Pick a low-blood:gas agent (desflurane or sevoflurane) when you want a rapid wake-up. A high oil:gas coefficient predicts a potent agent with a correspondingly low MAC.
Low blood:gas → fast onset/offset  (des < N2O < sevo < iso < halothane) Oil:gas ∝ potency ∝ 1 / MAC  (Meyer–Overton)
*Low blood:gas = fast induction; high oil:gas = high potency / low MAC; know the agent order.*

head: Agent
λ b:g
tables.note: Lower λ → faster onset/offset.

cards.title: Critical Temperature & Critical Pressure
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Properties of Gases & Vapors (I.A.2.c)
secs: The critical temperature is the temperature above which a gas can no longer be liquefied by pressure alone. The critical pressure is the pressure needed to liquefy a substance when it is exactly at its critical temperature. N₂O’s critical temperature is 36.5°C — above room temperature — so N₂O exists as a liquid (under pressure) inside its cylinder. Oxygen’s critical temperature is −118°C — far below room temperature — so it cannot be liquefied at room temperature and is stored as a compressed gas.

This is exactly why a N₂O gauge does not track its contents (liquid is present) while an O₂ gauge does (gas only). Storing bulk liquid oxygen therefore requires cryogenic temperatures below its critical temperature.
N2O: critical temp 36.5°C, critical pressure ~72 bar O2: critical temp −118°C
*N2O’s critical temp (36.5°C) is above room temp → liquid in the cylinder; O2’s is far below → compressed gas.*

head: Gas
Critical temp
Cylinder state
tables.note: Above the critical temperature, pressure alone can't liquefy a gas.

cards.title: Gas Laws
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Gas Laws (I.A.2.d)
secs: Boyle’s law (constant temperature): pressure and volume are inversely related — the basis for estimating cylinder contents from pressure. Charles’s law (constant pressure): volume is proportional to absolute temperature. Gay-Lussac’s law (constant volume): pressure is proportional to absolute temperature. Dalton’s law: the total pressure of a gas mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures. Henry’s law: the amount of gas dissolved in a liquid is proportional to its partial pressure.

Clinically, Boyle’s law underlies cylinder-volume and bellows calculations, Dalton’s underlies alveolar partial pressures, and Henry’s underlies how much gas dissolves in blood. Avogadro’s principle: equal volumes of gas contain equal numbers of molecules (one mole = 22.4 L at standard temperature and pressure).
Boyle: P1V1 = P2V2   │   Charles: V1/T1 = V2/T2   │   Gay-Lussac: P1/T1 = P2/T2 Ideal gas: PV = nRT Dalton: P(total) = Σ P(i)   │   Henry: C = k × P(partial)
*Boyle (cylinder content), Dalton (partial pressures), and Henry (dissolved gas) are the most clinically applied; PV = nRT unites them.*

cards.title: Vapor Pressure & Anesthetic Concentration Calculation
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Vaporizers (I.A.2.e)
secs: Vapor pressure depends only on the agent and its temperature — it is independent of the surrounding (ambient) pressure. The saturated vapor concentration (%) equals the vapor pressure divided by the ambient pressure, times 100. Sevoflurane’s vapor pressure is ~157 mmHg (≈ 21% saturated), and isoflurane and halothane are ~240 mmHg (≈ 32%). Desflurane’s vapor pressure is ~669 mmHg and it boils at ~23°C, so it requires a specially heated, pressurized vaporizer.

A variable-bypass vaporizer delivers a constant PARTIAL PRESSURE of agent (and so a constant clinical effect) across altitudes, even though its percentage output rises as ambient pressure falls. The desflurane vaporizer is the exception that must be corrected for altitude, because it targets a set concentration rather than a partial pressure.
Saturated vapor % = (VP_agent / P_ambient) × 100 Clinically, output PARTIAL PRESSURE — not % — determines effect
*Saturated concentration = VP / ambient × 100; desflurane’s high VP forces a heated vaporizer; variable-bypass delivers constant partial pressure at altitude.*

cards.title: Vaporizer Types & Safety Features
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Vaporizers (I.A.2.e)
secs: Modern vaporizers are variable-bypass, temperature-compensated, agent-specific, and located out of the breathing circuit (e.g., the Tec series). Temperature compensation — typically a bimetallic strip that adjusts the bypass — keeps output steady as temperature and vapor pressure change. The desflurane vaporizer (Tec 6) is the exception: it is electrically heated to ~39°C and pressurized, working as a dual-gas blender rather than a variable-bypass device.

An interlock prevents more than one vaporizer being switched on at once, and agent-specific keyed fillers prevent cross-filling with the wrong drug. Filling a low-vapor-pressure agent’s vaporizer with a higher-vapor-pressure agent can deliver a dangerous overdose.
Tec 6 desflurane vaporizer: heated to ~39°C, pressurized to ~2 atm
*Modern = variable-bypass / temperature-compensated / agent-specific; desflurane is the heated, pressurized exception; an interlock blocks two agents at once.*

cards.title: Uptake & Distribution (FA/FI; Ventilation & Circulation Effects)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Uptake & Distribution (I.A.2.f)
secs: FA/FI is the ratio of alveolar to inspired anesthetic concentration; during induction it climbs toward 1, and a faster climb means a faster induction. FA/FI rises FASTER with a low blood:gas solubility, increased alveolar ventilation, and — counter-intuitively — a DECREASED cardiac output. A HIGH cardiac output actually slows an inhalational induction, because more blood passing the alveoli carries agent away and keeps the alveolar level low. A right-to-left shunt slows an inhalational induction (most noticeably with insoluble agents) but speeds an intravenous induction.
Practically, a low-output, hypovolemic patient takes up volatile agent faster — and can be overdosed faster. The time constant of any compartment equals its volume divided by the flow through it.
FA/FI rises faster with: ↓ solubility, ↑ ventilation, ↓ cardiac output ↑ Cardiac output → SLOWER inhalational induction
*Induction speeds up with low solubility, high ventilation, and LOW cardiac output; a right-to-left shunt slows gas induction.*

cards.title: Concentration Effect
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Uptake & Distribution (I.A.2.f)
secs: The higher the inspired concentration of a gas, the faster its alveolar concentration (FA/FI) rises toward the inspired value. Two mechanisms drive it: the concentrating effect (as a large volume of gas is taken up, the remaining alveolar gas shrinks and the agent becomes more concentrated) and augmented inflow (fresh gas is drawn in to replace what was absorbed). It is clinically meaningful mainly for N₂O, the only agent given in high enough concentrations.
This is why N₂O reaches effective alveolar levels so quickly. Volatile agents are delivered at low concentrations, so the effect is small for them on their own.
*A high inspired concentration speeds the FA/FI rise via the concentrating effect plus augmented inflow — relevant for N2O.*

cards.title: Second Gas Effect
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Uptake & Distribution (I.A.2.f)
secs: When a “first gas” (N₂O) is taken up rapidly and in large volume, it speeds the alveolar rise of a “second gas” given at the same time (a volatile agent plus oxygen). It is simply the concentration effect acting on the co-administered second gas, through the same concentrating and augmented-inflow mechanisms. The net result is a faster onset of the volatile agent when N₂O is running alongside it.
This underlies the classic practice of co-inducing with N₂O plus a volatile to speed onset. The effect is modest and shrinks as N₂O uptake tapers off.
*N2O’s large uptake concentrates and speeds the onset of the co-administered volatile — the “second gas.”*

cards.title: Nitrous Oxide & Closed Spaces
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Uptake & Distribution (I.A.2.f)
secs: N₂O is about 34× more soluble than nitrogen, so it diffuses INTO an air-filled space faster than the nitrogen already there can diffuse out. In compliant (stretchy) spaces this causes EXPANSION — bowel gas, a pneumothorax, air emboli, the endotracheal-tube cuff, and a pneumoperitoneum all enlarge. In non-compliant (fixed) spaces it instead builds PRESSURE — the middle ear or a tympanic graft, an intraocular gas bubble, and the closed cranium.

With 75% N₂O, a pneumothorax can roughly double in ~10 minutes and quadruple in ~30 minutes. Avoid N₂O with a pneumothorax, bowel obstruction, recent intraocular gas (SF₆ or C₃F₈), middle-ear surgery, or any risk of air embolism.
N2O is ~34× more soluble than N2 → net diffusion INTO closed spaces 75% N2O: pneumothorax ≈ doubles in ~10 min, quadruples in ~30 min
*N2O enters air spaces faster than N2 leaves → expansion/pressure; avoid with pneumothorax, bowel obstruction, intraocular gas, and middle-ear surgery.*

head: Space
Effect
tables.note: N₂O is ~34× more soluble than N₂ → diffuses in faster than N₂ leaves.

cards.title: Breathing System Physics (Dead Space, Rebreathing, Leaks)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Anesthesia Machine & Breathing System (I.A.2.g)
secs: Apparatus (mechanical) dead space is the part of the breathing circuit beyond the Y-piece, where inspired and expired gas can mix and then be rebreathed. Increasing that dead space means more CO₂ is rebreathed, which raises the end-tidal CO₂ (EtCO₂) and the arterial CO₂ (PaCO₂). Circuit performance is also shaped by resistance, turbulent flow, leaks, humidity, and heat. The key components are the connectors, mask, endotracheal tube (ETT), reservoir bag, unidirectional valves, and corrugated tubing.

Pediatric circuits are designed to minimize dead space, using low-compliance tubing and keeping the Y-piece close to the patient. Disconnections and leaks are among the most common critical incidents — capnography and low-airway-pressure alarms detect them first.
Apparatus dead space = circuit volume distal to the Y-piece ↑ dead space → ↑ rebreathing → ↑ EtCO2
*Apparatus dead space is the circuit volume beyond the Y-piece; adding to it means more CO₂ rebreathing and a higher EtCO₂ — which is why pediatric circuits keep it to a minimum.*

head: Type
Definition
tables.note: ↑ dead space widens the PaCO₂–EtCO₂ gradient.

cards.title: Unidirectional Valves, APL Valve & LMA Components
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Anesthesia Machine & Breathing System (I.A.2.g)
secs: The two unidirectional (one-way) valves of a circle system direct gas toward the patient on inspiration and back toward the absorber on expiration, which is what prevents CO₂ rebreathing. An incompetent expiratory valve lets exhaled gas back in, causing rebreathing and a rising EtCO₂; a stuck inspiratory valve increases the work of breathing. The adjustable pressure-limiting (APL, or “pop-off”) valve vents excess gas to scavenging — keep it open during spontaneous ventilation so pressure doesn’t build. The laryngeal mask airway (LMA) is a supraglottic airway that seats in the hypopharynx; it is not a definitive airway and (in standard forms) does not protect against aspiration.

Leaving the APL valve closed during manual ventilation lets airway pressure climb dangerously, risking barotrauma. The LMA is valuable for difficult-airway rescue and as an intubation conduit, but avoid it with a full stomach, poor lung compliance, or high airway pressures.
*Unidirectional-valve failure causes CO₂ rebreathing; keep the APL valve open during spontaneous breathing to avoid barotrauma; and remember the LMA is supraglottic and not aspiration-proof.*

cards.title: Circle Systems (Closed & Semi-Closed)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Anesthesia Machine & Breathing System (I.A.2.g)
secs: A circle system pairs a CO₂ absorber with two unidirectional valves so that exhaled gas, minus its CO₂, is rebreathed — conserving heat, humidity, and anesthetic agent. In the usual “semi-closed” setup the fresh gas flow (FGF) exceeds the patient’s uptake and the excess is vented; in a true “closed” system the FGF exactly matches uptake and nothing is vented. Low-flow anesthesia saves agent and reduces pollution, but it raises the risk of a hypoxic mixture, rebreathing, and accumulation of Compound A or carbon monoxide (CO).

The valves must sit between the patient and the absorber, and an inspired-oxygen (FiO₂) analyzer is mandatory whenever you run low-flow or closed technique. Low flow also slows how quickly a vaporizer change reaches the patient, because the system has a large time constant.
Closed circuit: FGF = patient uptake (no gas vented) Low flow → slower change in delivered concentration
*A circle system is an absorber plus two one-way valves that enable rebreathing; low flow conserves agent and heat but demands FiO₂ monitoring and risks Compound A and carbon monoxide.*

cards.title: Non-Circle (Mapleson) Systems
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Anesthesia Machine & Breathing System (I.A.2.g)
secs: Mapleson circuits have no CO₂ absorber, so rebreathing is prevented purely by running an adequate fresh gas flow (FGF). The Mapleson A (Magill) is the most efficient for spontaneous ventilation, needing an FGF roughly equal to minute ventilation. The Mapleson D (and its Bain variant) is the most efficient for controlled ventilation. The Bain is a coaxial Mapleson D (fresh gas runs through an inner tube), and the Jackson-Rees is a Mapleson F used in pediatrics.
Mapleson systems are handy for transport, induction, and pediatric cases. If the inner fresh-gas tube of a Bain circuit kinks or disconnects, the patient rebreathes CO₂ and becomes hypercarbic.
Spontaneous efficiency: A > DFE > CB Controlled efficiency: DFE > BC > A
*Mapleson A is best for spontaneous ventilation and the D/Bain best for controlled; with no absorber, only an adequate fresh gas flow prevents rebreathing.*

head: Mode
Best → worst
tables.note: “All Dogs Can Bite, Dammit.” Bain = coaxial D.

cards.title: CO2 Absorption (Absorbents, Canisters, Efficiency)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Anesthesia Machine & Breathing System (I.A.2.g)
secs: Absorbents neutralize CO₂ through an acid–base reaction; soda lime is mostly calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)₂) with some sodium/potassium hydroxide, producing calcium carbonate, water, and heat (an exothermic reaction). An ethyl-violet pH indicator turns purple when the absorbent is exhausted — though the color can fade after the canister rests, so trust capnography over the color. Channeling (gas tunneling through the granules) and simple exhaustion reduce efficiency, and the reaction releases heat and water.

When the absorbent dries out, strong-base formulations react with volatile agents to form Compound A (from sevoflurane) and carbon monoxide (from desflurane); strong-base-free (calcium-hydroxide-only) absorbents avoid both.
*Soda lime neutralizes CO₂ exothermically (ethyl violet signals exhaustion); a dried-out strong-base absorbent generates Compound A and carbon monoxide.*

head: Issue
Detail
tables.note: Avoid desiccated absorbent; keep fresh-gas flow up with sevoflurane.

cards.title: Compound A & Carbon Monoxide (Absorbent Toxicity)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Anesthesia Machine & Breathing System (I.A.2.g)
secs: Compound A is a potentially nephrotoxic vinyl ether formed when SEVOFLURANE meets a desiccated strong-base absorbent; the risk rises with low fresh gas flow, high sevoflurane concentration, and a hot, dry absorbent. Carbon monoxide (CO) forms when volatile agents (DESFLURANE > enflurane > isoflurane) meet a desiccated absorbent — the classic “Monday-morning” first case, after gas left running over the weekend dried the canister out. Both problems are essentially eliminated by strong-base-free calcium-hydroxide absorbents.

Historic FDA guidance recommends a sevoflurane fresh gas flow of at least 1–2 L/min to limit Compound A, though its nephrotoxicity in humans remains debated. Prevent CO by turning off the gas flow when the machine is idle and replacing any absorbent that may have dried out.
Compound A ↑ with: low FGF, high sevo %, hot/dry absorbent CO production: desflurane > enflurane > isoflurane
*Sevoflurane plus a dry strong-base absorbent makes nephrotoxic Compound A; desflurane plus a dry absorbent makes carbon monoxide — both are avoided by strong-base-free absorbent.*

cards.title: Oxygen Supply Systems & FiO2 Safeguards
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Anesthesia Machine & Breathing System (I.A.2.g)
secs: Pipeline oxygen is supplied at about 50 psi, and the cylinder backup is regulated down to about 45 psi so the pipeline normally takes priority. The inspired-oxygen (FiO₂) analyzer in the inspiratory limb is the LAST line of defense against delivering a hypoxic mixture, and is therefore mandatory. The fail-safe (oxygen-failure protection) device cuts off or limits the other gases if oxygen pressure falls, and oxygen-ratio proportioning keeps the delivered FiO₂ at roughly ≥ 25%.

A crossed pipeline (the wrong gas in the oxygen line) is caught by the O₂ analyzer — NOT by the fail-safe, which only senses pressure, not gas identity. The oxygen flush delivers ~35–75 L/min at full pipeline pressure, so it risks barotrauma and can dilute the anesthetic (causing awareness).
Pipeline ~50 psi; cylinder regulated ~45 psi; O2 flush ~35–75 L/min Proportioning keeps FiO2 ≥ ~25%
*The inspired-O₂ analyzer is the ultimate safeguard against hypoxia; the fail-safe and proportioning systems keep FiO₂ ≥ ~25% but cannot detect a crossed pipeline.*

head: Device
FiO₂
tables.note: Low-flow device FiO₂ varies with the patient's minute ventilation.

cards.title: Waste Gas Scavenging Systems
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Anesthesia Machine & Breathing System (I.A.2.g)
secs: Scavenging collects waste anesthetic gas from the APL valve and the ventilator relief valve, reducing operating-room pollution and staff exposure. Systems are either open (passive) or closed (active); active systems require pressure-relief valves to protect the patient. A positive-pressure relief valve protects the patient’s circuit if the vacuum fails, while a negative-pressure relief valve protects it if the vacuum is excessive.

A blocked transfer or exhaust line can transmit pressure back into the breathing circuit, risking barotrauma. NIOSH exposure limits are ≤ 2 ppm for a halogenated agent alone (≤ 0.5 ppm when combined with N₂O) and ≤ 25 ppm for N₂O.
*Scavenging protects staff from waste gas; an active system needs both positive- and negative-pressure relief valves so a vacuum problem can’t injure the patient’s airway.*

cards.title: Machine Safety Features (Proportioning, Fail-Safe, Flowmeter Sequence)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Anesthesia Machine & Breathing System (I.A.2.g)
secs: Oxygen-ratio proportioning (the Link-25 chain or the Sensitive Oxygen Ratio Controller) prevents a hypoxic oxygen:nitrous-oxide mixture by keeping FiO₂ at roughly ≥ 25%. The fail-safe (oxygen-failure protection device) shuts off or proportionally reduces nitrous oxide and other gases whenever oxygen supply pressure falls. The oxygen flowmeter is positioned downstream-most, so that a leak in another flow tube is less likely to create a hypoxic mixture. The Pin Index Safety System (cylinders) and Diameter Index Safety System (pipelines) prevent the wrong gas from being connected.

These safeguards still FAIL against the wrong gas being piped into the oxygen line, leaks downstream of the flowmeters, and mechanical failure of the proportioning system — which is why the machine checkout and O₂ analyzer remain essential.
*Proportioning, the fail-safe, a downstream oxygen flowmeter, and pin/diameter indexing all guard against hypoxic delivery — but none of them replaces the O₂ analyzer and a proper machine checkout.*

cards.title: Neuromuscular Monitoring (TOF, Nerve Stimulators, EMG)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Monitoring Methods (I.A.2.h)
secs: Train-of-four (TOF) monitoring delivers four stimuli at 2 Hz; the TOF ratio (the height of the fourth twitch divided by the first, T4/T1) quantifies non-depolarizing block and the “fade” it produces. The TOF count maps to depth of block: 0 twitches is deep block, 1–2 indicates surgical relaxation, and neostigmine reversal needs at least 2–3 twitches present. Adequate recovery means a TOF ratio of at least 0.9 — below that, clinical signs are unreliable. Other modes include double-burst stimulation (easier to feel fade), post-tetanic count (for very deep block), and tetanus.

A depolarizing block (succinylcholine) shows NO fade and NO post-tetanic potentiation, whereas a non-depolarizing block shows BOTH — a key way to tell them apart. Monitor the adductor pollicis (the ulnar nerve at the thumb); facial and eye muscles recover sooner and overestimate true recovery.
TOF ratio = T4 / T1; recovery target ≥ 0.9 Neostigmine at ≥ 2–3 twitches; sugammadex reverses deep block
*A TOF ratio ≥ 0.9 confirms adequate recovery; fade plus post-tetanic potentiation signals a non-depolarizing block; always monitor the adductor pollicis rather than the face.*

head: Response
Receptors blocked
tables.note: Deep block: use post-tetanic count (PTC). Sustained 5-s tetanus ≈ recovery.

cards.title: Ventilation Monitoring (Spirometry, Flow-Volume Loops)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Monitoring Methods (I.A.2.h)
secs: Machine spirometry displays tidal volume, minute ventilation, and pressure–volume and flow–volume loops in real time. On the pressure–volume loop, the slope reflects compliance, and “beaking” at the top suggests overdistension. The shape of the flow–volume loop helps localize airway obstruction (fixed versus variable, intrathoracic versus extrathoracic). Loops also reveal leaks (the loop fails to close), bronchospasm (a scooped-out expiratory limb), and circuit obstruction. Two overdistension patterns appear on the PRESSURE-VOLUME loop (see figure): pressure overdistension shows terminal "beaking" where volume barely rises despite climbing airway pressure (lower the tidal volume or plateau pressure), while volume overdistension shifts the loop up and right with an increased FRC from air trapping / auto-PEEP.
Watching the loops live helps you titrate PEEP and tidal volume and quickly flags an endobronchial intubation or a kinked endotracheal tube.
*Real-time pressure–volume and flow–volume loops expose compliance changes, leaks, bronchospasm, and obstruction as they happen — a quick visual check on the airway and circuit.*

cards.title: Gas Concentration Monitoring (Capnography)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Monitoring Methods (I.A.2.h)
secs: Carbon dioxide, nitrous oxide, and the volatile agents are measured by infrared absorption; oxygen needs a different method (a paramagnetic analyzer or a galvanic/polarographic cell), because O₂ has no useful infrared absorption. The capnogram has four phases: I baseline, II the expiratory upstroke, III the alveolar plateau, and IV the inspiratory downstroke — read the EtCO₂ at the very end of phase III. The normal gap between PaCO₂ and EtCO₂ is about 2–5 mmHg, and it widens when dead space increases (pulmonary embolism, low cardiac output).

Capnography is the most reliable confirmation that the tube is in the trachea, and it detects disconnection, embolism (a sudden drop in EtCO₂), malignant hyperthermia (a rising EtCO₂), and rebreathing (a raised baseline). A sloping, “shark-fin” plateau indicates airway obstruction or bronchospasm.
PaCO2 − EtCO2 gradient ≈ 2–5 mmHg (widens with dead space) CO2/N2O/volatiles → infrared; O2 → paramagnetic or fuel cell

*Capnography is the gold-standard confirmation of intubation and a powerful trend monitor; gases are read by infrared and O₂ by a paramagnetic/fuel-cell method, and a widening PaCO₂–EtCO₂ gap means increased dead space.*

cards.title: Temperature Monitoring
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Monitoring Methods (I.A.2.h)
secs: Core temperature sites include the pulmonary artery (the gold standard), the distal esophagus, the nasopharynx, and the tympanic membrane; bladder and rectal probes lag behind during rapid temperature change. General anesthesia blunts thermoregulation, so heat redistributes from the warm core to the cool periphery — this redistribution is the biggest source of early heat loss, in the first hour. Heat is then lost by radiation (the largest route), convection, conduction, and evaporation.

Hypothermia causes coagulopathy, surgical-site infection, prolonged drug effects, shivering (which raises oxygen consumption), and arrhythmias. Unexpected intraoperative hyperthermia should prompt thought of malignant hyperthermia, sepsis, thyroid storm, or simple overwarming.
Redistribution = dominant early heat loss; radiation = largest ongoing route
*The pulmonary-artery catheter is the core-temperature gold standard; redistribution drives the first-hour temperature drop, and radiation is the largest ongoing route of heat loss.*

head: Site
Note
tables.note: Core falls first from redistribution; needed to detect MH & hypothermia.

cards.title: Pulse Oximetry & Co-oximetry
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Monitoring Methods (I.A.2.h)
secs: Pulse oximetry shines two wavelengths of light — 660 nm (red) and 940 nm (infrared) — through pulsatile (arterial) blood and applies the Beer–Lambert law. Deoxyhemoglobin absorbs more red light (660 nm), while oxyhemoglobin absorbs more infrared (940 nm), and the ratio gives the saturation. Co-oximetry uses four or more wavelengths and can detect carboxyhemoglobin and methemoglobin, which a standard pulse oximeter cannot.

Carboxyhemoglobin makes the SpO₂ read falsely high, while methemoglobin pulls the SpO₂ toward ~85% regardless of the true saturation. Other sources of error include motion, low perfusion, methylene blue, and nail polish; fetal hemoglobin and bilirubin do NOT interfere.
Wavelengths 660 nm (red) & 940 nm (IR); Beer-Lambert law MetHb → SpO2 plateaus ~85%; COHb → falsely high SpO2
*Pulse oximetry uses 660/940 nm light and the Beer–Lambert law; methemoglobin drags the SpO₂ toward 85% and carboxyhemoglobin falsely raises it — use co-oximetry to see what’s really there.*

head: Condition
Effect on SpO₂
tables.note: Uses 660 nm (deoxy) & 940 nm (oxy); co-oximetry detects COHb/MetHb.

cards.title: Blood Pressure Monitoring (NIBP, Invasive, Korotkoff)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Monitoring Methods (I.A.2.h)
secs: Oscillometric noninvasive blood pressure (NIBP) measures the MEAN pressure most accurately (at the point of maximum oscillation); the systolic and diastolic values are derived by algorithm. The cuff bladder should be about 40% of the arm’s circumference — too small a cuff reads falsely high, too large a cuff reads falsely low. An invasive arterial line gives beat-to-beat pressure; level its transducer at the phlebostatic axis (about the right-atrial level). Korotkoff sounds are the basis of manual (auscultatory) measurement.

An underdamped trace overshoots the systolic; an overdamped trace reads a low systolic and a narrow pulse pressure — diagnose either with a square-wave (fast-flush) test. Mis-leveling the transducer shifts the reading by about 0.74 mmHg for every cm of height difference.
Oscillometry reads MEAN most accurately; ~0.74 mmHg per cm height error Cuff bladder width ≈ 40% of limb circumference
*Oscillometric NIBP is most accurate for the MEAN; level an arterial transducer at the phlebostatic axis and use the square-wave test to check for over- or underdamping.*

cards.title: BIS & Processed-EEG Depth-of-Anesthesia Monitoring
secs: The Bispectral Index (BIS) condenses a single-channel frontal EEG into a dimensionless 0 to 100 number using bispectral (phase-coupling), power-spectral, and time-domain burst-suppression analysis. A forehead sensor feeds a processor that refreshes about every second but reflects a 15 to 30 second smoothing window, so the value LAGS abrupt changes.

Besides the index the monitor shows SQI (Signal Quality Index, percent: below about 50 the reading is unreliable), EMG (frontal muscle power in decibels: shivering or light planes RAISE it and can falsely elevate BIS), SR (Suppression Ratio, the percent of the last 63 seconds that is isoelectric: climbs with deep anesthesia), and SEF95 (Spectral Edge Frequency, the frequency below which 95 percent of EEG power lies).

Work top-down: confirm SQI is high and EMG low before trusting the number, then keep BIS 40 to 60 for surgery. Scan the RAW EEG for the higher-amplitude, slower waves of deeper planes and for burst suppression (flat stretches that drive up the SR), and follow the Density Spectral Array (DSA), a color plot of EEG power versus frequency over time, watching for a leftward (slowing) shift as depth increases.

BIS is validated for GABAergic agents (propofol, volatiles). It is UNRELIABLE with ketamine and nitrous oxide (which keep BIS high) and with dexmedetomidine; hypothermia, hypoglycemia, and cerebral ischemia LOWER it; and EMG, warming blankets, pacemakers, and eye movement cause artifact. B-Unaware and BAG-RECALL showed BIS is NOT superior to an end-tidal agent alarm for preventing awareness.

Target BIS 40 to 60 for general anesthesia, but trust it only when SQI is high and EMG low, corroborate it with the raw EEG and suppression ratio, and remember it is unreliable with ketamine, nitrous oxide, and dexmedetomidine.

cards.title: ECG & Echocardiogram Basics
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Monitoring Methods (I.A.2.h)
secs: Standard intraoperative monitoring uses lead II (good for P waves, rhythm, and inferior-wall ischemia) plus V5 (the anterolateral / left-anterior-descending territory); together they catch roughly 80–90% of ischemia. ST-segment analysis detects ischemia, so the correct filter mode (diagnostic vs monitoring) and accurate lead placement matter. Basic echocardiography (transesophageal or transthoracic) shows wall-motion abnormalities — the earliest sign of ischemia — along with volume status, valve function, tamponade, and embolism.
Use the five-lead system with correct electrode placement to limit artifact. A new regional wall-motion abnormality on echo appears before the ECG changes in ischemia.
*Monitor leads II and V5 for rhythm and ischemia (≈80–90% sensitivity); on echo, a new wall-motion abnormality is the earliest sign of ischemia.*

cards.title: Blood Gas Electrodes & Temperature Correction
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Instrumentation (I.A.2.i)
secs: Each blood-gas value has its own electrode: pH uses the Sanz (glass) electrode, PCO₂ the Severinghaus electrode, and PO₂ the Clark (polarographic) electrode. Samples are analyzed at 37°C and can optionally be temperature-corrected to the patient’s actual temperature. The alpha-stat approach manages the values uncorrected at 37°C, while the pH-stat approach corrects them to the patient’s temperature — a key distinction during hypothermic cardiopulmonary bypass.

In a hypothermic patient, uncorrected values overestimate the true PaCO₂ and underestimate the true pH of the cooled blood. Pre-analytic errors matter: an air bubble raises PO₂ and lowers PCO₂, and a delay before analysis (or a high white-cell count) lowers the measured PO₂.
pH → Sanz; PCO2 → Severinghaus; PO2 → Clark electrode Alpha-stat (uncorrected) vs pH-stat (corrected)
*Clark measures PO₂, Severinghaus PCO₂, and Sanz pH; whether you correct values to patient temperature (pH-stat) or not (alpha-stat) is the key decision on hypothermic bypass.*

cards.title: Gas Analysis (Infrared, Mass Spectrometry, Raman)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Instrumentation (I.A.2.i)
secs: Infrared absorption is the standard method for CO₂, nitrous oxide, and the volatile agents — a molecule must have at least two dissimilar atoms to absorb infrared. Infrared therefore cannot measure oxygen, nitrogen, or helium, so oxygen is measured separately (paramagnetically, or with a galvanic fuel cell or polarographic cell). Mass spectrometry and Raman scattering can analyze many gases, including O₂ and N₂, but are now largely historical in clinical practice.

Collision (pressure) broadening means coexisting nitrous oxide can falsely raise the measured CO₂ — modern analyzers correct for this. Water vapor and very high respiratory rates can also reduce accuracy.
IR analyzers: only molecules with ≥ 2 dissimilar atoms (no O2/N2/He)
*Infrared analyzers read CO₂, nitrous oxide, and volatiles but not oxygen or nitrogen; oxygen needs a paramagnetic or fuel-cell method.*

head: Method
Measures
tables.note: Infrared is standard for capnography; O₂ needs paramagnetic/fuel cell.

cards.title: Pressure Transducers (Resonance, Damping)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Instrumentation (I.A.2.i)
secs: An invasive pressure line is a fluid-filled system with its own natural (resonant) frequency and damping coefficient. You want a high natural frequency, well above the pressure waveform’s harmonics; short, stiff, wide, bubble-free tubing achieves this. An optimal damping coefficient of about 0.6–0.7 reproduces the waveform faithfully.

Use the square-wave (fast-flush) test to check: too few oscillations after the flush means overdamping (a falsely low systolic), while ringing or excess oscillations mean underdamping (a falsely high systolic) — the MEAN stays accurate either way. Common culprits are air bubbles, clots, long compliant tubing, and kinks.
Optimal damping coefficient ≈ 0.6–0.7; high natural frequency desired Over/underdamping distorts systolic/diastolic; MEAN remains accurate
*Aim for a high natural frequency and a damping coefficient near 0.6–0.7; the square-wave test diagnoses over- and underdamping, and the mean pressure stays accurate through both.*

head: State
Waveform
Effect
tables.note: Overdamp: bubbles/clots/long tubing. Underdamp: stiff tubing, tachycardia.

cards.title: Fluid Warmers, Autotransfusion & Forced-Air Warming
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Instrumentation (I.A.2.i)
secs: Forced-air warming is the single most effective active measure to prevent and treat perioperative hypothermia. Fluid and blood warmers prevent the hypothermia and arrhythmias that come from rapid cold transfusion; high-flow warmers are used for massive transfusion. Cell salvage (autotransfusion) collects, washes, and returns the patient’s own red cells, avoiding exposure to donor (allogeneic) blood.

Used incorrectly, warming devices can burn the skin — only use them as designed. Salvaged blood contains red cells but no platelets or clotting factors, so large volumes cause a dilutional coagulopathy; it is also used cautiously with malignancy, infection, or amniotic fluid in the surgical field.
*Forced-air warming is the best active warming method; warm all rapid transfusions; and remember cell salvage returns washed red cells but no clotting factors.*

cards.title: Ventilator Modes (Volume vs Pressure; A/C, SIMV)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Ventilators (I.A.2.j)
secs: Volume control delivers a set tidal volume with whatever pressure that takes — it guarantees minute ventilation but risks barotrauma if compliance falls. Pressure control delivers a set inspiratory pressure with a decelerating flow, so the volume varies — it caps peak pressure but the delivered volume drops if compliance worsens. Assist-control (A/C) fully supports every breath, whether the patient triggers it or the timer does; synchronized intermittent mandatory ventilation (SIMV) delivers a set number of mandatory breaths and lets the patient breathe spontaneously in between.

In VOLUME assist-control, every delivered breath — patient-triggered or time-triggered — gives the full SET tidal volume; the “assist” simply adds fully supported breaths above the set rate, each at the set volume. Pressure-regulated volume control is a hybrid that targets a set volume at the lowest possible pressure. Favor pressure control when there is a leak or low compliance (an LMA, pediatrics, or lung-protective ventilation in ARDS).
Volume control → fixed Vt, variable P; pressure control → fixed P, variable Vt

*Volume control guarantees the tidal volume while pressure varies; pressure control caps the pressure while volume varies; A/C supports every breath and SIMV blends mandatory with spontaneous breaths.*

cards.title: PEEP, CPAP, Pressure Support & APRV
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Ventilators (I.A.2.j)
secs: PEEP (positive end-expiratory pressure) holds pressure at end-expiration, which recruits alveoli, raises functional residual capacity (FRC), and improves oxygenation; too much PEEP causes barotrauma and reduces venous return and cardiac output. CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) applies a constant pressure to a spontaneously breathing patient, while pressure support boosts each spontaneous breath to overcome the resistance of the circuit and endotracheal tube. Airway pressure release ventilation (APRV) is essentially a high CPAP with brief timed releases — it improves oxygenation in ARDS while still allowing spontaneous breathing.

PEEP can raise the measured central venous pressure (CVP) and pulmonary artery occlusion pressure and lower preload, so the “best PEEP” balances oxygenation against hemodynamics. Watch for auto-PEEP (breath stacking) when the expiratory time is too short — it causes hyperinflation and hypotension. Noninvasive ventilation (CPAP/BiPAP) is contraindicated after recent upper-GI/esophageal surgery such as Nissen fundoplication — gastric insufflation can disrupt the fresh suture line; other contraindications include depressed consciousness, vomiting or high aspiration risk, facial trauma, an untreated pneumothorax, and hemodynamic instability.
*PEEP raises FRC and oxygenation but can drop cardiac output; pressure support eases spontaneous breathing; watch for auto-PEEP — and avoid noninvasive ventilation after fresh upper-GI surgery.*

cards.title: Compliance Monitoring (Static vs Dynamic)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Ventilators (I.A.2.j)
secs: Compliance is the change in volume per change in pressure (ΔV/ΔP) — how easily the lungs and chest wall inflate. Static compliance = tidal volume ÷ (plateau pressure − PEEP), measured with no flow, so it reflects the elastance of the lung and chest wall. Dynamic compliance = tidal volume ÷ (peak pressure − PEEP), which also includes airway resistance. A widening gap between peak and plateau pressure points to a RESISTANCE problem (bronchospasm, a kink, secretions), whereas a rise in both peak and plateau points to a COMPLIANCE problem (pulmonary edema, pneumothorax, mainstem intubation, raised abdominal pressure).
Tracking these pressures and the loops lets you localize an intraoperative ventilation problem quickly.
Cstatic = Vt / (Pplateau − PEEP) Cdynamic = Vt / (Ppeak − PEEP) ↑ peak with normal plateau = resistance; ↑ both = compliance problem

*Static compliance uses the plateau pressure and dynamic uses the peak; a widening peak-to-plateau gap points to a resistance problem such as bronchospasm, while both rising together point to a compliance problem.*

cards.title: Alarms & Safety Features (Monitoring)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Alarms & Safety Features (I.A.2.k)
secs: Low-airway-pressure (disconnect) and high-pressure alarms are the main defense against circuit disconnection and obstruction. Capnography apnea and low-EtCO₂ alarms are the most reliable way to detect a disconnection. Critical alarms — oxygen supply failure, low FiO₂, and sustained high pressure — are deliberately designed so they cannot be fully defeated.

“Alarm fatigue” from poorly chosen limits leads to alarms being silenced or ignored, so set thresholds that fit the individual patient. The pulse oximeter and capnograph alarms are the most important continuous safety monitors.
*Capnography apnea and low-airway-pressure alarms best detect a disconnection; oxygen-failure and low-FiO₂ alarms are deliberately non-defeatable safety alarms.*

cards.title: Defibrillators, AEDs & Cardioversion
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Electrical Safety & Devices (I.A.2.l)
secs: Defibrillation is an UNsynchronized shock for ventricular fibrillation (VF) or pulseless ventricular tachycardia (VT); cardioversion is a SYNCHRONIZED shock (timed to the R wave) for organized rhythms such as atrial fibrillation, supraventricular tachycardia, or VT with a pulse. Biphasic waveforms defibrillate at lower energy and with a higher first-shock success rate than older monophasic devices. Typical biphasic defibrillation energy is ~120–200 J, while synchronized cardioversion uses lower, rhythm-specific energies.

With a pacemaker or implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD), place the pads anterior–posterior and at least 8 cm from the generator; a magnet suspends ICD shocks (device-specific) and can switch a pacemaker to asynchronous pacing. Never use synchronized mode for VF — there is no R wave for the device to track.
Biphasic defibrillation ~120–200 J; synchronize only for organized rhythms
*Defibrillation is unsynchronized (for VF or pulseless VT); cardioversion is synchronized (for organized rhythms); biphasic devices need less energy with better first-shock success.*

cards.title: Electrical Safety (Macroshock, Microshock, Isolation)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Electrical & Fire Hazards (I.A.2.m)
secs: Macroshock is current crossing intact skin: roughly 1 mA is perceptible, ~5 mA is the “let-go” threshold, and ~100 mA can cause VF. Microshock is current delivered through a direct conduit to the heart (a pacing wire or a saline-filled central line) that bypasses skin resistance — as little as ~100 µA (0.1 mA) can cause VF. The line isolation monitor (LIM) warns when a first electrical fault has effectively grounded the OR’s isolated power system (it alarms at about 2–5 mA) — but it does NOT cut the power. Isolation transformers and proper equipment grounding reduce the shock risk.

When the LIM alarms, unplug the last device you added; the system remains safe until a SECOND fault occurs. Ground-fault circuit interrupters are used where isolated power isn’t feasible — they cut power the moment they sense an imbalance.
Macroshock VF ~100 mA; microshock VF ~100 µA (0.1 mA) Perception ~1 mA; let-go ~5 mA; LIM alarms ~2–5 mA
*The microshock VF threshold (~100 µA) is about a thousandth of the macroshock threshold (~100 mA); the LIM warns of the first fault but keeps the power on, so unplug the last device added.*

head: Current
Effect
tables.note: LIM alarms at ~2 mA leakage; isolation transformers prevent macroshock.

cards.title: Fire Safety & NFPA Standards (Intraoperative Fire)
cards.cat: Basic Sciences › Physics, Monitoring & Delivery Devices › Electrical & Fire Hazards (I.A.2.m)
secs: The fire triad is an oxidizer (oxygen or nitrous oxide) plus an ignition source (electrocautery, laser, or a fiberoptic light) plus a fuel (drapes, prep solution, the endotracheal tube, or hair). The highest risk is head, neck, and airway surgery that combines open oxygen delivery with electrocautery — the setting of most operating-room fires.
