Prevent fires by keeping FiO₂ as low as is safe (avoid open oxygen above 30% near cautery), letting alcohol-based prep dry fully, using cuffed or laser-resistant tubes, and communicating before using cautery near the airway.

If an airway fire occurs, STOP: remove the endotracheal tube and stop the gases first, pour saline, then re-ventilate, re-secure the airway, and assess for injury. NFPA 99 is the standard governing operating-room electrical, gas, and fire safety.
Fire triad = oxidizer + ignition source + fuel
*The OR fire triad is oxidizer plus ignition plus fuel; airway fires cluster in head-and-neck cases, so minimize FiO₂ near cautery — and if a fire starts, remove the tube and stop the gases first.*

cards.title: ASA Monitoring Standards
secs: A qualified anesthesia provider must be continuously present throughout the anesthetic. Oxygenation is monitored with an FiO₂ analyzer and pulse oximetry; ventilation with continuous capnography and clinical signs; circulation with continuous ECG and a blood pressure and heart rate at least every 5 minutes; and temperature whenever a significant change is expected. Together these make up the ASA Standards for Basic Anesthetic Monitoring.
Capnography is required to confirm ventilation and intubation in general anesthesia and deep sedation (and increasingly in moderate sedation). Alarms must be audible and set to appropriate limits.
Continuous O2/SpO2 + EtCO2 + ECG;  BP at least every 5 min;  temp when indicated

*ASA basic monitoring is continuous oxygenation (SpO₂/FiO₂), ventilation (capnography), and circulation (ECG with blood pressure at least every 5 minutes), plus temperature when relevant — with a provider always present.*

cards.title: Vaporizer Filled with the Wrong Agent
secs: Variable-bypass (agent-specific) vaporizers set the splitting ratio between bypass and vaporizing-chamber flow based on each agent's saturated vapor pressure (SVP). Filling a vaporizer with an agent of HIGHER SVP than it was calibrated for (e.g. isoflurane in a sevoflurane vaporizer) delivers MORE vapor than the dial reads (overdose risk). A LOWER-SVP agent in a higher-SVP vaporizer under-delivers. Relative potency (MAC) further modifies the net clinical effect.

Cross-filling is a classic equipment hazard. Modern vaporizers have keyed, agent-specific filling systems specifically to prevent this. Desflurane is the exception: its high SVP (near 1 atm at body temperature) mandates a separate heated, pressurized vaporizer — it cannot be used in a variable-bypass vaporizer at all.
SVP at 20 degrees C: sevoflurane ~157 mmHg, isoflurane ~238 mmHg, halothane ~243 mmHg, desflurane ~669 mmHg. MAC (approx): sevoflurane 2.0%, isoflurane 1.15%, desflurane 6.0%.
Agent with higher SVP than the vaporizer expects -> output HIGHER than the dial; keyed fillers prevent cross-filling.

cards.title: Absolute vs. Relative Humidity
secs: ABSOLUTE HUMIDITY is the actual mass of water vapor contained in a given volume of gas (mg/L or g/m3). RELATIVE HUMIDITY is the ratio of the absolute humidity to the maximum water vapor the gas can hold at that temperature (its saturation point), expressed as a percentage. Warmer gas can hold more water, so for a fixed water content the RELATIVE HUMIDITY falls as temperature rises and climbs as temperature drops. Fully saturated gas at body temperature (37 C) holds about 44 mg/L; typical room air at 20-22 C and ~50% RH holds roughly 10 mg/L.

Medical gases from cylinders and pipelines are essentially anhydrous (0% humidity). Delivering dry, cold gas directly to the trachea impairs mucociliary clearance, dries and damages airway epithelium, inspissates secretions, and causes patient heat and water loss. This is the rationale for HEAT AND MOISTURE EXCHANGERS (artificial noses) and active humidifiers. A circle system partially conserves humidity through rebreathing of exhaled gas and through water generated by the CO2 absorbent reaction.

Saturated water vapor content: ~44 mg/L at 37 C, ~17 mg/L at 20 C. Saturated vapor PRESSURE at 37 C = 47 mmHg. Relative humidity (%) = (absolute humidity / saturated humidity at that temperature) x 100.

Absolute humidity is the mass of water per volume of gas; relative humidity expresses that as a percent of saturation at a given temperature, so warmer gas holds more and RH falls as temperature rises.

cards.title: Seven Components of the Circle Breathing System
secs: The CIRCLE SYSTEM is the standard anesthesia breathing circuit; it conserves heat, humidity, and anesthetic gas by allowing partial rebreathing while a CO2 ABSORBER removes exhaled carbon dioxide (see figure). Its SEVEN primary components are: (1) FRESH GAS INFLOW source; (2) two UNIDIRECTIONAL VALVES, one inspiratory and one expiratory, which enforce one-way flow; (3) two CORRUGATED TUBES (inspiratory and expiratory limbs); (4) the Y-PIECE CONNECTOR joining both limbs to the patient; (5) the ADJUSTABLE PRESSURE-LIMITING (APL) valve; (6) the RESERVOIR BAG; and (7) the CARBON DIOXIDE ABSORBER canister. The figure shows the closed-loop arrangement of these parts.

Component arrangement, not just presence, determines function. The UNIDIRECTIONAL valves are what make partial rebreathing without CO2 accumulation possible; a stuck or incompetent valve is a classic cause of rebreathing (rising inspired CO2) or increased work of breathing. The APL valve vents excess gas during spontaneous/manual ventilation and is bypassed during mechanical ventilation. Rebreathing of exhaled CO2 occurs if fresh gas flow is too low relative to absorber capacity or if a valve fails. The circle can be run as a low-flow or closed system to reduce anesthetic use, but this raises the risk of hypoxic mixtures and volatile agent degradation, so an oxygen analyzer and capnography are mandatory safeguards.

The circle system's seven parts are fresh gas inflow, two unidirectional (inspiratory/expiratory) valves, two corrugated tubes, Y-piece, APL valve, reservoir bag, and CO2 absorber — the unidirectional valves are what permit safe partial rebreathing.

noteimg.cap: Schematic of the circle breathing system showing the seven components and unidirectional gas flow.

cards.title: Conditions to Prevent CO2 Rebreathing in the Circle System
secs: For a circle system to prevent rebreathing of exhaled CO2, three RULES (attributed to the classic circle-system placement rules) must hold: (1) a UNIDIRECTIONAL VALVE must sit between the patient and the reservoir bag on BOTH the inspiratory and expiratory limbs; (2) FRESH GAS FLOW must NOT enter the circuit between the patient and the EXPIRATORY valve; and (3) the APL (pop-off) VALVE must NOT be located between the INSPIRATORY valve and the patient. When these are satisfied, exhaled gas is directed through the CO2 absorber rather than back to the patient on the next breath.

These rules explain why valve and fresh-gas placement matter more than fresh gas flow rate for preventing rebreathing in a properly built circle system. If fresh gas entered downstream of the expiratory valve, it would be vented before reaching the patient and exhaled gas would be rebreathed; if the APL valve sat between the inspiratory valve and the patient, fresh/inspired gas would be preferentially vented. Practical failure modes that cause rebreathing despite correct design include an INCOMPETENT or STUCK unidirectional valve (look for rising inspired CO2 with a raised capnogram baseline) and an EXHAUSTED CO2 ABSORBENT. Unlike the circle, MAPLESON (open) systems have no CO2 absorber and rely entirely on high fresh gas flows to prevent rebreathing.

Prevent circle-system rebreathing with three rules: a unidirectional valve between patient and bag on both limbs, no fresh gas inflow between patient and the expiratory valve, and the APL valve NOT between the inspiratory valve and the patient.

cards.title: Cardiac Cycle; Impulse Propagation; Normal ECG
secs: Conduction runs from the SA node (the pacemaker, about 60–100/min) to the atria, the AV node (where it is delayed), the bundle of His, the bundle branches, and the Purkinje fibers. The ECG shows the P wave (atrial depolarization), the QRS (ventricular depolarization), and the T wave (ventricular repolarization); the PR interval is 120–200 ms, the QRS is under 120 ms, and the QT is rate-corrected (QTc). The cardiac cycle is systole (isovolumic contraction then ejection) and diastole (isovolumic relaxation then filling); most left-ventricular coronary perfusion occurs in DIASTOLE.
The pacemaker hierarchy descends: SA node (60–100) > AV junction (40–60) > ventricular (20–40). A prolonged QT raises the risk of torsades (from drugs or electrolyte abnormalities).
PR 120–200 ms; QRS < 120 ms; SA 60–100, AV 40–60, ventricular 20–40 LV coronary perfusion mainly in diastole

*Conduction runs SA → AV (delay) → His-Purkinje; the ECG shows P-QRS-T (PR 120–200, QRS under 120 ms); left-ventricular coronary perfusion happens in diastole, and the pacemaker hierarchy descends from the SA node (60–100).*

cards.title: Ion Channels & Cardiac Electrophysiology
secs: The ventricular action potential has phases: 0, rapid depolarization (sodium in); 1, early repolarization (a transient potassium efflux); 2, the plateau (calcium in balancing potassium out — a long plateau unique to cardiac muscle); 3, repolarization (potassium out); and 4, the resting potential (the Na/K-ATPase). The SA and AV nodal cells have a spontaneous phase-4 depolarization (the funny current and calcium) and a slower calcium-dependent upstroke, giving them automaticity. The refractory periods prevent tetany and re-entry.

The Vaughan-Williams antiarrhythmic classes are: I sodium blockers, II beta-blockers, III potassium blockers (which prolong the QT), and IV calcium-channel blockers. Hyperkalemia (peaked T waves, a wide QRS) and hypokalemia (U waves, torsades) alter excitability.
AP: 0 Na+ in, 1 K+ out, 2 Ca2+ plateau, 3 K+ out, 4 rest Vaughan-Williams: I Na / II beta / III K / IV Ca

*The cardiac action potential has a calcium plateau (phase 2) unique to myocardium, and nodal cells auto-depolarize (phase 4); the antiarrhythmics map to Vaughan-Williams I–IV (sodium, beta, potassium, calcium).*

cards.title: Frank-Starling Law; Preload & Afterload
secs: The Frank-Starling law: increasing preload (the end-diastolic volume, or sarcomere stretch) increases stroke volume up to a point (the Starling curve), and a failing heart operates on a flatter, lower curve. Preload is approximately the ventricular end-diastolic volume (estimated, with limitations, by the CVP or pulmonary artery occlusion pressure), and afterload is approximately the wall stress or resistance the ventricle ejects against (roughly the SVR and aortic impedance). Stroke volume depends on preload, afterload, and contractility, and cardiac output = stroke volume × heart rate.

Increasing the afterload reduces the stroke volume (more so in a failing heart), so afterload reduction helps systolic failure and mitral regurgitation. Contractility (inotropy) shifts the Starling curve up or down independently of preload.
CO = SV × HR; SV depends on preload, afterload, contractility Frank-Starling: ↑ preload → ↑ SV (to a plateau); failing heart = flatter curve

*Frank-Starling: more preload raises the stroke volume to a plateau (failing hearts sit on a flatter curve); stroke volume also falls with higher afterload and rises with contractility — and cardiac output = stroke volume × heart rate.*

cards.title: Myocardial Contractility; Systolic & Diastolic Function
secs: Contractility (inotropy) is the intrinsic force of contraction, independent of preload and afterload; it is reflected by the ejection fraction and by the slope of the end-systolic pressure-volume relationship (ESPVR). Systolic function is the ability to eject (a normal ejection fraction is about 55–70%) and is reduced in dilated or ischemic cardiomyopathy (heart failure with reduced EF). Diastolic function is the ability to relax and fill at a low pressure; diastolic dysfunction (heart failure with preserved EF) gives a stiff ventricle with high filling pressures and a preserved ejection fraction.

Contractility is increased by catecholamines (beta-1), calcium, and digoxin, and decreased by ischemia, acidosis, beta-blockers, and volatiles. On the pressure-volume loop, the ESPVR reflects contractility and the EDPVR reflects compliance.
EF normal ~55–70%; contractility = ESPVR slope (load-independent) HFrEF (systolic) vs HFpEF (diastolic, preserved EF)

*Contractility is the load-independent contraction force (ejection fraction 55–70%, the ESPVR slope); systolic failure lowers the EF (HFrEF) while diastolic dysfunction stiffens the ventricle with a preserved EF (HFpEF).*

cards.title: Cardiac Output: Determinants & Fick Principle
secs: Cardiac output = stroke volume × heart rate, and stroke volume depends on preload, afterload, and contractility; the normal cardiac output is about 4–8 L/min and the cardiac index about 2.5–4 L/min/m². The Fick principle: cardiac output = oxygen consumption divided by the arteriovenous oxygen content difference (VO₂ / (CaO₂ − CvO₂)). It is measured by thermodilution (a pulmonary artery catheter), the Fick method, or pulse-contour and echo techniques.
Extremes of heart rate reduce cardiac output (tachycardia limits filling, bradycardia limits frequency). The cardiac index normalizes cardiac output to body size.
CO = SV × HR (~4–8 L/min); CI ~2.5–4 L/min/m² Fick: CO = VO2 / (CaO2 − CvO2)

*Cardiac output = stroke volume × heart rate (normal about 4–8 L/min, index 2.5–4); the Fick principle computes it as VO₂/(CaO₂ − CvO₂), and stroke volume reflects preload, afterload, and contractility.*

cards.title: Venous Return & Vascular Compliance
secs: Venous return is driven by the gradient between the mean systemic filling pressure and the right atrial pressure; at steady state, venous return equals cardiac output. The veins hold about 70% of the blood volume (they are capacitance vessels), so venoconstriction or leg elevation raises the mean systemic filling pressure and the preload. The venous return curve falls as the right atrial pressure rises, and its intersection with the cardiac function curve sets the operating point.

A spinal or epidural sympathectomy and vasodilators reduce the mean systemic filling pressure, lowering venous return and causing hypotension. Positive-pressure ventilation and PEEP raise intrathoracic pressure, reducing venous return.
Venous return ∝ (MSFP − RAP); veins hold ~70% of blood volume

*Venous return is driven by the mean systemic filling pressure minus the right atrial pressure; the venous system holds about 70% of the blood volume, so venodilation, sympathectomy, and PEEP cut preload and cardiac output.*

cards.title: Baroreceptor Function
secs: The baroreceptors (in the carotid sinus, via cranial nerve IX, and the aortic arch, via cranial nerve X) sense arterial stretch and feed the medullary cardiovascular center. A rise in blood pressure increases their firing, which reflexively decreases sympathetic and increases vagal tone, lowering heart rate, contractility, and SVR; a fall does the opposite (tachycardia and vasoconstriction). This provides rapid, beat-to-beat buffering of short-term blood pressure.

Anesthetics (the volatiles) blunt the baroreceptor reflex, leaving less compensation for hypotension. Chronic hypertension resets the baroreflex to a higher set point, and carotid sinus pressure can trigger bradycardia and hypotension.
↑ BP → ↑ baroreceptor firing → ↓ sympathetic + ↑ vagal (↓ HR)

*Baroreceptors (the carotid sinus via cranial nerve IX, the aortic arch via cranial nerve X) buffer acute blood pressure — a rise triggers a reflex bradycardia and vasodilation; volatiles blunt the reflex and chronic hypertension resets its set point.*

cards.title: Microcirculation; Starling’s Law; Osmotic Pressure
secs: The Starling forces govern capillary fluid exchange: filtration depends on the difference in hydrostatic pressure (capillary minus interstitial) minus the difference in oncotic pressure (capillary minus interstitial), scaled by the reflection coefficient. There is net filtration at the arteriolar end (where hydrostatic pressure dominates) and reabsorption at the venular end, with excess interstitial fluid returning through the lymphatics. The plasma oncotic pressure (about 25–28 mmHg, mainly from albumin) opposes filtration, so hypoalbuminemia causes edema.

The revised model emphasizes the endothelial glycocalyx, which is damaged in sepsis (causing capillary leak and edema). Edema results from increased capillary pressure (venous congestion, fluid overload) or increased leak (inflammation).
Jv = Kf × [(Pc − Pi) − σ(πc − πi)]; plasma oncotic ~25–28 mmHg

*The Starling forces (hydrostatic versus oncotic, with the glycocalyx and reflection coefficient) set capillary filtration; the plasma oncotic pressure of about 25–28 mmHg (albumin) opposes edema, and glycocalyx damage in sepsis causes capillary leak.*

head: Force
Direction
tables.note: Low albumin (↓ oncotic) or ↑ hydrostatic → edema; glycocalyx matters.

cards.title: Regional Blood Flow Regulation
secs: Coronary flow occurs mainly in DIASTOLE (for the left ventricle); it is autoregulated and matched to myocardial oxygen demand (via adenosine and local metabolites), and the high baseline oxygen extraction means flow must rise to meet demand. The cerebral circulation is autoregulated (over a mean pressure of about 50–150), CO₂-reactive, and flow-metabolism coupled; the renal circulation is autoregulated (by myogenic and tubuloglomerular feedback) over a mean pressure of about 80–180. The uteroplacental circulation is NOT autoregulated — it is pressure-passive, so maternal hypotension directly reduces fetal perfusion.

Local metabolic control dominates the coronary and cerebral beds, while sympathetic tone affects the skin, splanchnic, and renal beds. Avoid maternal hypotension, since uteroplacental flow is pressure-dependent.
Coronary flow in diastole, high O2 extraction; uteroplacental NOT autoregulated (pressure-passive)

*Coronary flow occurs in diastole with a high oxygen extraction; the cerebral and renal beds autoregulate, but the uteroplacental flow is pressure-passive — so maternal hypotension directly threatens the fetus.*

cards.title: Mixed Venous Oxygen Saturation
secs: The mixed venous oxygen saturation (SvO₂, from the pulmonary artery) reflects the balance of oxygen delivery against consumption, and is normally about 65–75%. It falls with decreased delivery (low cardiac output, anemia, hypoxemia) or increased consumption (fever, shivering, pain), and it rises with increased delivery, decreased extraction (sepsis, cyanide), or a left-to-right shunt or a wedged catheter. The central venous saturation (ScvO₂) approximates the SvO₂ (running a few percent higher) and is more practical to obtain.
A falling SvO₂ is an early warning of inadequate oxygen delivery. It is useful for goal-directed resuscitation and for assessing weaning.
SvO2 normal ~65–75%; reflects O2 delivery/consumption balance ↓ SvO2: low CO / anemia / hypoxemia or ↑ consumption

*The SvO₂ (normally about 65–75%, from the pulmonary artery) tracks the oxygen delivery-to-consumption balance — it falls with low cardiac output, anemia, or hypoxemia, or with high consumption, and the ScvO₂ is the practical surrogate.*

head: SvO₂
Interpretation
rows: ↓ DO₂ or ↑ VO₂ (low CO, anemia, hypoxemia)
tables.note: Reflects global O₂ supply–demand balance.

cards.title: BLS & ACLS Algorithms; Defibrillation
secs: Basic life support is high-quality compressions (a rate of 100–120/min, a depth of at least 2 inches/5 cm, full recoil, and minimal interruptions), 30:2 until the airway is secured, and early defibrillation. For the shockable rhythms (ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia): defibrillate immediately and continue CPR; give epinephrine 1 mg every 3–5 minutes and amiodarone 300 mg (then 150 mg) for refractory cases. For the non-shockable rhythms (asystole or PEA): give CPR and epinephrine, do NOT shock, and search for reversible causes (the Hs and Ts).

The Hs are hypoxia, hypovolemia, hydrogen ion (acidosis), hypo- or hyperkalemia, and hypothermia; the Ts are tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, and thrombosis (pulmonary embolism or myocardial infarction). Capnography guides quality (an EtCO₂ under 10 means poor CPR; an abrupt rise signals return of spontaneous circulation).
Compressions 100–120/min, ≥ 5 cm; epinephrine 1 mg q3–5 min; amiodarone 300 then 150 mg Shock VF/pVT only; asystole/PEA = no shock; find the Hs & Ts

*High-quality CPR (100–120/min, at least 5 cm) plus early defibrillation for VF/pulseless VT, epinephrine 1 mg every 3–5 minutes, and amiodarone 300 then 150 mg; asystole and PEA aren’t shocked — find the Hs and Ts, and let EtCO₂ guide quality and signal return of circulation.*

cards.title: Coronary Circulation; Conduction System
secs: The right coronary artery supplies the right ventricle, the SA node (about 60% of people) and AV node (about 90%), and the inferior left ventricle (in right dominance), so its occlusion causes an inferior MI with bradyarrhythmias and heart block. The left main artery divides into the LAD (the anterior wall, septum, and apex) and the circumflex (the lateral wall); the LAD is the most commonly occluded (the “widow-maker”). The coronary venous drainage is mainly through the coronary sinus into the right atrium.

“Dominance” refers to which artery supplies the posterior descending artery (the RCA in about 85%). The left-ventricular subendocardium is the most vulnerable to ischemia (it is perfused in diastole and has the highest wall tension).
RCA → SA/AV nodes + inferior wall; LAD → anterior/septum; LCx → lateral Subendocardium most ischemia-prone (diastolic perfusion)

*The right coronary artery supplies the SA and AV nodes and the inferior wall (occlusion → block and bradycardia); the LAD feeds the anterior wall and septum (the widow-maker) and the circumflex the lateral wall; the subendocardium is the most ischemia-prone.*

head: Artery
Wall
ECG leads
tables.note: RCA usually supplies SA & AV nodes → inferior MI risks bradyarrhythmia.

cards.title: Digitalis: Actions & Toxicity
secs: Digoxin inhibits the Na/K-ATPase, so more intracellular sodium means less calcium is extruded by the Na/Ca exchanger, raising intracellular calcium for a positive inotropy; it also increases vagal tone (slowing AV conduction for rate control in atrial fibrillation). It has a narrow therapeutic index, and its toxicity is worsened by HYPOKALEMIA (digoxin and potassium compete at the pump), hypomagnesemia, hypercalcemia, and renal failure (it is renally cleared). Toxicity causes nausea and vomiting, visual changes (yellow halos), and arrhythmias (classically paroxysmal atrial tachycardia with block, and bidirectional ventricular tachycardia).

Treat severe toxicity with digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Fab), correct the potassium and magnesium, and avoid calcium (which worsens it). It is used for rate control in atrial fibrillation and for symptom control in heart failure with reduced EF.
Inhibits Na/K-ATPase → ↑ intracellular Ca (inotropy); ↑ vagal tone (AV slowing) Toxicity ↑ with hypokalemia; treat with digoxin-Fab

*Digoxin inhibits the Na/K-ATPase (raising intracellular calcium for inotropy) and slows AV conduction (vagal); hypokalemia precipitates toxicity (visual halos, arrhythmias) — treat with digoxin-Fab and correct the potassium and magnesium.*

head: Aspect
Detail
tables.note: Narrow therapeutic index.

cards.title: Inotropes
secs: The catecholamines act on adrenergic receptors: dopamine (dose-dependent, dopaminergic then beta then alpha), dobutamine (beta-1 inotropy with mild beta-2 vasodilation), epinephrine (beta at low doses, alpha at high), norepinephrine (alpha more than beta — a vasopressor with some inotropy), and isoproterenol (pure beta). They all raise contractility, heart rate, and myocardial oxygen demand, so choose by whether you also want vasoconstriction or vasodilation. Use dobutamine for low-output failure with an adequate pressure, and epinephrine or norepinephrine for shock with hypotension.
They all raise myocardial oxygen demand and can cause arrhythmias and tachycardia. Calcium and milrinone are non-catecholamine inotropes.
Dobutamine beta-1; epinephrine beta(low)/alpha(high); norepinephrine alpha>beta; isoproterenol pure beta

*Inotropes work through adrenergic receptors — dobutamine (beta-1), epinephrine (beta then alpha by dose), norepinephrine (alpha more than beta), and isoproterenol (pure beta); all raise myocardial oxygen demand and arrhythmia risk.*

cards.title: Phosphodiesterase III Inhibitors (Milrinone)
secs: Phosphodiesterase III inhibitors increase cAMP, which raises intracellular calcium in the myocardium (positive inotropy) AND relaxes vascular smooth muscle (vasodilation) — an “inodilator.” They reduce preload and afterload while boosting contractility, which is useful in decompensated heart failure and after cardiac surgery (especially with pulmonary hypertension and right-ventricular failure). They act independently of the beta-receptors, so they work despite beta-blockade or receptor downregulation.

The main side effects are hypotension (from vasodilation) and arrhythmias; milrinone has a longer half-life than the catecholamines and is renally cleared (accumulating in renal failure). It is often paired with a vasopressor to offset the hypotension.
PDE III inhibition → ↑ cAMP → inotropy + vasodilation (“inodilator”)

*Milrinone (a phosphodiesterase III inhibitor) raises cAMP for inotropy plus systemic and pulmonary vasodilation (an “inodilator”) and works despite beta-blockade — but it causes hypotension and accumulates in renal failure.*

cards.title: Antiarrhythmics
secs: Class I (sodium-channel blockers): Ia (quinidine, procainamide — prolong the QT), Ib (lidocaine — for ventricular arrhythmias, shortens the action potential), and Ic (flecainide — avoid in structural heart disease). Class II (beta-blockers): rate control and slowed AV conduction. Class III (potassium-channel blockers — prolong repolarization and the QT): amiodarone (which also has class I, II, and IV effects) and sotalol. Class IV (calcium-channel blockers): verapamil and diltiazem, which slow the AV node.

Adenosine (a transient AV block) treats SVT; magnesium treats torsades; and amiodarone is first-line for many ventricular arrhythmias. Amiodarone’s toxicities are pulmonary fibrosis, thyroid and hepatic dysfunction, and corneal and skin deposits.
I Na / II beta / III K (QT) / IV Ca; adenosine for SVT, Mg for torsades
*Antiarrhythmics map to Vaughan-Williams I–IV (sodium, beta, potassium, calcium); amiodarone (class III, mixed) is broadly used but organ-toxic — adenosine treats SVT and magnesium treats torsades.*

head: Class
Action
Examples
tables.note: Other: adenosine, digoxin, magnesium.

cards.title: Vasodilators (Nitroprusside, Nitroglycerin, Hydralazine, CCBs)
secs: Nitroprusside gives balanced arterial and venous dilation via nitric oxide; it is rapid and titratable but risks cyanide toxicity (a metabolic acidosis and a high SvO₂) at high doses or prolonged use, and it can cause coronary steal and a reflex tachycardia. Nitroglycerin is predominantly a venodilator (reducing preload) at low doses, plus a coronary dilator, so it treats ischemia and reduces preload. Hydralazine is an arterial dilator (reducing afterload); the calcium-channel blockers nicardipine and clevidipine are arterial dilators, while verapamil and diltiazem also slow the rate.

For nitroprusside cyanide toxicity, treat with sodium thiosulfate or hydroxocobalamin and limit the dose and duration. Vasodilators can raise the ICP (through cerebral vasodilation), so use caution in neurosurgery.
Nitroprusside = balanced (NO), cyanide risk; nitroglycerin = venodilator (↓ preload)

*Nitroprusside is a balanced nitric-oxide dilator (watch for cyanide toxicity); nitroglycerin is mainly a venodilator (reducing preload and treating ischemia); hydralazine and dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers reduce afterload — and all can raise the ICP.*

head: Drug
Site
Note
tables.note: Nicardipine/clevidipine give smoother control.

cards.title: ACE Inhibitors & ARBs
secs: ACE inhibitors (the “-prils”) block the conversion of angiotensin I to II and reduce bradykinin breakdown (causing cough and angioedema); ARBs (the “-sartans”) block the AT1 receptor (with less cough). They reduce afterload and remodeling, benefiting hypertension, heart failure with reduced EF, diabetic nephropathy, and the post-MI patient. They are often HELD on the morning of surgery to reduce refractory intraoperative hypotension (vasoplegia).

They cause hyperkalemia and renal effects (especially with bilateral renal-artery stenosis or with NSAIDs and diuretics). ACE-inhibitor angioedema is a rare airway emergency, and refractory vasoplegic hypotension responds to vasopressin.
ACE inhibitor: ↓ angiotensin II + ↑ bradykinin (cough/angioedema); ARB: AT1 block Often held morning of surgery; refractory hypotension → vasopressin

*ACE inhibitors (cough and angioedema from bradykinin) and ARBs reduce afterload and remodeling; they are often held on the morning of surgery, and ACE-inhibitor-related vasoplegic hypotension responds to vasopressin.*

cards.title: Vasopressin & Congeners
secs: Vasopressin (ADH) acts at V1 receptors (on vascular smooth muscle, causing vasoconstriction) and V2 receptors (on the renal collecting duct, causing water reabsorption via aquaporins). It is a non-catecholamine vasopressor that works in acidosis and in catecholamine-refractory or vasoplegic shock (sepsis, post-bypass, ACE-inhibitor vasoplegia), and at low doses it preserves coronary and renal perfusion. Its congeners are terlipressin and desmopressin (DDAVP); DDAVP (V2-selective) treats diabetes insipidus and boosts factor VIII and von Willebrand factor (for mild hemophilia A, von Willebrand disease, and uremic platelet dysfunction).

The adverse effects are excessive vasoconstriction (splanchnic, coronary, or digital ischemia) and hyponatremia (via V2). ACLS no longer prioritizes vasopressin over epinephrine, but it remains useful for vasoplegia.
V1 = vasoconstriction; V2 = renal water reabsorption (DDAVP also raises VIII/vWF) Effective in acidosis / catecholamine-refractory vasoplegia

*Vasopressin constricts via V1 (working in acidosis and catecholamine-refractory vasoplegia) and conserves water via V2; DDAVP is the V2 congener that treats diabetes insipidus and raises factor VIII and von Willebrand factor.*

cards.title: Vascular Pressures: CVP, PAP, PAOP, LVEDP
secs: The CVP (normally about 2–6 mmHg) estimates right-heart filling; its waveform has an a wave (atrial contraction), a c wave (tricuspid bulge), and a v wave (atrial filling), with x and y descents. The pulmonary artery catheter reads the PA pressure (about 25/10) and then the wedge (the PAOP, about 6–12 mmHg), which estimates the left atrial pressure and LVEDP (preload) when there is no mitral disease or high PEEP. The LVEDP is the true left-sided preload, and the PAOP approximates it under ideal conditions.

The CVP and PAOP poorly predict fluid responsiveness — dynamic measures (stroke-volume or pulse-pressure variation, a passive leg raise) are better. Large v waves (mitral regurgitation) and cannon a waves (AV dissociation) are diagnostic clues.
CVP ~2–6; PAP ~25/10; PAOP ~6–12 mmHg ≈ LAP/LVEDP a/c/v waves; dynamic indices beat static CVP for fluid responsiveness

*The CVP (about 2–6) estimates right-sided preload and the PAOP (about 6–12) approximates the LVEDP and left-sided preload; both poorly predict fluid responsiveness — prefer dynamic indices, and read the a/c/v waves for clues.*

head: Parameter
Normal
tables.note: SVR/PVR in dyn·s·cm⁻⁵.

cards.title: TEE & Echocardiography
secs: Transesophageal echo assesses ventricular function and volume, wall-motion abnormalities (the earliest sign of ischemia), valve pathology, tamponade, embolism, and aortic disease, and it guides valve and structural repair. The standard views include the mid-esophageal four-chamber and the transgastric short-axis (for volume and ischemia). Doppler quantifies gradients and regurgitation (using the modified Bernoulli equation, ΔP = 4v²).

The indications include hemodynamic instability, cardiac surgery, and suspected embolism or tamponade. The relative contraindications are esophageal pathology (strictures, varices, recent surgery), and complications (perforation) are rare.
Modified Bernoulli: ΔP = 4v²; a new regional wall-motion abnormality = earliest ischemia sign

*TEE shows ventricular function, valves, tamponade, and embolism — a new wall-motion abnormality is the earliest sign of ischemia; Doppler grades gradients via ΔP = 4v², and it is avoided with esophageal pathology.*

cards.title: Cardiac Output: Thermodilution, Pulse Contour, Stroke Volume
secs: Thermodilution (via a pulmonary artery catheter) injects cold saline and measures the downstream temperature change; the area under the curve is inversely proportional to the cardiac output, and errors arise from tricuspid regurgitation, shunts, and the wrong injectate volume or temperature. Pulse-contour analysis derives the stroke volume and cardiac output from the arterial waveform (calibrated or uncalibrated) and gives the stroke-volume variation for fluid responsiveness. Other methods are the Fick principle, esophageal Doppler, bioimpedance, and echo.

Stroke-volume and pulse-pressure variation predict fluid responsiveness only in mechanically ventilated patients in sinus rhythm with adequate tidal volumes. The trend matters more than any single absolute value.
Thermodilution: AUC inversely ∝ CO; SVV/PPV predict fluid responsiveness (ventilated, sinus rhythm)

*Thermodilution measures cardiac output from a downstream temperature curve (errors with tricuspid regurgitation or a shunt); pulse-contour analysis yields the cardiac output and the stroke-volume/pulse-pressure variation for fluid responsiveness — valid only in ventilated, sinus-rhythm patients.*

cards.title: Coagulation Monitors
secs: The standard labs are the PT/INR (the extrinsic pathway, and warfarin), the aPTT (the intrinsic pathway, and heparin), the platelet count, and the fibrinogen; the ACT is used for high-dose heparin (the bypass target is over 400–480 seconds). Viscoelastic testing (TEG or ROTEM) is a whole-blood, point-of-care assessment of clot initiation (R or CT), kinetics, strength (MA or MCF), and fibrinolysis (LY30 or ML), which guides targeted product therapy. Anti-Xa levels monitor the effect of low-molecular-weight heparin, heparin, and the DOACs.
Viscoelastic-guided transfusion reduces blood-product use (in trauma, cardiac, and liver surgery). Heparin is reversed by protamine; confirm reversal with the ACT.
ACT > 400–480 s for CPB heparinization; TEG/ROTEM: R/CT, MA/MCF, LY30/ML

*Use the PT, aPTT, platelets, and fibrinogen, plus the ACT (a bypass target over 400–480 seconds); viscoelastic tests (TEG/ROTEM) show clot initiation, strength, and fibrinolysis to target transfusion — and confirm heparin reversal with the ACT.*

head: Parameter
Reflects
Treat with
tables.note: Guides targeted, component-specific transfusion.

cards.title: Point-of-Care Ultrasound (Lung, IVC, Gastric, Bladder)
secs: On lung ultrasound: A-lines (normal aeration), B-lines (interstitial edema), lung sliding (its absence suggests pneumothorax), and pleural effusion or consolidation. The IVC diameter and its respiratory collapsibility estimate volume status and CVP (a small, collapsing IVC suggests hypovolemia, while a plethoric IVC suggests high filling pressure). Gastric ultrasound assesses aspiration risk (the antral content and area), and bladder ultrasound assesses retention and volume.

Focused cardiac/echo and FAST exams extend the assessment (effusion, function, free fluid). It is operator-dependent, so integrate it with the clinical picture. Focused cardiac ultrasound (subcostal or apical four-chamber) identifies the chambers around the central crux — the septum and AV-valve plane form an X, with the ventricles on the apex side and the atria on the base side — to assess chamber size, function, and effusion at the bedside.
Lung: B-lines = edema, absent sliding = pneumothorax; IVC collapse → hypovolemia

*POCUS rapidly assesses volume and pathology: B-lines (edema) or absent sliding (pneumothorax) on the lung, the IVC size and collapse for volume, the gastric antrum for aspiration risk, and the bladder for retention.*

cards.title: Pacemakers: Design, Nomenclature, Malfunction
secs: The NBG code: position I is the chamber Paced, II the chamber Sensed, III the Response (I = inhibited, T = triggered, D = dual), IV is Rate modulation, and V is multisite (for example, DDD or VVI). A magnet over a pacemaker produces asynchronous pacing (VOO or DOO) at a fixed rate; a magnet over an ICD suspends the tachytherapy (shocks) but does NOT change the pacing. Electrocautery (electromagnetic interference) can inhibit pacing or trigger inappropriate ICD shocks, so use bipolar cautery and short bursts, and place the grounding pad to keep the current away from the device.

For a pacemaker-dependent patient, reprogram to asynchronous mode or apply a magnet; disable the ICD tachytherapy and have external pads and defibrillation ready. Interrogate the device before and after surgery.
NBG: Paced / Sensed / Response (I/T/D) / Rate-mod; magnet → asynchronous pacing, suspends ICD shocks

*Know the NBG code (Paced/Sensed/Response); a magnet sets a pacemaker to asynchronous pacing and suspends ICD shocks (not pacing) — for surgery use bipolar cautery, disable ICD tachytherapy, and have external pads ready.*

head: Position
Meaning
tables.note: Magnet → asynchronous (e.g., VOO/DOO); reprogram or disable ICD for cautery.

cards.title: Ischemic Heart Disease; Perioperative Risk Modification
secs: Myocardial oxygen supply depends on coronary flow (the diastolic time, the perfusion pressure, and the oxygen content), and demand depends on heart rate, contractility, and wall tension (preload and afterload); ischemia occurs when supply is less than demand. Optimize the balance: avoid tachycardia (which shortens diastole and raises demand — the biggest lever), maintain the coronary perfusion pressure, treat anemia and hypoxemia, and balance preload and afterload. Continue beta-blockers and statins, manage antiplatelets and stents with cardiology, and do NOT stop dual antiplatelet therapy prematurely after recent stents.

Use the RCRI and the ACC/AHA stepwise evaluation; a functional capacity of at least 4 METs is reassuring. Avoid elective surgery within the high-risk window after stenting (about 1 month for bare-metal; drug-eluting per current guidance).
Supply (diastolic flow, perfusion pressure, O2 content) vs demand (HR, contractility, wall tension) Avoid tachycardia; continue beta-blockers/statins; don’t stop DAPT early after stents

*Ischemia is supply less than demand — avoid tachycardia (which shortens diastolic coronary filling), keep the perfusion pressure and oxygen content up, continue beta-blockers and statins, and never stop dual antiplatelet therapy prematurely after recent stents.*

cards.title: MI & Acute Coronary Syndrome; STEMI vs Demand Ischemia
secs: A Type 1 MI is plaque rupture and thrombosis (classic acute coronary syndrome) — either a STEMI (ST elevation, a full-thickness occlusion needing emergent reperfusion) or an NSTEMI/unstable angina. A Type 2 MI (demand ischemia) is a supply-demand mismatch without plaque rupture (from tachycardia, anemia, hypotension, or hypertension), which is common perioperatively — treat the underlying imbalance, not primarily a clot. A perioperative MI is often silent (anesthesia and analgesia mask the symptoms), peaks in the first 48–72 hours, and is detected by troponin surveillance and the ECG.

A STEMI activates reperfusion (PCI), while demand ischemia is corrected by fixing the heart rate, blood pressure, anemia, and oxygenation. Antiplatelet and anticoagulant decisions balance bleeding against thrombosis (especially after recent surgery).
Type 1 (plaque rupture; STEMI → reperfuse) vs Type 2 (demand mismatch → fix HR/BP/anemia)

*Distinguish a Type 1 MI (plaque rupture; a STEMI needs emergent reperfusion) from a Type 2 or demand ischemia (a supply-demand mismatch — treat tachycardia, anemia, or hypotension); a perioperative MI is often silent and troponin-detected.*

head: Type
ECG
Troponin
tables.note: Perioperative MI is usually type 2 — fix the imbalance.

cards.title: Valvular Heart Disease & Anesthetic Considerations
secs: Aortic stenosis is a fixed obstruction — maintain SINUS rhythm and preload and avoid tachycardia and hypotension (to preserve coronary perfusion); the maxim is “full, slow, tight” (avoid vasodilation and a spinal sympathectomy). Mitral and aortic regurgitation want “fast, full, forward” — a slightly faster rate and reduced afterload promote forward flow, so avoid bradycardia and a high SVR. Mitral stenosis: avoid tachycardia (the ventricle needs diastolic filling time), maintain preload and SVR, control the atrial-fibrillation rate, and watch for pulmonary hypertension.

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (dynamic LV outflow obstruction) behaves like aortic stenosis — full, slow, and high SVR; avoid inotropes, hypovolemia, and vasodilation. Tailor to severity, and give endocarditis prophylaxis only for high-risk lesions. ACUTE aortic regurgitation with hypotension (endocarditis, dissection): support forward flow with an inotrope/chronotrope (epinephrine) PLUS afterload reduction (nitroglycerin/nitroprusside), and AVOID phenylephrine — raising SVR increases the regurgitant fraction; it is often a surgical emergency.
Stenosis (AS/MS): slow + full + maintain SVR/sinus; Regurgitation (AR/MR): fast + full + low afterload

*Stenotic lesions (aortic and mitral stenosis) want full, slow, sinus rhythm with maintained SVR; regurgitant lesions (aortic and mitral regurgitation) want a faster rate and reduced afterload for forward flow — and HCM behaves like aortic stenosis.*

head: Lesion
HR
Preload
Afterload
tables.note: AR/MR: “fast & forward.” AS/HOCM: avoid tachycardia & hypotension.

cards.title: Endocarditis Prophylaxis
secs: The current guidelines RESTRICT prophylaxis to HIGH-RISK cardiac conditions: prosthetic valves or material, prior infective endocarditis, unrepaired cyanotic congenital disease (or repaired with a residual defect, or within 6 months), and cardiac-transplant valvulopathy. It is given only for high-risk PROCEDURES: dental work involving the gingiva, periapical region, or mucosal perforation, and incision of the respiratory mucosa — NOT routine GI or GU procedures. The standard regimen is amoxicillin 2 g orally (or ampicillin or cefazolin IV) about 30–60 minutes before.
Mitral valve prolapse and most acquired valve disease no longer warrant prophylaxis. Good oral hygiene matters more than episodic prophylaxis.
Prophylaxis only: prosthetic valve, prior IE, certain congenital, transplant valvulopathy → dental/respiratory procedures

*Endocarditis prophylaxis is now limited to high-risk hearts (a prosthetic valve, prior endocarditis, certain congenital lesions, transplant valvulopathy) undergoing dental or respiratory procedures — amoxicillin 2 g before; routine GI/GU is no longer covered.*

cards.title: Rhythm Disorders; AICD; Ablations; Maze
secs: Atrial fibrillation: choose rate versus rhythm control, and anticoagulate by the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score; in Wolff-Parkinson-White, avoid AV nodal blockers (which can accelerate accessory conduction — use procainamide or cardioversion). An AICD senses ventricular tachycardia or fibrillation and delivers a shock or anti-tachycardia pacing; a magnet suspends the shocks (NOT the pacing), so disable the tachytherapy for surgery with electromagnetic interference and have external defibrillation ready. Catheter ablation (pulmonary-vein isolation, accessory pathways) and the surgical Maze procedure create conduction barriers to interrupt re-entry.
Adenosine treats SVT (a transient AV block), and synchronized cardioversion treats unstable tachyarrhythmias. Bradyarrhythmias and heart block may need pacing.

*Know atrial-fibrillation management (rate or rhythm control, CHA₂DS₂-VASc, avoiding AV blockers in Wolff-Parkinson-White); a magnet suspends AICD shocks but not pacing — disable the tachytherapy for surgery, and ablation or Maze interrupts re-entry circuits.*

head: Factor
Points
tables.note: Score ≥ 2 (men) → anticoagulate.

cards.title: Heart Failure & Cardiomyopathy
secs: Heart failure with reduced EF (systolic or dilated): a reduced ejection fraction — support contractility and preload and reduce afterload; continue guideline therapy (beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors/ARBs/ARNI, mineralocorticoid antagonists, SGLT2 inhibitors), but hold the ACE inhibitor or ARB on the morning. Heart failure with preserved EF (diastolic): a stiff ventricle — maintain sinus rhythm, adequate preload, and rate control, and avoid tachycardia. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (dynamic LV outflow obstruction): keep full, slow, and high SVR, and AVOID inotropes, hypovolemia, vasodilation, and tachycardia.
Decompensated heart failure is a major perioperative risk, so optimize it before elective surgery. Restrictive cardiomyopathy resembles constriction (impaired filling).

*Heart failure with reduced EF (reduce afterload, support contractility) versus preserved EF (sinus rhythm, avoid tachycardia); HCM needs full, slow, and high SVR with no inotropes or vasodilation — and decompensated failure is a top perioperative risk.*

head: Feature
HFrEF
HFpEF
tables.note: HOCM behaves like AS: full, slow, high SVR; avoid inotropes.

cards.title: Pulmonary Hypertension & Cardiogenic Shock
secs: Pulmonary hypertension (a mean PA pressure of 20 mmHg or more): the right ventricle is afterload-sensitive, so avoid the factors that raise pulmonary vascular resistance (hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis, hypothermia, pain, and high airway pressure). Support the right ventricle: maintain preload (without overload), maintain coronary perfusion (norepinephrine or vasopressin for systemic pressure), and use pulmonary vasodilators (inhaled NO or prostacyclin, milrinone). Cardiogenic shock is low output with congestion — use inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone, epinephrine), mechanical support (an intra-aortic balloon pump, ECMO, or a ventricular assist device), and treat the cause (the MI).
An RV ischemia → failure → hypotension spiral can be fatal, so keep the MAP above the PA pressure. Avoid systemic hypotension in pulmonary hypertension (the RV coronary perfusion depends on it).
PH = mean PAP ≥ 20; avoid ↑ PVR (hypoxia/hypercarbia/acidosis); inhaled NO/prostacyclin lower PVR

*In pulmonary hypertension (mean PA pressure 20 or more) protect the right ventricle — avoid hypoxia, hypercarbia, and acidosis (all raise pulmonary vascular resistance), maintain systemic pressure (norepinephrine or vasopressin), and use inhaled NO, prostacyclin, or milrinone.*

head: Group
Cause
tables.note: Avoid ↑ PVR: hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis, high airway pressure.

cards.title: Cardiac Transplantation
secs: The transplanted heart is DENERVATED, giving a resting tachycardia (about 90–100) and no response to atropine or to direct vagal or sympathetic maneuvers; it relies on circulating catecholamines and the Frank-Starling mechanism (it is preload-dependent). Use DIRECT-acting agents (epinephrine, or isoproterenol for rate); the indirect agents (ephedrine, atropine) are ineffective. Recipients are immunosuppressed (an infection risk, needing strict asepsis) with accelerated coronary allograft vasculopathy and silent ischemia (no angina, because of the denervation).
The ECG may show two P waves (the donor and the recipient atrial remnant). Maintain preload and avoid sudden vasodilation or hypovolemia.
Denervated heart: resting HR ~90–100, no atropine response; use direct agents (epi/isoproterenol)

*The transplanted heart is denervated (a resting heart rate of about 90–100, no atropine response, and preload-dependent) — use direct agents like epinephrine or isoproterenol; recipients have silent ischemia from allograft vasculopathy.*

head: Stimulus
Response
tables.note: Treat bradycardia with isoproterenol or pacing; two P waves may appear.

cards.title: Cardiac Tamponade & Constrictive Pericarditis
secs: Tamponade is pericardial fluid under pressure, causing impaired filling, equalization of the diastolic pressures, pulsus paradoxus, and Beck’s triad (hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds) with tachycardia. Maintain “full, fast, forward” (preload, rate, contractility, and SVR) and AVOID bradycardia and myocardial depression; the definitive treatment is pericardiocentesis or drainage — consider draining the patient awake before induction in severe cases (since induction can cause collapse). Constrictive pericarditis is a rigid pericardium that limits diastolic filling (with Kussmaul’s sign); pericardiectomy is the treatment.
Positive-pressure ventilation worsens the tamponade physiology (it further reduces venous return). Keep the patient breathing spontaneously until the pericardium is decompressed if possible.
Tamponade: full/fast/forward + maintain SVR; drain before induction in severe cases

*Tamponade (Beck’s triad, pulsus paradoxus) needs full, fast, forward hemodynamics and prompt drainage — induction can cause collapse, so consider draining awake; positive-pressure ventilation worsens it.*

head: Feature
Detail
rows: Full, fast, tight — avoid brady & vasodilation
tables.note: Definitive treatment is pericardiocentesis/drainage.

cards.title: Cardiopulmonary Bypass; ACT; Protamine
secs: Cardiopulmonary bypass diverts blood through an oxygenator and pump; it needs full heparinization (300–400 units/kg) to an ACT over 400–480 seconds before cannulation, reversed afterward with protamine (about 1 mg per 100 units of heparin). The on-bypass goals are an adequate flow and MAP, anticoagulation, temperature management, and hemodilution; monitor for awareness (and give amnestics). Protamine reactions: hypotension (from rapid infusion and histamine), true anaphylaxis, and a catastrophic pulmonary vasoconstriction with RV failure (a higher risk with prior protamine, NPH insulin, or a fish allergy).

After bypass: coagulopathy (platelet dysfunction, residual heparin, hemodilution) and vasoplegia. Consider pulsatile versus non-pulsatile flow and cerebral protection during cooling. Heparin resistance = a failure to reach the target ACT despite full dosing; the usual cause is antithrombin deficiency, classically from prior or ongoing heparin exposure depleting antithrombin — treat with antithrombin concentrate or FFP.
Heparin 300–400 u/kg → ACT > 400–480 s; protamine ~1 mg / 100 u heparin

*Cardiopulmonary bypass needs full heparinization (an ACT over 400–480 seconds) reversed with protamine (about 1 mg per 100 units); protamine can cause hypotension, anaphylaxis, or a catastrophic pulmonary vasoconstriction — give it slowly.*

head: Item
Detail
rows: Prior protamine, NPH insulin, fish allergy
tables.note: Give slowly; have vasopressors ready.

cards.title: Deep Hypothermic Circulatory Arrest
secs: Profound hypothermia (about 18°C) reduces the CMRO₂ to allow a bloodless field with the circulation stopped (for aortic arch surgery). Hypothermia is the principal protection (the CMRO₂ falls about 7% per °C), and the safe arrest time extends as the temperature falls (roughly 30–40 minutes at 18°C). The adjuncts are antegrade or retrograde cerebral perfusion, topical head cooling, pH-stat cooling, and (controversially) steroids and barbiturates.
The risks are neurologic injury, coagulopathy (from the hypothermia), and a prolonged rewarming. Cool and rewarm slowly to avoid gradients and gas emboli.
DHCA ~18°C; CMRO2 ↓ ~7% per °C; safe arrest ~30–40 min at 18°C

*Deep hypothermic circulatory arrest (about 18°C) uses deep hypothermia (the CMRO₂ falls about 7% per °C) for a bloodless, arrested field in arch surgery (about 30–40 minutes safe at 18°C), often with cerebral-perfusion adjuncts — risking neurologic injury and coagulopathy.*

cards.title: Aminocaproic & Tranexamic Acid
secs: The antifibrinolytics (lysine analogues) block plasminogen activation to stabilize clot, reducing bleeding and transfusion in cardiac, trauma, orthopedic, and obstetric hemorrhage. Tranexamic acid is about 10 times more potent than aminocaproic acid; in trauma (the CRASH-2 trial) and postpartum hemorrhage (the WOMAN trial) it reduces mortality when given early (within 3 hours). Aprotinin (a serine-protease inhibitor) was withdrawn and then partially reintroduced (over thrombosis and renal concerns).
The risks are thrombosis and (with high-dose tranexamic acid) seizures. Use caution in active thromboembolic disease and in some cases of DIC.
TXA/aminocaproic = antifibrinolytics; early TXA (< 3 h) reduces mortality in trauma/PPH

*Tranexamic and aminocaproic acid are antifibrinolytics that stabilize clot and cut transfusion; early tranexamic acid (within 3 hours) lowers mortality in trauma and postpartum hemorrhage — watch for thrombosis and, at high doses, seizures.*

cards.title: OPCAB/MIDCAB; Transcatheter Valve (TAVR)
secs: Off-pump CABG (OPCAB) avoids cardiopulmonary bypass but needs the patient to tolerate heart positioning and displacement (causing transient ischemia and hypotension), and they are still heparinized. MIDCAB is minimally invasive LIMA-to-LAD grafting. TAVR is a catheter-based valve for high- or intermediate-risk aortic stenosis; its risks include conduction block (needing pacing), paravalvular leak, stroke, and vascular injury, and it is done under sedation/MAC or general anesthesia with TEE.
OPCAB needs careful coordination during the anastomoses (bradycardia and positioning). Rapid ventricular pacing is used during balloon valvuloplasty and deployment.

*OPCAB and MIDCAB avoid bypass but need tolerance of heart positioning; TAVR is a catheter-based aortic valve replacement for higher-risk stenosis (watch for conduction block needing pacing, paravalvular leak, and stroke) under sedation or general anesthesia.*

cards.title: Intra-aortic Balloon Pump
secs: The intra-aortic balloon pump sits in the descending aorta; it inflates in DIASTOLE (augmenting coronary perfusion) and deflates in early SYSTOLE (reducing afterload), thereby improving myocardial oxygen supply and reducing demand. It is timed to the ECG or arterial waveform (inflating at the dicrotic notch) and is used in cardiogenic shock, refractory ischemia, and as a bridge to surgery or recovery. It is contraindicated in significant aortic regurgitation and in aortic dissection.
The complications are limb ischemia, dissection, thrombocytopenia, and balloon rupture. Mistiming reduces the benefit or causes harm (early or late inflation or deflation).
IABP: inflate in diastole (↑ coronary perfusion), deflate in systole (↓ afterload)

*The intra-aortic balloon pump inflates in diastole (boosting coronary perfusion) and deflates in systole (cutting afterload) for cardiogenic shock or ischemia; it is contraindicated in aortic regurgitation and dissection.*

head: Phase
Timing
Effect
tables.note: Contraindicated in aortic regurgitation & aortic dissection.

cards.title: ECMO
secs: Venoarterial (VA) ECMO drains venous blood and returns oxygenated blood to the ARTERIAL system, supporting BOTH the heart and the lungs (in cardiogenic shock or arrest). Venovenous (VV) ECMO drains and returns to the VENOUS system, supporting the LUNGS only (in severe respiratory failure or ARDS), so the native heart must function. Both require anticoagulation; monitor for bleeding, thrombosis, limb or cerebral ischemia, and (with peripheral VA) differential or “north-south” hypoxia (the Harlequin syndrome).
VA ECMO provides hemodynamic support, while VV ECMO provides only oxygenation and CO₂ removal. Weaning and complication management are central.
VA ECMO = heart + lung support (arterial return); VV ECMO = lung support only (venous return)

*VA ECMO returns blood arterially to support the heart and lungs (in cardiogenic shock), while VV ECMO is venous-to-venous lung support only (needing a working heart) — both require anticoagulation and vigilance for bleeding and thrombosis.*

cards.title: Ventricular Assist Devices
secs: A continuous-flow LVAD unloads the left ventricle as a bridge to transplant or recovery, or as destination therapy; it produces a low or absent pulse pressure (so a noninvasive blood pressure is unreliable — use a Doppler or arterial line). It is preload-dependent and afterload-sensitive, so avoid hypovolemia and right-ventricular failure (the right ventricle must fill the LVAD). Anticoagulation is required, and the complications include bleeding (from an acquired von Willebrand syndrome), thrombosis, infection, RV failure, and suction events.
Maintain the volume status and RV function and avoid excessive afterload. Many patients are on chronic anticoagulation and antiplatelets.
Continuous-flow LVAD → low pulse pressure (Doppler/art line for BP); preload-dependent, avoid RV failure

*Continuous-flow LVADs unload the left ventricle with little pulse pressure (use a Doppler or arterial line for blood pressure); they are preload-dependent and need a functioning right ventricle — and anticoagulation is required (acquired von Willebrand syndrome causes bleeding).*

cards.title: Pulmonary Embolism (Blood, Air, Fat, Amniotic)
secs: An acute pulmonary embolism causes a sudden EtCO₂ drop, hypoxemia, hypotension, and an increased RV afterload and strain; treat it with anticoagulation, thrombolysis, or embolectomy, plus RV support. An air embolism (venous air embolism) causes a sudden EtCO₂ drop and a mill-wheel murmur — flood the field, give 100% oxygen, stop nitrous oxide, and aspirate. A fat embolism (after a long-bone fracture) gives petechiae, hypoxemia, and confusion at 24–72 hours; an amniotic fluid embolism (in labor or delivery) gives sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypoxemia, and DIC — managed with supportive resuscitation.
The common thread is an acute rise in pulmonary vascular resistance leading to RV failure. Maintain RV perfusion (systemic pressure) and oxygenation, and treat the cause.
All emboli → sudden ↓ EtCO2 + RV strain; AFE adds DIC; fat = petechiae + confusion

*Emboli (thrombus, air, fat, amniotic) cause a sudden EtCO₂ drop and RV strain; air → aspirate and stop nitrous oxide, fat → petechiae and confusion at 24–72 hours, amniotic → collapse and DIC — support the right ventricle and oxygenation.*

cards.title: Hypertension: Etiology & Anesthetic Risk
secs: Hypertension is mostly essential; the secondary causes include renal disease, renovascular disease, pheochromocytoma, hyperaldosteronism, Cushing’s disease, OSA, and coarctation. Chronic hypertension right-shifts the autoregulation (cerebral and renal), so the patient tolerates higher pressures but risks ischemia at “normal” pressures, with exaggerated intraoperative blood-pressure lability. Continue most antihypertensives (holding the ACE inhibitor or ARB on the morning) and avoid severe perioperative hypertension or hypotension.

Severe uncontrolled hypertension (for example, above 180/110) may warrant delaying elective surgery. A smooth induction and emergence blunt the swings, and treat the cause of intraoperative hypertension.

*Chronic hypertension right-shifts autoregulation (blood-pressure lability, ischemia at “normal” pressures); continue antihypertensives (holding the ACE inhibitor or ARB on the morning), avoid extremes, and consider delaying elective surgery if it is severely uncontrolled.*

head: Drug
Class
tables.note: Lower BP ~10–20% initially; control rate first in aortic dissection.

cards.title: Shock States Management
secs: The categories are hypovolemic (hemorrhage — give fluids and blood), cardiogenic (pump failure — inotropes and support), obstructive (tamponade, tension pneumothorax, pulmonary embolism — relieve the obstruction), and distributive (sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic — vasopressors plus the cause). Distinguish them by the preload, the pump, and the afterload (using the CVP, cardiac output, SVR, SvO₂, and echo or POCUS). The goal is to restore tissue perfusion and oxygen delivery — fluids, vasopressors or inotropes per the type, and treatment of the underlying cause.
In septic shock, norepinephrine is first-line with vasopressin added, plus source control and antibiotics. Lactate and the ScvO₂ guide the adequacy of resuscitation.
Shock types: hypovolemic / cardiogenic / obstructive / distributive — match therapy to mechanism

*Classify shock as hypovolemic, cardiogenic, obstructive, or distributive (by the preload, pump, and afterload) and treat the mechanism — fluids or blood, inotropes, relieving obstruction, or vasopressors; norepinephrine is first-line in sepsis.*

head: Type
CO
SVR
Filling
tables.note: SvO₂ low in all except distributive (high — impaired extraction).

cards.title: Carotid Endarterectomy; Cerebral Perfusion Monitoring
secs: Carotid endarterectomy risks cerebral ischemia during the carotid cross-clamping (which is collateral-dependent) and hyperperfusion afterward, so tight blood-pressure control is essential (avoid hypotension during the clamp). Neuromonitoring options are an awake patient (the gold standard — a neurologic exam), the EEG, SSEPs, the stump pressure, transcranial Doppler, and cerebral oximetry — used to decide on placing a shunt. Maintain the blood pressure at or slightly above baseline during clamping, and manage the postoperative hypertension and baroreceptor dysfunction (carotid sinus manipulation causes lability).

The complications are stroke, hyperperfusion syndrome, MI, neck hematoma (with airway compromise), and nerve injury. Aim for a smooth emergence to allow a neurologic exam. The EEG marker of inadequate perfusion after cross-clamping is IPSILATERAL attenuation of the fast (beta) activity with slowing → which prompts shunting; a GENERALIZED slowing instead suggests a global cause (anesthetic depth, hypotension).

*Carotid endarterectomy needs tight blood-pressure control (keep the pressure up during carotid clamping) and ischemia monitoring (an awake exam is the gold standard, otherwise EEG, SSEP, or stump pressure) to decide on shunting; watch for hyperperfusion, neck hematoma, and MI.*

cards.title: Aortic Aneurysm (AAA/TAAA, incl. Endovascular)
secs: Open repair: aortic cross-clamping causes a severe increase in afterload, proximal hypertension, and distal ischemia; unclamping causes hypotension and reperfusion (a washout of acid and vasodilators) — coordinate with the surgeon and load volume before release. Thoracoabdominal repair risks spinal cord ischemia (the anterior spinal artery, or the artery of Adamkiewicz) leading to paraplegia; protect with CSF drainage, distal perfusion, MAP support, and mild hypothermia. Endovascular repair (EVAR or TEVAR) carries less physiologic stress but risks an endoleak, contrast nephropathy, and access injury, and uses rapid pacing or controlled hypotension for deployment.

There is potential for massive hemorrhage — use large-bore access, have blood available, and use cell salvage. Provide renal protection during suprarenal clamping. Motor evoked potentials are the most sensitive monitor for the at-risk anterior cord during thoracoabdominal repair (a posterior-column SSEP can miss anterior spinal artery ischemia).
Cross-clamp ↑ afterload; unclamp → hypotension; TAAA → spinal cord ischemia (CSF drain protects)

*Aortic cross-clamping spikes afterload and unclamping drops the pressure (load volume first); thoracoabdominal repair risks spinal cord ischemia and paraplegia — protect with CSF drainage, distal perfusion, and MAP support, and EVAR adds endoleak and contrast risks.*

cards.title: Phases of Ventricular Diastole
secs: Ventricular diastole has four phases in order (see Wiggers diagram): (1) ISOVOLUMETRIC RELAXATION — from aortic valve closure until the mitral valve opens, no volume change; (2) EARLY RAPID FILLING — negative intraventricular pressure from active relaxation draws blood in (the E wave); (3) DIASTASIS — slow passive equilibration filling; (4) ATRIAL SYSTOLE — the atrial 'kick' completing filling (the A wave).

Atrial systole contributes ~20-30% of LV end-diastolic volume and becomes critical when the ventricle is stiff/non-compliant (LVH, aortic stenosis, HCM, diastolic dysfunction). Loss of the atrial kick — as in atrial fibrillation or a junctional rhythm — can precipitate decompensation in these patients. Tachycardia shortens diastole (and coronary filling time) disproportionately, worsening ischemia in AS/CAD.
Isovolumetric relaxation -> rapid filling -> diastasis -> atrial kick; the atrial kick matters most in stiff ventricles (AS, LVH, HCM).

noteimg.cap: Wiggers diagram — pressures, volume, ECG, and heart sounds across the cardiac cycle.

cards.title: CVP Waveform Abnormalities & the Cannon A Wave
secs: The normal CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP) tracing has three positive waves (a, c, v) and two descents (x, y): the a WAVE is atrial contraction, the c wave is tricuspid bulging during early RV systole, the x descent is atrial relaxation, the v wave is atrial filling against a closed tricuspid valve, and the y descent is early diastolic ventricular filling (see figures). A CANNON A WAVE is a giant a wave produced when the RIGHT ATRIUM CONTRACTS AGAINST A CLOSED TRICUSPID VALVE, most classically with AV DISSOCIATION (e.g., complete heart block, junctional/ventricular rhythms, ventricular pacing).

CVP waveform patterns are exam favorites: LARGE/regular a waves occur with tricuspid stenosis and pulmonary hypertension (any obstruction to atrial emptying); LOSS of the a wave occurs in ATRIAL FIBRILLATION; a large fused c-v (regurgitant) wave with LOSS of the x descent occurs in TRICUSPID REGURGITATION; and prominent, steep x AND y descents (M/W pattern) occur in CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS, whereas cardiac tamponade shows a dominant x descent with a blunted y descent. Intermittent cannon a waves suggest AV dissociation.
Cannon a wave = atrium contracting against a closed tricuspid valve (AV dissociation); atrial fib loses the a wave; TR gives a giant fused c-v wave.

noteimg.cap: Abnormal CVP waveforms by lesion (TR, TS, constriction, AFib, tamponade, complete AV block/cannon a wave)
Normal CVP tracing: a, c, v waves and x, y descents with mechanistic labels

cards.title: Blunting the SVR Drop at Initiation of Cardiopulmonary Bypass
secs: At the onset of CARDIOPULMONARY BYPASS, priming the circuit with crystalloid causes acute HEMODILUTION that lowers blood viscosity and plasma ONCOTIC pressure, producing an abrupt fall in SYSTEMIC VASCULAR RESISTANCE and mean arterial pressure. Adding a COLLOID such as 5% ALBUMIN to the pump prime raises oncotic pressure and viscosity, blunting this decrease in SVR. A MANNITOL bolus given on bypass serves a related role: it is an osmotic agent that supports intravascular volume and promotes DIURESIS and free-radical scavenging, and is commonly added for renal protection and to counter hemodilution.

The initial hypotension on bypass reflects diluted viscosity and oncotic pressure plus residual anesthetic vasodilation, not necessarily hypovolemia. Understanding the oncotic mechanism guides prime composition; colloid priming and mannitol are practical tools, whereas the definitive treatment of persistent low SVR on bypass is a VASOPRESSOR such as PHENYLEPHRINE or NOREPINEPHRINE.
MAP on bypass = pump flow (typically 2.2-2.5 L/min/m^2) x SVR. Target MAP on CPB is generally 50-80 mmHg. 5% albumin is iso-oncotic; 25% albumin is hyperoncotic.

Colloid such as 5% albumin in the pump prime (and a mannitol bolus on bypass) raises oncotic pressure and viscosity to blunt the sharp SVR fall that follows hemodilution at the start of cardiopulmonary bypass.

cards.title: ACE Inhibitors and ARBs in Heart Failure
secs: ACE INHIBITORS and ANGIOTENSIN RECEPTOR BLOCKERS (ARBs) are cornerstone, mortality-reducing therapy in HFrEF because they interrupt the RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE SYSTEM. Angiotensin II, acting on the AT1 receptor, drives vasoconstriction, aldosterone release, sodium/water retention, and - critically - pathologic myocardial and vascular REMODELING (hypertrophy and fibrosis). ACE inhibitors block conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II (and reduce bradykinin breakdown, hence the cough/angioedema); ARBs block the AT1 receptor directly (fewer bradykinin effects). By reducing afterload, preload, and remodeling, they improve ejection fraction trajectory, symptoms, and SURVIVAL.

Perioperatively these drugs blunt the compensatory RAAS response to anesthesia-induced vasodilation and hypovolemia, so patients can develop refractory INTRAOPERATIVE HYPOTENSION that may respond poorly to ephedrine/phenylephrine and require VASOPRESSIN. Many clinicians therefore hold ACE-I/ARBs on the morning of surgery, especially for major cases with large fluid shifts, though this is individualized. Watch for HYPERKALEMIA and worsening renal function, particularly with concurrent potassium-sparing diuretics or NSAIDs. ACE-I-induced ANGIOEDEMA is a life-threatening airway emergency.
MAP = CO x SVR - RAAS blockade lowers SVR (afterload). Refractory hypotension on ACE-I/ARB classically responds to VASOPRESSIN 1-2 units. Consider holding the dose ~10-24 h preop for major surgery.
ACE-I/ARBs help failing hearts by blocking AT1-mediated REMODELING - but they blunt RAAS reflexes, so expect refractory intraop hypotension that responds to VASOPRESSIN.

cards.title: SA Node Pacemaker Action Potential: Calcium-Dependent Upstroke
secs: The SA (sinoatrial) node has NO stable resting potential. During phase 4 the cells slowly and spontaneously depolarize, driven largely by the 'funny' current (I<sub>f</sub>, an inward Na<sup>+</sup> current) plus T-type Ca<sup>2+</sup> influx; because nodal cells are more permeable to sodium than potassium, they sit in a constant state of slow depolarization. Once the THRESHOLD (~-40 mV) is reached, the phase 0 UPSTROKE is produced by CALCIUM influx through L-type (long-lasting) Ca<sup>2+</sup> channels — NOT by fast sodium channels, which are inactivated at these voltages. Phase 3 repolarization is due to K<sup>+</sup> efflux (I<sub>Ks</sub>, I<sub>Kr</sub> delayed rectifiers). There is no phase 1 or 2 (see figure: phase 0 = Ca<sup>2+</sup> in, phase 3 = K<sup>+</sup> out, phase 4 = funny Na<sup>+</sup> current).

Heart rate is set by the slope of phase 4, the threshold potential, and the maximum diastolic potential. SYMPATHETIC stimulation (β1, cAMP) increases I<sub>f</sub> and I<sub>Ca</sub>, steepening the phase 4 slope and speeding the rate; VAGAL/parasympathetic tone (ACh on M2) opens GIRK potassium channels, hyperpolarizing the cell and flattening the slope to slow the rate. Because the upstroke is calcium-dependent, non-dihydropyridine CALCIUM CHANNEL BLOCKERS (verapamil, diltiazem) and beta-blockers slow SA node firing and AV conduction — the basis for rate control in tachyarrhythmias. This contrasts with fast, Na-channel-driven phase 0 upstrokes in atrial/ventricular working myocytes and Purkinje fibers.
Maximum diastolic potential ~-60 mV; threshold ~-40 mV; intrinsic SA rate ~60-100/min (dominant pacemaker).
SA node phase 0 upstroke is driven by CALCIUM influx (L-type channels), not fast sodium; phase 4 spontaneous depolarization uses the funny Na<sup>+</sup> current.

noteimg.cap: Pacemaker action potential: phase 0 Ca2+ influx, phase 3 K+ efflux, phase 4 funny Na+ current

cards.title: Bronchospasm
secs: Bronchospasm shows as intraoperative wheeze, a rising peak airway pressure, an upsloping “shark-fin” capnogram, and difficulty ventilating. The triggers are light anesthesia plus airway instrumentation (the most common), asthma or COPD, aspiration, anaphylaxis, an endotracheal tube touching the carina, and histamine-releasing drugs. Treat by deepening anesthesia (propofol and the volatile bronchodilators), giving 100% oxygen and an inhaled beta-2 agonist through the circuit, and epinephrine if severe — and exclude mechanical causes (a kink, a mainstem intubation, a mucus plug).

Ketamine and the volatiles are bronchodilators; avoid desflurane for induction in a reactive airway, since it is an irritant. Confirm the endotracheal tube’s position and patency first, since mechanical problems can mimic bronchospasm.
↑ peak pressure + shark-fin capnogram; treat: deepen + beta-2 agonist + epinephrine if severe

*Bronchospasm (wheeze, high peak pressures, a shark-fin capnogram) is usually light anesthesia plus instrumentation; deepen anesthesia, give a beta-2 agonist (epinephrine if severe), and exclude mechanical causes.*

cards.title: Laryngospasm
secs: Laryngospasm is a reflex closure of the glottis (from superior laryngeal nerve stimulation), often during light anesthesia or emergence, provoked by secretions or airway manipulation — especially in children. It shows as stridor (if partial) or silent complete obstruction, paradoxical chest movement, and desaturation, and it can cause negative-pressure pulmonary edema. Treat it by removing the stimulus, applying 100% oxygen with continuous positive pressure and a jaw thrust (Larson’s maneuver), and deepening with propofol; give succinylcholine (IV or IM) if it is refractory.
Prevent it by extubating either deep or fully awake (not in stage II) and by suctioning secretions. Be ready to reintubate and watch for post-obstructive pulmonary edema.
Treat: CPAP/jaw thrust (Larson’s notch) → propofol → succinylcholine if refractory

*Laryngospasm (light anesthesia or secretions, especially in children) is treated with CPAP plus a jaw thrust (Larson’s maneuver), deepening with propofol, and succinylcholine if refractory — extubate deep or fully awake.*

cards.title: Negative-Pressure (Post-Obstructive) Pulmonary Edema
secs: Negative-pressure pulmonary edema follows forceful inspiration against an obstructed airway (laryngospasm, or biting the endotracheal tube), which generates a very negative intrathoracic pressure that pulls fluid into the alveoli. It appears soon after the obstruction is relieved, with hypoxemia, pink frothy secretions, and bilateral infiltrates. Young, muscular patients generate the most negative pressure and are therefore at highest risk.
Management is supportive: oxygen, CPAP or PEEP, and sometimes diuresis; it usually resolves within 12–24 hours. Prevent it by avoiding or quickly relieving obstruction and by using a bite block.
Obstructed forceful inspiration → very negative intrathoracic pressure → alveolar fluid

*Negative-pressure pulmonary edema follows forceful inspiration against an obstructed airway (laryngospasm or tube-biting), especially in muscular patients; treat supportively with oxygen and CPAP — it resolves in 12–24 hours.*

cards.title: Lung Volumes & Capacities; Time Constants
secs: Capacities are sums of volumes: functional residual capacity (FRC) = expiratory reserve volume + residual volume; vital capacity = inspiratory reserve + tidal volume + expiratory reserve; total lung capacity = vital capacity + residual volume. The FRC (about 2.3 L) is the lung’s oxygen reservoir, and it FALLS with general anesthesia, the supine position, obesity, and pregnancy — which is why those patients desaturate faster. The closing capacity (the volume at which small airways close) rises with age and smoking; once it exceeds the FRC, airways close during normal tidal breathing, causing shunt and hypoxemia.
Residual volume and FRC cannot be measured by spirometry — they need body plethysmography or helium dilution. Preoxygenation builds an FRC oxygen reservoir before apnea.
FRC = ERV + RV; TLC = VC + RV; FRC falls under GA/supine/obesity/pregnancy Hypoxemia when closing capacity > FRC

*The FRC (the oxygen reservoir) falls under anesthesia, supine, obesity, and pregnancy, speeding desaturation; when the closing capacity exceeds the FRC, airways close and cause hypoxemia — and residual volume and FRC need plethysmography, not spirometry.*

head: Volume
Approx mL
tables.note: RV & FRC cannot be measured by spirometry (need plethysmography).

cards.title: Compliance; Laplace Law; Surfactant
secs: Lung compliance is the change in volume per change in pressure (ΔV/ΔP); the chest wall and the lung have opposing recoil forces that balance at the FRC. The Laplace law for a sphere or alveolus is P = 2T/r, so smaller alveoli have a higher collapsing pressure and would empty into larger ones without surfactant. Surfactant (from type II pneumocytes) lowers surface tension more in the small alveoli, stabilizing them, preventing atelectasis, and raising compliance; its deficiency causes neonatal respiratory distress syndrome and ARDS.

Static compliance reflects elastic recoil, while dynamic compliance also includes airway resistance. Compliance falls with edema, fibrosis, atelectasis, pneumothorax, obesity, and raised abdominal pressure.
Compliance = ΔV/ΔP; Laplace P = 2T/r; surfactant lowers tension in small alveoli
*Compliance is ΔV/ΔP; by the Laplace law (P = 2T/r) small alveoli tend to collapse, prevented by surfactant (from type II cells) — whose deficiency causes respiratory distress syndrome and ARDS.*

cards.title: Flow-Volume Loops & Hysteresis
secs: The shape of the flow-volume loop localizes obstruction: obstructive disease gives a “scooped” expiratory limb; a FIXED obstruction (tracheal stenosis) flattens BOTH limbs; a variable EXTRAthoracic lesion flattens the inspiratory limb; and a variable INTRAthoracic lesion flattens the expiratory limb. Restrictive disease gives a small but normally shaped loop (reduced volumes). Hysteresis is the difference between the inflation and deflation pressure-volume curves (the deflation limb holds more volume), due to surface tension, surfactant, and recruitment.

Loops are useful for diagnosing airway lesions and assessing severity before surgery. Mechanism: a variable EXTRAthoracic lesion collapses on inspiration (negative intraluminal pressure) → inspiratory flattening; a variable INTRAthoracic lesion collapses on expiration → expiratory flattening.
Variable extrathoracic = inspiratory flattening; intrathoracic = expiratory; fixed = both

*Flow-volume loops localize airway lesions (a variable extrathoracic lesion flattens inspiration, an intrathoracic one flattens expiration, and a fixed lesion flattens both limbs); hysteresis reflects surfactant and surface-tension differences between inflation and deflation.*

cards.title: Work of Breathing
secs: The work of breathing overcomes an ELASTIC load (compliance) plus a RESISTIVE load (airflow), and is normally a small fraction of total oxygen consumption. The body picks a respiratory rate that minimizes total work: a restrictive (stiff) lung favors rapid shallow breathing, while obstruction favors slow deep breathing. Increased work (from bronchospasm, edema, obesity, or ARDS) raises the oxygen cost of breathing and can fatigue the respiratory muscles into failure.
Mechanical ventilation and pressure support offload the work; auto-PEEP increases the inspiratory work. Optimizing analgesia, bronchodilation, and positioning reduces the work.
WOB = elastic + resistive work; restrictive → rapid shallow; obstructive → slow deep

*The work of breathing overcomes elastic and resistive loads; patients minimize it (restrictive disease → rapid and shallow, obstructive → slow and deep), and rising work causes muscle fatigue and respiratory failure.*

cards.title: V/Q Matching; West Zones; Hypoxic Pulmonary Vasoconstriction
