secs: The ideal ventilation-perfusion ratio is about 0.8; a high V/Q means dead space (ventilation without perfusion), and a low V/Q means shunt (perfusion without ventilation, which supplemental oxygen does not correct). The West zones (upright) are: Zone 1 at the apex (alveolar pressure > arterial > venous, so alveolar dead space), Zone 2 (arterial > alveolar > venous), and Zone 3 at the base (arterial > venous > alveolar, the best perfusion); gravity makes the base both better perfused and better ventilated. Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction diverts blood away from poorly oxygenated alveoli to improve matching, and it is inhibited by volatiles, vasodilators, and hypocapnia.
A true shunt (V/Q = 0) does not correct with 100% oxygen, whereas a low-but-nonzero V/Q mismatch does. One-lung ventilation relies on hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction in the non-ventilated lung.
Normal V/Q ≈ 0.8; dead space (high V/Q) vs shunt (low V/Q); HPV improves matching Zone 1 PA>Pa>Pv (apex); Zone 3 Pa>Pv>PA (base)

*A normal V/Q is about 0.8 (high = dead space, low = shunt, and a true shunt resists oxygen); the West zones run from apex (1) to base (3); hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction diverts flow from hypoxic lung and is blunted by volatiles and vasodilators.*

head: Zone
Pressures
Location
tables.note: PA = alveolar, Pa = arterial, Pv = venous. Zone 1 enlarges with ↓CO or ↑PEEP.

cards.title: Alveolar Gas Equation
secs: The alveolar gas equation is PAO₂ = FiO₂ × (atmospheric pressure − water vapor pressure) − PaCO₂ / R. At sea level on room air: PAO₂ ≈ 0.21 × (760 − 47) − 40/0.8 ≈ 150 − 50 = 100 mmHg. The respiratory quotient R is about 0.8, and the water vapor pressure is 47 mmHg at 37°C.

The A–a gradient is PAO₂ − PaO₂ (normally about 5–15 mmHg, rising with age) and is widened by shunt, V/Q mismatch, and diffusion limitation. Hypoventilation (a high PaCO₂) lowers the PAO₂ with a NORMAL A–a gradient, whereas shunt and mismatch widen it.
PAO2 = FiO2 × (Patm − 47) − PaCO2 / 0.8 A–a gradient = PAO2 − PaO2 (normal ~5–15, ↑ with age/shunt/mismatch)

*PAO₂ = FiO₂ × (atmospheric pressure − 47) − PaCO₂/0.8 (about 100 on room air at sea level); the A–a gradient (normally 5–15) stays normal in pure hypoventilation but widens with shunt, V/Q mismatch, or diffusion limitation.*

cards.title: Apneic Oxygenation; Diffusion Hypoxia
secs: In apneic oxygenation, with a patent airway and a high-FiO₂ reservoir, oxygen keeps being absorbed (about 250 mL/min) while CO₂ accumulates only slowly — so oxygenation can be maintained for minutes without ventilation. Preoxygenation (denitrogenation) fills the FRC with oxygen to extend the safe apnea time, and high-flow nasal oxygen (apneic oxygenation, or THRIVE) prolongs it further. Diffusion hypoxia occurs at the end of a nitrous-oxide anesthetic, when large volumes of nitrous oxide leave the blood into the alveoli and dilute the alveolar oxygen (and CO₂), causing a transient hypoxemia.

Prevent diffusion hypoxia by giving 100% oxygen for several minutes after stopping nitrous oxide. Obese, pregnant, and pediatric patients desaturate faster (a lower FRC and higher oxygen consumption).
Apneic O2 uptake ~250 mL/min; PaCO2 rises ~3–6 mmHg/min during apnea Diffusion hypoxia after N2O → give 100% O2 at emergence
*Apneic oxygenation keeps oxygen up for minutes given a patent airway and an oxygen reservoir (PaCO₂ rises about 3–6 mmHg/min); after nitrous oxide, give 100% oxygen to prevent diffusion hypoxia.*

cards.title: Oxyhemoglobin Dissociation Curve; P50; 2,3-DPG
secs: The oxyhemoglobin curve is sigmoid (cooperative oxygen binding), and the P50 — the PaO₂ at 50% saturation — is normally 26.6 mmHg. A right shift (lower affinity, releasing oxygen to the tissues) comes from increased CO₂ and H⁺ (acidosis), higher temperature, and more 2,3-DPG — the Bohr effect. A left shift (higher affinity, holding oxygen) comes from alkalosis, hypothermia, low 2,3-DPG, carboxyhemoglobin, methemoglobin, and fetal hemoglobin. (See figure for the sigmoid curve and its left/right shifts.)
Stored (banked) blood has low 2,3-DPG, so it is left-shifted and unloads oxygen poorly. Useful landmarks: a PaO₂ of about 27 → 50% saturation, about 40 → 75% (mixed venous), and about 60 → 90%.
P50 = 26.6 mmHg; right shift = ↑ CO2/H+/temp/2,3-DPG (Bohr) PaO2 27/40/60 → SaO2 50/75/90%

*The normal P50 is 26.6 mmHg; acidosis, heat, and 2,3-DPG right-shift the curve (unloading oxygen to tissues), while alkalosis, hypothermia, carbon monoxide, and fetal hemoglobin left-shift it (holding oxygen) — banked blood is left-shifted.*

noteimg.cap: Oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve: left vs right shifts

head: Right shift (unload O₂)
Left shift (hold O₂)
tables.note: “CADET face Right”: CO₂, Acid, 2,3-DPG, Exercise, Temp.

cards.title: CO2 Transport; Bohr & Haldane Effects
secs: CO₂ is carried three ways: about 70% as bicarbonate (formed by carbonic anhydrase in the red cells, with the chloride shift), about 20–23% as carbamino compounds (bound to hemoglobin), and about 7–10% dissolved. The Bohr effect: H⁺ and CO₂ shift the oxygen curve to the RIGHT, aiding oxygen unloading in the tissues. The Haldane effect: deoxygenated hemoglobin carries MORE CO₂, aiding CO₂ loading in the tissues and unloading in the lungs.
The CO₂ dissociation curve is more linear and steeper than the oxygen curve. Carbonic anhydrase allows the rapid interconversion of CO₂ and bicarbonate.
CO2: ~70% bicarbonate, ~20% carbamino, ~7–10% dissolved Bohr (CO2/H+ unload O2); Haldane (deoxy-Hb carries more CO2)
*CO₂ travels mostly as bicarbonate (about 70%), with carbamino and dissolved fractions; the Bohr effect unloads oxygen in the tissues, and the Haldane effect lets deoxyhemoglobin carry more CO₂.*

head: Form
Fraction
tables.note: Haldane: deoxy-Hb carries more CO₂; chloride shift in the RBC.

cards.title: Control of Ventilation; CO2/O2 Response Curves
secs: The central chemoreceptors in the medulla respond to CSF H⁺ and CO₂ — the dominant minute-to-minute drive — while the peripheral chemoreceptors (the carotid and aortic bodies) respond to PaO₂ (the hypoxic drive), PaCO₂, and pH. The CO₂ response curve is steep and roughly linear: minute ventilation rises about 1–3 L/min for every mmHg of PaCO₂. The hypoxic ventilatory drive becomes important only once the PaO₂ falls below about 60 mmHg.

Opioids and volatiles depress the CO₂ response (shifting it right and flattening it) and blunt the hypoxic drive (volatiles do so even at low doses). Chronic CO₂ retainers come to rely more on their hypoxic drive.
Minute ventilation ↑ ~1–3 L/min per mmHg PaCO2; hypoxic drive < ~60 mmHg PaO2 Opioids/volatiles depress CO2 response and hypoxic drive

*Central chemoreceptors (CSF H⁺ and CO₂) dominate ventilatory drive; the hypoxic drive (carotid bodies) matters only below a PaO₂ of about 60 — and opioids and volatiles blunt both the CO₂ response and the hypoxic drive.*

head: Type
Location
Responds to
tables.note: Opioids/volatiles blunt both; hypoxic drive matters in CO₂ retainers.

cards.title: Perioperative Smoking & Cessation
secs: Smoking produces carboxyhemoglobin (which left-shifts the oxygen curve and reduces oxygen carrying), airway hyperreactivity, increased secretions, impaired ciliary and immune function, and worse wound healing. The carbon-monoxide half-life shortens within hours of stopping, and the nicotine and CO effects improve over about 12–24 hours. The pulmonary benefits (cilia and secretions) take weeks, so ideally stop at least 4–8 weeks before surgery.

Even brief abstinence lowers carbon monoxide and nicotine, so encourage cessation at every opportunity. A transient increase in secretions shortly after quitting is debated but should not deter cessation.
CO half-life falls within hours; full lung benefit takes ~4–8 weeks
*Stopping smoking even 12–24 hours before surgery lowers carboxyhemoglobin and improves oxygen delivery, but the full pulmonary benefit (cilia and secretions) needs about 4–8 weeks of abstinence.*

head: Before surgery
Benefit
tables.note: Even brief cessation improves oxygen carrying.

cards.title: Larynx: Innervation, Muscles, Cartilages
secs: The laryngeal cartilages are the thyroid, the cricoid (the only complete ring), the epiglottis, and the paired arytenoid, corniculate, and cuneiform cartilages. Sensation above the cords comes from the internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve (SLN) and below the cords from the recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN); motor supply to all the intrinsic muscles is by the RLN EXCEPT the cricothyroid (the external branch of the SLN). The posterior cricoarytenoid is the only ABDUCTOR (it opens the cords).

A unilateral RLN injury causes hoarseness, while an acute bilateral RLN injury causes stridor and airway obstruction (from unopposed adduction). Superior laryngeal, glossopharyngeal, and transtracheal blocks anesthetize the airway for an awake intubation.
Above cords = SLN (internal); below = RLN; cricothyroid = external SLN Posterior cricoarytenoid = only abductor

*The recurrent laryngeal nerve supplies all the intrinsic laryngeal muscles except the cricothyroid (external SLN); the posterior cricoarytenoid is the only abductor, and a bilateral RLN injury causes stridor.*

head: Nerve
Motor
Sensory
tables.note: Bilateral RLN injury → stridor/airway emergency; unilateral → hoarseness.

cards.title: Bronchodilators (Beta-Agonists, Anticholinergics)
secs: Beta-2 agonists (albuterol short-acting, salmeterol long-acting) stimulate beta-2 receptors to cause bronchodilation; the side effects are tachycardia, tremor, and hypokalemia. Anticholinergics (ipratropium, tiotropium) block muscarinic M3 receptors to bronchodilate, especially in COPD, with minimal systemic effect (they are quaternary). Methylxanthines (theophylline) have a narrow therapeutic index, causing arrhythmias and seizures at toxic levels (now largely historical).

For intraoperative bronchospasm, deepen anesthesia and give an inhaled beta-2 agonist through the circuit, with epinephrine if it is severe. Combining a beta-agonist with an anticholinergic gives an additive effect.
Beta-2 agonist (bronchodilation, ↓ K, tremor); ipratropium = M3 antagonist

*Beta-2 agonists (albuterol) and anticholinergics (ipratropium) bronchodilate; the beta-agonists cause tachycardia, tremor, and hypokalemia — for intraoperative bronchospasm deepen anesthesia and give inhaled beta-2 (epinephrine if severe).*

head: Class
Example
Note
tables.note: Intraop bronchospasm: inhaled β₂-agonist + deepen anesthetic.

cards.title: Anti-inflammatory: Steroids, Leukotriene Modifiers, IgE Blockers
secs: Inhaled corticosteroids are the controller mainstay for asthma (reducing airway inflammation and hyperreactivity), and systemic steroids treat acute exacerbations. Leukotriene modifiers (montelukast, zafirlukast) reduce bronchoconstriction and inflammation, and are useful for exercise- and aspirin-sensitive asthma. Anti-IgE (omalizumab) and anti-IL-5 biologics target severe allergic or eosinophilic asthma.

Continue inhaled and controller therapy perioperatively, and consider stress-dose steroids if the patient is chronically steroid-dependent. Optimize asthma control before elective surgery to reduce bronchospasm.

*Inhaled corticosteroids are the asthma controller mainstay (continue them perioperatively); leukotriene modifiers and anti-IgE/IL-5 biologics treat specific phenotypes — and consider stress-dose steroids if the patient is steroid-dependent.*

cards.title: Obstructive Disease: Asthma, COPD, Bronchiectasis, CF
secs: The obstructive pattern is a reduced FEV1/FVC ratio (under 0.7) with air trapping, hyperinflation, and prolonged expiration; it is reversible in asthma but largely fixed in COPD. For asthma (airway hyperreactivity): optimize the controllers, avoid instrumenting a lightly anesthetized airway, use the bronchodilating agents (ketamine, volatiles, propofol), and extubate deep if appropriate. For COPD: avoid a high FiO₂ in CO₂ retainers (it removes the hypoxic drive), watch for auto-PEEP and dynamic hyperinflation (allow a long expiration), and remember the risk of bulla rupture and pneumothorax.

Cystic fibrosis and bronchiectasis bring copious secretions and chronic infection, needing chest physiotherapy and careful airway clearance. Regional or neuraxial techniques, where feasible, reduce pulmonary complications.
Obstructive: FEV1/FVC < 0.7; COPD CO2 retainers — avoid excess FiO2, watch auto-PEEP

*Obstructive disease lowers the FEV1/FVC ratio (under 0.7) with air trapping; optimize asthma controllers and avoid light-anesthesia instrumentation, and in COPD avoid excess FiO₂ and allow a long expiration to prevent auto-PEEP.*

head: Feature
Asthma
COPD
tables.note: Allow long expiration to avoid auto-PEEP; avoid histamine-releasers.

cards.title: Mediastinal Masses
secs: Anterior mediastinal masses (lymphoma, thymoma, teratoma, thyroid) can compress the airway, the great vessels, the heart, and the pulmonary artery, risking cardiovascular or airway collapse under general anesthesia. The danger is that losing spontaneous ventilation and adding muscle relaxation removes the splinting that holds the airways open, producing a “can’t ventilate” situation when supine. Evaluate the patient with positional symptoms, a CT scan, flow-volume loops, and echocardiography.

Maintain SPONTANEOUS ventilation; consider an awake or semi-upright induction and have rigid bronchoscopy and cardiopulmonary bypass on standby, with position changes (lateral or prone) as rescue. Avoid paralysis until the airway and ventilation are secured.
Anterior mass: keep spontaneous ventilation; rescue = reposition (lateral/prone) or rigid bronch/CPB

*Anterior mediastinal masses can collapse the airway or great vessels under general anesthesia — keep spontaneous ventilation, avoid paralysis until secured, and have repositioning, rigid bronchoscopy, and bypass as rescue.*

head: Concern
Detail
rows: Keep spontaneous ventilation; awake/sitting; rigid bronch standby
tables.note: Worst case: cardiopulmonary collapse on induction — consider bypass standby.

cards.title: Restrictive Disease: ARDS; Interstitial; Fibrosis
secs: The restrictive pattern is reduced volumes (FVC, total lung capacity) with a PRESERVED or increased FEV1/FVC ratio and decreased compliance. ARDS (the Berlin definition) is acute, bilateral infiltrates not due to cardiac failure, with a PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio of 300 or less (mild), 200 or less (moderate), or 100 or less (severe). Lung-protective ventilation uses a tidal volume of 6 mL/kg of ideal body weight, a plateau pressure under 30 cmH₂O, PEEP, permissive hypercapnia, and prone positioning for severe cases.
Pulmonary fibrosis and interstitial disease bring poor reserve and hypoxemia, needing careful fluid and ventilation management. Avoid volutrauma and titrate the FiO₂ and PEEP.
Restrictive: ↓ volumes, normal/↑ FEV1/FVC; ARDS P/F ≤ 300/200/100 Lung-protective: Vt 6 mL/kg IBW, plateau < 30 cmH2O

*Restrictive disease lowers the volumes with a preserved FEV1/FVC ratio; ARDS is defined by a PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio of 300/200/100 (Berlin) — ventilate protectively (6 mL/kg ideal body weight, plateau under 30, PEEP, prone for severe).*

head: Severity
P/F ratio
tables.note: Acute (≤ 1 wk), bilateral infiltrates, not cardiac; PEEP ≥ 5. Use lung-protective ventilation.

cards.title: Pneumothorax, Bronchopleural Fistula, Chylothorax
secs: A tension pneumothorax (air trapped under pressure) causes hypotension, hypoxia, a rising airway pressure, absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, and distended neck veins, and needs immediate needle decompression and then a chest tube; nitrous oxide worsens any pneumothorax (it diffuses in). A bronchopleural fistula is a persistent air leak that risks soiling the healthy lung and an inability to ventilate — consider lung isolation, spontaneous ventilation, and low airway pressures. A chylothorax (from thoracic-duct injury) produces milky pleural fluid with nutritional and immune losses.
Avoid nitrous oxide and high positive pressure with bullae, blebs, or a bronchopleural fistula. Positive-pressure ventilation can convert a simple pneumothorax into a tension pneumothorax.
Tension PTX → needle decompression then chest tube; avoid N2O (expands air)

*A tension pneumothorax (hypotension, high airway pressure, absent sounds, tracheal deviation) needs immediate decompression; avoid nitrous oxide (it expands air spaces); a bronchopleural fistula needs lung isolation and low pressures.*

cards.title: One-Lung Ventilation & Thoracoscopy
secs: Lung isolation (a double-lumen tube or a bronchial blocker) collapses the operative lung for surgical access while the dependent lung is ventilated. Hypoxemia during one-lung ventilation is common (from shunt through the non-ventilated lung), and hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction in the collapsed lung reduces it over time. Manage one-lung-ventilation hypoxemia by confirming the tube position (fiberoptic), raising the FiO₂, applying CPAP to the non-dependent lung and PEEP to the dependent lung, recruiting, using intermittent two-lung ventilation, or clamping the operative pulmonary artery.

Use protective settings — a lower tidal volume (about 4–6 mL/kg) on the dependent lung and limited pressures. Volatiles mildly inhibit hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (a modest effect at or below 1 MAC).
OLV hypoxemia: check position → FiO2 → non-dependent CPAP + dependent PEEP → recruit

*One-lung ventilation collapses the operative lung; hypoxemia (shunt) is managed by checking tube position, raising the FiO₂, non-dependent CPAP plus dependent PEEP, and recruitment — and ventilate the dependent lung protectively.*

head: Step
Action
rows: 100% O₂; confirm tube position with fiberoptic
Intermittent re-inflation; clamp PA if refractory
tables.note: Volatiles blunt hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction.

cards.title: Mediastinoscopy
secs: Cervical mediastinoscopy (for lymph-node biopsy or staging) risks compression of the innominate (brachiocephalic) artery by the scope, which causes a loss of the right-arm pulse and a falsely low right-sided SpO₂ and blood pressure. Place the pulse oximeter or arterial line on the RIGHT arm to detect innominate compression, and monitor blood pressure on the left. The major risk is massive hemorrhage from the great vessels requiring a sternotomy; also pneumothorax, RLN injury, and air embolism.
Maintain large-bore intravenous access and have blood available. If the right-arm pulse is lost, ask the surgeon to release the compression.
Right-arm SpO2/line detects innominate-artery compression; left-arm for true BP

*During mediastinoscopy, scope compression of the innominate artery drops the right-arm pulse — monitor SpO₂ and pressure on the right (true blood pressure on the left); the dreaded complication is great-vessel hemorrhage.*

head: Complication
Detail
rows: Loss of RIGHT radial pulse / false ↓ BP
tables.note: Monitor right arm for perfusion, left arm for blood pressure.

cards.title: Mechanical Ventilation Modes; PEEP/CPAP; Prone
secs: The basic modes are volume versus pressure control, assist-control versus SIMV, with pressure support augmenting spontaneous breaths. PEEP and CPAP recruit alveoli, raise the FRC, and improve oxygenation, but excess PEEP causes barotrauma and reduces venous return and cardiac output. Prone positioning improves V/Q matching and oxygenation in moderate-to-severe ARDS and reduces mortality (the PROSEVA trial).

Lung-protective ventilation is 6 mL/kg of ideal body weight, a plateau under 30 cmH₂O, titrated PEEP, and permissive hypercapnia. Proning risks airway and line dislodgement, facial and ocular pressure injury, and hemodynamic change.
Prone ARDS improves oxygenation/mortality (PROSEVA); protective Vt 6 mL/kg IBW

*PEEP and CPAP recruit alveoli and raise the FRC (excess lowers cardiac output); prone positioning improves oxygenation and mortality in moderate-to-severe ARDS — combine it with protective ventilation (6 mL/kg, plateau under 30).*

cards.title: Volutrauma, Barotrauma, Biotrauma
secs: Ventilator-induced lung injury comes in forms: barotrauma (high pressure causing pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum), volutrauma (overdistension from a high tidal volume), atelectrauma (shear from repeated opening and collapse), and biotrauma (inflammatory mediator release causing systemic injury and multi-organ dysfunction). The driving pressure (plateau minus PEEP) correlates with mortality, so keep the plateau under 30 cmH₂O and the driving pressure under about 15. Lung-protective ventilation minimizes all of these.
Use adequate PEEP to prevent atelectrauma and low tidal volumes to prevent volutrauma. Permissive hypercapnia is accepted in order to protect the lung.

*Ventilator injury includes baro-, volu-, atelec-, and biotrauma; minimize it with a low tidal volume, a plateau under 30, adequate PEEP, and a driving pressure under about 15 (which tracks mortality).*

cards.title: Nitric Oxide, Heliox, Steroids
secs: Inhaled nitric oxide is a selective pulmonary vasodilator (it acts only in ventilated areas), so it improves V/Q matching and lowers the pulmonary artery pressure (in pulmonary hypertension, RV failure, and refractory hypoxemia); it carries a risk of rebound on abrupt withdrawal and of methemoglobinemia. Heliox (a helium-oxygen mix) is a low-density gas that reduces turbulent flow and the work of breathing through narrowed airways (upper-airway obstruction, severe asthma), though the benefit is limited by the high helium fraction needed (and so a low FiO₂). Steroids reduce airway inflammation (in asthma and COPD exacerbations, croup, and post-extubation edema).

Inhaled NO and heliox are bridges or adjuncts, not definitive therapies. Heliox needs about 60–70% helium to help, which limits its use when a high FiO₂ is required. Abrupt withdrawal of inhaled NO causes REBOUND pulmonary hypertension and an acute rise in RV afterload → falling cardiac output and worsening hypoxemia; wean gradually. (NO₂ formation and methemoglobinemia are hazards of GIVING NO, not of stopping it.)
iNO = selective pulmonary vasodilator (improves V/Q, lowers PAP); heliox lowers turbulent WOB

*Inhaled NO selectively dilates ventilated pulmonary vessels (improving V/Q and lowering pulmonary pressure), with a rebound and methemoglobinemia risk; heliox cuts turbulent work in narrowed airways but needs a high helium fraction, and steroids reduce airway inflammation.*

cards.title: Lung Transplantation Anesthetic Implications
secs: Lung-transplant candidates have end-stage lung disease with severe gas-exchange and hemodynamic compromise, often with pulmonary hypertension and right-ventricular dysfunction. They need one-lung ventilation and face hemodynamic instability when the pulmonary artery is clamped, frequently requiring cardiopulmonary bypass or ECMO; manage the reperfusion (primary graft dysfunction and pulmonary edema). The transplanted lung is DENERVATED, so there is no cough reflex below the anastomosis and the mucociliary clearance is altered; disrupted lymphatics make it edema-prone.
Watch for acute right-ventricular failure when the pulmonary artery is clamped — have inotropes, pulmonary vasodilators, and ECMO ready. Use meticulous fluid restriction to limit reperfusion edema.

*Lung-transplant patients have severe pulmonary hypertension and RV dysfunction needing one-lung ventilation and often ECMO or bypass; the graft is denervated (no cough below the anastomosis) and edema-prone — keep them dry and protect the right ventricle.*

cards.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea; STOP-BANG
secs: Obstructive sleep apnea is the recurrent collapse of the upper airway during sleep, causing hypoxemia and hypercarbia and, over the long term, pulmonary and systemic hypertension and arrhythmia. The STOP-BANG screen counts Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apnea, Pressure (hypertension), BMI over 35, Age over 50, Neck over 40 cm, and male Gender; a score of 3 or more is intermediate risk and 5 or more is high risk. These patients are highly sensitive to sedatives and opioids (airway collapse and respiratory depression) and often have a difficult mask or intubation, so continue their CPAP perioperatively.
Use multimodal, opioid-sparing analgesia, continuous pulse oximetry, and extubate fully awake. They have a higher risk of postoperative respiratory complications.

*OSA causes repetitive airway collapse with high sensitivity to sedatives and opioids; screen with STOP-BANG (3 or more intermediate, 5 or more high), continue CPAP, use opioid-sparing analgesia, and extubate fully awake.*

head: Criterion
tables.note: ≥ 3 intermediate, ≥ 5 high risk for OSA.

cards.title: Chemoreceptor Control of Ventilation & the Carotid Bodies
secs: The PERIPHERAL CHEMORECEPTORS in the CAROTID BODIES (at the carotid bifurcation, glossopharyngeal/CN IX afferents) are responsible for essentially ALL of the ventilatory response to HYPOXEMIA — carotid body blockade or bilateral carotid endarterectomy abolishes the hypoxic ventilatory drive. For HYPERCAPNIA, the response is mostly CENTRAL: the medullary CENTRAL CHEMORECEPTORS (sensing CSF H+/PaCO2) provide about 60-80% of the response, with peripheral chemoreceptors contributing the remainder. Do not confuse the carotid BODY (chemoreceptor) with the carotid SINUS, a dilated segment at the end of the common carotid containing BARORECEPTORS that reflexly modulate HEART RATE and blood pressure.

Even subanesthetic concentrations of volatile agents (~0.1 MAC) markedly blunt the carotid-body hypoxic response, which is why residual anesthesia impairs the protective response to hypoxemia in recovery. Carotid endarterectomy or carotid body resection can permanently impair the hypoxic drive. Carotid sinus stimulation (e.g., surgical manipulation, carotid sinus hypersensitivity) can trigger bradycardia and hypotension.
Central chemoreceptors: ~60-80% of the hypercapnic ventilatory response. Peripheral (carotid body): ~100% of the hypoxic response. Volatile agents blunt hypoxic drive at ~0.1 MAC.
Carotid BODIES drive the hypoxic response; central chemoreceptors give 60-80% of the CO2 response; the carotid SINUS is a baroreceptor.

cards.title: Respiratory Quotient (RQ) & Substrate Metabolism
secs: The RESPIRATORY QUOTIENT (RQ) is the ratio of CO2 PRODUCED to O2 CONSUMED (VCO2/VO2) at the cellular level during metabolism of a substrate. It depends on the fuel being oxidized: FATS have the LOWEST RQ (~0.7), PROTEINS intermediate (~0.8), and CARBOHYDRATES the highest (~1.0). A typical mixed Western diet gives an RQ around 0.8. The analogous whole-body measurement of expired gas is the respiratory exchange ratio (RER), which equals RQ at steady state.

RQ matters in critical care nutrition: OVERFEEDING, especially with excess carbohydrate, drives RQ toward or above 1.0 and increases CO2 production, which can impede weaning from mechanical ventilation in patients with limited ventilatory reserve. An RQ >1.0 suggests lipogenesis/overfeeding, whereas a very low RQ suggests fat oxidation/starvation. Balanced feeding limits excess CO2 production.
RQ = VCO2 / VO2. Fat ~0.7, protein ~0.8, carbohydrate ~1.0; mixed diet ~0.8; >1.0 implies overfeeding/lipogenesis.
RQ = CO2 produced / O2 consumed: fat 0.7, protein 0.8, carbs 1.0 — carbohydrate overfeeding raises CO2 and hinders vent weaning.

cards.title: Pulmonary Metabolism of Circulating Substances
secs: The LUNGS are metabolically active and clear or partially metabolize numerous endogenous and exogenous substances during their passage through the pulmonary circulation. Substances METABOLIZED/taken up by the lung include FENTANYL, PROPOFOL, and LIDOCAINE (significant first-pass pulmonary uptake), plus BRADYKININ (largely inactivated), ANGIOTENSIN I (converted to angiotensin II by endothelial ACE), SEROTONIN, and NOREPINEPHRINE. Importantly, the lung does NOT significantly metabolize EPINEPHRINE, HISTAMINE, or DOPAMINE, which pass through essentially unchanged.

Pulmonary first-pass uptake explains why a large fraction of an IV bolus of lipophilic drugs (fentanyl, propofol, lidocaine) is transiently sequestered in the lung, buffering peak arterial concentrations. ACE-mediated conversion of angiotensin I to II in the pulmonary endothelium underlies the renin-angiotensin system and the cough/angioedema side effects of ACE inhibitors (from reduced bradykinin breakdown).
Lungs metabolize fentanyl, propofol, lidocaine, bradykinin, angiotensin I, serotonin, and norepinephrine — but NOT epinephrine, histamine, or dopamine.

cards.title: Transpulmonary Pressure in Restrictive Lung Disease
secs: TRANSPULMONARY PRESSURE (Ptp = alveolar pressure - pleural pressure) is the distending pressure across the lung and is inversely related to COMPLIANCE (C = ΔV/ΔP). In RESTRICTIVE lung disease (e.g., pulmonary fibrosis, ARDS, chest-wall/pleural restriction), lung compliance is REDUCED (stiff lungs), so a HIGHER transpulmonary pressure is required to achieve any given TIDAL VOLUME. This produces the characteristic rapid, shallow breathing pattern and reduced lung volumes.

During mechanical ventilation of restrictive/low-compliance lungs, higher airway (plateau) pressures are needed to deliver a set tidal volume, raising the risk of BAROTRAUMA and VOLUTRAUMA. Lung-protective strategy uses LOW tidal volumes (~6 mL/kg ideal body weight), plateau pressure <30 cmH2O, and adequate PEEP. Esophageal (pleural) pressure monitoring can estimate true transpulmonary pressure to titrate PEEP and limit lung stress. Contrast with obstructive disease, where the problem is expiratory flow limitation and air trapping rather than low compliance.
Ptp = P_alveolar - P_pleural. Compliance C = ΔV/ΔP (lower in restrictive disease). Lung-protective: Vt ~6 mL/kg IBW, plateau pressure <30 cmH2O.
Stiff (low-compliance) restrictive lungs need HIGHER transpulmonary pressures for a given tidal volume — ventilate with low Vt and limit plateau pressure.

cards.title: Cerebral Blood Flow & Autoregulation
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: Normal cerebral blood flow is about 50 mL/100 g/min (around 15% of cardiac output); ischemia begins below about 20 and the risk of infarction below about 10. Autoregulation keeps cerebral blood flow constant across a mean arterial (or cerebral perfusion) pressure of roughly 50–150 mmHg; outside that range, flow becomes pressure-passive. Cerebral perfusion pressure equals the mean arterial pressure minus the intracranial pressure (or central venous pressure, whichever is higher).

Chronic hypertension shifts the autoregulation curve to the right, so higher pressures are needed. Volatiles above 1 MAC impair autoregulation; maintain cerebral perfusion pressure after brain injury. Raising MAP within the autoregulatory range (e.g., phenylephrine 80→100 mmHg) holds CBF constant by INCREASING cerebral vascular resistance (autoregulatory vasoconstriction); phenylephrine doesn’t cross the blood–brain barrier, so CMRO₂ is unchanged.
CBF ≈ 50 mL/100 g/min; autoregulation MAP ~50–150 mmHg CPP = MAP − ICP (or CVP)

*Normal cerebral blood flow is about 50 mL/100 g/min, autoregulated over a mean pressure of roughly 50–150; cerebral perfusion pressure is MAP minus ICP; chronic hypertension right-shifts the curve and high-dose volatiles abolish autoregulation.*

head: Parameter
Value
tables.note: Volatiles impair autoregulation & uncouple flow; hyperventilation ↓ CBF.

cards.title: CMRO2 & Effects of PaCO2 / PaO2 / pH (Inverse Steal)
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: The cerebral metabolic rate for oxygen (CMRO₂) is about 3–3.5 mL O₂/100 g/min, and flow-metabolism coupling matches cerebral blood flow to demand. PaCO₂ has a powerful, roughly linear effect (about 1–2 mL/100 g/min per mmHg between 20 and 80 mmHg): hyperventilation lowers cerebral blood flow and ICP (transiently), and hypoventilation raises them. PaO₂ has little effect until it falls below about 50–60 mmHg, after which cerebral blood flow rises sharply. Temperature: CMRO₂ and (via coupling) CBF change ~6–7% per 1 °C — so a rise from 37 °C to 39 °C raises CBF ~14%; hypothermia lowers both (the basis of cerebral protection).

Inverse steal (the “Robin Hood” effect): hyperventilation constricts the normal vessels and diverts flow toward the maximally dilated vessels of ischemic areas. Volatiles uncouple flow and metabolism (raising CBF while lowering CMRO₂), whereas propofol and barbiturates lower both.
CMRO2 ≈ 3–3.5 mL/100 g/min; CBF ~1–2 mL/100 g/min per mmHg PaCO2 PaO2 effect only below ~50–60 mmHg Temperature: ~6–7% change in CMRO₂/CBF per 1 °C (37→39 °C ≈ +14%)

*CMRO₂ is about 3–3.5 mL/100 g/min; cerebral blood flow tracks PaCO₂ (about 1–2 mL/100 g/min per mmHg) and temperature (~6–7% per °C) and rises only once PaO₂ falls below ~50–60; hyperventilation can cause inverse steal toward ischemic brain.*

head: Agent
CMRO₂
CBF
tables.note: Volatiles uncouple flow-metabolism; propofol preserves coupling.

cards.title: CSF Formation, Flow & Pressure
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: CSF is produced mainly by the choroid plexus (about 500 mL/day, with a total volume of about 150 mL); it flows from the lateral to the third to the fourth ventricle, into the subarachnoid space, and out through the arachnoid granulations into the venous sinuses. Normal intracranial pressure is 5–15 mmHg (supine). The Monro-Kellie doctrine holds that the cranial volume (brain plus blood plus CSF) is fixed, so a rise in one component must be offset by a fall in another or the ICP climbs. Acetazolamide reduces CSF production.

Raised ICP threatens herniation; treat it with head elevation, brief hyperventilation, osmotherapy (mannitol or hypertonic saline), CSF drainage, and sedation. Compliance falls as volume rises (the elastance curve), so late increases cause large pressure jumps.
CSF ~150 mL total, ~500 mL/day; normal ICP 5–15 mmHg; Monro-Kellie doctrine

*CSF (about 150 mL total, 500 mL/day from the choroid plexus) and the Monro-Kellie doctrine govern ICP (normal 5–15 mmHg); raised ICP is treated with head-up positioning, osmotherapy, CSF drainage, and brief hyperventilation.*

head: Parameter
Value
tables.note: Turns over 3–4×/day; acetazolamide reduces production.

cards.title: Blood-Brain Barrier
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: Tight junctions between the cerebral capillary endothelial cells restrict passage, so small, lipophilic, unionized, non-protein-bound molecules cross most readily. The barrier excludes large or ionized molecules and most drugs unless they are lipid-soluble; the circumventricular organs lack a barrier (the chemoreceptor trigger zone and area postrema). It is disrupted by trauma, tumor, infection, and severe hypertension, leading to edema and altered drug penetration.

Mannitol osmotically draws water across an intact barrier to reduce brain water. It explains CNS-effect differences between drugs (for example, glycopyrrolate, which does not cross, versus atropine, which does).
Crosses the BBB: small, lipophilic, unionized, unbound molecules

*The blood-brain barrier (endothelial tight junctions) admits small, lipophilic, unionized, unbound molecules; it is disrupted by injury or tumor and is bypassed at the circumventricular organs (the area postrema).*

cards.title: Evoked Potentials
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: Intraoperative neuromonitoring in spine, neurologic, and vascular surgery uses somatosensory evoked potentials (SSEPs, the dorsal columns), motor evoked potentials (MEPs, the corticospinal tracts), brainstem auditory evoked potentials, and visual evoked potentials. Volatile agents and nitrous oxide DEPRESS the amplitude and increase the latency in a dose-dependent way, so keep them below about 0.5–1 MAC; TIVA (propofol plus an opioid) preserves the signals best. Muscle relaxants abolish MEPs, so avoid or limit paralysis when monitoring them.

A significant change (a fall in amplitude or a rise in latency) prompts checking position, perfusion, anesthetic depth, and the surgical cause. SSEPs are relatively robust, while MEPs are the most anesthetic-sensitive. For thoracoabdominal aortic repair, MOTOR evoked potentials best detect anterior spinal cord (anterior spinal artery) ischemia — more sensitive/specific there than SSEPs, which monitor only the posterior columns and can stay normal during anterior cord infarction.
Volatiles/N2O ↓ amplitude, ↑ latency; TIVA preserves; relaxants abolish MEPs

*Evoked potentials (SSEP/MEP) are depressed by volatiles and nitrous oxide (keep a low MAC and prefer TIVA), and muscle relaxants abolish MEPs; a loss of signal prompts a perfusion, position, and surgical check.*

head: Modality
Pathway
tables.note: SSEP misses anterior cord ischemia — add MEP in spine surgery.

cards.title: Membrane & Action Potentials; Synaptic Transmission
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: The resting membrane potential (about −70 mV) is set by the potassium gradient and the sodium/potassium-ATPase; depolarization to threshold opens voltage-gated sodium channels, producing an all-or-none action potential, after which potassium efflux repolarizes the cell. Local anesthetics block the voltage-gated sodium channels, preventing propagation. Synaptic transmission goes: action potential → calcium influx → neurotransmitter release → activation of postsynaptic receptors (excitatory or inhibitory).
Saltatory conduction along myelinated fibers (jumping between the nodes of Ranvier) speeds conduction. Many anesthetics act on ligand-gated channels (GABA-A, NMDA, nicotinic).
Resting ~−70 mV (K+ + Na/K-ATPase); AP = Na+ in → K+ out

*The action potential (resting about −70 mV) is sodium influx then potassium efflux; local anesthetics block sodium channels, and synaptic release is calcium-dependent — the targets of the GABA- and NMDA-acting anesthetics.*

cards.title: Pain Mechanisms; Nociceptors; Wind-Up
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: Nociception has four steps: transduction (at the nociceptors), transmission (along fast, sharp A-delta fibers and slow, dull C fibers), modulation (in the dorsal horn), and perception. Wind-up is the progressive amplification of dorsal-horn responses to repeated C-fiber input (an NMDA-mediated central sensitization) that produces hyperalgesia and allodynia. Pre-emptive, multimodal analgesia and NMDA antagonists (ketamine) blunt wind-up and central sensitization.
Peripheral sensitization from inflammatory mediators lowers the nociceptor threshold. Chronic pain involves central sensitization and reorganization.
A-delta (fast/sharp) vs C (slow/dull); wind-up = NMDA-mediated central sensitization
*Pain travels along A-delta (sharp) and C (dull) fibers; repeated C-fiber input causes NMDA-mediated wind-up and central sensitization — blunted by multimodal analgesia and ketamine.*

head: Fiber
Function
Myelin
tables.note: Local anesthetic blocks small/myelinated first: B, C, A-δ before motor.

cards.title: Dorsal Horn Transmission & Modulation
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: Primary afferent fibers synapse in the dorsal horn (especially the substantia gelatinosa), where the excitatory transmitters are glutamate and substance P. The gate-control theory holds that large-fiber A-beta input (touch, or TENS) inhibits pain transmission, while descending pathways from the periaqueductal gray and raphe release serotonin, norepinephrine, and endogenous opioids. The drug targets here are neuraxial opioids, alpha-2 agonists, and local anesthetics.
This explains why neuraxial analgesia, TENS, and descending-pathway drugs (such as the SNRIs for chronic pain) work.
Dorsal-horn transmitters: glutamate + substance P; gate control via A-beta input

*The dorsal horn is the key site of pain modulation (glutamate and substance P); gate control (A-beta input) and descending serotonin, norepinephrine, and opioid pathways inhibit pain — the basis for neuraxial opioids, alpha-2 agonists, and TENS.*

cards.title: Autonomic Nervous System
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: The sympathetic system is thoracolumbar (T1–L2/3): short preganglionic fibers (acetylcholine on nicotinic receptors) reach the chain, then long postganglionic fibers release norepinephrine onto adrenergic receptors (the sweat glands are the exception, using acetylcholine); the adrenal medulla releases epinephrine directly. The parasympathetic system is craniosacral (cranial nerves III, VII, IX, X plus S2–S4): long preganglionic and short postganglionic fibers, all using acetylcholine (nicotinic then muscarinic). The receptors and their effects: alpha-1 (vasoconstriction), beta-1 (heart rate and contractility), beta-2 (bronchodilation and vasodilation), and muscarinic (M2 in the heart, M3 in glands and smooth muscle).
All autonomic ganglia and the neuromuscular junction use nicotinic acetylcholine receptors. At rest, vagal (parasympathetic) tone predominates in the heart.
Sympathetic = thoracolumbar (NE); parasympathetic = craniosacral (ACh) All preganglionic + NMJ = nicotinic ACh

*Sympathetic is thoracolumbar (postganglionic norepinephrine) and parasympathetic is craniosacral (all acetylcholine); every ganglion and the neuromuscular junction is nicotinic — know the alpha, beta, and muscarinic effects.*

head: Feature
Sympathetic
Parasympathetic
tables.note: Both use ACh-nicotinic at the ganglion.

cards.title: Temperature Regulation
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Physiology (I.C.1.a)
secs: The hypothalamus is the thermoregulatory center, normally defending a narrow interthreshold range of about 0.2°C — a range that anesthesia widens roughly 10–20-fold, impairing the responses. The cold responses are vasoconstriction, shivering, and non-shivering thermogenesis (in infants, via brown fat); the heat responses are sweating and vasodilation. Heat is lost by radiation (the largest route), convection, conduction, and evaporation.

Redistribution of heat from the core to the periphery causes the largest early intraoperative temperature drop. Forced-air warming and warmed fluids prevent hypothermia; treat shivering when it occurs.
Anesthesia widens the interthreshold range ~10–20× (~0.2°C → 2–4°C) Heat loss: radiation > convection > conduction > evaporation
*The hypothalamus defends temperature within about 0.2°C, a range anesthesia widens 10–20-fold, so redistribution (core to periphery) causes early hypothermia — radiation is the main route of loss.*

head: Mechanism
Share
tables.note: Redistribution causes the first-hour drop; forced-air warming counters it.

cards.title: Circle of Willis & Cerebral Circulation
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Anatomy (I.C.1.b)
secs: The anterior circulation (the internal carotids) and the posterior circulation (the vertebrobasilar system) join at the circle of Willis, which provides collateral flow. Its components are the anterior communicating, the paired anterior cerebral, the internal carotid, the posterior communicating, and the posterior cerebral arteries (which come off the basilar). A complete circle is present in only a minority of people; an incomplete circle raises the risk of stroke and watershed ischemia.
Watershed zones (between major arterial territories) are vulnerable to hypotension. This anatomy matters for carotid endarterectomy, aneurysm surgery, and perfusion management.
*The circle of Willis links the anterior (carotid) and posterior (vertebrobasilar) circulations for collateral flow; it is often incomplete, leaving watershed zones vulnerable to hypotension.*

cards.title: Meninges & Spinal Spaces
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Anatomy (I.C.1.b)
secs: There are three layers: the tough outer dura mater, the arachnoid mater, and the pia mater (which adheres to the cord); CSF lies in the subarachnoid space, between the arachnoid and the pia. The epidural space (outside the dura) contains fat, the epidural venous plexus, and nerve roots — the target for epidural anesthesia. The subdural space is a potential space (the site of a subdural hematoma and the rare subdural block).

The spinal-needle path runs: skin → subcutaneous tissue → supraspinous ligament → interspinous ligament → ligamentum flavum → epidural space → dura/arachnoid → subarachnoid space (CSF). The dural sac and the level of the conus (about L1) set the safe puncture levels.
Layers: dura → arachnoid → pia; CSF in subarachnoid space; epidural is outside the dura
*Dura, arachnoid, and pia surround the cord; CSF sits in the subarachnoid space and epidural drugs go outside the dura; the needle crosses the ligamentum flavum and then the dura to reach CSF.*

head: Layer
Note
tables.note: Epidural stops at loss of resistance; spinal pierces dura into the subarachnoid space.

cards.title: Spinal Nerves & Sensory Distribution
cards.cat: Organ-Based › Central & Peripheral Nervous System › Anatomy (I.C.1.b)
secs: There are 31 pairs of spinal nerves (8 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral, 1 coccygeal); each has a dorsal (sensory) and a ventral (motor) root. Dermatomes map the sensory levels: C6 thumb, C7 middle finger, C8 little finger, T4 nipple, T6 xiphoid, T10 umbilicus, L1 inguinal, S2–S4 perineum. The major plexuses are the cervical, brachial, lumbar, and sacral.
Dermatomes are used to assess neuraxial or regional block height and to localize lesions. The cord ends at the conus medullaris (about L1) and continues as the cauda equina.
31 nerve pairs; dorsal root = sensory, ventral root = motor Key dermatomes: T4 nipple, T6 xiphoid, T10 umbilicus, L1 inguinal
*There are 31 spinal nerve pairs (dorsal root sensory, ventral root motor); know the key dermatomes (T4 nipple, T6 xiphoid, T10 umbilicus, L1 inguinal) for assessing block height.*

cards.title: Intracranial Pressure; Elastance & Herniation
secs: Normal intracranial pressure is 5–15 mmHg (supine), and a sustained pressure above 20–22 mmHg needs treatment. By the Monro-Kellie doctrine the brain, blood, and CSF fill a fixed vault; early compensation (displacing CSF and venous blood) keeps ICP flat, but once that compliance is exhausted small added volumes cause steep ICP rises (the elastance curve). The Cushing triad (a late, ominous sign) is hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respiration — a warning of impending herniation.

Herniation syndromes include uncal (a third-nerve palsy and a “blown” pupil), central, subfalcine, and tonsillar (brainstem compression). Lower ICP with head elevation to 30°, normocapnia (brief hyperventilation only in a crisis), osmotherapy (mannitol 0.25–1 g/kg or hypertonic saline), CSF drainage, sedation, and treating fever and seizures; keep cerebral perfusion pressure (MAP − ICP) around 60.
Normal ICP 5–15 mmHg; treat > 20–22; CPP = MAP − ICP (~60 target) Mannitol 0.25–1 g/kg; Cushing triad = HTN + bradycardia + irregular breathing

*ICP above 20–22 mmHg needs treatment; once compliance is exhausted small volumes spike the pressure, and the Cushing triad signals herniation — treat with head-up positioning, osmotherapy, CSF drainage, and a cerebral perfusion pressure around 60.*

head: Type
Hallmark
tables.note: Cushing triad (HTN, bradycardia, irregular respiration) = impending herniation.

cards.title: EEG: Wave Patterns, Spectral Edge, Burst Suppression
secs: The EEG bands are delta (under 4 Hz, deep sleep or deep anesthesia), theta (4–8 Hz), alpha (8–13 Hz, awake but relaxed with eyes closed), and beta (over 13 Hz, alert or lightly anesthetized). Deepening anesthesia raises the amplitude and slows the frequency, progressing to burst suppression and then an isoelectric tracing at very deep levels. Processed EEG (a BIS of about 40–60 for general anesthesia) and the 95% spectral edge frequency summarize the raw EEG for depth monitoring.
Burst suppression is a target for cerebral protection (a barbiturate or propofol coma) and lowers CMRO₂. Most anesthetics slow the EEG; ketamine and nitrous oxide are activating exceptions.
Bands: delta < 4, theta 4–8, alpha 8–13, beta > 13 Hz; BIS ~40–60 for GA Deepening → slowing → burst suppression → isoelectric

*Deepening anesthesia slows the EEG toward burst suppression and an isoelectric line; a BIS of about 40–60 targets general anesthesia, and burst suppression lowers CMRO₂ for protection — ketamine and nitrous oxide are activating exceptions.*

head: Band
Frequency
State
tables.note: Anesthesia slows the EEG; burst suppression = very deep.

cards.title: Evoked Responses & Effects of Ischemia/Anesthetics
secs: Evoked potentials test the integrity of specific pathways: SSEPs (the dorsal column–medial lemniscus), MEPs (the corticospinal tract), brainstem auditory evoked potentials (very resistant), and visual evoked potentials (very sensitive and unreliable). Ischemia decreases the amplitude and increases the latency — the SAME direction as anesthetic effects, so the two must be distinguished. The order of anesthetic sensitivity (most to least affected) is VEP > MEP > cortical SSEP > BAEP.

Volatiles and nitrous oxide depress the signals dose-dependently, so keep them at or below 0.5–1 MAC, prefer TIVA, and avoid relaxants when monitoring MEPs. A sudden unilateral change suggests a surgical, positioning, or perfusion cause — alert the surgeon and optimize the MAP, hemoglobin, and position.
Ischemia & anesthetics both: ↓ amplitude, ↑ latency Sensitivity: VEP > MEP > cortical SSEP > BAEP (BAEP most robust)

*Both ischemia and anesthetics decrease evoked-potential amplitude and increase latency; brainstem auditory potentials are the most robust and visual and motor potentials the most sensitive — use TIVA, avoid relaxants for MEPs, and treat acute changes by optimizing perfusion.*

head: Agent
SSEP / MEP
tables.note: TIVA without NMB is favored when monitoring motor potentials.

cards.title: Anticonvulsants
secs: Phenytoin and carbamazepine are hepatic enzyme inducers, so chronic use causes RESISTANCE to non-depolarizing muscle relaxants and faster metabolism of many drugs. Mechanisms: phenytoin and carbamazepine block voltage-gated sodium channels, valproate is broad-spectrum, and gabapentin and pregabalin act at the alpha-2-delta calcium-channel subunit (also useful for neuropathic and perioperative pain). Levetiracetam is common perioperatively because it has few interactions.

Continue anticonvulsants through surgery (abrupt withdrawal provokes seizures); intravenous forms are available. Gabapentinoids add to opioid sedation and respiratory depression, so use caution in the elderly and in OSA.
Phenytoin/carbamazepine = enzyme inducers → resistance to non-depolarizers Gabapentinoids: alpha-2-delta subunit (neuropathic pain)

*Phenytoin and carbamazepine induce P450 (causing resistance to non-depolarizers); the gabapentinoids act at the alpha-2-delta subunit for neuropathic pain — and continue anticonvulsants perioperatively.*

cards.title: Alpha-2 Agonists: Clonidine, Dexmedetomidine
secs: Central alpha-2 agonism (at the locus coeruleus) produces sedation, analgesia, anxiolysis, and sympatholysis (lowering heart rate and blood pressure), and it reduces anesthetic and opioid requirements and MAC. Clonidine (oral, transdermal, or neuraxial) prolongs neuraxial blocks and treats withdrawal and shivering; stopping it abruptly causes rebound hypertension. Dexmedetomidine (intravenous) gives arousable sedation with minimal respiratory depression, but causes bradycardia and hypotension (and a bolus can cause a transient hypertension).
They are useful for awake fiberoptic intubation, ICU sedation, reducing emergence delirium and shivering, and opioid sparing. Use caution with heart block or severe bradycardia.
Central alpha-2 → ↓ sympathetic outflow; ↓ MAC/opioid need; minimal respiratory depression

*Alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, dexmedetomidine) give sedation, analgesia, and sympatholysis with little respiratory depression and lower MAC; expect bradycardia and hypotension, and clonidine rebound hypertension.*

cards.title: Sympathetic Agonists/Antagonists; Ganglionic Blockers
secs: The direct agonists are phenylephrine (alpha-1, causing vasoconstriction and a reflex bradycardia), epinephrine (alpha and beta), norepinephrine (alpha more than beta), dobutamine (beta-1), and isoproterenol (beta). The indirect agent ephedrine releases norepinephrine (with tachyphylaxis) and has mixed effects. The antagonists are the beta-blockers (esmolol is short-acting; labetalol blocks alpha and beta), the alpha-blockers (phentolamine and phenoxybenzamine for pheochromocytoma), and the historic ganglionic blockers (trimethaphan).

Choose the drug by its receptor target and your hemodynamic goal (preload, afterload, contractility, or rate). In pheochromocytoma, establish alpha-blockade BEFORE beta-blockade, or an unopposed-alpha crisis can occur.
Phenylephrine = alpha-1; ephedrine = indirect (NE release); dobutamine = beta-1 Pheochromocytoma: alpha-block before beta-block
*Know the receptor targets — phenylephrine (alpha-1), ephedrine (indirect norepinephrine release), dobutamine (beta-1) — and in pheochromocytoma always alpha-block before beta-block.*

cards.title: Anticholinesterases & Anticholinergics
secs: Anticholinesterases (neostigmine, pyridostigmine, edrophonium, physostigmine) inhibit acetylcholinesterase to raise acetylcholine, which reverses a non-depolarizing block and treats myasthenia. Their muscarinic side effects (bradycardia, secretions, bronchospasm, miosis) require a co-administered anticholinergic; physostigmine is a tertiary amine that crosses the blood-brain barrier and treats the central anticholinergic syndrome. The anticholinergics are atropine (tertiary, crosses the barrier, causes tachycardia and central effects), glycopyrrolate (quaternary, no CNS effect, a stronger antisialagogue), and scopolamine (sedation, antiemesis, amnesia).

Pair neostigmine with glycopyrrolate (their onsets match) and edrophonium with atropine. The central anticholinergic syndrome (from atropine or scopolamine) causes confusion and is reversed with physostigmine.
Neostigmine + glycopyrrolate; edrophonium + atropine Tertiary (atropine, scopolamine, physostigmine) cross BBB; glycopyrrolate does not

*Anticholinesterases boost acetylcholine (for reversal and myasthenia) but need an anticholinergic for the muscarinic effects; glycopyrrolate (quaternary) spares the brain while atropine, scopolamine, and physostigmine (tertiary) cross the blood-brain barrier.*

head: Drug
CNS
Antisialagogue
tables.note: Central anticholinergic syndrome (atropine/scopolamine) → physostigmine.

cards.title: Seizures
secs: Perioperative causes include subtherapeutic anticonvulsant levels, hyponatremia or other metabolic derangement, hypoxia, drugs (LAST, meperidine via normeperidine, high-dose enflurane), eclampsia, and withdrawal. Most anesthetics are anticonvulsant (propofol, barbiturates, benzodiazepines), and the first-line abortive drug is a benzodiazepine. For status epilepticus: address the ABCs, give a benzodiazepine, then load an anticonvulsant (levetiracetam, phenytoin, or valproate) and treat the cause.
Continue home anticonvulsants, check drug levels, and correct sodium and glucose. Methohexital and etomidate can activate seizure foci (relevant to electroconvulsive therapy and to epilepsy mapping).
Status: benzodiazepine first → load anticonvulsant; most anesthetics are anticonvulsant

*Most anesthetics are anticonvulsant; treat a perioperative seizure or status with a benzodiazepine first and then a loading anticonvulsant, and correct the cause (sodium, glucose, LAST, withdrawal).*

cards.title: Coma; Glasgow Coma Scale; TBI Management
secs: The Glasgow Coma Scale (3–15) scores Eye opening (out of 4), Verbal response (5), and Motor response (6); a score of 8 or less means securing the airway (“GCS 8, intubate”). In traumatic brain injury, avoid secondary injury: prevent hypoxia (keep SpO₂ at least 94%) and hypotension (systolic at least about 110), and maintain a cerebral perfusion pressure around 60–70 with normocapnia (PaCO₂ 35–40, hyperventilating only for impending herniation). Treat raised ICP (osmotherapy, head-up positioning, CSF drainage) and avoid hypotonic fluids and hyperglycemia.
Even a single episode of hypoxia or hypotension worsens the outcome after TBI. Keep the hemoglobin adequate for oxygen delivery, and control temperature and seizures.
GCS 3–15 (E4 V5 M6); GCS ≤ 8 → intubate TBI: avoid hypoxia/hypotension; CPP ~60–70; PaCO2 35–40

*A GCS of 8 or less means intubate (Eye 4, Verbal 5, Motor 6); in TBI prevent hypoxia and hypotension, keep cerebral perfusion pressure around 60–70 and PaCO₂ 35–40, and reserve hyperventilation for impending herniation.*

head: Score
Eye
Verbal
Motor
tables.note: Total 3–15; ≤ 8 → secure the airway.

cards.title: Therapeutic Barbiturate Coma
secs: High-dose barbiturates (or propofol) induce EEG burst suppression, which maximally reduces CMRO₂ (and the coupled cerebral blood flow and ICP) for cerebral protection in refractory intracranial hypertension. The dose is titrated to EEG burst suppression rather than fixed. It protects mainly against focal or incomplete ischemia (by cutting demand) and offers limited benefit in global ischemia.

The side effects are hypotension (myocardial depression and vasodilation, often needing vasopressors), immune suppression, ileus, and a prolonged emergence. It is used for refractory status epilepticus and refractory raised ICP.
Titrate to EEG burst suppression → minimal CMRO2; protects in focal ischemia

*A barbiturate or propofol coma titrated to EEG burst suppression minimizes CMRO₂ for refractory raised ICP or status epilepticus, protecting mainly against focal ischemia — at the cost of hypotension.*

cards.title: Autonomic Hyperreflexia; Spinal Shock; Quadriplegia
secs: Spinal shock (in acute spinal cord injury) causes flaccid paralysis, areflexia, and hypotension with bradycardia (from lost sympathetic tone) for days to weeks. Autonomic hyperreflexia (in chronic injury above about T6) is triggered by a stimulus below the lesion (bladder or bowel distension, surgery), producing a massive sympathetic discharge: severe hypertension with a reflex bradycardia, headache, and flushing above the lesion with pallor below it. Succinylcholine is contraindicated after about 24–48 hours post-injury (hyperkalemia from receptor upregulation).

Prevent and treat hyperreflexia with deep general or neuraxial anesthesia (which blocks the afferent limb), removing the stimulus, and fast-acting vasodilators (nitroprusside, nicardipine). High lesions cause respiratory compromise and temperature dysregulation; use airway and cervical-spine precautions.
Hyperreflexia (lesion above T6): severe HTN + bradycardia from a stimulus below the lesion Avoid succinylcholine > 24–48 h post-SCI (hyperkalemia)

*Autonomic hyperreflexia (cord injury above T6) causes severe hypertension and bradycardia from stimuli below the lesion — block the afferent with deep or neuraxial anesthesia; avoid succinylcholine after 24–48 hours (hyperkalemia).*

cards.title: Cervical Spine Disease & Airway Management
secs: An unstable cervical spine (from trauma, rheumatoid atlantoaxial instability, or Down syndrome) calls for minimizing neck movement with manual in-line stabilization during intubation. Awake fiberoptic intubation is preferred for known instability or a difficult airway, and video laryngoscopy moves the neck less than direct laryngoscopy. Rheumatoid arthritis adds atlantoaxial subluxation, cricoarytenoid involvement, and temporomandibular joint disease — several airway concerns at once.
Assess and document the neurologic exam before and after. Cricoid pressure can worsen an unstable cervical spine, so balance the aspiration risk against the movement risk.
Manual in-line stabilization; awake fiberoptic for known instability

*For an unstable cervical spine (trauma, rheumatoid instability, Down syndrome) minimize movement with manual in-line stabilization and prefer awake fiberoptic or video laryngoscopy; rheumatoid disease adds multilevel airway risk.*

cards.title: Increased ICP from Tumors/Hematomas/Hydrocephalus
secs: Mass effect (from a tumor, hematoma, or abscess) or obstructed CSF flow (hydrocephalus) raises ICP, causing headache, vomiting, papilledema, an altered level of consciousness, and focal deficits. The anesthetic goal is to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure while avoiding further ICP rises: a smooth induction, avoiding hypercarbia, hypoxia, hypertension, and coughing, head-up positioning, judicious hyperventilation, and osmotherapy. Favor TIVA or no more than 1 MAC (high-dose volatiles uncouple flow and metabolism and raise cerebral blood flow); succinylcholine causes a small transient ICP rise.

Position to aid venous drainage (avoiding extreme neck flexion or rotation). Use ICP-raising vasodilators (nitroprusside, nitroglycerin) cautiously. Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) specifically reduce the VASOGENIC edema around brain TUMORS (e.g., glioblastoma); they do NOT help the cytotoxic edema of anoxic/ischemic injury and are not indicated for head injury or intracranial hemorrhage.

*Raised ICP from a mass or hydrocephalus demands maintaining cerebral perfusion pressure while avoiding further rises — a smooth induction, normo-to-mild hypocapnia, head-up positioning, osmotherapy, and TIVA or no more than 1 MAC over high-dose volatiles.*

cards.title: Sitting Position & Venous Air Embolism
secs: The sitting position (for posterior-fossa or cervical surgery) improves surgical access and venous drainage but risks venous air embolism, hypotension, pneumocephalus, and quadriplegia (from excess neck flexion). In venous air embolism, air is entrained through open non-collapsible venous sinuses above the heart, causing a sudden EtCO₂ drop, hypotension, and a mill-wheel murmur; precordial Doppler and TEE are the most sensitive monitors. Manage it by notifying the surgeon and flooding the field, giving 100% oxygen, stopping nitrous oxide, aspirating air through a right-atrial catheter, lowering the head, and supporting hemodynamics.
Screen preoperatively for a patent foramen ovale (the route to paradoxical embolism) and avoid nitrous oxide. Maintain an adequate venous and right-heart pressure to reduce entrainment.
Sitting VAE: sudden ↓ EtCO2 + hypotension; Doppler/TEE most sensitive

*The sitting position aids access but risks venous air embolism (a sudden EtCO₂ drop, hypotension, and a mill-wheel murmur) — monitor with Doppler or TEE, avoid nitrous oxide, screen for a PFO, and aspirate air through a right-atrial catheter.*

cards.title: Cerebral Aneurysms, AVM, Vasospasm
secs: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (the “worst headache of life”): before the aneurysm is secured, avoid hypertension and rebleeding with smooth, controlled hemodynamics (brief deliberate hypotension or adenosine for clipping if the surgeon requests it). Vasospasm peaks 4–14 days after the bleed and causes delayed cerebral ischemia; treat it with euvolemic induced hypertension, nimodipine (which improves outcome), and endovascular therapy. Arteriovenous malformations risk hemorrhage and, after resection, normal-perfusion-pressure breakthrough.

Nimodipine is given to all subarachnoid-hemorrhage patients for neuroprotection. Balance the risk of rebleeding (a lower blood pressure before securing) against ischemia (a higher pressure once vasospasm appears). For established vasospasm, oral nimodipine is the single proven outcome-improving therapy (alongside induced hypertension/euvolemia and endovascular treatment); hyperventilation, mannitol, and nitroprusside do not relieve the spasm.
Vasospasm peaks days 4–14; nimodipine improves outcome; treat with induced hypertension

*Before a ruptured aneurysm is secured, avoid hypertension (rebleeding); afterward, vasospasm (days 4–14) is treated with induced hypertension and oral nimodipine — the blood-pressure goal flips by phase.*

head: Grade
Findings
tables.note: Vasospasm peaks days 4–14 (nimodipine; maintain euvolemia/perfusion).

cards.title: Interventional Neuroradiology; Coils & Embolization
secs: Endovascular procedures (aneurysm coiling, AVM or tumor embolization, stroke thrombectomy) take place in a remote, radiation-exposed location with limited access to the patient. Key considerations are immobility (often general anesthesia), anticoagulation (heparin with ACT monitoring), the contrast load (nephrotoxicity and allergy), and rapid management of an intraprocedural rupture or thrombosis. For acute stroke thrombectomy, avoid hypotension to preserve perfusion of the penumbra (tight blood-pressure goals).

Be ready for an emergent rupture (reverse heparin with protamine and control the blood pressure) or a vessel occlusion. Apply the standard remote-location safety measures: full monitoring, available help, and an airway plan.

*Neuro-interventional cases (coiling, embolization, thrombectomy) are a remote anesthetizing location needing immobility, heparin/ACT management, contrast caution, and readiness for rupture or thrombosis — and avoid hypotension during stroke thrombectomy.*

cards.title: Trans-sphenoidal Hypophysectomy
secs: Pituitary tumors are resected through the nose and sphenoid sinus; consider the endocrine status — acromegaly (a difficult airway), Cushing’s disease, and hypopituitarism (give stress-dose steroids and thyroid hormone as needed). Intraoperatively, use an oral RAE tube and a throat pack, avoid large afterload swings, and aim for a smooth emergence (no bucking or venous congestion). The key postoperative complications are diabetes insipidus (polyuria, hypernatremia, and dilute urine) and a CSF leak.

Acromegaly brings an enlarged tongue and soft tissues and OSA, so anticipate a possible difficult mask or intubation. Monitor the urine output and sodium afterward for diabetes insipidus, and give desmopressin if it develops.
Watch postoperative diabetes insipidus (polyuria + hypernatremia); acromegaly → difficult airway

*Trans-sphenoidal pituitary surgery hinges on the endocrine status (acromegaly means a difficult airway, hypopituitarism needs stress steroids) and on the postoperative risks of diabetes insipidus and a CSF leak.*

cards.title: Deep Brain Stimulator Placement; Awake Craniotomy
secs: An awake craniotomy (for a tumor near eloquent cortex or for epilepsy mapping) and deep-brain-stimulator placement (for Parkinson’s or tremor) need an awake, cooperative, comfortable patient for functional testing. The techniques are “asleep-awake-asleep” or MAC with dexmedetomidine, propofol, and remifentanil, plus scalp blocks for analgesia, avoiding the oversedation that would impair testing. The airway is shared and limited (the head is fixed in pins), so plan for rescue.

For deep brain stimulation, hold sedatives that suppress the tremor or the test signals, and manage hypertension and the rare intracranial hemorrhage, air embolism, or seizure. Patient selection and rapport are essential.
Awake craniotomy/DBS need cooperative testing: scalp block + dexmedetomidine/light sedation

*An awake craniotomy and deep brain stimulation need a cooperative patient for functional testing — use scalp blocks with dexmedetomidine or light sedation, avoid oversedation, and plan airway rescue with the head pinned.*

cards.title: Cerebral Protection: Hypothermia & Drugs
secs: Hypothermia reduces CMRO₂ for BOTH electrical activity and basal cellular function (about 7% per °C), giving the broadest protection; mild hypothermia (32–34°C) is used selectively (such as targeted temperature management after cardiac arrest). Anesthetics (barbiturates, propofol) reduce only the electrical component (through burst suppression), so they protect against focal or incomplete — but not global — ischemia. Maintain perfusion (cerebral perfusion pressure), control glucose, and avoid hyperthermia (which worsens injury).

Avoid hyperglycemia and fever in anyone at risk of cerebral ischemia. Evidence for routine intraoperative hypothermia (for example, in aneurysm surgery — the IHAST trial) is largely negative; the targeted use is post-arrest.
Hypothermia ↓ CMRO2 ~7% per °C (electrical + basal); drugs only electrical (focal)

*Hypothermia lowers CMRO₂ about 7% per °C (both the electrical and basal components — the broadest protection), while barbiturates and propofol cut only the electrical component (focal ischemia) — and always avoid fever and hyperglycemia.*

cards.title: NMJ Transmission; ACh Synthesis & Release
secs: A nerve action potential causes a voltage-gated calcium influx at the motor nerve terminal, which releases acetylcholine (synthesized from choline and acetyl-CoA by choline acetyltransferase); it binds the postsynaptic nicotinic receptors, depolarizing the endplate and producing a muscle action potential and contraction. Acetylcholinesterase in the cleft rapidly hydrolyzes the acetylcholine, and the choline is recycled. There is a large “safety margin” (more receptors and acetylcholine than needed), so a substantial receptor block is required before clinical weakness appears.
Botulinum toxin blocks acetylcholine release (presynaptically); calcium, aminoglycosides, and magnesium affect the release. The neuromuscular junction is the target of the muscle relaxants.
Nerve AP → Ca2+ influx → ACh release → nicotinic receptor → endplate depolarization; AChE hydrolyzes ACh

*Nerve depolarization triggers a calcium-dependent acetylcholine release onto the postsynaptic nicotinic receptors (then acetylcholinesterase hydrolyzes it); a large safety margin means much of the receptor pool must be blocked before weakness appears.*

cards.title: Prejunctional & Postjunctional ACh Receptors
secs: The postjunctional (mature) nicotinic receptors (with 2 alpha, beta, delta, and epsilon subunits) mediate normal transmission; binding two acetylcholine molecules opens the channel. The prejunctional receptors mediate a positive feedback (mobilizing acetylcholine for sustained release), and their block underlies the FADE seen with non-depolarizing agents on tetanic and train-of-four stimulation. The extrajunctional (immature, gamma-subunit) receptors UPREGULATE with denervation, burns, and immobility, causing an exaggerated hyperkalemia with succinylcholine and a resistance to non-depolarizers.
Fade is a hallmark of a non-depolarizing (prejunctional) block. Receptor upregulation explains the succinylcholine dangers in burns and denervation.
Prejunctional receptor block → fade; extrajunctional (immature) upregulation → sux hyperkalemia

*The postjunctional nicotinic receptors transmit the signal while the prejunctional receptors feed back acetylcholine (their block causes fade); the extrajunctional immature receptors upregulate with denervation and burns → succinylcholine hyperkalemia and non-depolarizer resistance.*

cards.title: Multiple Sclerosis & Demyelinating Diseases
secs: Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune CNS demyelination with relapsing and remitting deficits; stress, infection, and HYPERTHERMIA worsen the symptoms (so avoid temperature elevation). Avoid SUCCINYLCHOLINE (a hyperkalemia risk from the upregulated receptors in weak or affected muscle); the non-depolarizer response is variable, and neuraxial use is debated (the spinal route is more often implicated than the epidural — use the lowest effective concentration). Document the baseline neurology and counsel about a possible perioperative relapse (not necessarily caused by the anesthesia).
Maintain normothermia. There is respiratory muscle weakness in advanced disease.
MS: avoid hyperthermia + succinylcholine; document baseline deficits

*In MS avoid hyperthermia (which worsens the demyelination symptoms) and succinylcholine (hyperkalemia); the responses to non-depolarizers vary and relapses can occur perioperatively — document the baseline neurology and maintain normothermia.*

cards.title: ALS & Motor Neuron Diseases
secs: ALS is a progressive degeneration of the upper and lower motor neurons, causing weakness, bulbar dysfunction (an aspiration risk), and respiratory failure. Avoid SUCCINYLCHOLINE (hyperkalemia from the denervation and receptor upregulation); the patient is SENSITIVE to non-depolarizers (use reduced doses and monitor) and to respiratory depressants. There is a high risk of aspiration and of postoperative respiratory failure or ventilator dependence.
Bulbar involvement means careful airway and aspiration management. Limit long-acting respiratory depressants and consider regional anesthesia.
ALS: avoid succinylcholine; sensitive to non-depolarizers + respiratory depressants

*ALS degenerates the motor neurons (with bulbar and respiratory weakness) — avoid succinylcholine (hyperkalemia), use reduced non-depolarizer doses, and anticipate aspiration and postoperative respiratory failure.*

cards.title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome
secs: Guillain-Barré syndrome is an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (often post-infectious) with an ascending weakness or paralysis, areflexia, respiratory failure, and AUTONOMIC INSTABILITY (a labile blood pressure and heart rate). Avoid SUCCINYLCHOLINE (hyperkalemia from the denervation); expect exaggerated responses to vasoactive drugs (from the autonomic dysfunction) and a sensitivity to non-depolarizers. Monitor respiratory function (the patient may need ventilation) and treat with IVIG or plasma exchange.
The autonomic dysfunction causes unpredictable hemodynamics (so dose vasopressors cautiously). Postoperative ventilatory support may be needed.
GBS: avoid succinylcholine (hyperkalemia); autonomic instability → labile hemodynamics

*Guillain-Barré (an ascending paralysis, areflexia, respiratory failure, and autonomic instability) → avoid succinylcholine (hyperkalemia) and expect labile hemodynamics and possible postoperative ventilation.*

cards.title: Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease
secs: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is the most common inherited peripheral neuropathy, with a distal motor and sensory weakness and atrophy (foot deformities, “stork legs”). It is generally SAFE with most anesthetics; succinylcholine is usually avoided (a theoretical hyperkalemia, though the reports are reassuring); titrate the non-depolarizers and monitor. Reports of sensitivity vary, and respiratory or bulbar involvement is possible in advanced disease.
It is not associated with malignant hyperthermia. Position carefully (the nerves are vulnerable), and regional anesthesia is generally acceptable.

*Charcot-Marie-Tooth (the most common inherited neuropathy) is generally anesthesia-safe; avoid succinylcholine by convention, titrate the non-depolarizers, and position carefully — it is not linked to malignant hyperthermia.*

cards.title: Muscular Dystrophies (Duchenne, Becker)
secs: X-linked dystrophin deficiency (Duchenne is severe, Becker milder) causes progressive weakness, cardiomyopathy, respiratory failure, and scoliosis. AVOID succinylcholine (which causes rhabdomyolysis and a hyperkalemic arrest) and the volatile agents (which cause rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, and MH-like reactions) — use total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA). There is cardiac (dilated cardiomyopathy, conduction defects) and respiratory (restrictive disease, a weak cough) compromise, and the patient is sensitive to respiratory depressants.

It is not classic malignant hyperthermia, but the volatiles and succinylcholine can trigger a fatal rhabdomyolysis and hyperkalemia. Assess the cardiac and pulmonary function, and monitor respiration postoperatively.
DMD: avoid succinylcholine + volatiles (rhabdo/hyperkalemia) → use TIVA

*Duchenne and Becker dystrophy (an X-linked dystrophin loss) → avoid succinylcholine AND the volatiles (rhabdomyolysis and hyperkalemic arrest) by using TIVA; expect a cardiomyopathy and restrictive respiratory disease.*

head: Concern
Detail
tables.note: Use TIVA; Duchenne patients are highest risk.

cards.title: Mitochondrial Myopathies
secs: Defective oxidative phosphorylation causes a multi-organ (muscle, brain, heart) energy failure, with a sensitivity to prolonged fasting and metabolic stress. Avoid prolonged fasting (give glucose-containing fluids), lactate-containing solutions (impaired lactate handling), and a prolonged tourniquet; the anesthetic sensitivity is variable. These patients are often sensitive to propofol (use caution with prolonged or high-dose infusions — there is a propofol-infusion-syndrome-like risk) and to the neuromuscular blockers.
Maintain normothermia, avoid metabolic stressors, and monitor the lactate and glucose. Dose the anesthetics cautiously.
*Mitochondrial myopathies impair oxidative phosphorylation → avoid prolonged fasting (give glucose) and lactated fluids, be cautious with propofol infusions, and minimize metabolic stress.*

cards.title: Myasthenia Gravis
secs: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disease with antibodies against the POSTsynaptic acetylcholine receptors, causing a fatigable weakness (ocular, bulbar, respiratory) that improves with rest and anticholinesterases. It is RESISTANT to succinylcholine (fewer receptors — a higher dose may be needed) but very SENSITIVE to non-depolarizers (small doses, titrate, and monitor); thymectomy is common. Plan for possible postoperative ventilation, manage the pyridostigmine, steroids, and IVIG, and distinguish a myasthenic crisis (weakness) from a cholinergic crisis (an excess of anticholinesterase).
Bulbar and respiratory weakness creates an aspiration and ventilatory risk. Regional and neuraxial techniques reduce the need for neuromuscular blockers.
MG (postsynaptic): resistant to sux, SENSITIVE to non-depolarizers; plan postop ventilation

*Myasthenia gravis (postsynaptic acetylcholine-receptor antibodies) is resistant to succinylcholine but very sensitive to non-depolarizers (small doses) — anticipate bulbar and respiratory weakness and possible postoperative ventilation.*

cards.title: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome
secs: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome has antibodies against the PREsynaptic voltage-gated CALCIUM channels (a paraneoplastic effect, from small cell lung cancer), reducing acetylcholine release; the weakness IMPROVES with repeated use (the opposite of myasthenia gravis), plus there are autonomic symptoms. It is SENSITIVE to BOTH depolarizing AND non-depolarizing relaxants (use reduced doses), and the anticholinesterases are less effective than in myasthenia gravis. Search for an underlying malignancy, and plan for a prolonged block and possible postoperative ventilation.
Contrast it with myasthenia gravis (postsynaptic, fatigues with use, resistant to succinylcholine). 3,4-diaminopyridine enhances the acetylcholine release.
LEMS (presynaptic Ca channel, paraneoplastic): sensitive to BOTH relaxants; improves with use

*Lambert-Eaton (presynaptic calcium-channel antibodies, paraneoplastic with small cell lung cancer) gives a weakness that improves with use and a sensitivity to BOTH depolarizing and non-depolarizing relaxants — the opposite of myasthenia gravis.*

cards.title: Myotonias & Periodic Paralyses
secs: Myotonic dystrophy causes a sustained contraction (a delayed relaxation) — AVOID succinylcholine (which triggers a generalized myotonia or masseter spasm and a “can’t ventilate, can’t intubate” situation); the patient is sensitive to respiratory and cardiac depressants and has cardiac conduction defects and weak respiratory muscles.
