The myotonia is also triggered by hypothermia, shivering, and the anticholinesterases (neostigmine) — so maintain normothermia, and remember that a contracted muscle may not relax with neuromuscular blockers. The periodic paralyses (hypo- or hyperkalemic) require avoiding the triggers (glucose/insulin or potassium shifts) and managing the potassium carefully.
Myotonic dystrophy is multisystem (cardiac, endocrine, GI), so it carries a high anesthetic risk. Keep the patient warm and avoid succinylcholine and shivering.
Myotonic dystrophy: avoid succinylcholine (myotonia) + cold/shivering; cardiac conduction risk

*Myotonic dystrophy → avoid succinylcholine (which provokes myotonia and jaw rigidity), keep the patient normothermic (cold and shivering trigger it), and watch the cardiac conduction; the periodic paralyses need careful potassium and trigger management.*

cards.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) — Anesthetic Implications
secs: ALS (Lou Gehrig disease) is a progressive degeneration of BOTH UPPER and LOWER MOTOR NEURONS — within the CEREBRAL CORTEX, the medullary/brainstem nuclei of the CRANIAL NERVES, and the ANTERIOR (ventral) HORN of the spinal cord. Proposed mechanisms include GLUTAMATE-mediated EXCITOTOXICITY and oxidative stress; riluzole (a glutamate-release inhibitor) modestly slows progression. It produces progressive weakness, muscle atrophy, fasciculations, bulbar dysfunction, and eventual respiratory failure, with sensation and cognition typically preserved.

Key anesthetic concerns: SUCCINYLCHOLINE is CONTRAINDICATED because denervation-related extrajunctional acetylcholine receptor upregulation causes exaggerated HYPERKALEMIA and risk of cardiac arrest. Patients show marked SENSITIVITY to NONDEPOLARIZING blockers, so use reduced doses with quantitative monitoring. Bulbar involvement raises ASPIRATION risk, and reduced respiratory reserve predisposes to postoperative ventilatory failure and often need for postoperative ventilation. There is no established malignant hyperthermia link, but avoid succinylcholine regardless.
ALS destroys upper and lower motor neurons — avoid succinylcholine (hyperkalemia) and titrate down nondepolarizers; watch for aspiration and respiratory failure.

cards.title: Barbiturates (Thiopental): Cerebral Effects
secs: Barbiturates such as thiopental and methohexital produce a DOSE-DEPENDENT reduction in cerebral metabolic rate (CMRO2) and, through intact flow-metabolism coupling, a parallel fall in cerebral blood flow (CBF), cerebral blood volume, and intracranial pressure. Cerebral AUTOREGULATION and CO2 reactivity are PRESERVED. An induction dose of thiopental reduces CMRO2 by roughly 30-50%. Maximal metabolic suppression coincides with an isoelectric/BURST-SUPPRESSION EEG (~55% reduction); beyond that no further decrease occurs because only the metabolism supporting electrical activity is suppressed, not basal cellular metabolism.

This is the basis for using barbiturates to lower ICP and for barbiturate coma in cerebral protection against focal ischemia. Thiopental lowers ICP, in contrast to volatile agents which uncouple flow from metabolism and cause cerebral vasodilation that can raise ICP at higher MAC. The trade-off is that thiopental also lowers MAP, which can reduce cerebral perfusion pressure despite the drop in ICP.
CPP = MAP - ICP (use CVP if it exceeds ICP). Thiopental CMRO2 reduction ~30-50% at induction; maximal ~55% at burst suppression.
Thiopental causes coupled, dose-dependent reductions in CMRO2 and CBF (30-50% at induction, maximal at burst suppression) while preserving autoregulation and CO2 reactivity.

cards.title: Ethosuximide for Absence Seizures
secs: ETHOSUXIMIDE is a well-tolerated, FIRST-LINE treatment for ABSENCE (petit mal) seizures. Its mechanism is inhibition of T-TYPE (low-threshold) CALCIUM CHANNELS in thalamic neurons and the cerebral cortex, interrupting the thalamocortical 3-Hz spike-and-wave oscillations that generate absence seizures. It is NOT effective against generalized tonic-clonic or focal seizures. Valproate is an alternative (preferred when generalized tonic-clonic seizures coexist), and lamotrigine is another option.

This highlights the distinct mechanism of absence seizures (T-type calcium channels) versus other seizure types. Sodium-channel-blocking drugs such as phenytoin and carbamazepine are ineffective and can WORSEN absence seizures. Ethosuximide side effects are mainly GI upset and drowsiness, with rare blood dyscrasias and Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
Ethosuximide, first-line for absence seizures, works by blocking thalamic T-type calcium channels that generate 3-Hz spike-and-wave activity.

cards.title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy: Anesthetic Considerations
secs: BECKER MUSCULAR DYSTROPHY (BMD) is an X-linked recessive dystrophinopathy caused by an in-frame mutation in the DYSTROPHIN gene, producing a reduced/partially functional dystrophin protein. It is the milder allelic cousin of DUCHENNE muscular dystrophy (DMD), which results from an out-of-frame mutation and near-absent dystrophin. BMD presents LATER and progresses more slowly, with proximal muscle weakness, calf pseudohypertrophy, and markedly elevated CREATINE KINASE. Like DMD, it carries a high burden of DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY and conduction disease, which can be the leading cause of death and may be disproportionate to the skeletal weakness.

The critical anesthetic concern is that dystrophinopathy patients are at risk for life-threatening RHABDOMYOLYSIS and HYPERKALEMIC CARDIAC ARREST. SUCCINYLCHOLINE is CONTRAINDICATED (massive potassium efflux from unstable sarcolemma). VOLATILE ANESTHETICS can trigger rhabdomyolysis and a hypermetabolic, malignant-hyperthermia-LIKE reaction, so a TIVA (propofol-based, nontriggering) technique is preferred. These patients are NOT classically MH-susceptible by the ryanodine-receptor mechanism, but the volatile-induced rhabdomyolysis reaction is clinically similar and must be avoided. Preop cardiac evaluation (ECHO, ECG) is essential given the cardiomyopathy risk, and postoperative respiratory monitoring is warranted because of weak respiratory muscles and sensitivity to nondepolarizers.
Becker (like Duchenne) is a dystrophinopathy with cardiomyopathy risk - AVOID succinylcholine and volatiles, use a nontriggering TIVA, and beware hyperkalemic arrest and rhabdomyolysis.

cards.title: Becker Muscular Dystrophy
secs: Becker muscular dystrophy (BMD) is an X-LINKED RECESSIVE dystrophinopathy caused by IN-FRAME mutations in the DYSTROPHIN gene (Xp21). Dystrophin is REDUCED in quantity/quality but STILL PRESENT, so the phenotype is MILDER and has LATER onset than Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), where out-of-frame mutations leave dystrophin essentially ABSENT. It manifests as slowly progressive, symmetric PROXIMAL weakness of the pelvic girdle and lower extremities, calf pseudohypertrophy, and elevated serum creatine kinase. Associated features can include epilepsy, macroglossia, and color blindness. Ambulation is typically preserved beyond age 16 (vs loss of ambulation ~age 12 in DMD).

CARDIAC involvement (dilated cardiomyopathy, conduction disease) is a leading cause of death and is often DISPROPORTIONATELY SEVERE relative to skeletal muscle weakness — cardiac disease can exceed that seen in DMD even though skeletal symptoms are milder. Obtain a preoperative ECG and echocardiogram. Like all dystrophinopathies, BMD patients are at risk for anesthesia-related RHABDOMYOLYSIS and HYPERKALEMIC cardiac arrest. AVOID SUCCINYLCHOLINE (risk of massive potassium release/rhabdomyolysis) and AVOID VOLATILE ANESTHETICS when possible (can trigger rhabdomyolysis; there is an MH-like susceptibility concern). Prefer a total intravenous anesthetic (TIVA) with propofol. Non-depolarizing NMBs show PROLONGED and exaggerated response, so titrate to train-of-four and consider sugammadex. Watch for respiratory muscle weakness, aspiration risk, and cardiac depression.
Becker = X-linked recessive with REDUCED-but-PRESENT dystrophin (milder than Duchenne) yet cardiac involvement may be WORSE — avoid succinylcholine and volatiles, use TIVA.

cards.title: SAFE-TBI: Albumin vs Saline in Traumatic Brain Injury
secs: The SAFE trial (Saline versus Albumin Fluid Evaluation) compared 4% albumin with 0.9% normal saline for ICU resuscitation and was overall neutral. The SAFE-TBI post-hoc subgroup analysis of TRAUMATIC BRAIN INJURY patients found that ALBUMIN resuscitation was associated with HIGHER MORTALITY than saline. Proposed mechanism: albumin leaks across the injured blood-brain barrier and is relatively hypo-osmolar, worsening cerebral edema and raising ICP. Therefore albumin is AVOIDED in TBI. Isotonic crystalloid (normal saline) is preferred; HYPOtonic fluids (D5W, and even lactated Ringer which is mildly hypotonic) are avoided because they worsen cerebral edema. HYPERTONIC SALINE (e.g., 3%) is valuable because it raises MAP while LOWERING ICP by osmotically drawing water out of brain tissue - an alternative to mannitol.

The goal is adequate cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP = MAP - ICP) while avoiding cerebral edema. Even a single episode of HYPOTENSION or HYPOXIA worsens TBI outcome. Hypertonic saline is especially favored in the hypotensive TBI patient because it simultaneously expands intravascular volume and reduces ICP, whereas mannitol can cause diuresis and hypovolemia.
CPP = MAP - ICP; typical CPP target ~60-70 mmHg. 3% hypertonic saline for ICP control; mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg as an osmotic alternative.
SAFE-TBI: albumin increases mortality in TBI - resuscitate with normal saline, avoid hypotonic fluids, and use hypertonic saline to raise MAP while lowering ICP.

cards.title: Hypokalemic Periodic Paralysis (HypoPP)
secs: HypoPP is a NONMYOTONIC skeletal muscle CHANNELOPATHY, most often from a mutation in the dihydropyridine (L-type) skeletal muscle CALCIUM channel (CACNA1S; a minority involve the SCN4A sodium channel). Inheritance is autosomal dominant. It presents before age 40 with episodic PROXIMAL muscle weakness or flaccid paralysis occurring during HYPOKALEMIA. Attacks are triggered by anything that shifts potassium INTO cells: carbohydrate-rich meals and INSULIN, rest after strenuous exercise, stress, cold, and states such as sleep deprivation, dehydration, alcohol, and infection. Respiratory and bulbar muscles are usually spared; episodes last hours.

The most important perioperative consideration is a possible association with MALIGNANT HYPERTHERMIA - avoid triggering agents (volatile anesthetics and succinylcholine), plan a nontriggering TIVA technique, and have dantrolene available. Additionally, avoid large glucose/insulin loads and other potassium-lowering triggers, keep the patient WARM, monitor potassium closely, and titrate nondepolarizing NMB carefully (effects may be prolonged). Contrast with HYPERkalemic periodic paralysis (SCN4A sodium channel; weakness with high/normal potassium, often with myotonia).

HypoPP = calcium-channel skeletal muscle channelopathy causing episodic proximal weakness with hypokalemia after carbs/insulin/rest; avoid glucose-insulin triggers, keep warm and normokalemic, and treat as a possible MH risk.

cards.title: Crystalloid vs Colloid
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Fluid Management (I.B.5)
secs: Crystalloids (normal saline, lactated Ringer’s, Plasma-Lyte) distribute through the whole extracellular space, so only about 25–33% stays in the vasculature — meaning you need roughly 3 times the lost volume. Colloids (albumin, starches) stay intravascular longer (less volume needed), but the starches (hetastarch) cause kidney injury and coagulopathy and have largely been abandoned. There is no consistent mortality benefit of colloid over crystalloid; albumin is reasonable in selected cases.
Balanced crystalloids may reduce hyperchloremic acidosis and acute kidney injury compared with large-volume normal saline. Use blood products for hemorrhage.
Crystalloid replacement ~3:1 vs blood loss (only ~25–33% stays intravascular)

*Crystalloids need about 3 times the lost volume (most leaves the vasculature); colloids last longer but starches harm the kidneys and coagulation with no clear mortality benefit, and balanced fluids reduce hyperchloremic acidosis.*

head: Colloid
Note
tables.note: Stay intravascular longer than crystalloid, but no proven outcome benefit.

cards.title: Fluid Deficit & Requirement Calculations
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Fluid Management (I.B.5)
secs: Maintenance fluids follow the 4-2-1 rule: 4 mL/kg/h for the first 10 kg, plus 2 mL/kg/h for the next 10 kg, plus 1 mL/kg/h for each kilogram above 20. The NPO deficit is the hourly maintenance rate times the hours spent fasting (the classic plan replaces about 50% in the first hour and 25% in each of the next two — though modern practice is less aggressive). Add ongoing losses (blood, third-space, and insensible). Per-kilogram maintenance is HIGHEST in infancy (~100 mL/kg/day ≈ 4 mL/kg/h, around 2 months) because of the high metabolic-rate-to-surface-area ratio; on day 1 of life fluids are deliberately restricted (~60–80 mL/kg/day), and per-kg needs fall steadily with age. Pediatric estimated blood volume (EBV): preterm neonate ~90–100, term neonate ~85–90, infant ~80, child ~70, adult male ~70, adult female ~65 mL/kg.

Goal-directed therapy (using stroke-volume or pulse-pressure variation) is increasingly replacing fixed formulas. Estimate the maximal allowable blood loss from the estimated blood volume and the hematocrit.

4-2-1 rule: 4 (1–10 kg) + 2 (11–20 kg) + 1 (> 20 kg) mL/kg/h Deficit = maintenance rate × hours NPO Maintenance peaks in infancy (~100 mL/kg/day at ~2 mo); day 1 of life restricted (~60–80 mL/kg/day) MABL = EBV × (Hctᵢ − Hct_target)/Hctᵢ  (2-kg preterm: 200 × (45−30)/45 ≈ 65 mL)

*Maintenance follows the 4-2-1 rule and the NPO deficit is the hourly rate times the hours fasted; per-kg needs peak in infancy, and you estimate allowable blood loss from blood volume and hematocrit — though goal-directed therapy is replacing fixed formulas.*

head: Weight
Rate
tables.note: NPO deficit = rate × hours; replace ~½ in the first hour.

cards.title: NS vs LR vs Plasma-Lyte vs D5W
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Fluid Management (I.B.5)
secs: Normal saline (0.9%) has sodium 154 and chloride 154 mEq/L, so large volumes cause a hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis; it is useful with hyponatremia or hypochloremia and for compatibility with blood. Lactated Ringer’s is more physiologic (sodium 130, potassium 4, calcium, and lactate that becomes bicarbonate) and is slightly hypotonic. Plasma-Lyte is balanced, isotonic, and calcium-free (so blood-compatible), while D5W is essentially free water for a free-water deficit or hypernatremia — NOT for resuscitation.

Balanced solutions (lactated Ringer’s, Plasma-Lyte) reduce hyperchloremic acidosis and possibly acute kidney injury compared with large-volume normal saline. Lactated Ringer’s contains potassium, a minor caution in renal failure or hyperkalemia.
NS Na/Cl 154/154 (hyperchloremic acidosis); LR Na 130 + K 4 + lactate; D5W = free water

*Large-volume normal saline causes a hyperchloremic acidosis; lactated Ringer’s and Plasma-Lyte are balanced (Plasma-Lyte is calcium-free for blood), and D5W is free water for hypernatremia, not resuscitation.*

head: Fluid
Na
Cl
Other
Osm
tables.note: NS in excess → hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis.

cards.title: Acid-Base Regulation; Buffers; Compensation
secs: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is pH = 6.1 + log([HCO₃] / (0.03 × PaCO₂)); the normal values are pH 7.40, PaCO₂ 40, and HCO₃ 24. The buffers are bicarbonate (the main extracellular one), hemoglobin, phosphate, and protein; the lungs handle CO₂ quickly and the kidneys handle bicarbonate and H⁺ slowly (over hours to days). For compensation, respiratory disorders get slow renal compensation and metabolic disorders get fast respiratory compensation — and compensation never fully normalizes the pH.

Winter’s formula for a metabolic acidosis: the expected PaCO₂ = 1.5 × HCO₃ + 8 ± 2. In an acute respiratory disorder the bicarbonate changes about 1 per 10 mmHg of PaCO₂, and in a chronic one about 4 per 10.
pH = 6.1 + log([HCO3] / (0.03 × PaCO2)); normal 7.40 / 40 / 24 Winter’s: expected PaCO2 = 1.5 × HCO3 + 8 ± 2

*A normal blood gas is 7.40/40/24; the lungs buffer CO₂ fast and the kidneys buffer bicarbonate slowly; check compensation with Winter’s formula (PaCO₂ = 1.5 × HCO₃ + 8 ± 2) — an over- or undershoot means a mixed disorder.*

head: Disorder
pH
Primary
Compensation
tables.note: Winter's: expected PaCO₂ = 1.5×HCO₃⁻ + 8 ± 2.

cards.title: Anion Gap; Strong Ion Difference
secs: The anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO₃), normally about 8–12 mEq/L (correct it for albumin by adding about 2.5 for every 1 g/dL below normal). A high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis follows MUDPILES: Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Iron or INH, Lactate, Ethylene glycol, and Salicylates. A normal-anion-gap (hyperchloremic) acidosis comes from diarrhea, renal tubular acidosis, and large-volume normal saline.

Stewart’s strong ion difference (the strong cations minus the strong anions, about 40) explains acid-base through the SID, weak acids, and PaCO₂ — for example, saline lowers the SID and causes acidosis. The delta-delta gap assesses whether more than one disorder is present.
Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3); normal ~8–12; MUDPILES for high gap SID ≈ 40 mEq/L (Stewart approach)

*The anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO₃), normally about 8–12 (albumin-corrected); high-gap causes follow MUDPILES while normal-gap includes saline, diarrhea, and RTA — and Stewart’s strong ion difference explains saline-induced acidosis.*

head: Cause
tables.note: Normal gap 8–12 mEq/L; correct for albumin.

cards.title: Alpha-stat vs pH-stat
secs: Blood gases are measured at 37°C, but hypothermia (as on cardiopulmonary bypass) changes the actual pH and PaCO₂. The alpha-stat approach manages the 37°C (uncorrected) values and adds NO CO₂, which keeps a constant intracellular charge and preserves cerebral autoregulation — favored in adults. The pH-stat approach corrects the values to the patient’s temperature and adds CO₂ to keep the pH at 7.4 at the cooler temperature, which raises cerebral blood flow (luxury perfusion) — favored in some pediatric and deep-hypothermic cases.

pH-stat increases cerebral blood flow (cooling the brain better but delivering more emboli), whereas alpha-stat preserves autoregulation. Many centers use pH-stat while cooling for deep hypothermic circulatory arrest.
Alpha-stat = uncorrected (37°C), preserves autoregulation pH-stat = temperature-corrected + add CO2, raises CBF

*On hypothermic bypass, alpha-stat manages uncorrected (37°C) gases and preserves autoregulation (adults), while pH-stat temperature-corrects and adds CO₂ to raise cerebral blood flow (some pediatric and deep-hypothermia cases).*

cards.title: GFR; Tubular Reabsorption & Secretion
secs: The kidneys receive about 20–25% of the cardiac output; the GFR is about 125 mL/min (about 180 L/day filtered), of which more than 99% is reabsorbed. The proximal tubule reabsorbs the bulk (sodium, glucose, bicarbonate, amino acids), the loop of Henle concentrates the urine (the countercurrent mechanism), and the distal and collecting tubules fine-tune under aldosterone (sodium and potassium) and ADH (water). The GFR is autoregulated (by myogenic and tubuloglomerular feedback) over a mean pressure of about 80–180 and is estimated by the creatinine clearance.
The afferent and efferent arteriolar tone sets the GFR (NSAIDs constrict the afferent, and ACE inhibitors dilate the efferent — which can drop the GFR). Creatinine lags behind an acute change in GFR.
GFR ~125 mL/min (~180 L/day); > 99% reabsorbed; autoregulated MAP ~80–180

*The GFR is about 125 mL/min (about 180 L/day filtered, more than 99% reabsorbed), autoregulated over a mean pressure of about 80–180; the proximal tubule does the bulk reabsorption while aldosterone (sodium and potassium) and ADH (water) fine-tune distally.*

head: Segment
Function
tables.note: Diuretic sites map onto these segments.

cards.title: Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System
secs: Low renal perfusion or sodium, or sympathetic tone, triggers renin (from the juxtaglomerular cells), which makes angiotensin I, converted (by ACE, in the lung) to angiotensin II. Angiotensin II causes potent vasoconstriction (especially of the efferent arteriole, maintaining the GFR), aldosterone release (sodium and water retention with potassium excretion), ADH release, and thirst — raising blood pressure and volume. Aldosterone acts on the distal nephron (sodium reabsorption, potassium and H⁺ excretion).

The drug targets are the ACE inhibitors, ARBs, direct renin inhibitors, and mineralocorticoid antagonists (spironolactone). The system is chronically activated in heart failure (hence the benefit of RAAS blockade).
Renin → Ang I → (ACE) Ang II → vasoconstriction + aldosterone (Na retention, K loss)

*The RAAS responds to low perfusion or sodium: renin → angiotensin II (vasoconstriction, maintaining the GFR via efferent tone) → aldosterone (sodium and water retention, potassium loss); it is the target of ACE-inhibitor, ARB, and MRA therapy.*

cards.title: Acid-Base, Water & Electrolyte Distribution
secs: Total body water is about 60% of body weight (two-thirds intracellular, one-third extracellular; the extracellular fluid is three-quarters interstitial and one-quarter plasma). The kidney regulates acid-base by reabsorbing filtered bicarbonate (proximally) and excreting H⁺ (as titratable acid and ammonium, distally) — a slow compensation over hours to days. Sodium sets the extracellular fluid volume and osmolality, while potassium is mainly intracellular and shifts with pH, insulin, and beta-agonists.

Hyperkalemia (peaked T waves, arrhythmia) is treated with calcium (to stabilize the membrane), insulin/glucose plus a beta-agonist (to shift potassium), then removal (diuresis, dialysis, or binders). Renal failure causes a metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, and volume overload.
TBW ~60%; ICF 2/3, ECF 1/3 (interstitial 3/4, plasma 1/4); kidney handles HCO3/H+ slowly

*Total body water is about 60% (two-thirds intracellular); the kidney regulates acid-base slowly (bicarbonate reabsorption and H⁺ excretion); sodium controls the extracellular volume and potassium shifts with pH and insulin — treat hyperkalemia with calcium, a shift, and removal.*

head: Treatment
Action
tables.note: ECG: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave → arrest.

cards.title: Diuretics: Mechanisms & Electrolyte Effects
secs: Loop diuretics (furosemide) block the Na-K-2Cl transporter in the thick ascending limb for a powerful diuresis, causing hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, a metabolic alkalosis, and ototoxicity. Thiazides block the Na-Cl transporter in the distal tubule, causing hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hyperCALCEMIA, and hyperglycemia and hyperuricemia. Potassium-sparing agents (spironolactone, an aldosterone antagonist; amiloride) cause hyperkalemia; carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (acetazolamide) cause a bicarbonate diuresis and metabolic acidosis; and osmotic agents (mannitol) reduce ICP and cerebral edema.
A loop-plus-thiazide combination is synergistic. Monitor potassium and volume, and use mannitol with caution in heart failure or anuria.

*Loops (Na-K-2Cl) cause hypokalemia, hypocalcemia, and alkalosis; thiazides (Na-Cl) lower potassium and sodium but raise calcium; potassium-sparing agents raise potassium; acetazolamide causes a bicarbonate-wasting acidosis; and mannitol is osmotic (for ICP).*

cards.title: Dopaminergic Drugs
secs: Dopamine is dose-dependent — at low (“renal”) doses it is dopaminergic (D1, causing splanchnic and renal vasodilation and natriuresis), at moderate doses beta-1 (inotropy), and at high doses alpha (vasoconstriction). “Renal-dose” dopamine does NOT prevent renal failure (it has no outcome benefit) and has been abandoned for renal protection. Fenoldopam is a selective D1 agonist (vasodilation and natriuresis) used for hypertensive emergencies, with debated renal protection.

Dopaminergic drugs also matter in Parkinson’s disease (continue levodopa; avoid D2 antagonists like metoclopramide, droperidol, and the antipsychotics). Dopamine antagonists can precipitate extrapyramidal effects.

*Dopamine is dose-dependent (D1 → beta-1 → alpha); “renal-dose” dopamine does NOT protect the kidneys; fenoldopam (a D1 agonist) treats hypertensive emergencies — and avoid D2 antagonists in Parkinson’s disease.*

cards.title: Acute Renal Failure Risk Factors
secs: Prerenal failure (from hypoperfusion — the most common and reversible early) comes from hypovolemia, hypotension, or low cardiac output; it shows a FENa under 1% and a BUN/creatinine ratio over 20. Intrinsic failure (acute tubular necrosis from ischemia or nephrotoxins — contrast, aminoglycosides, myoglobin) shows a FENa over 2% and muddy-brown casts; postrenal failure is from obstruction. The perioperative AKI risk factors are pre-existing chronic kidney disease, age, diabetes, sepsis, nephrotoxins, contrast, major/cardiac/aortic surgery, and hypotension.
Prevent it by maintaining perfusion and euvolemia and avoiding nephrotoxins, contrast, and hypotension. No drug reliably prevents AKI (not dopamine, not diuretics).
Prerenal: FENa < 1%, BUN/Cr > 20; intrinsic ATN: FENa > 2%, muddy-brown casts

*AKI is prerenal (hypoperfusion, FENa under 1%), intrinsic (acute tubular necrosis from ischemia or nephrotoxins, FENa over 2%), or postrenal; prevent it by maintaining perfusion and euvolemia and avoiding nephrotoxins and contrast — no drug reliably prevents it.*

head: Stage
Creatinine
Urine output
tables.note: Prerenal: BUN:Cr > 20, FeNa < 1%. Intrinsic (ATN): FeNa > 2%.

cards.title: Anesthesia in Renal Failure & Transplantation
secs: End-stage renal disease brings hyperkalemia (avoid succinylcholine if the potassium is elevated — it raises it by about 0.5–1), acidosis, volume overload, anemia, uremic platelet dysfunction (DDAVP helps), hypertension, and autonomic neuropathy. Drugs: avoid or adjust renally cleared agents and active metabolites (morphine to M6G, meperidine to normeperidine, gabapentin), and prefer cisatracurium (Hofmann elimination) and remifentanil (esterases). Protect the AV fistula (no blood-pressure cuff or IV in that arm) and time surgery after dialysis (but not when the patient is hypovolemic or hypokalemic).
For transplant, maintain graft perfusion (avoid hypotension), continue immunosuppression, and give mannitol or diuretics per the surgeon at reperfusion. Check the potassium before induction.
ESRD: avoid sux if hyperkalemic; use cisatracurium/remifentanil; protect the fistula

*End-stage renal disease brings hyperkalemia, acidosis, anemia, and uremic platelet dysfunction — check and treat the potassium (avoid succinylcholine if it is high), use cisatracurium and remifentanil, protect the fistula, and maintain graft perfusion in transplant.*

cards.title: Dialysis & Hemofiltration
secs: Hemodialysis (intermittent) gives diffusive clearance across a membrane for efficient solute and volume removal, but it can cause hypotension and disequilibrium — schedule surgery after dialysis (and assess the post-dialysis potassium and volume). Continuous renal replacement therapy (such as CVVH) is slower and better tolerated hemodynamically in the critically ill, but it requires anticoagulation (heparin or regional citrate). Peritoneal dialysis uses the peritoneum as the membrane.
Post-dialysis patients may be hypovolemic or hypokalemic, while pre-dialysis patients may be hyperkalemic or overloaded. Manage the anticoagulation, the access, and the electrolytes.

*Hemodialysis gives efficient intermittent clearance (operate after it, but watch for hypovolemia and hypokalemia); continuous renal replacement is slower and better tolerated in the unstable ICU patient (needing anticoagulation) — assess potassium and volume around the dialysis timing.*

cards.title: TURP Syndrome / Hyponatremia; Lithotripsy
secs: TURP syndrome is the absorption of hypotonic irrigation (glycine, sorbitol) through open prostatic venous sinuses, causing a dilutional HYPONATREMIA, fluid overload, and (from glycine) visual disturbance and hyperammonemia — leading to confusion, seizures, and cardiovascular changes. The risk rises with the resection time (over about 1 hour), a low sodium in the irrigation fluid, and a high bag height; regional anesthesia lets you detect mental-status changes (an early sign). Treat it by stopping surgery, supporting the airway and circulation, restricting fluids, giving a loop diuretic, and using hypertonic saline for severe symptomatic hyponatremia (correcting slowly — there is a risk of central pontine myelinolysis).

Bladder or prostate perforation causes shoulder or abdominal pain (which regional anesthesia helps detect). Lithotripsy (ESWL) uses shock waves to fragment stones — with cautions around pacemakers and ICDs, positioning, and arrhythmia, and it is avoided in pregnancy and coagulopathy.
TURP syndrome: hypotonic irrigation absorption → dilutional hyponatremia; correct slowly (CPM risk)

*TURP syndrome is a dilutional hyponatremia and fluid overload (with possible glycine neuro and visual effects) from absorbed irrigation — limit the resection time, watch the mental status (regional helps), and correct severe hyponatremia slowly to avoid central pontine myelinolysis.*

head: Feature
Detail
tables.note: Regional detects it early; limit resection time & irrigant height.

cards.title: Endothelial Glycocalyx; Fluid Responsiveness
secs: The endothelial glycocalyx is a protective luminal layer that regulates vascular permeability; it is damaged by inflammation, sepsis, ischemia-reperfusion, and aggressive fluid boluses (hypervolemia releases natriuretic peptides that shed it), causing capillary leak and edema. This revises the classic Starling model: a healthy glycocalyx limits transcapillary filtration, so protecting it favors judicious (not liberal) fluids. Fluid responsiveness is whether a bolus will increase the stroke volume (only about 50% of patients are responsive).

Assess responsiveness with DYNAMIC measures (stroke-volume or pulse-pressure variation in ventilated sinus-rhythm patients, a passive leg raise, a fluid challenge) rather than the static CVP. Avoid both hypovolemia and fluid overload.

*The endothelial glycocalyx limits capillary leak and is damaged by sepsis and fluid overload (favoring restrictive fluids); judge fluid responsiveness with dynamic measures (stroke-volume or pulse-pressure variation, a passive leg raise) — only about half of patients respond.*

head: Index
Threshold
tables.note: Dynamic indices need controlled ventilation, sinus rhythm, no spontaneous effort.

cards.title: Goal-Directed Therapy
secs: Goal-directed therapy titrates fluids and inotropes or vasopressors to physiologic targets (optimizing the stroke volume) rather than to fixed formulas. The dynamic indices — pulse-pressure variation and stroke-volume variation from the arterial waveform, esophageal Doppler, and pulse-contour devices — predict fluid responsiveness (a threshold of about 12–13%). Optimizing the flow and oxygen delivery may reduce complications in major surgery (it is part of ERAS).
The validity limits: it needs controlled mechanical ventilation, sinus rhythm, and an adequate tidal volume (no spontaneous breathing or arrhythmia). Avoid both under- and over-resuscitation.
PPV/SVV > ~12–13% predicts fluid responsiveness (ventilated, sinus rhythm)

*Goal-directed therapy titrates fluids and inotropes to stroke-volume targets using dynamic indices (pulse-pressure or stroke-volume variation over about 12–13%, esophageal Doppler) — valid only in ventilated, sinus-rhythm patients — to avoid over- and under-resuscitation.*

cards.title: Plasma Osmolality — Sodium as the Chief Determinant
secs: SODIUM (with its accompanying anions) is the MOST IMPORTANT determinant of plasma OSMOLALITY. Calculated osmolality is estimated from serum SODIUM, GLUCOSE, and BUN (urea): Osm ≈ 2 x [Na+] + [glucose]/18 + [BUN]/2.8 (mg/dL units), giving a normal value of about 275-295 mOsm/kg. Because sodium is doubled (to account for its anions), changes in [Na+] dominate the calculated value.

The measured-minus-calculated OSMOLAR GAP (normally <10) identifies unmeasured osmoles such as ethanol, methanol, ethylene glycol, or mannitol. Note that urea is an INEFFECTIVE osmole (it crosses cell membranes) so it raises measured osmolality without driving water shifts — that is why effective/tonicity calculations often omit BUN. Hyperglycemia and hyponatremia are the common clinical causes of abnormal calculated osmolality; each 100 mg/dL rise in glucose lowers measured Na by ~1.6-2.4 mEq/L.
Calculated Osm = 2 x Na + glucose/18 + BUN/2.8 (mg/dL). Normal ~275-295 mOsm/kg. Normal osmolar gap <10 mOsm/kg.
Sodium is the dominant determinant of plasma osmolality: Osm ≈ 2Na + glucose/18 + BUN/2.8; a high osmolar gap signals a toxic alcohol.

cards.title: ATN vs Prerenal Azotemia — Urinary Indices (FENa, UNa)
secs: In ISCHEMIC ACUTE TUBULAR NECROSIS (ATN) — e.g., after prolonged hypoperfusion such as AAA repair — the tubular cells are injured and CANNOT reabsorb sodium, so FRACTIONAL EXCRETION OF SODIUM (FENa) is typically GREATER THAN 1% (often >2-3%), urine sodium (UNa) is >40 mEq/L, and urine osmolality is low (<350 mOsm/kg, isosthenuric). In PRERENAL azotemia the tubules are intact and avidly RETAIN sodium in response to hypoperfusion, so FENa is <1%, UNa is <20 mEq/L, urine osmolality is high (>500 mOsm/kg), and the BUN:creatinine ratio is elevated (>20:1). See the comparison table (figure).

These indices help distinguish reversible prerenal states (fluid-responsive) from established ATN (which needs supportive management), guiding whether to give volume. Caveat: FENa is unreliable after DIURETICS (use FEUrea <35% instead, which stays low in prerenal states), and can be misleading with contrast nephropathy, CKD, or CHF. Prevent perioperative ATN by maintaining renal perfusion and avoiding nephrotoxins.
FENa = (UNa x PCr)/(PNa x UCr) x 100. ATN: FENa >1%, UNa >40 mEq/L, Uosm <350. Prerenal: FENa <1%, UNa <20 mEq/L, Uosm >500, BUN:Cr >20:1. On diuretics use FEUrea <35% = prerenal.
Ischemic ATN → FENa >1%, UNa >40 (tubules can't retain Na); prerenal → FENa <1%, UNa <20 (avid Na retention).

noteimg.cap: Laboratory differentiation of AKI: FENa, UNa, Uosm, and urine-to-plasma osmolar ratio in ATN vs prerenal azotemia

cards.title: Post-Transfusion Metabolic Alkalosis
secs: The CITRATE in transfused blood is metabolized by the LIVER, and each citrate molecule generates BICARBONATE. After MASSIVE TRANSFUSION this citrate-derived bicarbonate load can produce a delayed METABOLIC ALKALOSIS. Normally the kidneys excrete the excess bicarbonate, but in patients with impaired RENAL function the bicarbonate is retained, unmasking or worsening the alkalosis. (By contrast, in the acute setting, citrate chelation of calcium plus lactic acid from stored blood and hypoperfusion can cause acidosis; the alkalosis is a later, metabolism-dependent phenomenon.)

This explains a delayed metabolic alkalosis after large-volume transfusion, exaggerated in renal failure and dependent on adequate hepatic function to convert citrate. Alkalosis shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve LEFT (impairing oxygen offloading) and lowers ionized calcium.
Each mmol of citrate is metabolized to roughly 3 mmol of bicarbonate.
Hepatic metabolism of transfused citrate generates bicarbonate, so after massive transfusion, especially with renal impairment that cannot excrete it, a delayed metabolic alkalosis develops.

cards.title: Acetazolamide: Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibition and Metabolic Acidosis
secs: ACETAZOLAMIDE is a potent CARBONIC ANHYDRASE INHIBITOR acting in the PROXIMAL TUBULE, where carbonic anhydrase normally drives BICARBONATE reabsorption. Inhibition causes urinary WASTING of SODIUM and BICARBONATE, producing an alkaline urine and a HYPERCHLOREMIC (normal anion gap) METABOLIC ACIDOSIS. Because bicarbonate is lost, chloride is retained to preserve electrical neutrality. It also produces mild diuresis and can cause HYPOKALEMIA as increased distal sodium delivery enhances potassium secretion.

Uses include GLAUCOMA (reduced aqueous humor formation), ACUTE MOUNTAIN SICKNESS prophylaxis (the induced metabolic acidosis stimulates ventilation and offsets the respiratory alkalosis of altitude), IDIOPATHIC INTRACRANIAL HYPERTENSION (reduced CSF production), and to alkalinize urine. In anesthesia it can be used to correct the METABOLIC ALKALOSIS from loop/thiazide diuresis or post-hypercapnic states. Being a SULFONAMIDE, it carries cross-reactivity risk in sulfa allergy.

Reaction catalyzed: CO<sub>2</sub> + H<sub>2</sub>O <-> H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> <-> H<sup>+</sup> + HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>. Acetazolamide produces a NORMAL anion gap (hyperchloremic) acidosis; typical dosing 250-500 mg.

Acetazolamide inhibits proximal-tubule carbonic anhydrase, wasting sodium and bicarbonate to cause a hyperchloremic (normal anion gap) metabolic acidosis, which is exactly why it helps at altitude and in metabolic alkalosis.

cards.title: Tourniquet-Induced Ischemic Myonecrosis & Rhabdomyolysis
secs: Prolonged pneumatic TOURNIQUET inflation produces distal limb ISCHEMIA. Muscle can tolerate only about 2 hours of continuous ischemia before ISCHEMIC MYONECROSIS begins; most guidelines limit inflation to 90-120 minutes and recommend a 10-15 minute reperfusion interval before reinflation. Ischemic muscle undergoes anaerobic metabolism, ATP depletion, and failure of the Na/K-ATPase and Ca-ATPase pumps. On reperfusion (tourniquet deflation), damaged myocytes leak their intracellular contents into the circulation, producing RHABDOMYOLYSIS with elevated CREATINE KINASE (CK) and MYOGLOBINEMIA. Myoglobin is filtered and precipitates in tubules, causing pigment-induced ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY, especially in a volume-depleted, acidic patient.

The classic laboratory picture of rhabdomyolysis reflects release of INTRACELLULAR contents plus renal injury: HYPERKALEMIA (can be life-threatening and arrhythmogenic), HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA, HYPERURICEMIA, and early HYPOCALCEMIA (calcium deposits in injured muscle), along with a markedly elevated CK and an anion-gap METABOLIC ACIDOSIS. Reperfusion also washes out accumulated CO2, lactate, and potassium, which can cause a transient DROP in end-tidal CO2 correction, acidosis, and hemodynamic changes at deflation. Management is aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to maintain urine output, treat hyperkalemia, and consider urinary alkalinization; watch the EKG for peaked T waves. Prevention centers on limiting tourniquet time and inflation pressure (typically 100 mmHg above SBP for the lower limb, 50-75 mmHg for the upper limb).

Safe tourniquet time ~90-120 min; reperfusion break 10-15 min. Rhabdo suggested by CK > ~5x upper limit (often > 1000-5000 U/L). Lower-extremity cuff pressure ~ SBP + 100 mmHg; upper-extremity ~ SBP + 50-75 mmHg.
Prolonged tourniquet use causes ischemic myonecrosis and rhabdomyolysis on reperfusion: expect HYPERKALEMIA, hyperphosphatemia, hyperuricemia, hypocalcemia, high CK, and myoglobinuric AKI.

cards.title: Sodium Deficit & Correction of Hyponatremia
secs: The SODIUM DEFICIT estimates how much sodium is needed to reach a target: Sodium deficit (mEq) = (140 - measured serum Na) x TOTAL BODY WATER, where TBW = weight(kg) x 0.6. Roughly 50% of the deficit is corrected during the FIRST 24 hours. Symptomatic patients with severe hyponatremia (Na <120, e.g., seizures or obtundation) are treated with 3% HYPERTONIC SALINE, and the infusion rate should generally NOT exceed ~100 mL/hr. Determine whether hyponatremia is acute vs chronic and hypo-, eu-, or hypervolemic to guide therapy.

The key safety limit is avoiding OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME (central pontine myelinolysis) from over-rapid correction of CHRONIC hyponatremia: do not raise serum sodium by more than ~8-10 mEq/L in 24 hours (use a stricter ~6-8 mEq/L in high-risk patients: alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia, liver disease). Conversely, correcting HYPERnatremia too fast causes cerebral edema. Reassess sodium frequently during correction.

Na deficit = (140 - Na) x (0.6 x kg); TBW = 0.6 x kg (use ~0.5 in women/elderly). Replace ~50% in the first 24 h. For symptomatic Na <120: 3% saline at <=100 mL/hr. Max correction ~8-10 mEq/L per 24 h.
Na deficit = (140 - Na) x (0.6 x weight); replace about half in 24 h, use 3% saline (<=100 mL/hr) for symptomatic Na <120, and never raise Na >8-10 mEq/L/day to avoid central pontine myelinolysis.

cards.title: Anticoagulants, Antithrombotics, Antiplatelets
secs: Heparin (which potentiates antithrombin; monitored by the aPTT or ACT; reversed with protamine; with a risk of HIT); low-molecular-weight heparin (anti-Xa; partially reversed); and fondaparinux (anti-Xa). Warfarin (which affects the vitamin K-dependent factors II, VII, IX, X; monitored by the INR; reversed with vitamin K or 4-factor PCC). The DOACs: dabigatran (a direct thrombin inhibitor, reversed by idarucizumab) and apixaban or rivaroxaban (factor Xa inhibitors, reversed by andexanet or PCC). The antiplatelets: aspirin (an irreversible COX-1 inhibitor), the P2Y12 inhibitors (clopidogrel, ticagrelor, prasugrel), and the GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors.

HIT is an immune reaction with a platelet drop at about 5–10 days and paradoxical thrombosis — stop the heparin and use a non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban, bivalirudin). Follow the ASRA timing for neuraxial procedures.
Heparin→protamine; warfarin→vit K/PCC; dabigatran→idarucizumab; Xa inhibitors→andexanet/PCC

*Know the anticoagulant targets and their reversals (heparin → protamine, warfarin → vitamin K or PCC, dabigatran → idarucizumab, Xa inhibitors → andexanet or PCC) and the antiplatelets (aspirin, P2Y12); suspect HIT with a platelet drop at 5–10 days plus thrombosis.*

cards.title: Alternatives to Transfusion
secs: Preoperatively: treat anemia (iron, erythropoietin) and consider autologous predonation. Intraoperatively: acute normovolemic hemodilution (removing blood, replacing it with crystalloid or colloid, then retransfusing later), cell salvage (washing and returning red cells — traditionally avoided with malignancy or infection), controlled hypotension, antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid), and meticulous hemostasis. Restrictive transfusion thresholds (a hemoglobin of about 7 g/dL in most stable patients) reduce exposure.

This planning is important for Jehovah’s Witnesses (discuss the acceptable options preoperatively) and to limit the risks of transfusion. Cell salvage lacks platelets and clotting factors after large volumes.
Restrictive transfusion threshold ~7 g/dL; ANH, cell salvage, EPO/iron, TXA

*Reduce transfusion with preoperative iron and erythropoietin, acute normovolemic hemodilution, cell salvage, antifibrinolytics, and a restrictive threshold (about 7 g/dL) — essential planning for Jehovah’s Witnesses.*

cards.title: Immunosuppressive & Anti-rejection Drugs
secs: The calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) cause nephrotoxicity, hypertension, neurotoxicity, and P450 interactions; there are also the mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus), the antimetabolites (azathioprine, mycophenolate), and corticosteroids. Continue the immunosuppression perioperatively (to avoid rejection), maintain stress-dose steroids, and use strict asepsis (because of the infection risk). Azathioprine mildly antagonizes a non-depolarizing block, while the calcineurin inhibitors can potentiate it.
Watch for organ toxicity (renal, from the calcineurin inhibitors) and for interactions. Transplant recipients have blunted infection signs and chronic comorbidity.

*Continue immunosuppressants perioperatively (calcineurin inhibitors — nephrotoxic and hypertensive — mTOR inhibitors, antimetabolites, steroids) with stress-dose steroids and strict asepsis; the calcineurin inhibitors are nephrotoxic and interact via P450.*

cards.title: Blood Preservation, Storage & Components
secs: Stored red cells use an anticoagulant-preservative (CPDA or additive solutions) for up to about 42-day storage; the “storage lesion” is a decreased 2,3-DPG (a left shift), increased potassium and lactate, a decreased pH, and microaggregates. The components: packed red cells (to raise the hemoglobin), platelets (stored at room temperature for about 5 days, with a bacterial risk), fresh frozen plasma (all the coagulation factors), and cryoprecipitate (fibrinogen, factor VIII, von Willebrand factor, factor XIII). One unit of packed red cells raises the hemoglobin by about 1 g/dL (the hematocrit by about 3%); platelet and plasma dosing is by weight.

Leukoreduction reduces febrile reactions, CMV, and alloimmunization; irradiation prevents graft-versus-host disease. Older blood has a higher potassium (a caution in neonates, renal failure, and massive transfusion).
1 unit PRBC → Hb ~+1 g/dL; storage lesion: ↓2,3-DPG, ↑K, ↓pH; cryo = fibrinogen/VIII/vWF/XIII

*Stored red cells (up to about 42 days) develop the storage lesion (low 2,3-DPG, high potassium); one unit raises the hemoglobin by about 1 g/dL; fresh frozen plasma has all the factors and cryoprecipitate has fibrinogen, factor VIII, von Willebrand factor, and factor XIII — irradiate to prevent graft-versus-host disease.*

head: Component
Effect
tables.note: Give cryo when fibrinogen < 150–200 mg/dL.

cards.title: Type & Cross vs Type & Screen; Uncrossmatched
secs: A type and screen is ABO and Rh typing plus an antibody screen (no unit is reserved) — for low-bleeding-risk cases. A type and crossmatch is full compatibility testing with reserved units — for expected significant blood loss. In an emergency, give uncrossmatched O-negative red cells (the universal donor; O-positive is acceptable for males and non-childbearing women), and AB plasma is the universal donor for plasma.
The crossmatch detects incompatibility the screen might miss, and an emergency release trades safety for speed. ABO incompatibility (a clerical error) causes an acute hemolytic reaction.
Emergency: O-neg RBCs (universal); AB plasma universal donor; type&screen vs type&cross

*A type and screen (typing plus an antibody screen, no units held) versus a type and crossmatch (units reserved for expected loss); in emergencies give O-negative red cells (AB plasma is the universal plasma donor) — and ABO clerical errors cause fatal hemolysis.*

head: Test
Use
tables.note: Electronic crossmatch is fast when the screen is negative.

cards.title: Transfusion Reactions: Febrile, Allergic, Hemolytic
secs: An acute hemolytic reaction (from ABO incompatibility, usually a clerical error) causes fever, hypotension, hemoglobinuria, DIC, and flank pain — and under anesthesia, unexplained hypotension, bleeding, or hemoglobinuria means STOP, support, and maintain urine output; it is the most dangerous reaction. A febrile non-hemolytic reaction (from cytokines and leukocytes) is common and benign — give antipyretics, and it is reduced by leukoreduction. An allergic reaction (urticaria progressing to anaphylaxis, especially in IgA-deficient patients) is treated with antihistamines for mild cases and epinephrine for anaphylaxis.
Always stop the transfusion, recheck the identity, and send labs (a direct antiglobulin test, a repeat type). Delayed hemolytic reactions occur days later (from anamnestic antibodies).

*Acute hemolytic reactions (an ABO clerical error) are the deadliest — fever, hypotension, hemoglobinuria, and DIC → stop and support; febrile non-hemolytic reactions are common and benign (leukoreduction helps); allergic reactions range up to anaphylaxis (especially in IgA deficiency).*

cards.title: Citrate Intoxication
secs: Citrate (the anticoagulant in stored blood) chelates calcium, so rapid or massive transfusion (or impaired hepatic citrate metabolism — in liver transplant, hypothermia, or neonates) causes HYPOCALCEMIA (and hypomagnesemia). Hypocalcemia causes hypotension, decreased contractility, a prolonged QT, coagulopathy, and perioral tingling. Treat it with IV calcium (chloride or gluconate) and monitor the ionized calcium during massive or rapid transfusion.

The liver normally metabolizes citrate rapidly, so problems arise only with high transfusion rates or liver dysfunction. Citrate also causes a later metabolic alkalosis (as it is metabolized to bicarbonate).
Citrate chelates Ca → hypocalcemia (hypotension, ↓ contractility, long QT); give calcium

*Citrate in stored blood chelates calcium, so rapid or massive transfusion (or liver dysfunction) causes hypocalcemia (hypotension, a long QT, coagulopathy) — monitor the ionized calcium and give IV calcium.*

cards.title: TRALI & TACO
secs: TRALI (transfusion-related acute lung injury) is non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema within about 6 hours (from donor anti-leukocyte antibodies), causing hypoxemia, bilateral infiltrates, and NORMAL filling pressures — a leading cause of transfusion-related death; the treatment is supportive (lung-protective ventilation). TACO (transfusion-associated circulatory overload) is hydrostatic, cardiogenic pulmonary edema from volume overload, causing hypertension and elevated filling pressures — and it responds to diuresis and slowing the transfusion. Distinguish them by the volume status and filling pressures and the blood pressure (TRALI normal or low, TACO high).

TRALI is supportive with no diuretics needed; using male-predominant plasma donors reduces the risk. TACO needs a slow transfusion and diuretics; it is a higher risk in cardiac, renal, and elderly patients. Because both cause hypoxemia and pulmonary edema, use the distinguishing features: a transient LEUKOPENIA/neutropenia and a normal or low blood pressure favor TRALI, whereas HYPERTENSION, an elevated BNP, and a diuretic response favor TACO.
TRALI: non-cardiogenic edema < 6 h, normal filling pressures; TACO: volume overload, high pressures

*TRALI is non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (within 6 hours, normal filling pressures — supportive care) and a top cause of transfusion death; TACO is volume-overload edema (high pressures — diuresis and a slow transfusion).*

cards.title: Massive Transfusion: Coagulopathy & Hypothermia
secs: Massive transfusion (more than about 10 units in 24 hours, or one blood volume) causes a dilutional thrombocytopenia and coagulopathy, hypothermia, citrate-induced hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia (from old blood), and acidosis. The “lethal triad” is hypothermia plus acidosis plus coagulopathy (each worsening the others). Use balanced ratios (about 1:1:1 of red cells to plasma to platelets), warm all fluids and blood, give calcium, and use viscoelastic-guided correction.
Use permissive hypotension and early hemorrhage control in trauma. Monitor the ionized calcium, potassium, temperature, and coagulation.
Massive transfusion: 1:1:1 ratio, warm everything, give calcium; lethal triad = hypothermia+acidosis+coagulopathy

*Massive transfusion causes a dilutional coagulopathy, hypothermia, hypocalcemia, and the lethal triad (hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy) — transfuse balanced 1:1:1, warm everything, and give calcium.*

cards.title: Transfusion-Transmitted Infections
secs: Modern nucleic-acid testing has made viral transmission very rare; the current residual risk is lowest for HIV and hepatitis C (about 1 in a million or less) and somewhat higher for hepatitis B. Bacterial contamination is highest in PLATELETS (because of their room-temperature storage) and is now a leading infectious risk, with possible sepsis. CMV is transmitted via leukocytes, so use leukoreduced or CMV-negative blood for at-risk patients (the immunocompromised, neonates, and in pregnancy).
The emerging and other agents are West Nile, Zika, Chagas, and (theoretically) prions. Leukoreduction reduces CMV and febrile reactions.

*Viral transmission (HIV and hepatitis C about 1 in a million, hepatitis B higher) is now rare with nucleic-acid testing; bacterial contamination of platelets is a leading infectious risk — use leukoreduced or CMV-negative blood for the immunocompromised, neonates, and pregnancy.*

cards.title: Graft-vs-Host Disease & Immunosuppression
secs: Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease is when donor lymphocytes attack the recipient (the skin, gut, marrow, and liver), and it is usually fatal; it occurs in immunocompromised patients and when the donor and recipient share an HLA haplotype (in relatives). PREVENT it with IRRADIATION of cellular blood products (which inactivates the donor lymphocytes) for at-risk recipients (the immunocompromised, neonates, intrauterine transfusions, and directed donations from relatives). Leukoreduction alone is NOT sufficient to prevent it.

The symptoms appear about 1–6 weeks after transfusion, and a refractory pancytopenia is characteristic. It is the same mechanism as transplant graft-versus-host disease, but transfusion-driven. Washed, Rh-negative, or directed-donor units do NOT prevent it — only irradiation does; directed donations from blood relatives actually raise the risk (the shared HLA haplotype).

*Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (donor lymphocytes attacking the host) is usually fatal in immunocompromised patients or with a shared HLA (relatives) — prevent it with IRRADIATED cellular products (leukoreduction is not enough).*

cards.title: Anemias & Compensatory Mechanisms
secs: Anemia reduces the oxygen-carrying capacity; the compensations are an increased cardiac output, a rightward shift of the oxygen curve (via 2,3-DPG), increased extraction, and flow redistribution. Classify it by the MCV: microcytic (iron deficiency, thalassemia), normocytic (acute blood loss, chronic disease, hemolysis), or macrocytic (B12 or folate deficiency). A restrictive transfusion threshold (about 7 g/dL) suits most stable patients, with a higher threshold for active ischemia or ongoing bleeding.
Chronic anemia is better tolerated (it is compensated) than acute anemia. Optimize iron, B12, and folate, and treat the cause preoperatively.

*Anemia is compensated by an increased cardiac output, a right-shifted oxygen curve (2,3-DPG), and higher extraction; classify it by the MCV, and transfuse most stable patients only below about 7 g/dL.*

head: MCV
Type
Causes
tables.note: Reticulocyte count separates production from loss.

cards.title: Polycythemia
secs: Primary polycythemia (polycythemia vera) is a clonal JAK2 marrow disorder causing an elevated red-cell mass, hyperviscosity, thrombosis, AND a paradoxical bleeding (from platelet dysfunction); it is treated with phlebotomy, hydroxyurea, and aspirin. Secondary polycythemia is an appropriate erythropoietin response to chronic hypoxia (high altitude, COPD, cyanotic heart disease, smoking) or an inappropriate erythropoietin (from tumors). Hyperviscosity raises the thrombosis risk, so optimize the patient perioperatively (phlebotomy to a target hematocrit) and hydrate.
Cyanotic congenital heart disease causes a compensatory secondary polycythemia. Balance the thrombosis against the bleeding risk.

*Polycythemia is primary (polycythemia vera, JAK2 — thrombosis plus bleeding, treated with phlebotomy and hydroxyurea) or secondary (hypoxia-driven erythropoietin); hyperviscosity raises the thrombosis risk — phlebotomize toward a target hematocrit and hydrate preoperatively.*

cards.title: Thrombocytopenia & Thrombocytopathy
secs: Thrombocytopenia is from decreased production (a marrow problem), increased destruction (ITP, TTP/HUS, HIT, DIC), or sequestration (splenomegaly). The general thresholds: about 50,000 for surgery, about 70–80,000 for a neuraxial procedure, and spontaneous bleeding below about 10–20,000. Thrombocytopathy (a normal count but abnormal function) comes from uremia (DDAVP helps), antiplatelet drugs, von Willebrand disease, and cardiopulmonary bypass.
In TTP, do NOT transfuse platelets (it worsens the microthrombi) — use plasma exchange. In HIT, stop the heparin, avoid platelets, and use a non-heparin anticoagulant.
Platelets: > 50k surgery, > 70–80k neuraxial; TTP/HIT → don’t transfuse platelets

*Thrombocytopenia is from decreased production, destruction (ITP, TTP, HIT, DIC), or sequestration; aim for over 50,000 for surgery and over 70–80,000 for neuraxial — but don’t give platelets in TTP or HIT, and uremic platelet dysfunction responds to DDAVP.*

cards.title: Congenital & Acquired Factor Deficiencies
secs: Hemophilia A (factor VIII) and B (factor IX) are X-linked, with a prolonged aPTT but a normal PT and platelets; treat with factor concentrates (and DDAVP for mild hemophilia A), and avoid intramuscular injections. Von Willebrand disease (the most common inherited bleeding disorder) is defective platelet adhesion plus a low factor VIII; treat it with DDAVP (type 1) or von Willebrand factor and VIII concentrates. The acquired causes are vitamin K deficiency or warfarin (affecting II, VII, IX, X), liver disease, and acquired inhibitors.

Correct the factor levels before surgery or a neuraxial procedure, and involve hematology. DDAVP releases stored von Willebrand factor and factor VIII (working in mild hemophilia A and type 1 von Willebrand disease).
Hemophilia: ↑ aPTT, normal PT; vWD most common inherited; DDAVP for mild A / type 1 vWD

*Hemophilia A and B (X-linked, a prolonged aPTT) are treated with factor concentrates (DDAVP for mild A); von Willebrand disease (the most common inherited bleeding disorder) responds to DDAVP or von Willebrand factor concentrates — correct the levels before surgery.*

head: Disorder
Defect
Treatment
tables.note: vWD is the most common inherited bleeding disorder; avoid DDAVP in type 2B.

cards.title: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
secs: DIC is a systemic activation of coagulation (from sepsis, trauma, obstetric emergencies, or malignancy) that consumes platelets and factors, causing simultaneous thrombosis AND bleeding. The labs show low platelets, low fibrinogen, a prolonged PT and aPTT, an elevated D-dimer and fibrin degradation products, and schistocytes. Treat the UNDERLYING CAUSE and support with platelets, fresh frozen plasma, and cryoprecipitate (for fibrinogen) as needed for bleeding.
Use heparin only in selected thrombosis-predominant cases. Obstetric DIC (from abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, or retained products) is a key scenario.
DIC: ↓ platelets, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ PT/aPTT, ↑ D-dimer; treat the cause + replace factors

*DIC (from sepsis, trauma, or obstetric causes) consumes platelets and factors, causing both thrombosis and bleeding (low platelets and fibrinogen, a high D-dimer) — treat the underlying cause and replace platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate for bleeding.*

head: Lab
Direction
tables.note: Treat the underlying cause; replace with FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets.

cards.title: Hemoglobinopathies & Porphyrias
secs: Sickle cell disease: HbS polymerizes when deoxygenated, causing vaso-occlusion and crises; AVOID hypoxia, hypothermia, acidosis, dehydration, and stasis, and maintain oxygenation, warmth, hydration, and analgesia, transfusing per indication. The thalassemias are imbalanced globin chains causing anemia and iron overload, with airway issues from marrow expansion in severe forms. Acute porphyrias: avoid the triggers (barbiturates, etomidate, certain drugs) that induce ALA synthase and precipitate an attack (pain, autonomic instability, neuropsychiatric features); use the safe agents (propofol, opioids).
In sickle cell disease, keep the patient warm, well-oxygenated, and hydrated; the use of tourniquets is debated. In porphyria, thiopental and etomidate are contraindicated, while propofol is safe.
Sickle: avoid hypoxia/cold/acidosis/dehydration/stasis; porphyria: avoid barbiturates/etomidate
*In sickle cell disease avoid hypoxia, cold, acidosis, dehydration, and stasis; in acute porphyria avoid barbiturates and etomidate (which induce ALA synthase) — propofol and opioids are safe.*

cards.title: Massive Transfusion Protocol; RBC:Plasma Ratios
secs: A massive transfusion protocol delivers blood products rapidly in fixed ratios to treat hemorrhagic shock and prevent a dilutional coagulopathy. Balanced resuscitation (about 1:1:1 of red cells to plasma to platelets) approximates whole blood and improves outcomes (the PROPPR trial) versus red-cell-heavy strategies. The adjuncts are early tranexamic acid, calcium (for the citrate-induced hypocalcemia), warming, and damage-control or permissive hypotension until hemorrhage control.
Whole blood is increasingly used in trauma. Viscoelastic testing can further guide targeted component therapy.
Balanced MTP ~1:1:1 (RBC:plasma:platelets); early TXA; give calcium; keep warm

*Massive transfusion protocols use balanced (about 1:1:1 of red cells to plasma to platelets) resuscitation (PROPPR) to prevent a dilutional coagulopathy, plus early tranexamic acid, calcium, and warming — with damage-control resuscitation until the bleeding is controlled.*

head: Problem
Effect
tables.note: Transfuse PRBC:FFP:platelets ≈ 1:1:1; treat the lethal triad.

cards.title: Coagulopathy of Hemorrhagic Shock
secs: Trauma-induced coagulopathy develops early (before any dilution) from tissue injury plus shock, which activates protein C and damages the endothelium and glycocalyx, causing hyperfibrinolysis and platelet dysfunction. It is worsened by the lethal triad: hypothermia, acidosis, and hypofibrinogenemia/coagulopathy. Manage it with balanced transfusion (1:1:1), early tranexamic acid, fibrinogen (cryoprecipitate), calcium, warming, and rapid hemorrhage control.
Permissive hypotension limits clot disruption until the bleeding is controlled. Viscoelastic-guided therapy targets the specific defect.
Trauma coagulopathy: early (shock + injury), hyperfibrinolysis; treat 1:1:1 + TXA + fibrinogen + warm

*Hemorrhagic shock causes an early trauma-induced coagulopathy (hyperfibrinolysis, glycocalyx and platelet dysfunction) worsened by the lethal triad — treat it with balanced 1:1:1 transfusion, early tranexamic acid, fibrinogen, calcium, warming, and rapid control.*

cards.title: Heparin, Antithrombin III, and PT/PTT
secs: Unfractionated heparin potentiates antithrombin III (AT III), which then inhibits factor IIa (thrombin) and factor Xa; this prolongs the PTT but not the PT. PTT (intrinsic + common pathway) reflects factors XII, XI, IX, VIII and X, V, II, I. PT/INR (extrinsic + common pathway) reflects factor VII and X, V, II, I. Low-molecular-weight heparin acts more selectively on Xa (monitored by anti-Xa, not PTT); fondaparinux is a pure Xa inhibitor.

Monitor UFH with PTT (or anti-Xa; intraoperatively with ACT during CPB). AT III DEFICIENCY causes heparin resistance — inadequate ACT/PTT prolongation despite full dosing — treated with AT III concentrate or fresh frozen plasma. Protamine reverses UFH (and partially LMWH). Factor VII has the shortest half-life, so the PT/INR is the earliest coagulation study to prolong in warfarin therapy, liver disease, and vitamin K deficiency.
Heparin -> AT III -> inhibits IIa & Xa -> prolongs PTT (intrinsic), not PT; AT III deficiency = heparin resistance.

cards.title: Citrate Distribution & Citrate Toxicity
secs: CITRATE is the anticoagulant in stored blood products; it works by CHELATING ionized calcium (and magnesium), preventing clot formation. About 90% of the citrate in whole blood partitions into the plasma-rich fractions (FFP and platelets), NOT into pRBCs, which are relatively plasma-depleted. Citrate is normally metabolized rapidly by the liver into bicarbonate. Toxicity arises with rapid or massive transfusion, particularly of FFP, or when hepatic metabolism is impaired (liver disease, the anhepatic phase of transplant, HYPOTHERMIA, neonates).

Citrate toxicity is functionally acute HYPOCALCEMIA: HYPOTENSION, myocardial depression, narrow pulse pressure, prolonged QT, and concurrent hypomagnesemia. Monitor IONIZED CALCIUM during massive transfusion and treat with IV calcium. FFP-heavy resuscitation delivers the greatest citrate load per unit.
1 g calcium chloride contains ~273 mg (3x) elemental calcium versus ~93 mg in 1 g calcium gluconate. Ionized Ca target > ~1.1 mmol/L during transfusion.
90% of whole-blood citrate resides in FFP, not pRBCs; citrate chelates calcium, so massive/FFP-heavy transfusion causes hypocalcemia with hypotension, myocardial depression, and hypomagnesemia.

cards.title: TRALI: Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury
secs: TRALI results from DONOR ANTIBODIES (anti-HLA or anti-neutrophil, most often from multiparous female donors) that activate recipient LEUKOCYTES, which sequester in and damage the PULMONARY CAPILLARY bed, producing non-cardiogenic PULMONARY EDEMA. It presents within 6 HOURS of transfusion with acute HYPOXEMIA, bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, FEVER, chills, and HYPOTENSION. It is the leading cause of transfusion-related MORTALITY. Plasma-rich products (FFP, platelets) carry the highest risk.

The critical distinction is from TACO (transfusion-associated circulatory overload), which is CARDIOGENIC/hydrostatic edema with HYPERTENSION, elevated BNP, and a positive fluid balance; TRALI instead has normal-to-low filling pressures and often hypotension. Treatment of TRALI is SUPPORTIVE with lung-protective ventilation; DIURETICS help TACO but not TRALI. It is usually self-limited, resolving in 48-96 hours.

TRALI is non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema from donor antibodies activating recipient neutrophils in the lung, presenting within 6 hours with hypoxemia, fever, and hypotension, and distinguished from TACO by its low filling pressures and lack of response to diuretics.

cards.title: Protamine: Heparin Reversal and the Three Reaction Types
secs: PROTAMINE is the only clinically available reversal agent for HEPARIN. It is a highly POSITIVELY charged (polycationic) protein derived from fish sperm that binds and neutralizes the highly NEGATIVELY charged (polyanionic) heparin molecule, forming an inactive complex. There are three recognized classes of protamine reaction (see figure): TYPE I is systemic HYPOTENSION from histamine release and mast cell degranulation caused by RAPID administration (the polycationic structure is responsible), TYPE II is IgE-mediated ANAPHYLAXIS that is dose-independent and requires prior sensitization (e.g., NPH insulin, which contains protamine, or prior protamine exposure), and TYPE III is a catastrophic PULMONARY HYPERTENSIVE CRISIS with pulmonary vasoconstriction and RIGHT HEART FAILURE driven by THROMBOXANE A2 released from platelets and macrophages stimulated by protamine-heparin complexes.

Give protamine SLOWLY to minimize the type I histamine reaction. Patients on NPH INSULIN, those with fish/seafood allergy, and vasectomized men are at higher risk of anaphylaxis. Excess protamine itself has ANTICOAGULANT and antiplatelet effects, so over-dosing is counterproductive. Type III presents as acute RV failure and hypoxemia after protamine on separation from bypass.
Dose roughly 1 mg protamine neutralizes about 100 units of heparin; typical reversal is 1-1.3 mg per 100 units of the initial heparin dose. Protamine effect can be titrated to the ACT.

Protamine, a polycationic protein, reverses heparin by ionic binding, but it can cause three reactions: rapid-injection histamine hypotension (I), IgE-mediated anaphylaxis in prior-exposed patients such as those on NPH insulin (II), and a thromboxane-mediated pulmonary hypertensive crisis with right heart failure (III).

noteimg.cap: The three classes of protamine reactions: Type I (histamine-mediated hypotension), Type II (IgE anaphylaxis), Type III (thromboxane A2 pulmonary hypertensive crisis).

cards.title: Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia & Thrombosis (HIT/HITT)
secs: HIT is an IMMUNE-MEDIATED, prothrombotic reaction to heparin. IgG antibodies form against PLATELET FACTOR 4 (PF4)-heparin complexes; the immune complex binds the platelet Fc-gamma-RIIa receptor, causing platelet ACTIVATION and aggregation and a hypercoagulable state. The platelet count typically falls 30-50% from baseline about 5-10 days after starting heparin (or within hours if pre-existing antibodies from recent exposure = rapid-onset). Despite thrombocytopenia the hallmark is THROMBOSIS (venous > arterial), not bleeding. More common with unfractionated than low-molecular-weight heparin, and after cardiac/orthopedic surgery.

Diagnosis is clinical probability by the 4T score (degree of Thrombocytopenia, Timing of the platelet fall, Thrombosis, absence of oTher cause), confirmed by PF4-heparin ELISA (sensitive) and the serotonin release assay (specific gold standard). Treatment: STOP ALL heparin (including flushes and heparin-bonded catheters) and start a NON-HEPARIN anticoagulant - direct thrombin inhibitors argatroban or bivalirudin (or fondaparinux/DOAC). Do NOT transfuse platelets (fuels thrombosis) and do NOT start warfarin until platelets recover (risk of venous limb gangrene/skin necrosis). For a HIT patient needing cardiopulmonary bypass, bivalirudin is preferred.
HIT = PF4-heparin IgG activating platelets: thrombocytopenia WITH thrombosis 5-10 days after heparin; stop all heparin, anticoagulate with argatroban/bivalirudin, and avoid platelets and warfarin.

cards.title: Pituitary, Thyroid, Parathyroid, Adrenal Physiology
secs: The pituitary: the anterior lobe (ACTH, TSH, growth hormone, FSH/LH, prolactin) is under hypothalamic control, and the posterior lobe stores and releases ADH and oxytocin. The thyroid (T4 and T3) sets the metabolic rate (via the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis); the parathyroid raises calcium (through bone resorption, renal reabsorption, and vitamin D activation). The adrenal cortex makes glucocorticoids (cortisol — for stress and gluconeogenesis), mineralocorticoids (aldosterone — for sodium and volume), and androgens, while the medulla makes catecholamines.
Cortisol is essential for the stress response and vascular tone (its deficiency causes hypotension). For calcium homeostasis, PTH and vitamin D raise it while calcitonin lowers it (a minor effect).
Anterior pit: ACTH/TSH/GH/FSH-LH/prolactin; posterior: ADH/oxytocin; PTH raises Ca; cortisol = stress

*The anterior pituitary drives ACTH, TSH, growth hormone, gonadotropins, and prolactin (the posterior stores ADH and oxytocin); PTH and vitamin D raise calcium; and the adrenal cortex makes cortisol (for stress and vascular tone) and aldosterone (for sodium and volume).*

head: Drug
Equiv
Gluco
Mineralo
tables.note: Stress dose: hydrocortisone 100 mg then 100 mg/8 h.

cards.title: Carbohydrate Metabolism; Insulin; Stress Response
secs: Insulin (the only hypoglycemic hormone) promotes glucose uptake and storage, while the counter-regulatory hormones (glucagon, cortisol, catecholamines, growth hormone) raise glucose. The surgical stress response is a catecholamine and cortisol surge that causes hyperglycemia, insulin resistance, catabolism, and sodium and water retention. Hyperglycemia worsens outcomes (infection, wound healing), but tight control risks hypoglycemia — so target a moderate range (about 140–180 mg/dL perioperatively).
Regional anesthesia and opioids blunt the stress response. Avoid both hyper- and hypoglycemia.
Stress → ↑ cortisol/catecholamines → hyperglycemia + insulin resistance; target ~140–180 mg/dL

*Insulin lowers glucose while the counter-regulatory hormones (cortisol, catecholamines, glucagon, growth hormone) raise it; surgical stress causes hyperglycemia and insulin resistance — target about 140–180 mg/dL perioperatively (avoiding hypoglycemia).*

head: Type
Example
Onset / duration
tables.note: Hold/halve long-acting perioperatively; monitor glucose.

cards.title: Perioperative Insulin Management
secs: Hold short-acting and prandial insulin when the patient is NPO, and give a reduced dose (about half to two-thirds) of basal or long-acting insulin to avoid ketosis while preventing hypoglycemia. Hold the oral hypoglycemics on the day of surgery (metformin per protocol — a lactic-acidosis concern with contrast or renal injury), and HOLD SGLT2 inhibitors for several days (euglycemic DKA). Monitor glucose frequently, use an insulin infusion for major surgery or poor control, and target about 140–180 mg/dL.
Type 1 diabetics always need basal insulin (or they develop DKA). Resume the usual regimen once the patient is eating.
NPO: reduce basal insulin (~50–67%), hold prandial/orals; SGLT2i held days (euglycemic DKA)
*When NPO, hold prandial insulin and oral agents but give reduced basal insulin (type 1 always needs basal); hold SGLT2 inhibitors for several days (euglycemic DKA) and target glucose about 140–180.*

cards.title: Diabetes Insipidus & SIADH
secs: Diabetes insipidus (an ADH deficiency — central, as after pituitary surgery or TBI; or nephrogenic) causes a polyuria of DILUTE urine, leading to HYPERnatremia and hypovolemia; central DI is treated with desmopressin (DDAVP) and free-water replacement. SIADH (an ADH excess — from CNS, pulmonary, drug, or malignant causes) causes water retention, leading to a euvolemic HYPOnatremia with concentrated urine; treat it with fluid restriction (plus hypertonic saline if severe or symptomatic, and vaptans). Correct sodium SLOWLY (rapid correction of a chronic hyponatremia causes central pontine myelinolysis, and over-rapid correction of hypernatremia causes cerebral edema).

DI shows a high urine output, a low urine osmolality, and a rising serum sodium. SIADH shows a low serum sodium, a high urine osmolality, and euvolemia. Post-transsphenoidal polyuria with DILUTE urine (a low urine sodium and osmolality) is central DI → desmopressin and free-water replacement; fluid restriction or hypertonic saline would be the wrong (SIADH) treatment.
DI: dilute urine + hypernatremia (DDAVP); SIADH: concentrated urine + hyponatremia (fluid restrict)

*DI is an ADH deficiency → dilute urine plus hypernatremia (treat with DDAVP and free water); SIADH is an ADH excess → concentrated urine plus euvolemic hyponatremia (fluid restriction) — and correct the sodium slowly either way.*

cards.title: Acromegaly & Airway Management
secs: Growth-hormone excess (from a pituitary adenoma) enlarges the facial bones, tongue (macroglossia), and soft tissues and narrows the glottis and subglottis, causing DIFFICULT mask ventilation and intubation. The associated features are OSA, hypertension, cardiomyopathy, diabetes, and entrapment neuropathies, and the patient may need a smaller endotracheal tube. Plan for a difficult airway (awake fiberoptic or video laryngoscopy, smaller tubes, and expert help).
It often presents for trans-sphenoidal resection (watch for postoperative diabetes insipidus). Assess for cardiac and respiratory comorbidity.

*Acromegaly (growth-hormone excess) enlarges the tongue and soft tissues and narrows the airway → difficult mask ventilation and intubation (plan awake fiberoptic and a smaller tube); it carries OSA, cardiomyopathy, and diabetes.*

cards.title: Hyperthyroidism & Thyroid Storm
secs: Hyperthyroidism (Graves disease, a toxic nodule) causes tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, tremor, heat intolerance, and weight loss; render the patient EUTHYROID before elective surgery (with antithyroid drugs, beta-blockers, and iodine). Thyroid storm is a life-threatening hypermetabolic crisis (fever, tachyarrhythmia, agitation, heart failure), often triggered by surgery, stress, or infection — and it mimics malignant hyperthermia and pheochromocytoma. Treat a storm with beta-blockers (propranolol or esmolol), PTU or methimazole, iodine (after the PTU), steroids, cooling, and supportive care.
Avoid drugs that stimulate the sympathetic system; ketamine and pancuronium are less ideal. Give iodine after the antithyroid drugs (to avoid the Jod-Basedow effect).
Render euthyroid preop; storm → beta-blocker + PTU + iodine (after PTU) + steroids + cooling

*Render hyperthyroid patients euthyroid before elective surgery; thyroid storm (fever, tachyarrhythmia, agitation) is treated with beta-blockers, PTU or methimazole, iodine (after the PTU), steroids, and cooling — and it mimics malignant hyperthermia and pheochromocytoma.*

head: Target
Agent
tables.note: Avoid aspirin (displaces T4); resembles MH & pheochromocytoma.

cards.title: Hypothyroidism & Myxedema Coma
secs: Hypothyroidism causes fatigue, bradycardia, cold intolerance, and a decreased metabolic rate, leading to an increased sensitivity to anesthetics and opioids, hypotension, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, and delayed gastric emptying. Myxedema coma is a severe decompensation — hypothermia, hypoventilation, hypotension, and altered consciousness — treated with IV thyroid hormone (T4 with or without T3), stress-dose steroids, supportive care, and slow rewarming. Mild-to-moderate hypothyroidism rarely requires delaying urgent surgery, but severe disease should be treated first.
The anesthetic requirement is reduced, so dose cautiously. There is associated adrenal insufficiency, so give steroids in myxedema coma.
Hypothyroid: ↑ anesthetic sensitivity; myxedema coma → IV thyroid hormone + steroids + supportive

*Hypothyroidism increases the sensitivity to anesthetics and opioids, with bradycardia and hypotension; myxedema coma (hypothermia, hypoventilation, altered mental status) needs IV thyroid hormone, stress-dose steroids, and support.*

cards.title: RLN Injury; Hypocalcemia Post-Thyroidectomy
secs: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury: a unilateral injury causes hoarseness, while a BILATERAL injury causes stridor and airway obstruction (which may need reintubation) — assess cord function, and use nerve monitoring intraoperatively. Hypocalcemia from inadvertent parathyroid removal or injury causes perioral tingling, tetany, Chvostek and Trousseau signs, laryngospasm, and a prolonged QT — typically about 24–72 hours postoperatively; treat it with calcium. The other risks are a neck hematoma (with airway compromise — open it immediately) and tracheomalacia.

Check the voice, airway, and calcium postoperatively. An expanding neck hematoma is an airway emergency. Timing localizes post-thyroidectomy stridor: immediate (on emergence) suggests a bilateral RLN injury, whereas stridor appearing about 24 hours later is usually a hypocalcemic laryngospasm — check the calcium.
Bilateral RLN injury → stridor; post-thyroid hypocalcemia (tetany, long QT) at 24–72 h

*After thyroidectomy, watch for RLN injury (bilateral → stridor), hypocalcemia from parathyroid injury (tetany, a long QT at 24–72 hours → give calcium), and a neck hematoma (open it immediately for the airway).*

cards.title: Hyperparathyroidism & Hypoparathyroidism
secs: Hyperparathyroidism (PTH excess) causes HYPERcalcemia — “stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric overtones,” polyuria and dehydration, a short QT, and weakness; treat it with hydration plus a loop diuretic (after volume repletion), bisphosphonates, and calcitonin, and with parathyroidectomy. Hypoparathyroidism causes HYPOcalcemia — tetany, laryngospasm, a prolonged QT, and paresthesias; treat it with calcium and vitamin D. Calcium affects neuromuscular function and cardiac conduction.
Severe hypercalcemia causes arrhythmia and altered mental status — rehydrate aggressively. Monitor the ionized calcium and the QT.
Hyperparathyroid → hypercalcemia (short QT); hypoparathyroid → hypocalcemia (long QT, tetany)
