*Hyperparathyroidism causes hypercalcemia (stones, bones, and groans, with a short QT — treat with hydration plus a diuretic); hypoparathyroidism causes hypocalcemia (tetany, a long QT — give calcium and vitamin D).*

head: Disorder
Cause
Signs
tables.note: Ionized calcium is what matters clinically.

cards.title: Cushing, Aldosteronism, Addison
secs: Cushing syndrome (cortisol excess) causes hypertension, hyperglycemia, central obesity, a hypokalemic alkalosis, fragile skin and osteoporosis, and easy bruising. Primary aldosteronism (Conn syndrome) causes hypertension plus HYPOkalemia and a metabolic alkalosis; treat it with spironolactone and correct the potassium. Addison disease (primary adrenal insufficiency) causes hypotension, hyponatremia, HYPERkalemia, and hypoglycemia, leading to an adrenal crisis under stress — give STRESS-DOSE STEROIDS perioperatively.
Any chronically steroid-treated patient may need perioperative stress-dose steroids (a suppressed HPA axis). Treat an adrenal crisis with hydrocortisone, fluids, and glucose.
Cushing (cortisol excess, hypokalemia); Conn (aldosterone, HTN + hypoK); Addison → stress-dose steroids

*Cushing (cortisol excess: hypertension, hyperglycemia, hypokalemia), Conn (aldosterone: hypertension plus hypokalemia), and Addison (adrenal insufficiency: hypotension, hyperkalemia) — give stress-dose steroids to insufficient or chronically steroid-treated patients to prevent a crisis.*

head: Disorder
Hormone
Features
tables.note: Give stress-dose steroids for chronic exogenous steroids or Addison.

cards.title: Pheochromocytoma
secs: A catecholamine-secreting tumor causes paroxysmal hypertension, headache, palpitations, and sweating; surgery or tumor manipulation can trigger a hypertensive crisis, then a post-resection hypotension. Establish preoperative ALPHA-blockade FIRST (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) for 1–2 weeks, then beta-blockade if needed (NEVER beta before alpha — unopposed alpha causes a crisis), plus volume expansion. Intraoperatively, have rapid vasodilators (nitroprusside, phentolamine, nicardipine) and short-acting beta-blockers (esmolol) ready, and after tumor-vein ligation treat the hypotension with fluids and vasopressors.

Avoid drugs that release catecholamines or histamine (ephedrine, ketamine, morphine; succinylcholine is debated). It is associated with the MEN syndromes. Persistent paroxysmal hypertension AFTER ligation of the adrenal veins is usually residual circulating catecholamines (released by earlier manipulation), exaggerated in obesity by a larger volume of distribution — it resolves as they are metabolized; a sustained HYPOtension is the more typical post-ligation state.
Alpha-block BEFORE beta-block (1–2 wk) + volume; post-resection hypotension after vein ligation

*Pheochromocytoma needs preoperative alpha-blockade FIRST (then beta, never reversed) plus volume loading; intraoperatively control the surges (phentolamine, nitroprusside, esmolol) and expect hypotension after tumor-vein ligation.*

head: Step
Agent / goal
tables.note: NEVER β-block first → unopposed α → hypertensive crisis.

cards.title: Carcinoid Syndrome
secs: Neuroendocrine tumors secrete serotonin, histamine, kallikrein, and other mediators; carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhea, bronchospasm, right-heart valve disease) occurs with hepatic metastases or extra-portal tumors. A carcinoid CRISIS (severe flushing, bronchospasm, and profound hypo- or hypertension) can be triggered by tumor manipulation, stress, or catecholamines — treat and PREVENT it with OCTREOTIDE (a somatostatin analogue). Avoid histamine- and catecholamine-releasing drugs, and give perioperative octreotide.

Right-sided valvular disease (tricuspid and pulmonary) results from the serotonin. Have octreotide ready, and avoid ephedrine and epinephrine for hypotension (which can cause paradoxical mediator release).
Carcinoid crisis → octreotide (somatostatin analogue); avoid catecholamine/histamine-releasing drugs

*Carcinoid tumors release serotonin and histamine (flushing, diarrhea, bronchospasm, right-heart valve disease); prevent and treat a carcinoid crisis with octreotide and avoid catecholamine- and histamine-releasing drugs.*

head: Aspect
Detail
tables.note: Right-sided carcinoid heart disease; octreotide is key.

cards.title: Diabetes Mellitus: DKA, HHS; Pancreas Transplant
secs: DKA (in type 1 diabetes): hyperglycemia, an anion-gap metabolic acidosis, ketones, dehydration, and total-body potassium depletion — treat with FLUIDS first, an insulin infusion, and potassium replacement (insulin drives potassium intracellularly, so replace it before or with the insulin if it is low) and find the trigger. The hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (in type 2 diabetes) is extreme hyperglycemia, profound dehydration, minimal ketosis, and altered mentation — treat with fluids, insulin, and electrolytes. The chronic complications are autonomic neuropathy (gastroparesis — an aspiration risk, plus hemodynamic lability), nephropathy, coronary disease (with silent ischemia), and a stiff-joint difficult airway.
Delay elective surgery to correct DKA or HHS. Pancreas or islet transplant restores insulin, with immunosuppression considerations.
DKA: fluids + insulin + K (replace K, insulin lowers it); HHS: profound dehydration, minimal ketosis

*DKA (type 1: an anion-gap acidosis, ketones, potassium depletion) is treated with fluids, insulin, and potassium (insulin lowers potassium, so replace it); HHS (type 2) is extreme hyperglycemia and dehydration with minimal ketosis — and diabetic autonomic neuropathy adds aspiration and cardiac risk.*

head: Feature
DKA
HHS
tables.note: Both: insulin, fluids, K⁺ replacement, find the trigger.

cards.title: Primary Hyperaldosteronism (Conn Syndrome)
secs: Autonomous aldosterone excess (adrenal adenoma or bilateral hyperplasia) drives renal sodium/water retention with potassium and hydrogen-ion excretion, producing HYPOKALEMIA, METABOLIC ALKALOSIS, and hypertension. Screening: elevated plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio (high aldosterone, SUPPRESSED renin — distinguishing it from secondary hyperaldosteronism where renin is high).

Correct hypokalemia and control blood pressure before elective surgery; spironolactone or eplerenone (aldosterone antagonists) are mainstays. Untreated hypokalemia potentiates neuromuscular blockers, predisposes to arrhythmias, and can cause ileus and muscle weakness. Metabolic alkalosis can blunt the ventilatory drive.
Primary hyperaldosteronism = hypertension + hypokalemia + metabolic alkalosis with HIGH aldosterone and LOW renin.

cards.title: Myxedema Coma
secs: Myxedema coma is severe, decompensated hypothyroidism characterized by HYPOTHERMIA, altered mental status/coma, HYPOVENTILATION with hypercapnia, HYPONATREMIA, HYPOGLYCEMIA, bradycardia, and hypotension. Often precipitated by infection, cold exposure, surgery, trauma, or sedatives/opioids in an undertreated hypothyroid patient. It carries high mortality.

A medical emergency: give IV levothyroxine (T4) with or without T3, plus STRESS-DOSE hydrocortisone — steroids are given before/with thyroid hormone to cover possible concurrent adrenal insufficiency and prevent precipitating adrenal crisis. Supportive care: secure the airway and ventilate, passive rewarming, cautious correction of hyponatremia and glucose, and treat the precipitant. DEFER elective surgery until the patient is euthyroid; if emergent, proceed with these measures and anticipate sensitivity to anesthetics and delayed drug metabolism.
Myxedema coma: hypothermia, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia — give IV thyroid hormone AND stress-dose steroids.

cards.title: Somatostatin (GHIH): Universal Endocrine Inhibitor
secs: SOMATOSTATIN, also called growth-hormone-inhibiting hormone (GHIH), is a broad INHIBITORY peptide secreted by hypothalamic neurons and by pancreatic islet DELTA CELLS as well as the GI tract. It suppresses release of GROWTH HORMONE and TSH from the anterior pituitary and inhibits the pancreatic islet hormones INSULIN (beta cells) and GLUCAGON (alpha cells). It also broadly suppresses GI secretions and hormones (gastrin, secretin, CCK, VIP) and slows gut motility and splanchnic blood flow. Its actions are essentially the mirror image of most stimulatory GI/endocrine hormones - a global 'off switch.'

The synthetic analog OCTREOTIDE exploits these effects clinically: control of ACROMEGALY (inhibits GH), symptom control in CARCINOID and VIPoma (inhibits secretory hormones), reduction of pancreatic/GI fistula output, and reduction of splanchnic flow to control VARICEAL and GI bleeding. Because somatostatin suppresses both insulin and glucagon, octreotide can cause dysglycemia (usually mild hyperglycemia from greater insulin suppression, but hypoglycemia is possible). It is a mainstay in the perioperative management of carcinoid patients to blunt mediator release and CARCINOID CRISIS.
Somatostatin is the universal inhibitor - it turns OFF growth hormone, TSH, insulin, and glucagon; its analog octreotide treats acromegaly, carcinoid, and variceal bleeding.

cards.title: Addison Disease (Primary Adrenal Insufficiency)
secs: Addison disease is PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency: destruction of the adrenal CORTEX causing deficiency of BOTH cortisol (glucocorticoid) and aldosterone (mineralocorticoid). Most common cause in developed countries is autoimmune adrenalitis; worldwide TB, and also adrenal hemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen), metastases, or drugs (etomidate inhibits cortisol synthesis). Because the defect is at the gland, the ACTH (cosyntropin) STIMULATION test shows NO rise in cortisol. ACTH is elevated from loss of negative feedback, causing HYPERPIGMENTATION. Labs: HYPONATREMIA and HYPERKALEMIA (aldosterone loss), mild HYPERCALCEMIA, HYPOGLYCEMIA (cortisol loss), and a non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Symptoms: orthostatic hypotension, fatigue, weakness, anorexia, weight loss, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. Contrast SECONDARY (pituitary ACTH deficiency): aldosterone and RAAS are intact, so NO hyperkalemia and NO hyperpigmentation.

Surgical stress, infection, or abrupt steroid withdrawal can precipitate an ADRENAL (Addisonian) CRISIS: refractory hypotension/shock, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypoglycemia. Treat immediately with stress-dose hydrocortisone, aggressive isotonic saline, and dextrose; do not delay for confirmatory testing. Patients on chronic glucocorticoids need perioperative stress-dose coverage because of a suppressed HPA axis.
Cosyntropin 250 mcg IV/IM; normal response = cortisol rises to >18-20 mcg/dL. Stress-dose hydrocortisone ~100 mg IV, then 50-100 mg every 8 h. Resuscitate with 0.9% saline +/- dextrose.

Addison = primary adrenal failure (low cortisol AND aldosterone): hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, hypotension, flat cortisol on ACTH stim; treat crisis with stress-dose hydrocortisone and saline.

cards.title: Primary Hyperparathyroidism & Hypercalcemia
secs: Primary hyperparathyroidism — usually a solitary parathyroid ADENOMA — is the most common NON-malignant cause of hypercalcemia (malignancy is the most common cause overall, especially among inpatients). Typical labs: elevated PTH, HYPERCALCEMIA, HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA, a normal-to-elevated urinary calcium, and often a non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA). PTH raises serum calcium by increasing renal calcium reabsorption, increasing intestinal absorption (by stimulating renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase, which makes active vitamin D / calcitriol), and increasing osteoclastic bone resorption; PTH also DECREASES renal phosphate reabsorption (causing phosphaturia) and decreases renal bicarbonate reclamation (contributing to the NAGMA).

Classic symptoms are stones, bones, abdominal groans, and psychiatric moans (nephrolithiasis, osteopenia, constipation, weakness, altered mentation). Anesthetic concerns from hypercalcemia: a SHORTENED QT interval and dysrhythmias, hypovolemia from calcium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, and unpredictable neuromuscular-blocker response with baseline muscle weakness. First-line treatment of severe hypercalcemia is aggressive isotonic IV FLUID, then calcitonin and bisphosphonates. Contrast with familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia, which shows a characteristically LOW urinary calcium.
Normal total calcium ~8.5-10.5 mg/dL; correct total calcium up 0.8 mg/dL for each 1 g/dL the albumin is below 4 g/dL. Severe hypercalcemia is over 14 mg/dL.

*Primary hyperparathyroidism = high PTH, high calcium, LOW phosphate, sometimes NAGMA; PTH raises calcium via kidney, gut (calcitriol), and bone while wasting phosphate — the most common NON-malignant hypercalcemia.*

cards.title: Hepatic Dual Blood Supply & Regulation
secs: The liver receives about 25% of the cardiac output through a dual supply: the hepatic artery (about 25% of the flow and 50% of the oxygen) and the portal vein (about 75% of the flow and 50% of the oxygen). The hepatic arterial buffer response: the hepatic artery dilates when the portal flow falls (maintaining total flow), but the portal vein cannot autoregulate in the same way. Anesthesia and surgery (especially upper-abdominal), hypotension, and positive-pressure ventilation reduce hepatic blood flow.
Volatiles reduce the portal flow, but the buffer response preserves the arterial supply (isoflurane and sevoflurane are better than halothane). Maintain perfusion to protect the liver.
Hepatic artery ~25% flow / 50% O2; portal vein ~75% flow / 50% O2; arterial buffer response

*The liver has a dual supply — the portal vein (about 75% of the flow) and the hepatic artery (about 25% of the flow, each providing about 50% of the oxygen); the hepatic arterial buffer response dilates the artery when portal flow drops, and anesthesia and hypotension reduce total flow.*

head: Source
Flow
O₂
tables.note: Hepatic arterial buffer response; hypotension & volatiles reduce flow.

cards.title: Hepatic Metabolic & Synthetic Functions; P450
secs: Synthesis: the liver makes albumin, the clotting factors (all except VIII and von Willebrand factor, which are made by the endothelium), and cholinesterase; it maintains glucose (through glycogen and gluconeogenesis) and converts ammonia to urea. Biotransformation: Phase I (cytochrome P450 oxidation) and Phase II (conjugation) clear drugs, so hepatic disease prolongs many of them. It also stores glycogen, vitamins, and iron, and produces bile.

Liver disease causes hypoalbuminemia (altering drug binding), coagulopathy (from reduced factors — give vitamin K and FFP), hypoglycemia, and encephalopathy (from ammonia). Severe disease lowers pseudocholinesterase, prolonging succinylcholine.
Liver makes all clotting factors except VIII/vWF; P450 (Phase I) + conjugation (Phase II)

*The liver synthesizes albumin, cholinesterase, and all the clotting factors except VIII and von Willebrand factor, handles glucose and urea, and clears drugs via P450 and conjugation — so liver disease causes hypoalbuminemia, coagulopathy, and slowed drug clearance.*

cards.title: Morbid Obesity / Bariatric Anesthesia
secs: Airway: a higher risk of difficult mask ventilation and intubation, so use a ramped position (ear to sternal notch), preoxygenate well (a low FRC desaturates fast), and consider awake or video laryngoscopy. Physiology: a reduced FRC and atelectasis, OSA or obesity-hypoventilation, aspiration risk, and hypertension, coronary disease, and diabetes; dose lipophilic drugs by total body weight and most others by lean or ideal body weight. Use PEEP and recruitment, regional techniques where possible, multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia, and VTE prophylaxis.

Account for position-related issues and equipment sizing, and provide postoperative respiratory monitoring (for OSA). Fully reverse the neuromuscular blockade (residual paralysis is poorly tolerated).
Ramp + preoxygenate (low FRC); dose lipophilic drugs by TBW, others by IBW/LBW

*Morbid obesity raises the risk of a difficult airway and rapid desaturation (ramp, preoxygenate, PEEP); dose by lean versus total body weight, use opioid-sparing analgesia, and monitor postoperatively for OSA.*

cards.title: Hepatic Disease; Portal Hypertension; Hepatorenal
secs: Cirrhosis and portal hypertension cause varices (which bleed), ascites, hypersplenism (thrombocytopenia), coagulopathy, encephalopathy, and a hyperdynamic circulation (low SVR, high cardiac output). Grade the severity by the Child-Pugh and MELD scores (bilirubin, INR, creatinine), which predict perioperative mortality. Hepatorenal syndrome is functional renal failure from splanchnic vasodilation and renal vasoconstriction in advanced liver disease — treat it with vasoconstrictors (terlipressin or norepinephrine) plus albumin, with liver transplant as the definitive treatment.

Drug handling is altered (hypoalbuminemia, reduced clearance, low pseudocholinesterase), so prefer organ-independent metabolism (cisatracurium, remifentanil). Maintain hepatic and renal perfusion and correct the coagulopathy as needed.
Child-Pugh / MELD (bili, INR, Cr) grade risk; hepatorenal → vasoconstrictors + albumin, transplant

*Cirrhosis brings varices, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, and a hyperdynamic low-SVR circulation (grade by Child-Pugh and MELD); hepatorenal syndrome is functional renal failure treated with vasoconstrictors plus albumin and ultimately transplant.*

head: Factor
tables.note: A = 5–6, B = 7–9, C = 10–15 (rising surgical mortality).

cards.title: Hepatic Transplantation
secs: There are three phases: the preanhepatic (dissection, with bleeding and coagulopathy), the anhepatic (caval clamping reduces preload, there is no hepatic metabolism, and citrate accumulates causing hypocalcemia), and reperfusion. Postreperfusion syndrome — hyperkalemia, acidosis, hypothermia, hypotension, arrhythmia, and air or thromboembolism as the graft is unclamped — must be anticipated and treated (calcium, vasopressors, correcting the potassium). It requires massive-transfusion capability, viscoelastic-guided coagulation management, and large-bore rapid-infusion access.
Maintain perfusion to the new graft and manage citrate (give calcium). The coagulation state evolves through the phases (fibrinolysis at reperfusion — use tranexamic acid cautiously).
Phases: preanhepatic / anhepatic (↓ preload, citrate→↓Ca) / reperfusion (↑K, acidosis, hypotension)

*Liver transplant runs preanhepatic → anhepatic (caval clamp, citrate-induced hypocalcemia) → reperfusion, where postreperfusion syndrome (hyperkalemia, acidosis, hypotension) hits — prepare massive transfusion, calcium, and viscoelastic-guided coagulation.*

cards.title: Intestinal Obstruction; Full Stomach; Nitrous Oxide
secs: Bowel obstruction means a full stomach and a high aspiration risk, so use a rapid-sequence induction with cricoid pressure, decompress with a nasogastric tube, and resuscitate fluids and electrolytes (there are large third-space losses). Avoid nitrous oxide — it diffuses into and expands bowel gas, worsening the distension and the operating conditions. Anticipate hypovolemia, electrolyte and acid-base derangement, and hemodynamic instability on induction.
Extubate awake with intact airway reflexes. Give aspiration prophylaxis (a non-particulate antacid, an H2 blocker) but avoid metoclopramide in complete obstruction.

*Bowel obstruction is a full-stomach emergency — rapid-sequence induction with cricoid pressure, nasogastric decompression, and fluid and electrolyte resuscitation; avoid nitrous oxide (it expands bowel gas) and extubate awake.*

cards.title: Antiemetics & Aspiration Prophylaxis
secs: The antiemetic classes (used multimodally, at different receptors) are the 5-HT3 antagonists (ondansetron, at the end of surgery), corticosteroids (dexamethasone, at induction), NK-1 antagonists (aprepitant), D2 antagonists (droperidol, metoclopramide), antihistamines, and anticholinergics (scopolamine). Aspiration prophylaxis: a non-particulate antacid (sodium citrate, working immediately), H2 blockers (famotidine, to raise the gastric pH), and metoclopramide (a promotility agent that raises lower-esophageal-sphincter tone — avoid it in obstruction). Combine classes for high PONV risk (by the Apfel score).
Droperidol carries a QT-prolongation warning. Metoclopramide is contraindicated in complete bowel obstruction and in Parkinson’s disease.

*Use multimodal antiemetics across receptor classes (dexamethasone at induction, ondansetron at the end, plus NK-1 or droperidol); aspiration prophylaxis is sodium citrate plus an H2 blocker, with metoclopramide (but not in obstruction).*

cards.title: Liver Reperfusion: RV Failure & Hypotension
secs: During the REPERFUSION (neohepatic) phase of liver transplantation, unclamping releases cold, acidic, HYPERKALEMIC, vasoactive-laden blood into the systemic circulation, producing POST-REPERFUSION SYNDROME: hypotension, bradycardia, arrhythmias, and an acute rise in PULMONARY VASCULAR RESISTANCE. The abrupt PVR increase can precipitate acute RIGHT VENTRICULAR FAILURE, which further worsens systemic hypotension. When hypotension is refractory to VASOPRESSIN or NOREPINEPHRINE, EPINEPHRINE is the next-line agent, adding inotropy to vasoconstriction.

Anticipate hemodynamic collapse at unclamping: have calcium and epinephrine ready, correct hyperkalemia (calcium, insulin/glucose) and acidosis, and consider pulmonary vasodilators such as inhaled nitric oxide to unload the failing right ventricle. RV support is central because the acute PVR spike plus volume shifting overwhelms the RV.
At liver reperfusion a spike in pulmonary vascular resistance can drive right-heart failure and hypotension; if refractory to vasopressin or norepinephrine, epinephrine is next-line.

cards.title: Hepatic Artery Buffer Response
secs: The liver receives a DUAL blood supply: roughly 75% of flow from the PORTAL VEIN and 25% from the HEPATIC ARTERY, but each contributes about half of oxygen delivery. The HEPATIC ARTERY BUFFER RESPONSE maintains total hepatic blood flow when portal flow falls. ADENOSINE is produced at a constant rate and is normally washed away by portal flow; when PORTAL VENOUS flow decreases, adenosine ACCUMULATES around the resistance vessels and triggers HEPATIC ARTERY VASODILATION, increasing arterial flow to preserve hepatocyte perfusion. Critically, this buffer is UNIDIRECTIONAL: the portal system canNOT increase its flow to compensate for a fall in hepatic arterial flow.

This explains why the hepatic artery is vulnerable and vital during states of low portal flow (cirrhosis, portal clamping, high intra-abdominal pressure). Because the portal vein cannot buffer arterial loss, hepatic artery compromise (e.g., thrombosis after LIVER TRANSPLANT) is catastrophic. Anesthetic drugs and hypotension that reduce portal flow rely on an intact buffer response; agents or conditions that blunt it threaten hepatic oxygenation.

When portal flow drops, locally accumulating adenosine dilates the hepatic artery to preserve liver perfusion (the hepatic artery buffer response), but this compensation is one-way: the portal vein cannot offset a loss of hepatic arterial flow.

cards.title: Anesthetic Effects on Hepatic Blood Flow
secs: The liver has a DUAL blood supply: the HEPATIC ARTERY provides ~25-30% of total hepatic blood flow (and ~50% of the oxygen), while the PORTAL VEIN provides ~70-75% of flow (and the remaining oxygen). The HEPATIC ARTERIAL BUFFER RESPONSE allows hepatic arterial flow to rise (via adenosine washout) when portal flow falls, helping keep TOTAL hepatic blood flow relatively constant. Most volatile and intravenous anesthetics have MINIMAL effect on total hepatic blood flow at clinical doses. The classic EXCEPTION is PROPOFOL, which INCREASES total hepatic blood flow through splanchnic vasodilation and increased portal and arterial flow. (See figure.)

Preserving hepatic perfusion matters most in cirrhosis or hepatic dysfunction, where perfusion-dependent drug clearance and the buffer response are impaired. Hepatic blood flow is reduced by anything that lowers cardiac output or raises sympathetic tone, as well as by positive-pressure ventilation, high PEEP, pneumoperitoneum (increased intra-abdominal pressure), hypocapnia, and surgical retraction near the porta hepatis. Older high-dose volatiles (historically halothane) reduced flow more than modern agents.
Liver = dual supply (~25% artery, ~75% portal vein) with a hepatic arterial buffer response; anesthetics minimally change total hepatic blood flow EXCEPT propofol, which increases it.

noteimg.cap: Anesthetic effects on hepatic blood flow - propofol is the notable agent that increases total flow.

cards.title: Multimodal Postoperative Analgesia
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Postoperative Care (I.B.7)
secs: Multimodal analgesia combines agents with different mechanisms to improve pain control and cut opioid use and side effects: acetaminophen, NSAIDs or COX-2 inhibitors, selected gabapentinoids, NMDA antagonists (ketamine), alpha-2 agonists, and local/regional techniques. Regional anesthesia and local infiltration are cornerstones of opioid sparing. Reserve opioids for breakthrough pain at the lowest effective dose.
Tailor the plan to the patient (renal function for NSAIDs, obstructive sleep apnea for opioids, opioid tolerance). It is a central element of enhanced-recovery (ERAS) pathways.

*Multimodal analgesia layers acetaminophen, NSAIDs, ketamine, alpha-2 agonists, and regional/local techniques to spare opioids; reserve opioids for breakthrough pain and tailor the plan to comorbidities.*

cards.title: PCA, Epidural & Regional Analgesia Routes
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Postoperative Care (I.B.7)
secs: Patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) gives a demand dose with a lockout interval; avoid a background (continuous) infusion in opioid-naive or OSA patients, since it raises the risk of respiratory depression. Epidural analgesia (local anesthetic with or without an opioid) is excellent for thoracic, abdominal, and lower-limb pain but risks hypotension, motor block, urinary retention, and a rare hematoma or abscess. Peripheral nerve catheters provide site-specific analgesia with fewer systemic effects.

Monitor for respiratory depression (capnography in high-risk patients) and for sedation. Neuraxial opioids differ: lipophilic ones act segmentally, while hydrophilic morphine causes delayed respiratory depression.

*PCA gives demand dosing with a lockout (avoid background infusions in opioid-naive or OSA patients); epidurals excel for trunk and lower-limb pain (watch for hypotension and hematoma), and nerve catheters localize the analgesia.*

head: Drug
Bolus
Lockout
tables.note: Avoid routine basal infusion in opioid-naive patients (respiratory depression).

cards.title: TENS, Cryotherapy & Acupuncture
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Postoperative Care (I.B.7)
secs: Non-pharmacologic adjuncts reduce analgesic needs: TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation, working by gate-control modulation), cryotherapy (less inflammation and nociception), and acupuncture/acupressure (for example, the P6 point for nausea). They fit within multimodal and enhanced-recovery strategies and help patients limited by drug side effects. They are generally low-risk and opioid-sparing.
The evidence is variable, so they are best used as adjuncts rather than sole therapy. P6 (pericardium 6) acupressure reduces postoperative nausea and vomiting.
*TENS, cryotherapy, and acupuncture (the P6 point for nausea) are low-risk, opioid-sparing adjuncts within multimodal/ERAS care — not standalone therapies.*

cards.title: Addiction vs Tolerance; Anesthetic Implications
secs: Tolerance is a reduced effect with repeated exposure (pharmacologic), dependence is withdrawal on cessation (physiologic), and addiction is compulsive use despite harm (behavioral) — these are distinct. Chronic opioid users have higher baseline requirements and hyperalgesia — use multimodal and regional analgesia, continue the baseline opioids (or buprenorphine or methadone — coordinate this), and avoid withdrawal. Acute intoxication or withdrawal (alcohol raises MAC and then causes withdrawal and DTs; stimulants cause hemodynamic lability; opioids) affects management.
Buprenorphine (a partial agonist) is increasingly CONTINUED perioperatively with multimodal analgesia. Treat the withdrawal, and avoid undertreating pain.

*Tolerance (less effect), dependence (withdrawal), and addiction (compulsive use) are distinct; chronic opioid users need multimodal and regional analgesia and continuation of their baseline opioids or buprenorphine to control pain and avoid withdrawal.*

head: Term
Definition
rows: Need more for the same effect
tables.note: Tolerance & dependence are expected; addiction is behavioral.

cards.title: Acute Pain
secs: Nociceptive pain from tissue injury follows transduction → transmission → modulation → perception, involving peripheral and central sensitization (windup) with a sustained input. Undertreated acute pain leads to chronic pain, pulmonary complications, and delayed recovery; preventive multimodal analgesia reduces this. Assess pain regularly and tailor the treatment to the surgery and the patient.
Central sensitization causes hyperalgesia and allodynia, which multimodal and regional analgesia blunts. Balance the analgesia against the opioid side effects.

*Acute nociceptive pain (transduction → transmission → modulation → perception) can sensitize centrally (windup) and become chronic if undertreated — preventive multimodal analgesia reduces the chronification and complications.*

cards.title: Cancer-Related Pain
secs: Cancer pain is multifactorial (from tumor invasion, bone metastases, neuropathic mechanisms, and the treatment itself), and it often requires escalating opioids without a ceiling plus adjuvants. Follow the WHO ladder and use adjuvants (steroids for bone and nerve pain, bisphosphonates for bone metastases, antidepressants and anticonvulsants for neuropathic pain). The interventional options are neurolytic blocks (the celiac plexus for pancreatic and upper-abdominal pain) and intrathecal pumps.

Opioid tolerance and escalation are expected — treat the breakthrough pain. Address the total pain (psychosocial and spiritual). Match the neurolytic target to the viscus: the celiac plexus for upper-abdominal and pancreatic pain, the SUPERIOR HYPOGASTRIC plexus for pelvic (uterine/endometrial, ovarian, bladder) pain, and the ganglion impar for perineal pain.

*Cancer pain is multifactorial and often needs ceilingless opioids plus adjuvants (steroids, bisphosphonates, neuropathic agents) per the WHO ladder, with interventional options like celiac plexus neurolysis or intrathecal pumps.*

cards.title: Chronic Neck & Low Back Pain
secs: The common causes are degenerative disc and facet disease, radiculopathy (nerve-root compression), spinal stenosis, and muscular or myofascial pain. Management is conservative first (exercise, physical therapy, NSAIDs, neuropathic agents), then interventional (epidural steroid injections for radicular pain; facet and medial-branch blocks and radiofrequency ablation for facet pain). The “red flags” (cauda equina, infection, malignancy, fracture) require an urgent workup.
Avoid long-term opioids where possible (limited benefit, real harm). Match the intervention to the pain generator (radicular versus facet versus discogenic).

*Chronic neck and back pain (disc, facet, radicular, stenosis) is managed conservatively first, then with targeted interventions (epidural steroids for radicular pain, medial-branch blocks and RFA for facet pain) — screen for red flags and limit chronic opioids.*

cards.title: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (Types I & II)
secs: CRPS is a regional pain (usually in a limb) out of proportion to the injury, with autonomic and trophic changes (edema, color and temperature change, sweating, motor and skin changes); Type I has no nerve injury, while Type II (causalgia) has a definite nerve injury. It is diagnosed clinically (the Budapest criteria); treat it early with physical and occupational therapy, neuropathic agents, sympathetic blocks (stellate ganglion or lumbar sympathetic), and spinal cord stimulation. Early mobilization and multidisciplinary care improve the outcomes.
A sympathetically maintained pain may respond to sympathetic blockade. Avoid immobilization and emphasize functional restoration.
CRPS I (no nerve injury) vs II (nerve injury/causalgia); sympathetic blocks + PT + SCS

*CRPS is a disproportionate regional pain with autonomic and trophic changes (Type I has no nerve injury, Type II has) — treat it early with physical therapy, neuropathic agents, sympathetic blocks, and spinal cord stimulation.*

head: Type
Feature
tables.note: Treat early: physical therapy, sympathetic blocks, neuropathic agents.

cards.title: Postherpetic Neuralgia
secs: Postherpetic neuralgia is a neuropathic pain that persists after herpes zoster (shingles) in the affected dermatome; it is more common in the elderly. Acute herpes zoster most commonly erupts in the THORACIC dermatomes (about 50% of cases), followed by the trigeminal (ophthalmic) distribution. Treat it with neuropathic agents (gabapentinoids, TCAs) and topical lidocaine or capsaicin; early sympathetic or epidural blocks may help. Risk factors for PHN include older age (over 60), IMMUNOSUPPRESSION (e.g., HIV with a CD4 count below 200), greater acute pain severity during the eruption, severe prodromal symptoms, and ophthalmic (CN V1) or brachial-plexus involvement.

Allodynia in the dermatome is characteristic. Address it early to reduce chronification; PREVENT it with zoster vaccination and the early antiviral treatment of acute zoster. Treatment ladder: FIRST-line gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin), then TCAs; SECOND-line opioids, topical lidocaine, and capsaicin; interventional therapy when medical management fails.

*Postherpetic neuralgia is a dermatomal neuropathic pain after shingles (in the elderly) — treat with gabapentinoids or TCAs and topical lidocaine or capsaicin; prevent it with zoster vaccination and early antivirals.*

cards.title: Phantom Limb & Post-Stroke Pain
secs: Phantom limb pain is a painful sensation in a missing limb (from central reorganization); the risk may be reduced by good perioperative and preventive analgesia, and it is treated with neuropathic agents, mirror therapy, and desensitization. Phantom pain is distinct from stump pain (which is local) and from non-painful residual sensations. Central post-stroke pain (thalamic) is a neuropathic pain after a stroke — treat it with neuropathic agents (it is often refractory).
Pre-emptive analgesia and regional techniques may reduce the incidence of phantom pain. Both are central neuropathic syndromes (challenging to treat).

*Phantom limb pain (from central reorganization after amputation) is treated with neuropathic agents and mirror therapy and may be reduced by preventive analgesia; central post-stroke (thalamic) pain is neuropathic and often refractory.*

cards.title: Diabetic & Peripheral Neuropathies
secs: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is a symmetric distal “stocking-glove” sensory loss and burning pain from chronic hyperglycemia. The first-line agents are gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin), SNRIs (duloxetine), and TCAs, with topical agents as adjuncts. Optimize the glycemic control; the other neuropathies (chemotherapy-induced, alcoholic, B12 deficiency) are managed similarly.
Document the baseline deficits before regional anesthesia. Autonomic neuropathy adds perioperative risk (gastroparesis, lability).

*Diabetic peripheral neuropathy (a distal stocking-glove burning pain) is treated with gabapentinoids, duloxetine, or TCAs plus glycemic control — document the baseline deficits before regional anesthesia.*

cards.title: Myofascial Pain; Facet Arthropathy
secs: Myofascial pain is a regional muscle pain with trigger points and referred pain; treat it with physical therapy, trigger-point injections, and posture and ergonomic measures. Facet (zygapophyseal) arthropathy is an axial pain worse with extension and rotation; it is diagnosed with medial-branch blocks and treated with radiofrequency ablation of the medial branches. Both are common mechanical pain generators.
Diagnostic medial-branch blocks confirm facet pain before RFA. Myofascial pain is a clinical diagnosis (trigger points).

*Myofascial pain (trigger points, referred pain) responds to physical therapy and trigger-point injections; facet arthropathy (axial pain worse with extension) is confirmed by medial-branch blocks and treated with radiofrequency ablation.*

cards.title: Sickle Cell Pain
secs: Vaso-occlusive crises cause severe acute pain (and chronic pain), precipitated by hypoxia, cold, dehydration, acidosis, infection, and stress. Treat crises aggressively with hydration, oxygen, warmth, and multimodal analgesia (often high-dose opioids — chronic users have tolerance), plus treating the triggers and any infection. Perioperatively, maintain oxygenation, warmth, and hydration, avoid stasis and acidosis, and transfuse per indication.
Patient-controlled analgesia helps in crises; avoid undertreating the pain (and the stigma). Chronic pain and opioid tolerance complicate the management.

*Sickle cell vaso-occlusive pain (triggered by hypoxia, cold, dehydration, or acidosis) needs aggressive hydration, oxygen, warmth, and multimodal opioid analgesia — and perioperatively, avoid the triggers of sickling.*

cards.title: Multimodal & Neuraxial Analgesia; PCA
secs: Multimodal analgesia combines mechanisms (opioids, acetaminophen, NSAIDs, gabapentinoids, ketamine, local and regional techniques, dexmedetomidine) for better analgesia with fewer opioid side effects (opioid-sparing). Neuraxial (epidural or intrathecal) analgesia uses a local anesthetic plus an opioid — excellent for thoracic, abdominal, and labor analgesia; the risks are hypotension, urinary retention, pruritus, and respiratory depression (from neuraxial opioids). IV patient-controlled analgesia gives patient-controlled opioid boluses with a lockout for good titration — but avoid a background infusion in the opioid-naive (respiratory depression).

Lipophilic neuraxial opioids (fentanyl) act segmentally and quickly, while hydrophilic ones (morphine) spread rostrally (a delayed respiratory depression). Monitor for respiratory depression with neuraxial opioids.

*Multimodal analgesia (opioids plus acetaminophen, NSAIDs, gabapentinoids, ketamine, regional) is opioid-sparing; neuraxial analgesia is excellent but risks hypotension, retention, and respiratory depression; and PCA titrates the opioid (avoid a background infusion in the opioid-naive).*

cards.title: WHO Analgesic Ladder
secs: Step 1 is the non-opioids (acetaminophen, NSAIDs) plus adjuvants; Step 2 is the weak opioids (codeine, tramadol) for mild-to-moderate pain; and Step 3 is the strong opioids (morphine) for moderate-to-severe pain — escalating as needed. It was originally for cancer pain and emphasizes by-the-clock dosing, the oral route, and individualization, with adjuvants at every step. It is a framework now applied more broadly (with caution about opioid escalation in non-cancer pain).
Adjuvants (anticonvulsants, antidepressants, steroids) can be used at any step. Reassess and titrate to effect.
WHO ladder: Step 1 non-opioid → Step 2 weak opioid → Step 3 strong opioid (+ adjuvants throughout)

*The WHO ladder escalates from non-opioids (Step 1) to weak opioids (Step 2) to strong opioids (Step 3), with adjuvants at every step — originally for cancer pain, dosed by-the-clock and individualized.*

cards.title: Neurolytic & Non-neurolytic Blocks for Cancer Pain
secs: Neurolytic blocks (with alcohol or phenol) destroy nerves for severe cancer pain: the celiac plexus (for pancreatic and upper-abdominal visceral pain), the superior hypogastric plexus (for pelvic pain), and the ganglion impar (for perineal pain). They are reserved for refractory cancer pain (there is a risk of motor and sensory deficits and neuritis) — do a diagnostic local-anesthetic block first. Intrathecal drug delivery (pumps) is used for diffuse pain.
Celiac plexus neurolysis can cause hypotension (from the sympathetic block) and diarrhea. Confirm the benefit with a local-anesthetic block before the neurolysis.

*Neurolytic blocks (alcohol or phenol) treat refractory cancer pain — the celiac plexus (upper abdominal), superior hypogastric (pelvic), and ganglion impar (perineal); confirm with a diagnostic local block first (celiac neurolysis causes hypotension and diarrhea).*

cards.title: Spinal Cord Stimulation; Neuroablation
secs: Spinal cord stimulation uses implanted epidural electrodes to deliver current that modulates pain (gate control or neuromodulation) for failed-back-surgery syndrome, CRPS, and refractory neuropathic or ischemic pain; a trial precedes the permanent implant. Neuroablation (radiofrequency, cryo, or chemical) destroys the pain pathways (for example, medial-branch RFA for facet pain). Manage the implanted device (like a pacemaker) for surgery — its MRI compatibility and electrocautery precautions.
Spinal cord stimulation is reversible and adjustable (unlike ablation). Turn the device off for cautery and confirm its MRI compatibility.

*Spinal cord stimulation neuromodulates refractory neuropathic or ischemic pain (failed back, CRPS) with a trial before the implant; neuroablation (RFA) destroys the pain pathways — manage these devices like pacemakers around cautery and MRI.*

cards.title: Anti-epileptics & Antidepressants for Chronic Pain
secs: Gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin) bind the alpha-2-delta calcium-channel subunit to reduce neuropathic pain; they cause sedation and dizziness and are renally cleared. TCAs (amitriptyline, nortriptyline) and SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine) augment the descending inhibition (norepinephrine and serotonin) for neuropathic pain; the TCAs have anticholinergic and cardiac effects. Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia.
These are first-line for many neuropathic conditions — titrate to effect and side effects. Watch the serotonin-syndrome risk with multiple serotonergic drugs.

*Neuropathic pain responds to gabapentinoids (alpha-2-delta ligands), TCAs, and SNRIs (descending inhibition); carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia — titrate against sedation, cardiac, and anticholinergic effects.*

cards.title: Intrathecal Drug Delivery & Baclofen Withdrawal
secs: Implanted intrathecal pumps deliver a drug directly to the CSF (cerebrospinal fluid) at tiny doses for refractory pain or spasticity: baclofen (a GABA-B agonist, for spasticity), morphine and ziconotide (for pain), and clonidine (an adjuvant). Intrathecal doses are a fraction of the systemic equivalents (intrathecal morphine is about 1/300 of the oral dose), so an abrupt loss of delivery — a missed refill, a catheter kink or disconnect, or a pump failure — causes a drug-specific withdrawal syndrome. Refills and dose changes are sterile, programmed procedures; a reservoir running dry is the classic preventable cause of withdrawal.

Intrathecal BACLOFEN withdrawal is a life-threatening emergency resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome or serotonin syndrome: a high fever, an altered mental status, a rebound spasticity and rigidity, and autonomic instability — progressing to rhabdomyolysis, DIC, and death. Treat baclofen withdrawal by restoring the baclofen (the intrathecal route is preferred; high-dose oral or enteral baclofen as a bridge) plus benzodiazepines and aggressive supportive care; dantrolene may help the rigidity. Intrathecal opioid (morphine) withdrawal is uncomfortable but not life-threatening and lacks the rigidity and hyperthermia — a key exam contrast.
Intrathecal morphine ~1/300 oral dose Baclofen withdrawal ≈ NMS (fever, AMS, rigidity) → restore baclofen + benzodiazepines

*An abrupt loss of intrathecal baclofen (a missed refill, a catheter or pump failure) causes a neuroleptic-malignant-syndrome-like emergency — a high fever, an altered mentation, and a rebound rigidity — treated by restoring the baclofen plus benzodiazepines; intrathecal opioid withdrawal, by contrast, is not life-threatening.*

cards.title: Opioid Addiction in Chronic Pain — Cancer vs Noncancer
secs: Opioid addiction (a behavioral disorder marked by compulsive use, loss of control, and continued use despite harm) has a HIGHER prevalence in chronic NONCANCER pain than in chronic CANCER pain. Contributing factors: noncancer populations tend to be younger with longer expected treatment durations and more baseline psychiatric comorbidity (depression, anxiety, substance-use history), whereas cancer pain is often shorter-course and in older patients. Distinguish addiction from PHYSICAL DEPENDENCE (withdrawal on cessation) and TOLERANCE (need for higher doses) — both are expected physiologic responses to chronic opioids and are NOT addiction. PSEUDOADDICTION describes drug-seeking behavior driven by UNDERTREATED pain that resolves with adequate analgesia.

Risk-stratify chronic noncancer pain patients before initiating opioids (screening tools, PDMP checks, prior substance-use history), favor multimodal and non-opioid strategies, use the lowest effective dose, and set clear treatment agreements. The WHO analgesic ladder historically guided cancer pain; chronic noncancer pain warrants greater caution given the higher addiction risk. On the ITE, recognize that a patient meeting DSM criteria for compulsive use has an opioid use disorder, whereas isolated tolerance or dependence does not.
Opioid addiction is MORE prevalent in chronic NONcancer pain than cancer pain — and addiction is distinct from tolerance and physical dependence.

cards.title: Sympathetic Ganglion Blocks for Chronic Pain
secs: Multiple SYMPATHETIC blocks target discrete ganglia to treat pain in specific neurologic distributions. STELLATE GANGLION (cervicothoracic ganglion) is formed by fusion of the inferior cervical and first thoracic sympathetic ganglia; it lies anterior to the C7 transverse process/neck of the first rib and carries sympathetic supply to the head, neck, upper extremity, and heart. It is blocked for CRPS, phantom limb pain, upper-extremity post-herpetic neuralgia, refractory ventricular tachycardia/electrical storm, and PTSD. The hallmark sign of a successful stellate block is ipsilateral HORNER SYNDROME (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) plus nasal congestion and conjunctival injection. SUPERIOR HYPOGASTRIC PLEXUS lies anterolateral from the lower third of L5 to the aortic bifurcation and supplies autonomic innervation to the pelvic viscera (uterus, ovaries, bladder, prostate); blocked for pelvic and gynecologic cancer pain. CELIAC PLEXUS sits at T12-L1 surrounding the abdominal aorta near the celiac artery and SMA, formed from the greater, lesser, and least splanchnic nerves with parasympathetic vagal contributions; it innervates the liver, gallbladder, pancreas, stomach, spleen, intestines, and adrenals, and is blocked for pancreatic cancer and chronic pancreatitis. GANGLION IMPAR (of Walther) is the single terminal fusion of the two sympathetic chains, lying anterior to the sacrococcygeal junction; blocked for sympathetically mediated perineal, coccygeal, anal, and vaginal pain.

Because these are sympathetic (not somatic) blocks, a successful block relieves visceral/sympathetically-maintained pain while largely sparing motor and somatic sensory function. Celiac plexus neurolysis with alcohol or phenol can dramatically reduce opioid requirements in pancreatic cancer; the classic complication is ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION and DIARRHEA from unopposed splanchnic vasodilation and parasympathetic GI activity, and the feared catastrophic complication is PARAPLEGIA from injury/spasm of the artery of Adamkiewicz. Stellate block risks include recurrent laryngeal nerve block (hoarseness), phrenic nerve block, intravascular (vertebral artery) injection causing SEIZURE, and pneumothorax.

Vertebral landmarks: Stellate ganglion - C7 (Chassaignac tubercle at C6 is the injection target); Celiac plexus - T12-L1; Superior hypogastric plexus - lower L5 to sacral promontory/aortic bifurcation; Ganglion impar - anterior to the sacrococcygeal ligament. Horner syndrome triad = ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis.
Ipsilateral HORNER SYNDROME confirms a stellate ganglion block; the celiac plexus (T12-L1) is the go-to target for pancreatic cancer pain.

cards.title: Tricyclic Antidepressants for Chronic Pain
secs: Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline, desipramine) are first-line for many chronic NON-cancer pain conditions, especially NEUROPATHIC pain such as diabetic neuropathy and postherpetic neuralgia, and are effective at doses LOWER than those needed to treat depression. Their analgesia is multimodal: inhibition of serotonin AND norepinephrine reuptake (enhancing descending inhibitory pathways), sodium-channel blockade, NMDA-receptor antagonism, and opioidergic effects; central alpha-adrenergic activity also contributes. The analgesic effect is often independent of, and appears faster than, the antidepressant effect.

Side effects follow the receptors blocked: ANTICHOLINERGIC (dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision), antihistaminic (sedation, weight gain), and alpha-1 blockade (orthostatic hypotension). The dangerous toxicity is CARDIAC — QRS widening, QT prolongation, and arrhythmias; overdose can be FATAL and wide-complex toxicity is treated with sodium BICARBONATE. Avoid combining with MAO inhibitors (serotonin syndrome). Secondary amines (nortriptyline, desipramine) have fewer anticholinergic effects than amitriptyline and are often better tolerated in the elderly.

*TCAs relieve NEUROPATHIC pain at sub-antidepressant doses via serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibition (plus sodium-channel and NMDA effects); watch anticholinergic effects and QRS/QT prolongation, and treat overdose with sodium BICARBONATE.*

cards.title: Septic Shock; SOFA Score
secs: Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction from a dysregulated host response to infection; septic shock is sepsis with vasopressor-requiring hypotension and a lactate over 2 despite adequate fluids. Management (Surviving Sepsis): early antibiotics plus source control, fluid resuscitation (about 30 mL/kg of crystalloid), NOREPINEPHRINE first-line (adding vasopressin), and reassessment of the perfusion and lactate. The SOFA score quantifies the organ dysfunction (respiratory, coagulation, liver, cardiovascular, CNS, renal); qSOFA (a respiratory rate of 22 or more, altered mentation, a systolic blood pressure of 100 or less) is a bedside screen.
Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor; add vasopressin or epinephrine. Early antibiotics and source control drive the outcome.
Septic shock: vasopressors + lactate > 2 despite fluids; norepinephrine first-line; qSOFA (RR/MS/SBP)

*Septic shock is sepsis with vasopressor-dependent hypotension and a lactate over 2 despite fluids — treat with early antibiotics and source control, about 30 mL/kg of crystalloid, and norepinephrine first-line; SOFA and qSOFA quantify the organ dysfunction.*

head: qSOFA criterion
Threshold
tables.note: Sepsis = infection + SOFA ↑ ≥ 2. Septic shock = pressors for MAP ≥ 65 + lactate > 2.

cards.title: Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS)
secs: The SIRS criteria (2 or more): a temperature over 38 or under 36°C, a heart rate over 90, a respiratory rate over 20 (or a PaCO₂ under 32), and a WBC over 12,000 or under 4,000 (or over 10% bands). It is a nonspecific inflammatory response (an infection means sepsis, but it also occurs with trauma, pancreatitis, burns, and surgery); the newer sepsis definitions emphasize organ dysfunction (SOFA) over SIRS. It identifies systemic inflammation but lacks specificity.
SIRS plus infection historically defined sepsis (which is now SOFA-based). Many non-infectious causes trigger SIRS.

*SIRS (2 or more of: a temperature over 38 or under 36, a heart rate over 90, a respiratory rate over 20, a WBC over 12,000 or under 4,000) is a nonspecific inflammatory response (infectious or not); modern sepsis definitions now emphasize organ dysfunction (SOFA) over SIRS.*

head: Criterion
Threshold
tables.note: Sepsis-3 now favors SOFA/qSOFA over SIRS.

cards.title: Multiple Organ Dysfunction Syndrome
secs: MODS is a progressive, potentially reversible dysfunction of 2 or more organ systems from a severe insult (sepsis, shock, trauma, ischemia-reperfusion); it is a leading cause of ICU mortality. It is driven by a dysregulated inflammation, microcirculatory and mitochondrial dysfunction, and hypoperfusion. Management is supportive: treat the cause, restore the perfusion and oxygen delivery, and provide organ support (ventilation, dialysis, vasopressors).
Prevent further injury (with lung-protective ventilation, perfusion, and source control). The more organs involved, the higher the mortality.

*MODS is a progressive dysfunction of 2 or more organ systems after a severe insult (sepsis, shock, trauma), driven by a dysregulated inflammation and hypoperfusion — management is treating the cause, restoring perfusion, and organ support; the mortality rises with each organ.*

cards.title: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning; Drug Overdose
secs: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin (with a high affinity) → impaired oxygen delivery; pulse oximetry reads FALSELY NORMAL (it can’t distinguish carboxyhemoglobin) → use co-oximetry; treat with 100% oxygen (which reduces the carboxyhemoglobin half-life) and hyperbaric oxygen for severe cases. Drug overdose: supportive care plus specific antidotes (naloxone for opioids, flumazenil cautiously for benzodiazepines, N-acetylcysteine for acetaminophen, bicarbonate for TCAs, and lipid emulsion for local-anesthetic or lipophilic toxicity). Consider co-ingestions, and protect the airway in an obtunded patient.
The SpO₂ is unreliable in CO poisoning (use co-oximetry or an ABG). Lipid emulsion rescues local-anesthetic systemic toxicity.
CO: SpO2 falsely normal → co-oximetry; treat 100% O2 (± HBO); antidotes per agent

*Carbon monoxide impairs oxygen delivery with a falsely normal SpO₂ (use co-oximetry) — treat with 100% oxygen (with or without hyperbaric); overdose care is supportive plus antidotes (naloxone, N-acetylcysteine, bicarbonate for TCAs, lipid emulsion for LAST).*

cards.title: Drowning
secs: Drowning causes hypoxemia from aspiration and laryngospasm and surfactant washout → an ARDS-like lung injury; the primary insult is HYPOXIA (the salt- versus fresh-water distinction is clinically minor). Management: oxygenation and ventilation (lung-protective, with PEEP), rewarming (hypothermia is common — and may be neuroprotective), and supportive critical care; watch for arrhythmias and a delayed pulmonary deterioration. The neurologic outcome depends on the duration and severity of the hypoxia.
Hypothermia in a cold-water drowning can be protective (so continue the resuscitation). Expect a delayed respiratory deterioration (so observe).

*Drowning's core insult is hypoxia (salt versus fresh water matters little) causing an ARDS-like injury — treat with oxygenation, lung-protective ventilation, and PEEP, plus rewarming (cold-water hypothermia may be neuroprotective); watch for a delayed pulmonary deterioration.*

cards.title: Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia; Catheter Sepsis
secs: VAP: a pneumonia 48 hours or more after intubation; prevent it with the ventilator bundle (head-of-bed elevation, daily sedation interruption and spontaneous breathing trials, oral care, DVT and ulcer prophylaxis, and subglottic suction). A catheter-related bloodstream infection: prevent it with the sterile-insertion bundle (hand hygiene, full barrier precautions, chlorhexidine, avoiding the femoral site, and removing unneeded lines). Healthcare-associated infections drive morbidity, mortality, and cost.
The bundles substantially reduce VAP and line infections. Remove invasive devices as soon as possible.

*Ventilator-associated pneumonia (48 hours or more after intubation) and catheter-related bloodstream infections are prevented by bundles — head-up positioning, sedation interruption, and oral care for VAP, and sterile insertion, chlorhexidine, and early removal for lines.*

cards.title: Antimicrobial Classes & Resistance
secs: The major classes: the beta-lactams (penicillins, cephalosporins, carbapenems — acting on the cell wall), vancomycin (for gram-positives and MRSA), the aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones (for gram-negatives), the macrolides, and others; choose by the spectrum and source. Surgical prophylaxis: give it within 60 minutes before incision (vancomycin or a fluoroquinolone within 120 minutes), redose for long cases or blood loss, and stop within 24 hours. Resistance (MRSA, VRE, ESBL, CRE) → antimicrobial stewardship; distinguish allergies from true anaphylaxis (the penicillin cross-reactivity is overestimated).
A timely, appropriately dosed prophylaxis reduces the surgical-site infection rate. Stewardship limits resistance.
Surgical prophylaxis: within 60 min of incision (vanco/FQ 120 min), redose long cases, stop ≤ 24 h

*Know the antimicrobial classes by spectrum (beta-lactams, vancomycin for MRSA, aminoglycosides and fluoroquinolones for gram-negatives); surgical prophylaxis goes within 60 minutes of incision (120 for vancomycin), redosed for long cases — and stewardship combats resistance (MRSA, VRE, CRE).*

head: Class
Example
Note
tables.note: Cefazolin within 60 min of incision is usual surgical prophylaxis.

cards.title: Volume vs Pressure Control; PEEP; Weaning
secs: Volume control: a set tidal volume (guaranteed) with a variable pressure; pressure control: a set inspiratory pressure (which limits barotrauma) with a variable volume. Lung-protective ventilation: about 6 mL/kg of ideal body weight, a plateau pressure under 30, and permissive hypercapnia (in ARDS). PEEP recruits alveoli and improves oxygenation but can reduce the venous return and overdistend; titrate it to the oxygenation and compliance. Weaning: daily spontaneous breathing trials; assess the readiness (oxygenation, hemodynamics, mental status, secretions, and the rapid-shallow-breathing index).
ARDS: a low tidal volume, a plateau under 30, adequate PEEP, and prone positioning. Avoid auto-PEEP (air trapping) in obstruction.
Lung-protective: ~6 mL/kg IBW, plateau < 30; PEEP recruits but ↓ venous return

*Volume control guarantees the tidal volume while pressure control limits the pressure; lung-protective ventilation is about 6 mL/kg of ideal body weight with a plateau under 30 and adequate PEEP (which recruits alveoli but lowers the venous return) — wean via daily spontaneous breathing trials.*

cards.title: Sodium Bicarbonate in Cardiac Arrest
secs: Bicarbonate buffers H+ to form carbonic acid (H2CO3), which dissociates into CO2 and water (H+ + HCO3- <-> H2CO3 <-> CO2 + H2O). During arrest, low pulmonary blood flow and inadequate ventilation prevent that CO2 from being exhaled, so it accumulates. CO2 diffuses freely across cell membranes, whereas HCO3- does not, producing a PARADOXICAL intracellular and cerebral acidosis even as arterial pH may look better.

Routine bicarbonate is NOT recommended in cardiac arrest (no outcome benefit, and it can cause hypernatremia, hyperosmolarity, leftward O2-Hgb shift, and hypokalemia/ionized hypocalcemia). Reserve it for specific indications: hyperkalemia, sodium-channel-blocker/tricyclic overdose (target QRS narrowing), and severe pre-existing metabolic acidosis. Adequate ventilation and chest compressions are the primary means of correcting arrest acidosis.
Bicarb in arrest generates CO2 you can't blow off -> paradoxical intracellular acidosis; reserve for hyperK+, TCA/Na-channel-blocker toxicity.

cards.title: Septic Shock — Elevated SvO2 from Impaired O2 Extraction
secs: SEPTIC SHOCK is the prototype of DISTRIBUTIVE (vasodilatory) shock. Unlike other shock states, mixed venous (SvO2) or central venous (ScvO2) oxygen saturation may be NORMAL OR ELEVATED because peripheral tissues cannot EXTRACT oxygen normally — a combination of microcirculatory shunting, maldistribution of flow, and cytopathic MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION. Oxygen delivery is often high (hyperdynamic, low SVR, high cardiac output) yet cellular utilization fails, so more oxygenated blood returns to the right heart. Lactate rises despite the high SvO2, signaling anaerobic metabolism.

Contrast with hypovolemic, cardiogenic, and obstructive shock, where LOW cardiac output causes a LOW SvO2 (increased extraction). In sepsis, a high SvO2 with a rising lactate is an ominous sign of impaired extraction, not adequate resuscitation — so lactate clearance is a better resuscitation target than SvO2 alone. Management centers on early antibiotics, source control, fluids, and norepinephrine as the first-line vasopressor.
In septic shock, SvO2 can be HIGH because tissues can't extract O2 — a rising lactate, not SvO2, reveals the shock.

cards.title: ATLS: Rapid Primary Assessment by Speech
secs: Per ATLS, a fast initial screen is simply asking the trauma patient their NAME and what happened. A coherent verbal answer simultaneously demonstrates a patent AIRWAY (able to phonate), adequate BREATHING (enough air movement to speak), sufficient CIRCULATION and cerebral perfusion, and adequate DISABILITY/neurologic status (alert enough to respond). In other words, if the patient can answer, the ABCs are grossly intact.

Clear phonation rules out immediate complete airway obstruction and severe hypoxia or hypotension at that moment, but it does NOT exclude an evolving injury, so the structured primary survey still follows. The ATLS primary survey proceeds as ABCDE: Airway with cervical-spine protection, Breathing, Circulation with hemorrhage control, Disability (GCS and pupils), and Exposure/Environment control.
A trauma patient who can state their name and what happened has grossly intact ABCs, because phonation implies a patent airway, adequate breathing, and cerebral perfusion.

cards.title: Adult ACLS Cardiac Arrest Algorithm
secs: The adult cardiac arrest algorithm branches on the rhythm (see figure). SHOCKABLE rhythms (VF / pulseless VT): high-quality CPR, DEFIBRILLATE immediately, resume CPR for 2 minutes, give EPINEPHRINE 1 mg after the second shock and every 3-5 minutes, and add AMIODARONE (300 mg then 150 mg) or lidocaine for refractory VF/pVT. NON-SHOCKABLE rhythms (ASYSTOLE / PEA): CPR with epinephrine 1 mg as soon as possible and every 3-5 minutes; do NOT shock. Cycles are 2 minutes of CPR with rhythm and pulse checks, minimizing interruptions.

Early defibrillation is the strongest determinant of survival in shockable arrest, whereas epinephrine timing differs by rhythm (immediate in asystole/PEA, after the second shock in VF/pVT). Simultaneously search for and treat REVERSIBLE causes, the Hs and Ts.

Compressions 100-120/min, depth 5-6 cm with full recoil; biphasic defibrillation ~120-200 J; epinephrine 1 mg q3-5min; amiodarone 300 mg then 150 mg. Hs: Hypoxia, Hypovolemia, Hydrogen ion/acidosis, Hypo/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia. Ts: Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis (coronary or pulmonary).
Shockable VF/pVT gets shock plus CPR plus epinephrine after the second shock and amiodarone; non-shockable asystole/PEA gets CPR plus immediate epinephrine and no shock, while you hunt the Hs and Ts.

noteimg.cap: AHA adult cardiac arrest algorithm showing the shockable (VF/pVT) and non-shockable (asystole/PEA) branches.

cards.title: Tricyclic Antidepressant (TCA) Overdose
secs: TCA overdose causes toxicity through (1) fast SODIUM-CHANNEL blockade in the myocardium (quinidine-like membrane stabilization), (2) ANTICHOLINERGIC effects, (3) alpha-adrenergic blockade (hypotension), and (4) antihistamine and central effects. The cardinal ECG finding is QRS PROLONGATION, plus a terminal R wave in lead aVR, sinus tachycardia, and risk of wide-complex ventricular tachycardia and hypotension. Anticholinergic toxidrome: hyperthermia, mydriasis, dry flushed skin, urinary retention, ileus, delirium. CNS: SEIZURES and coma. (See figure.)

IV SODIUM BICARBONATE is the critical early intervention for QRS widening and ventricular arrhythmias: the sodium load overcomes channel blockade and serum alkalinization increases drug protein binding, together improving myocardial conduction and survival. Also give benzodiazepines for seizures, hypertonic saline as an alternative sodium source, and norepinephrine for refractory (alpha-blockade) hypotension. AVOID class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmics, which worsen sodium-channel block.
QRS >100 ms predicts seizures; QRS >160 ms predicts ventricular arrhythmias. Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV boluses; target serum pH ~7.45-7.55.
TCA overdose = sodium-channel blockade: wide QRS, terminal R wave in aVR, VT and seizures; treat with IV sodium bicarbonate (target pH 7.45-7.55) and benzodiazepines, and avoid IA/IC antiarrhythmics.

noteimg.cap: TCA toxicity ECG - wide QRS with terminal R wave in aVR; sodium bicarbonate is first-line.

cards.title: Maternal Physiology: Aortocaval Compression; Cardiac Indices
secs: Pregnancy: an increased blood volume (about 40–50%, with the plasma rising more than the red cells → a dilutional anemia), cardiac output (about 40–50%, more in labor), and heart rate; a decreased SVR. Third-trimester specifics: both the stroke volume AND the heart rate rise (driving the cardiac-output increase); the red-cell mass actually increases (but the plasma rises more → a dilutional anemia); and the maternal oxyhemoglobin curve shifts RIGHT (an increased 2,3-DPG, a P50 of about 30) to favor oxygen offloading to the fetus. AORTOCAVAL COMPRESSION (supine hypotension): the gravid uterus compresses the IVC and aorta after about 20 weeks → reduced venous return, hypotension, and fetal compromise → LEFT UTERINE DISPLACEMENT (a left tilt) is essential.
The cardiac output peaks immediately postpartum (an autotransfusion from the contracted uterus). Maintain left uterine displacement for any supine parturient.
Pregnancy: ↑ blood volume/CO ~40–50%, ↓ SVR; aortocaval compression > 20 wk → LEFT tilt

*Pregnancy raises the blood volume and cardiac output about 40–50% with a lower SVR and a dilutional anemia; after about 20 weeks the uterus compresses the aorta and IVC supine (supine hypotension) — always use left uterine displacement.*

head: Parameter
Change at term
tables.note: ↑ CO + ↓ FRC → rapid desaturation; full stomach from ~12–20 wk.

cards.title: Maternal Pulmonary Changes; O2 Consumption; Airway Edema
secs: An increased minute ventilation (mainly from the tidal volume) causes a chronic respiratory alkalosis (a PaCO₂ of about 30, compensated with a low bicarbonate); a decreased FRC plus an increased oxygen consumption causes RAPID desaturation during apnea. The airway: capillary engorgement and edema and a friable mucosa → a DIFFICULT and bloody airway — use a SMALLER ETT; the Mallampati score worsens during pregnancy and labor. Preoxygenate well, and anticipate a difficult airway and rapid desaturation.
A normal pregnant PaCO₂ is about 30 mmHg (don’t “normalize” it to 40). Avoid nasal instrumentation (epistaxis).
Pregnancy: ↓ FRC + ↑ O2 consumption → fast desaturation; PaCO2 ~30; smaller ETT (airway edema)

*Pregnancy raises the minute ventilation (a PaCO₂ of about 30, a compensated alkalosis) while lowering the FRC and raising oxygen use → rapid desaturation; airway edema makes intubation difficult and bloody — preoxygenate and use a smaller ETT.*

cards.title: Maternal Gastric & GI Changes; Aspiration Risk
secs: Progesterone relaxes the lower esophageal sphincter and the gravid uterus raises the intragastric pressure → an increased reflux and ASPIRATION risk; treat every parturient as a full stomach (especially in labor or after opioids). Aspiration prophylaxis: a non-particulate antacid (sodium citrate), an H2 blocker, and metoclopramide; use rapid-sequence induction with cricoid pressure for general anesthesia. It was historically the leading cause of anesthetic maternal mortality (Mendelson’s syndrome) → so favor neuraxial over general anesthesia.
Gastric emptying is relatively preserved until labor (then delayed, especially with opioids). Neuraxial anesthesia avoids the airway and aspiration risk.

*Pregnancy lowers the LES tone and raises the gastric pressure → a high aspiration risk (a full stomach) → give aspiration prophylaxis and use RSI with cricoid pressure for general anesthesia; neuraxial is preferred partly to avoid this airway and aspiration danger.*

cards.title: Maternal Hematologic Changes & Hypercoagulability
secs: Pregnancy is a HYPERCOAGULABLE state (an increased fibrinogen and most clotting factors, a decreased protein S, and venous stasis) → an elevated VTE and pulmonary-embolism risk (a leading cause of maternal mortality) → use thromboprophylaxis. There is a dilutional anemia (the plasma volume rising more than the red cells), and a mild physiologic (gestational) thrombocytopenia is common. The increased fibrinogen means a “normal” non-pregnant fibrinogen may indicate consumption (for example, in abruption or DIC).

Time neuraxial procedures around the thromboprophylaxis (the ASRA and anticoagulation guidelines). Watch for obstetric DIC (from abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, retained products, or preeclampsia).

*Pregnancy is hypercoagulable (an increased fibrinogen and factors, a decreased protein S, and stasis) → a high VTE and pulmonary-embolism risk; a dilutional anemia and a mild gestational thrombocytopenia are normal, and a “normal” fibrinogen may signal consumption (abruption or DIC).*

cards.title: Placental Anatomy & Exchange
secs: The placenta exchanges gas, nutrients, and waste between the maternal and fetal circulations (separated by the placental membrane); the uteroplacental flow is about 700 mL/min at term and is PRESSURE-PASSIVE (not autoregulated) → so maternal hypotension reduces the fetal perfusion. The gas exchange depends on the maternal perfusion, the gradient, and the membrane; the fetus extracts oxygen efficiently (HbF, the Bohr and Haldane double effects). Avoid reductions in the uteroplacental flow (maternal hypotension, excessive uterine tone, and vasoconstrictors that reduce the uterine flow).

Phenylephrine and ephedrine maintain the maternal blood pressure (phenylephrine is now preferred — it causes less fetal acidosis). Maintain the maternal blood pressure and left tilt to protect the fetus.
Uteroplacental flow ~700 mL/min at term, pressure-passive (not autoregulated)

*The placenta exchanges gas and nutrients across its membrane with an about-700-mL/min pressure-passive (non-autoregulated) uteroplacental flow — so maternal hypotension directly lowers the fetal perfusion; maintain the maternal blood pressure and left tilt.*

cards.title: Fetal Circulation & Neonatal Transition
secs: Fetal circulation: the oxygenated blood from the placenta (the umbilical vein) bypasses the liver (the ductus venosus) and the lungs (the foramen ovale and ductus arteriosus); the most oxygenated blood is directed to the brain and heart. At birth: lung expansion plus the increased oxygen lowers the pulmonary vascular resistance, and cord clamping raises the systemic vascular resistance, with a functional closure of the foramen ovale and ductus arteriosus (the ductus closing with the rising oxygen and falling prostaglandins). Hypoxia, acidosis, and hypothermia raise the pulmonary vascular resistance and can reopen the fetal shunts (a persistent fetal circulation).
Prostaglandin E1 maintains the ductal patency for duct-dependent lesions. The transition can reverse with neonatal hypoxemia.

*Fetal circulation shunts the oxygenated placental blood past the liver and lungs (the ductus venosus, foramen ovale, and ductus arteriosus); at birth, the falling pulmonary resistance and rising systemic resistance close the shunts — but hypoxia, acidosis, and hypothermia can reopen them.*

head: Shunt
Connects
Becomes
tables.note: Close after birth with ↑ O₂ & ↓ PVR; prostaglandin keeps a PDA open.

cards.title: Oxytocic Drugs
secs: Oxytocin is the first-line uterotonic for uterine atony and postpartum hemorrhage; a rapid bolus causes HYPOTENSION, tachycardia, and (in large doses) water retention and hyponatremia → so give it diluted by a slow infusion. Methylergonovine (an ergot) causes vasoconstriction and HYPERTENSION → AVOID it in hypertension and preeclampsia. Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) causes BRONCHOSPASM → AVOID it in asthma; it also causes fever and GI effects. Misoprostol (PGE1) is an alternative.
Match the uterotonic to the comorbidity (avoid ergot in hypertension, carboprost in asthma). Treat atony stepwise (oxytocin → second-line agents).
Oxytocin → hypotension (give slow); methylergonovine → HTN (avoid in preeclampsia); carboprost → bronchospasm (avoid in asthma)

*Oxytocin (first-line for atony) causes hypotension if bolused; methylergonovine causes hypertension (avoid it in preeclampsia); carboprost causes bronchospasm (avoid it in asthma) — match the uterotonic to the comorbidities.*

head: Drug
Caution
tables.note: First-line for atony is oxytocin; escalate stepwise.

cards.title: Tocolytic Drugs
secs: Tocolytics inhibit preterm labor: the beta-2 agonists (terbutaline — causing tachycardia, hyperglycemia, pulmonary edema), magnesium sulfate (which also gives fetal neuroprotection), the calcium-channel blockers (nifedipine), the oxytocin antagonists (atosiban), and the NSAIDs (indomethacin — with ductus-closure and oligohydramnios concerns). The side effects matter for anesthesia: the beta-agonists cause tachycardia and pulmonary edema, and magnesium potentiates neuromuscular blockade and causes hypotension. Tocolysis is generally short-term (for example, to allow steroids or transfer).
Magnesium prolongs and potentiates the non-depolarizers (reduce the dose). Watch for pulmonary edema with the beta-agonists.

*Tocolytics for preterm labor (beta-agonists, magnesium, nifedipine, atosiban, indomethacin) carry anesthetic-relevant effects — the beta-agonists cause tachycardia and pulmonary edema, and magnesium potentiates neuromuscular blockade and causes hypotension.*

head: Drug
Mechanism
Caution
tables.note: Used to delay preterm labor; watch maternal & fetal effects.

cards.title: Magnesium Sulfate (Maternal vs Fetal)
secs: Magnesium is used for preeclampsia and eclampsia seizure prophylaxis and for fetal neuroprotection; the therapeutic range is about 4–7 mEq/L (about 5–9 mg/dL). The toxicity is progressive: a loss of the deep tendon reflexes (at about 10 mEq/L) → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest; treat the toxicity with IV CALCIUM and stop the magnesium. Magnesium POTENTIATES the neuromuscular blockers (reduce the relaxant doses), causes vasodilation and hypotension, and crosses the placenta (causing neonatal hypotonia and respiratory depression).
Monitor the reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output during the infusion. Reduce the neuromuscular-blocker dosing and have calcium available.
Mg therapeutic ~4–7 mEq/L; lost DTRs ~10; respiratory depression higher → treat with IV calcium

*Magnesium (for preeclampsia seizure prophylaxis, therapeutic at about 4–7 mEq/L) is toxic progressively — lost reflexes (at about 10) → respiratory and cardiac depression (treat with IV calcium); it potentiates the neuromuscular blockers and causes hypotension.*

cards.title: Placental Drug Transfer
secs: Most drugs cross the placenta by passive diffusion, favored by a LOW molecular weight, high lipid solubility, low protein binding, and being non-ionized. Highly ionized or large molecules cross poorly: the muscle relaxants (quaternary, ionized), heparin (large), and glycopyrrolate (quaternary) → minimal fetal transfer; the lipophilic drugs (most anesthetics, opioids, local anesthetics) cross readily. “Ion trapping”: a more acidic fetus traps ionized weak bases (such as local anesthetics) → fetal accumulation (worse with fetal acidosis).
Atropine crosses (causing fetal tachycardia); glycopyrrolate does not. Consider the fetal effects of maternally administered drugs.
Cross readily: low MW, lipophilic, unionized, low protein binding; poorly: relaxants/heparin/glyco

*Drugs cross the placenta by diffusion favored by a low molecular weight, lipophilicity, and non-ionization (anesthetics and opioids cross; muscle relaxants, heparin, and glycopyrrolate don’t) — and fetal acidosis “ion-traps” local anesthetics.*

cards.title: Fetal Heart Rate Monitoring; NICHD Criteria
secs: The normal fetal heart-rate baseline is 110–160; a moderate VARIABILITY is reassuring (reflecting an intact autonomic function and oxygenation). Accelerations are reassuring; the decelerations — EARLY (head compression, benign), VARIABLE (cord compression), and LATE (uteroplacental insufficiency; repetitive late decelerations suggest fetal hypoxia and acidosis). The NICHD three-tier system: Category I (normal), II (indeterminate), and III (abnormal — an absent variability with recurrent late or variable decelerations, or bradycardia → act).

Intrauterine resuscitation: a left tilt, oxygen, fluids, stopping the oxytocin, treating hypotension (a vasopressor), and considering delivery. A loss of variability plus late decelerations is the worrisome pattern. Early decelerations MIRROR the contraction (their onset, nadir, and recovery coincide with it) and reflect a benign head compression — not hypoxia.
FHR baseline 110–160; LATE decels = uteroplacental insufficiency; NICHD Cat I/II/III

*The normal fetal heart rate is 110–160 with moderate variability (reassuring); late decelerations signal uteroplacental insufficiency and variable decelerations signal cord compression — the NICHD Category III pattern (absent variability plus recurrent decelerations) demands action.*

head: Category
Interpretation
rows: Normal: 110–160, moderate variability, no late/variable decels
Absent variability + recurrent late/variable decels or bradycardia; sinusoidal
tables.note: Decels: early = head compression; variable = cord; late = uteroplacental insufficiency.

cards.title: Biophysical Profile; Nonstress/Stress Test
secs: The nonstress test (NST): fetal heart-rate accelerations with movement → “reactive” (reassuring); a nonreactive test prompts further testing. The biophysical profile (BPP): the NST plus an ultrasound of the fetal breathing, movement, tone, and amniotic-fluid volume (each scored 0 or 2, for a maximum of 10) → assesses fetal wellbeing. The contraction stress test: the fetal heart-rate response to contractions; late decelerations suggest uteroplacental insufficiency.
A low BPP or abnormal testing may prompt delivery. Oligohydramnios is a concerning component.
BPP: NST + breathing + movement + tone + fluid (each 0/2, max 10)

*The nonstress test (reactive = accelerations with movement) and the biophysical profile (the NST plus breathing, movement, tone, and fluid, for a maximum of 10) assess fetal wellbeing; a contraction stress test with late decelerations suggests uteroplacental insufficiency.*

cards.title: Epidural, Spinal, CSE, Dural-Puncture Epidural
secs: Epidural: a catheter in the epidural space — titratable, with a slower onset, good for labor and for conversion to cesarean anesthesia. Spinal: a single intrathecal dose — rapid, with a dense block (for cesarean), but a limited duration. The combined spinal-epidural (CSE): a fast spinal onset plus an epidural catheter for prolongation.
