The dural-puncture epidural (DPE): a dural puncture without intrathecal drug (which improves the spread and symmetry) with epidural dosing — fewer side effects than a CSE.
A test dose detects intravascular or intrathecal placement. The choice balances the onset, density, duration, and side effects.

*Labor neuraxial options: the epidural (a titratable catheter), the spinal (fast, dense, single-shot), the CSE (a fast onset plus a catheter), and the dural-puncture epidural (a better spread than a plain epidural, with fewer side effects than a CSE).*

cards.title: PIEB & PCEA Infusion Methods
secs: Patient-controlled epidural analgesia (PCEA): patient-triggered boluses, with or without a background infusion → individualized labor analgesia with lower drug use. Programmed intermittent epidural bolus (PIEB): automated timed boluses → a better spread than a continuous infusion (the higher injection pressure distributes it farther) → improved analgesia and lower local-anesthetic consumption. These are often combined (PIEB plus PCEA) for modern labor analgesia.
PIEB improves the spread versus a continuous infusion. A dilute local anesthetic plus an opioid minimizes the motor block.

*Modern labor epidural delivery uses programmed intermittent boluses (PIEB — a better spread than a continuous infusion) plus patient-controlled boluses (PCEA), reducing the local-anesthetic use while improving the analgesia.*

cards.title: Epidural Fever; Hypotension; Pruritus
secs: Hypotension: from the sympathetic block → treat with a left tilt, fluids, and vasopressors (phenylephrine); it is the most common side effect. Epidural-associated maternal fever: a noninfectious (inflammatory) temperature rise with labor epidurals → which can prompt sepsis workups and antibiotics; the mechanism is debated. Pruritus: from the neuraxial OPIOIDS (not histamine) → treat with low-dose naloxone or nalbuphine (not primarily antihistamines).
Phenylephrine is preferred for maternal hypotension (it causes less fetal acidosis). Neuraxial-opioid pruritus responds to opioid antagonists.

*Labor epidural side effects: hypotension (treat with a left tilt, fluids, and phenylephrine), a noninfectious maternal fever (which can trigger a sepsis workup), and a neuraxial-opioid pruritus (treat with naloxone or nalbuphine, not antihistamines).*

cards.title: Post-Dural Puncture Headache & Epidural Blood Patch
secs: PDPH: a CSF leak after a dural puncture → a POSITIONAL headache (worse upright, better supine), often frontal or occipital, with or without neck stiffness, photophobia, and tinnitus; the risk is higher with larger or cutting needles and in young parturients. Conservative treatment: time, hydration, caffeine, analgesics, and recumbency. The definitive treatment: an epidural blood patch (autologous blood into the epidural space seals the leak and raises the pressure), effective in most cases.
Use small-gauge pencil-point needles to reduce the risk. Rule out other (dangerous) causes of a postpartum headache.
PDPH: positional headache (upright worse); definitive treatment = epidural blood patch

*A post-dural puncture headache is a positional headache (worse upright) from a CSF leak (larger or cutting needles, young parturients) — treat it conservatively first, then with an epidural blood patch (the definitive treatment).*

head: Feature
Detail
rows: Conservative; epidural blood patch if severe
tables.note: Higher risk: large/cutting needle, young, female, pregnant.

cards.title: Sphenopalatine Ganglion Block
secs: A minimally invasive option for PDPH: a topical local anesthetic applied transnasally to the sphenopalatine ganglion → which modulates the parasympathetic cerebral vasomotor tone. It is less invasive than an epidural blood patch and may provide relief or bridge to a blood patch. It is simple, repeatable, and low-risk.
The evidence is growing but less definitive than for a blood patch. It is a reasonable first-line procedural option for PDPH.

*The sphenopalatine ganglion block (a transnasal topical local anesthetic) is a minimally invasive treatment for a post-dural puncture headache — a low-risk option or bridge before an epidural blood patch.*

cards.title: Trial of Labor After Cesarean
secs: TOLAC offers a vaginal birth after a prior cesarean; the main risk is UTERINE RUPTURE (which is higher with classical or vertical incisions, which contraindicate TOLAC). Epidural analgesia is appropriate and does NOT mask a rupture (the breakthrough pain, the abnormal fetal heart rate — often the first sign — and the hemodynamic change remain detectable). It requires the capability for an emergency cesarean; counsel on the risks and benefits.

A sudden fetal bradycardia, a loss of station, and severe pain suggest a rupture. Continuous fetal heart-rate monitoring is essential. An early-deceleration pattern that mirrors the contractions is a benign head compression, not a rupture; a uterine rupture instead shows recurrent variable or late decelerations or bradycardia, often with a loss of station, rising pain, and a loss of uterine tone.

*TOLAC’s main risk is uterine rupture (a classical incision contraindicates it); epidural analgesia is appropriate and doesn’t mask a rupture (fetal heart-rate changes are often the first sign) — emergency cesarean capability is required.*

cards.title: Neuraxial Hypotension: Prophylaxis & Treatment
secs: Spinal anesthesia for cesarean causes a rapid sympathectomy → hypotension (which is common) → maternal nausea and fetal compromise. Prophylaxis and treatment: a left uterine displacement, co-loading with fluids, and a PHENYLEPHRINE infusion (first-line — it causes less fetal acidosis than ephedrine); use ephedrine if the patient is bradycardic. Prevent rather than react (start the vasopressor early).
Phenylephrine can cause a reflex bradycardia (treat it with glycopyrrolate or ephedrine). Maintain the maternal blood pressure near baseline to protect the fetus.
Cesarean spinal hypotension: left tilt + fluid co-load + phenylephrine infusion (first-line)

*Spinal anesthesia for cesarean causes a rapid hypotension — prevent and treat it with a left uterine displacement, fluid co-loading, and a prophylactic phenylephrine infusion (first-line, with less fetal acidosis than ephedrine).*

cards.title: GA for Cesarean: Difficult Airway & Aspiration Prophylaxis
secs: General anesthesia for cesarean is higher-risk: a failed intubation (from the airway edema, weight gain, and breast engorgement) and aspiration (from the full stomach) — leading causes of obstetric anesthetic morbidity. Use rapid-sequence induction with cricoid pressure, aspiration prophylaxis (sodium citrate, an H2 blocker, metoclopramide), preoxygenation, a smaller ETT, a video laryngoscope, and a difficult-airway plan. Neuraxial anesthesia is preferred when possible; awareness is a risk if the anesthesia is light before delivery.
Have a failed-intubation and airway plan (an LMA; wake up versus proceed). Volatile-induced uterine relaxation can worsen bleeding (limit the MAC after delivery).

*General anesthesia for cesarean is higher-risk (a difficult airway from edema and weight; aspiration from a full stomach) — use RSI with cricoid pressure, aspiration prophylaxis, preoxygenation, a smaller ETT and video laryngoscope, and a difficult-airway plan; neuraxial is preferred.*

cards.title: Enhanced Recovery After Cesarean; TAP & ESP Blocks
secs: ERAC bundles: neuraxial morphine for analgesia, multimodal non-opioid analgesia (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), early feeding and mobilization, euvolemia, normothermia, and PONV prophylaxis. Fascial-plane blocks (TAP, QL, ESP) provide a somatic abdominal-wall analgesia when neuraxial morphine isn’t used, or as a supplement (they don’t cover visceral pain). The goal is an opioid-sparing recovery and early bonding and breastfeeding.
Neuraxial morphine is the analgesic backbone, and the blocks are adjuncts or rescue. Watch the total local-anesthetic dose with bilateral blocks.

*Enhanced recovery after cesarean uses neuraxial morphine plus multimodal non-opioid analgesia and early feeding and mobilization; TAP, QL, and ESP blocks add a somatic abdominal-wall analgesia (not visceral) when neuraxial morphine isn’t used.*

cards.title: Apgar Scoring; Umbilical Cord Blood Gas
secs: The Apgar score (at 1 and 5 minutes): Appearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, and Respiration — each scored 0–2 (for a maximum of 10); it assesses the neonatal transition (it is not designed to diagnose asphyxia). The umbilical cord blood gas (arterial) objectively assesses the fetal acid-base status at birth (a metabolic acidosis suggests a hypoxic-ischemic insult). A low or declining Apgar prompts resuscitation per the NRP.
The Apgar guides the response, and the cord gas documents the acid-base status. The heart rate is the most important resuscitation indicator.
Apgar (A-P-G-A-R), each 0–2, at 1 & 5 min; cord arterial gas = fetal acid-base

*The Apgar score (Appearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, Respiration — each 0–2, at 1 and 5 minutes) assesses the neonatal transition, while the umbilical cord arterial gas objectively documents the fetal acid-base status (a metabolic acidosis = a hypoxic insult).*

head: Sign
tables.note: Assess at 1 and 5 min; resuscitation is not delayed for scoring.

cards.title: Neonatal Resuscitation Program
secs: The NRP algorithm prioritizes VENTILATION (the key to neonatal resuscitation — most arrests are respiratory): warm, dry, and stimulate → and if the infant is apneic or the heart rate is under 100, give positive-pressure ventilation. The HEART RATE guides escalation: a heart rate under 60 despite effective ventilation → chest compressions (3:1 with ventilation) → then epinephrine. Start a term resuscitation in room air (titrating the oxygen to preductal saturation targets); use delayed cord clamping when the infant is stable.
Effective ventilation usually corrects the neonatal bradycardia. The compressions-to-ventilations ratio is 3:1 in neonates.
NRP: ventilate first; HR < 60 despite ventilation → compressions (3:1) → epinephrine

*Neonatal resuscitation centers on VENTILATION (most arrests are respiratory) — warm, dry, and stimulate, then positive-pressure ventilation; if the heart rate stays under 60 despite effective ventilation, add 3:1 compressions, then epinephrine.*

cards.title: Ectopic Pregnancy; Cerclage; D&C/D&E
secs: A ruptured ectopic pregnancy is a hemorrhagic-shock emergency → resuscitate, get large-bore access and blood, and use a rapid-sequence induction (the patient has a full stomach). Cervical cerclage is a suture for cervical insufficiency — usually done under neuraxial anesthesia; uterine relaxation may be needed. D&C and D&E are short procedures, often under MAC or general anesthesia; the risks are bleeding, perforation, and (for D&E) hemorrhage — give oxytocics for uterine tone.
An ectopic is a surgical emergency with the potential for massive hemorrhage. Use aspiration precautions in pregnancy.

*A ruptured ectopic pregnancy is a hemorrhagic-shock emergency (resuscitate, blood, RSI); cervical cerclage treats cervical insufficiency (usually neuraxial); and D&C and D&E risk bleeding and perforation.*

cards.title: External Cephalic Version
secs: External cephalic version is the manual rotation of a breech fetus to cephalic before delivery; it is performed near term with tocolysis (terbutaline) and continuous fetal heart-rate monitoring. Neuraxial anesthesia improves the success and comfort; be ready for an emergency cesarean (the risks are fetal distress, abruption, and cord issues). It is done in a setting prepared for immediate delivery.
Tocolysis (uterine relaxation) facilitates the version. Monitor for fetal compromise during and after.

*External cephalic version manually rotates a breech fetus to cephalic near term with tocolysis and fetal heart-rate monitoring; neuraxial anesthesia improves the success — perform it where an emergency cesarean is immediately available.*

cards.title: Fetal Surgery & EXIT Procedures
secs: Fetal surgery (for example, myelomeningocele repair) requires a profound UTERINE RELAXATION (high-dose volatile, with or without nitroglycerin) and the maintenance of uteroplacental perfusion; both the mother and the fetus are anesthetized (the fetus via placental transfer or directly). EXIT (ex utero intrapartum treatment) secures the fetal airway while the fetus is still on placental support (with the uteroplacental gas exchange maintained) before cord clamping — for airway-obstructing lesions. Manage the maternal hemorrhage risk (uterine atony from the relaxation) and the fetal monitoring.
Uterine relaxation is essential but increases the bleeding risk. Maintain the placental circulation during EXIT until the airway is secured.

*Fetal surgery and EXIT procedures need a profound uterine relaxation (high-dose volatile, with or without nitroglycerin) while maintaining uteroplacental perfusion; EXIT secures the fetal airway on placental support before cord clamping — watch for uterine atony and hemorrhage.*

cards.title: Non-Obstetric Surgery During Pregnancy
secs: The principles: avoid teratogens, maintain the maternal oxygenation and perfusion (avoiding hypoxia, hypotension, and hypocarbia to preserve the uteroplacental flow), and avoid preterm labor; defer elective surgery, ideally to the second trimester. No anesthetic is proven teratogenic at clinical doses; nitrous oxide is often limited in early pregnancy (this is debated). Use fetal monitoring per the gestational age and left uterine displacement after about 20 weeks. Use aspiration precautions (treat the patient as having a full stomach) in later pregnancy.
The best fetal protection is a healthy, well-perfused, normocapnic mother. Obtain obstetric consultation and perioperative fetal and uterine monitoring.

*For non-obstetric surgery in pregnancy: avoid teratogens, maintain the maternal oxygenation, perfusion, and normocarbia (to protect the uteroplacental flow), prevent preterm labor, and defer elective surgery to the second trimester — the healthy mother is the best fetal protection.*

cards.title: Preeclampsia / Gestational Hypertension
secs: Preeclampsia is a new hypertension after 20 weeks, with or without proteinuria or end-organ dysfunction; the severe features are a blood pressure of 160/110 or higher, headache or visual changes, pulmonary edema, thrombocytopenia, hepatic or renal involvement, and HELLP; eclampsia is the addition of seizures. Management: control the blood pressure (labetalol, hydralazine, nicardipine), give MAGNESIUM for seizure prophylaxis, and delivery (the definitive treatment); neuraxial is preferred (check the platelets — there can be coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia). Watch for airway edema (a harder intubation), an exaggerated pressor response to laryngoscopy (blunt it), pulmonary edema, and the magnesium-neuromuscular-blocker interaction.
HELLP is hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets. Avoid hypertensive surges at intubation (with esmolol, remifentanil, or labetalol).
Preeclampsia: BP control + magnesium (seizure ppx) + delivery; neuraxial if platelets adequate

*Preeclampsia (hypertension after 20 weeks, with or without end-organ involvement; severe at 160/110 or higher, or HELLP) is managed with blood-pressure control, magnesium seizure prophylaxis, and delivery; neuraxial is preferred (check the platelets) and blunt the intubation pressor response.*

head: Feature
Threshold
tables.note: Magnesium for seizure prophylaxis (therapeutic 4–7 mg/dL); calcium for toxicity.

cards.title: Maternal Cardiac Disease & Pulmonary Hypertension
secs: Pregnancy's increased volume and cardiac output stress fixed cardiac lesions; cardiac disease is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Pulmonary hypertension and Eisenmenger syndrome carry a very high mortality — avoid increases in the pulmonary vascular resistance and decreases in the SVR, maintain the preload and RV function, and consider a slow incremental neuraxial. Stenotic lesions tolerate pregnancy and delivery poorly (a fixed output); peripartum cardiomyopathy presents in late pregnancy or postpartum.
The postpartum autotransfusion and fluid shift is a high-risk period. Use tailored (often invasive) monitoring and a multidisciplinary plan.

*Pregnancy's volume and cardiac-output rise stresses cardiac lesions (a top cause of maternal death); pulmonary hypertension and Eisenmenger are very high-risk (avoid pulmonary-resistance rises and SVR drops, and support the right ventricle) — plan carefully with a slow incremental neuraxial and monitoring.*

cards.title: Gestational Diabetes; Thyroid; Autoimmune Disease
secs: Gestational diabetes: macrosomia (with shoulder dystocia), neonatal hypoglycemia, and polyhydramnios; control the maternal glucose (and watch for neonatal hypoglycemia after delivery). Thyroid: hyperthyroidism (with a risk of storm and heart failure) and hypothyroidism affect both the mother and the fetus; manage as in the non-pregnant patient plus fetal considerations. Autoimmune disease (for example, SLE, antiphospholipid syndrome): thrombosis and anticoagulation (affecting neuraxial timing), organ involvement, and fetal effects (neonatal lupus and heart block).
Antiphospholipid syndrome → anticoagulation affecting the neuraxial timing. Optimize the maternal disease before delivery where possible.

*Gestational diabetes risks macrosomia and neonatal hypoglycemia (control the glucose); thyroid disease affects the mother and fetus; and autoimmune disease (SLE, antiphospholipid) brings thrombosis and anticoagulation (neuraxial timing) and fetal effects.*

cards.title: Maternal Hemorrhage & Coagulopathy (ITP, vWD)
secs: Obstetric hemorrhage (atony — the most common; placenta previa or accreta, abruption, retained products, or rupture) is a leading cause of maternal death → treat with uterotonics, massive transfusion (1:1:1), tranexamic acid, and surgical or interventional-radiology control. ITP (immune thrombocytopenia): the platelet count guides the neuraxial safety; treat it (with steroids, IVIG) if it is low. Von Willebrand disease (the most common inherited bleeding disorder): the von Willebrand factor and factor VIII often rise in pregnancy (in type 1) — which may allow neuraxial; use DDAVP or concentrates as needed.
The placenta accreta spectrum → a planned massive transfusion and a possible hysterectomy. Check the platelets and coagulation before neuraxial.
OB hemorrhage: uterotonics + 1:1:1 transfusion + TXA; accreta → planned massive transfusion

*Obstetric hemorrhage (atony, accreta, abruption) is a leading maternal killer — treat with uterotonics, balanced 1:1:1 transfusion, and tranexamic acid; ITP and von Willebrand disease (which often improves in pregnancy) guide the neuraxial safety by the platelet and factor levels.*

head: Cause
tables.note: Uterotonics: oxytocin, methylergonovine (avoid in HTN), carboprost (avoid in asthma).

cards.title: Substance Use Disorder in Pregnancy
secs: Opioids: continue the maintenance (methadone, buprenorphine), expect higher analgesic needs and neonatal abstinence syndrome, and avoid precipitating withdrawal. Cocaine and stimulants: hypertension, arrhythmia, placental abruption, and hyperthermia → avoid ephedrine (an exaggerated response), treat the hypertension directly, and use ketamine cautiously. Alcohol: fetal alcohol effects, plus withdrawal management.
Use multimodal and regional analgesia, and coordinate the addiction care. Cocaine intoxication contraindicates certain sympathomimetics.

*In pregnancy, continue opioid maintenance (methadone, buprenorphine; expect higher needs and neonatal abstinence); cocaine brings hypertension, arrhythmia, and abruption (avoid ephedrine, treat the hypertension directly) — coordinate multimodal analgesia and addiction care.*

cards.title: Umbilical Cord Circulation & Normal Fetal Blood Gas Values
secs: In the fetal circulation the UMBILICAL VEIN carries OXYGENATED blood from the placenta TO the fetus, while the paired UMBILICAL ARTERIES carry DEOXYGENATED, waste-laden blood AWAY from the fetus back to the placenta (the reverse of the postnatal artery/vein oxygen convention). At delivery, umbilical ARTERIAL cord gas best reflects the fetal acid-base status. Normal umbilical artery values are approximately: pH 7.2-7.3, PaCO2 50-55 mmHg, PaO2 18-25 mmHg, bicarbonate 22-25 mEq/L, and base excess -2.7 to -4.7. Fetal PaO2 is normally very low compared with adults yet adequate because fetal hemoglobin has high O2 affinity.

A low umbilical artery pH with a large negative base excess (metabolic acidosis) indicates significant intrapartum hypoxia/asphyxia; a pH <7.0 or 7.1 with base deficit >12 mEq/L is a marker of pathologic acidemia. A respiratory pattern (high PaCO2, near-normal base excess) suggests a more acute, recoverable cord-compression event. The umbilical VEIN gas is less acidotic and higher in PaO2 than the artery.
Normal umbilical ARTERY: pH 7.2-7.3, PaCO2 50-55 mmHg, PaO2 18-25 mmHg, HCO3 22-25 mEq/L, BE -2.7 to -4.7. Pathologic acidemia: pH <7.0, base deficit >12 mEq/L.
Umbilical ARTERY carries deoxygenated blood away from the fetus; normal cord-artery pH is 7.2-7.3 with PaO2 only 18-25 mmHg.

cards.title: Neuraxial Labor Analgesia: Effects
secs: Neuraxial (epidural or combined spinal-epidural) labor analgesia PROLONGS the SECOND STAGE of labor, and ACOG permits an additional hour before diagnosing arrest of descent. Importantly, it does NOT increase the cesarean delivery rate, and early placement does not raise cesarean risk. Documented benefits include reduced maternal CATECHOLAMINE (epinephrine) release, which improves UTEROPLACENTAL PERFUSION and fetal oxygen delivery, reduced systemic opioid and naloxone requirements, a lower likelihood of neonatal RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION, and a lower likelihood of umbilical artery pH < 7.2 (less fetal acidosis).

Neuraxial analgesia is the gold standard for labor pain. Its blunting of the maternal stress-catecholamine surge is especially valuable in preeclampsia and maternal cardiac disease. Recognized trade-offs include maternal hypotension, pruritus, maternal fever, and a modest increase in instrumented vaginal deliveries.
Umbilical artery pH threshold of concern < 7.2; neuraxial may extend the second stage by roughly 1 hour.
Neuraxial labor analgesia lengthens the second stage but not the cesarean rate, and by blunting catecholamines it improves uteroplacental perfusion and reduces fetal acidosis (UA pH < 7.2).

cards.title: Placenta Accreta Spectrum (PAS)
secs: Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) describes ABNORMAL, overly firm placental attachment due to defective decidua basalis, so the placenta fails to separate after delivery. Severity increases along a continuum: ACCRETA (villi adhere to myometrium WITHOUT muscle invasion) < INCRETA (villi INVADE the myometrium) < PERCRETA (villi penetrate THROUGH the uterine serosa and may invade adjacent organs such as bladder or bowel). The dominant risk factors are PLACENTA PREVIA combined with PRIOR CESAREAN DELIVERIES (uterine scarring); other risks include prior uterine surgery/curettage and advanced maternal age.

PAS is a leading cause of catastrophic POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE (PPH) and peripartum hysterectomy. Anesthetic management centers on antenatal diagnosis (ultrasound/MRI), delivery at a center with a multidisciplinary team, planned cesarean hysterectomy, LARGE-BORE IV access, blood products/massive transfusion protocol ready, cell salvage, and often invasive arterial/central monitoring. General anesthesia is frequently chosen or converted to given the likelihood of massive hemorrhage and prolonged surgery, though a neuraxial-to-general conversion plan is common. Anticipate DIC and coagulopathy; consider fibrinogen/cryoprecipitate and tranexamic acid.
Risk of PAS in a woman WITH placenta previa rises with the number of prior cesarean deliveries: 0 prior CD ~3%; 1 prior ~11%; 2 prior ~40%; 3 prior ~60%; 4 or more ~67%.
PAS severity is accreta < increta < percreta; placenta previa plus increasing prior cesareans drives the risk (up to ~60-67% with 3+ priors) — prepare for massive PPH and cesarean hysterectomy.

cards.title: Antenatal Corticosteroids in Preterm Labor
secs: Pre-delivery (antenatal) corticosteroids — BETAMETHASONE or dexamethasone — given to mothers in preterm labor reduce neonatal morbidity and mortality, chiefly by accelerating fetal lung maturity and surfactant production and thereby lowering respiratory distress syndrome, intraventricular hemorrhage, and necrotizing enterocolitis. Preterm delivery is defined as birth after 20 and before 37 weeks gestation and occurs in 12-13% of US pregnancies; prematurity is a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality. Tocolytic therapy (magnesium, nifedipine, terbutaline, indomethacin) has NOT been shown to reduce preterm birth or improve neonatal outcome.

Because tocolytics do not improve the baby's outcome, their real value is briefly delaying delivery about 48 hours to allow the steroid course to take effect and to permit maternal transport to a center with neonatal care. Steroids are most beneficial when given roughly 24 hours to 7 days before delivery, generally between 24 and 34 weeks. Magnesium sulfate given before anticipated early preterm birth also provides fetal NEUROPROTECTION (reduces cerebral palsy); note that magnesium potentiates non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockers and can cause maternal hypotension and weakness.
*Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone/dexamethasone) reduce neonatal RDS, IVH, and death in preterm labor; tocolytics do NOT improve neonatal outcome but buy ~48 hours for the steroids to work.*

cards.title: Pediatric Breathing Circuits; Cuffed vs Uncuffed ETT
secs: Children have a higher metabolic rate and oxygen consumption and a smaller FRC, so they desaturate quickly; the circuits were historically Mapleson and Jackson-Rees (low resistance and dead space), though circle systems are now common. ETT sizing: the uncuffed internal diameter (mm) is about (age/4) + 4, the cuffed about (age/4) + 3.5, and the depth about 3 × the tube size; modern cuffed tubes are widely used (monitor the cuff pressure). The functionally narrowest point is at the cricoid in young children, and an air leak at about 20–30 cmH₂O was traditionally checked.
Cuffed tubes reduce reintubation and aspiration and allow accurate ventilation and capnography. Avoid an excessive cuff pressure (mucosal injury, post-extubation croup).

*Size pediatric ETTs by an uncuffed internal diameter of about (age/4)+4 (cuffed about (age/4)+3.5), depth about 3× the tube size; kids desaturate fast (high oxygen use, low FRC), and modern cuffed tubes are standard with cuff-pressure monitoring.*

head: Age
ETT ID
HR
RR
tables.note: Uncuffed ID = 4 + age/4; depth (cm) = ID × 3.

cards.title: Pediatric Drug Differences; Succinylcholine Complications
secs: In neonates and infants: a larger volume of distribution (more water), immature hepatic and renal clearance, and an immature blood-brain barrier alter the dosing; the MAC is higher in infants (peaking at about 1–6 months) and then decreases. Succinylcholine in children risks a hyperkalemic cardiac arrest in undiagnosed myopathy (such as Duchenne) — hence the BLACK BOX warning; reserve it for emergencies (rapid sequence, laryngospasm). It also causes bradycardia (give atropine) and masseter spasm. An immature neuromuscular junction gives a variable relaxant sensitivity.

Avoid routine succinylcholine in children (emergencies only). Atropine pretreatment prevents the bradycardia. Remifentanil is cleared by nonspecific esterases that are fully active at birth, so neonates clear it rapidly (unusual among opioids); and for a child with a recent URI, an IV propofol induction lowers the respiratory adverse events.
Sux in children: hyperkalemic-arrest risk (occult myopathy) → emergency use only; MAC peaks ~1–6 mo

*Pediatric pharmacology differs (a larger water volume, immature clearance, a MAC peaking at about 1–6 months); succinylcholine is reserved for emergencies in children (a hyperkalemic-arrest risk in occult myopathy, plus bradycardia — pretreat with atropine).*

cards.title: Pediatric NPO Guidelines; Glucose Requirements
secs: Fasting (the “2-4-6-8” rule): clear liquids 2 hours, breast milk 4 hours, formula or non-human milk 6 hours, and solids or a fatty meal 8 hours (many centers now allow clear fluids up to 1 hour). Neonates and infants are prone to hypoglycemia (limited glycogen) and dehydration with prolonged fasting, so they may need glucose-containing maintenance fluids; estimate the maintenance with the 4-2-1 rule. Their smaller fluid reserves demand careful fluid management.
The 4-2-1 rule: 4 mL/kg/h for the first 10 kg, plus 2 mL/kg/h for the next 10 kg, plus 1 mL/kg/h for each kg above 20. Monitor the glucose in neonates and at-risk infants.
NPO: clears 2 h, breast milk 4 h, formula 6 h, solids 8 h; maintenance 4-2-1 rule

*Pediatric NPO is 2/4/6/8 hours (clears, breast milk, formula, solids) and maintenance follows the 4-2-1 rule; neonates and infants risk hypoglycemia with fasting and may need glucose-containing fluids.*

head: Intake
Min hours
tables.note: “2-4-6-8”; clears reduce volume & acidity and ease induction.

cards.title: Difficult Airway Syndromes (Pierre Robin, Treacher Collins, Crouzon, Goldenhar, Hurler)
secs: Many congenital syndromes feature difficult airways: Pierre Robin (micrognathia, glossoptosis, cleft palate), Treacher Collins (mandibulofacial dysostosis), Goldenhar (hemifacial microsomia, cervical-spine anomalies), Crouzon and Apert (midface hypoplasia), and Hurler (mucopolysaccharidosis — a tissue infiltration that worsens with age). Anticipate a difficult mask and intubation — maintain spontaneous ventilation and have advanced tools (a video laryngoscope, LMA, fiberoptic scope) and expert help. Some (Hurler, Goldenhar, Klippel-Feil) also have cervical-spine instability.
The mucopolysaccharidoses become progressively harder to intubate (from the tissue deposition). Plan ahead, and avoid paralysis until the airway is secured if uncertain.

*Pierre Robin, Treacher Collins, Goldenhar, Crouzon/Apert, and Hurler all predict difficult pediatric airways — maintain spontaneous ventilation and have advanced airway tools and help ready; several add cervical-spine concerns.*

head: Syndrome
Airway concern
tables.note: Anticipate difficult mask/intubation; keep spontaneous ventilation.

cards.title: Neonatal Physiology: Surfactant; O2 Toxicity; Airway Differences
secs: Surfactant (made by the type II pneumocytes at about weeks 24–34) reduces the alveolar surface tension; its deficiency causes infant respiratory distress syndrome (in preterm infants) — treat with surfactant and CPAP. Pulmonary oxygen toxicity and hyperoxia cause retinopathy of prematurity and bronchopulmonary dysplasia — so titrate the FiO₂ to modest SpO₂ targets in neonates. The airway differences: a large head and tongue, an anterior and cephalad larynx, a long floppy epiglottis, and a narrow cricoid; they are obligate nasal breathers.
Preterm lungs are surfactant-deficient and prone to oxygen injury. Position and equipment must account for the neonatal airway anatomy.

*Surfactant (24–34 weeks) prevents neonatal RDS; neonates are vulnerable to oxygen toxicity (retinopathy of prematurity, bronchopulmonary dysplasia — titrate the FiO₂) and have distinct airways (a large tongue, a cephalad larynx, a narrow cricoid, and nasal breathing).*

head: Feature
Neonate
rows: Large head/tongue, anterior larynx, narrowest at cricoid
tables.note: Guard temperature and glucose.

cards.title: Fetal-to-Adult Circulation; Persistent Fetal Circulation
secs: The fetal shunts — the ductus venosus, the foramen ovale, and the ductus arteriosus — bypass the lungs (because of the high pulmonary vascular resistance in utero); the placenta is the gas-exchange organ. At birth, lung expansion plus the increased oxygen lowers the pulmonary vascular resistance, and clamping the cord raises the systemic vascular resistance, so the shunts functionally close (the ductus arteriosus closes via the rising oxygen and falling prostaglandins). Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN) is when the pulmonary vascular resistance fails to fall → right-to-left shunting and hypoxemia — treat it with oxygen and inhaled NO, and avoid acidosis, hypoxia, and hypothermia (all of which raise the resistance).
Prostaglandin E1 keeps the ductus open for duct-dependent lesions. Hypoxia and acidosis can reopen the fetal shunts.

*Fetal circulation (the ductus venosus, foramen ovale, and ductus arteriosus) bypasses the lungs; at birth, the falling pulmonary resistance plus the rising systemic resistance closes the shunts, and PPHN (where the resistance fails to fall → a right-to-left shunt) is treated with oxygen and inhaled NO while avoiding hypoxia, acidosis, and hypothermia.*

cards.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity
secs: Retinopathy of prematurity is an abnormal retinal vascular proliferation in preterm and low-birth-weight infants; HYPEROXIA is a key modifiable risk and can cause retinal detachment and blindness. Titrate the oxygen to modest target saturations (avoiding wide swings and hyperoxia) in at-risk neonates; it is multifactorial (prematurity itself being the biggest factor). Screening and treatment (laser or anti-VEGF) are done by ophthalmology.
Balance this against the harm of hypoxemia (avoid both extremes). It is most relevant in infants under about 32 weeks or of low birth weight.

*Retinopathy of prematurity is an abnormal retinal vascularization in preterm infants worsened by hyperoxia — titrate the oxygen to modest saturations (avoiding swings) in at-risk neonates, though prematurity itself is the main driver.*

cards.title: Apnea of Prematurity; Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia
secs: Ex-premature infants are at risk of POSTOPERATIVE APNEA (from immature respiratory control) → admit them for apnea monitoring after anesthesia (typically up to about 50–60 weeks postconceptual age); caffeine reduces the apnea. Bronchopulmonary dysplasia is a chronic lung disease of prematurity (from oxygen and ventilator injury) → airway reactivity, oxygen dependence, and pulmonary hypertension. Avoid outpatient surgery in young ex-premature infants (because of the apnea risk).
Consider regional (spinal) anesthesia to reduce the apnea risk in selected cases. Use caffeine for the apnea and bronchodilators for the bronchopulmonary-dysplasia reactivity.
Ex-prem infants: postop apnea risk → monitor (up to ~50–60 wk PCA); caffeine helps

*Ex-premature infants risk postoperative apnea (from immature control) → they require apnea monitoring (up to about 50–60 weeks postconceptual age; caffeine helps); bronchopulmonary dysplasia adds airway reactivity, oxygen dependence, and pulmonary hypertension.*

cards.title: Fetal Hemoglobin; Nonshivering Thermogenesis
secs: Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) has a higher oxygen affinity (it is left-shifted, with a low 2,3-DPG interaction), which favors placental oxygen uptake; it is replaced by adult hemoglobin over about 6 months (with a physiologic anemia nadir at about 2–3 months). Neonates rely on NONSHIVERING thermogenesis (brown-fat metabolism, driven by norepinephrine) for heat — they cannot shiver effectively; their large surface-area-to-mass ratio causes rapid heat loss. Maintain a warm environment for neonates (radiant warmers, warmed fluids).
Cold stress increases the oxygen consumption and metabolic demand in neonates. The HbF left shift means a lower P50 in neonates.

*Fetal hemoglobin is left-shifted (a high oxygen affinity for placental uptake, fading over about 6 months); neonates can’t shiver and use brown-fat nonshivering thermogenesis — so they lose heat fast and need active warming.*

cards.title: Cyanotic vs Acyanotic Congenital Heart Defects
secs: Acyanotic defects (left-to-right shunts) — VSD, ASD, PDA, AV canal — cause pulmonary overcirculation and heart failure; avoid an excessive pulmonary blood flow (don’t over-oxygenate or hyperventilate) and meticulously de-air the IV lines. Cyanotic defects (right-to-left shunts) — tetralogy of Fallot, transposition, truncus arteriosus, TAPVR, tricuspid atresia — cause hypoxemia; maintain the SVR and preload, avoid rises in pulmonary vascular resistance, and treat “tet spells” with oxygen, knee-to-chest positioning, fluids, phenylephrine, and beta-blockade. Use AIR-BUBBLE precautions for any shunt (a paradoxical embolism risk).

Eisenmenger syndrome is when a long-standing left-to-right shunt reverses (to right-to-left) with pulmonary hypertension — a very high risk; maintain the SVR and avoid pulmonary-resistance rises. Balance the SVR and pulmonary resistance by lesion.
Acyanotic = L-to-R (overcirculation); cyanotic = R-to-L (hypoxemia); avoid air bubbles (paradoxical emboli)

*Acyanotic defects (VSD, ASD, PDA) shunt left-to-right (pulmonary overcirculation); cyanotic defects (tetralogy of Fallot, transposition) shunt right-to-left (hypoxemia — maintain the SVR, lower the pulmonary resistance, treat tet spells) — and meticulously de-air all IV lines.*

head: Type
Examples
Shunt
tables.note: Meticulous air-bubble precautions (paradoxical emboli); balance SVR/PVR.

cards.title: Coarctation, PDA, Vascular Rings
secs: Coarctation of the aorta is a narrowing (usually juxtaductal) causing upper-extremity hypertension and lower-extremity hypoperfusion (a differential blood pressure and pulses) — monitor the blood pressure in the right arm (and a leg); critical neonatal coarctation is duct-dependent (prostaglandin E1). A patent ductus arteriosus is a left-to-right shunt causing pulmonary overcirculation; it is closed with indomethacin or ibuprofen (in premature infants) or with surgery or a device. Vascular rings encircle and compress the trachea and esophagus → stridor, feeding difficulty, and airway compromise.
Coarctation repair risks spinal cord ischemia (from the cross-clamp) and a paradoxical hypertension. Duct-dependent lesions need prostaglandin E1 to maintain the ductal patency.
Coarctation: upper-extremity HTN, lower hypoperfusion; duct-dependent neonate → PGE1

*Coarctation causes upper-extremity hypertension with lower hypoperfusion (right-arm blood-pressure monitoring; neonatal critical coarctation is duct-dependent → prostaglandin E1); a PDA shunts left-to-right; and vascular rings compress the trachea and esophagus.*

cards.title: Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia
secs: In congenital diaphragmatic hernia, the abdominal contents herniate into the chest (usually on the left, through the foramen of Bochdalek) → pulmonary HYPOPLASIA and pulmonary hypertension; it presents with respiratory distress, a scaphoid abdomen, and bowel in the chest. AVOID high airway pressures and bag-mask ventilation (gastric distension worsens the lung compression) → intubate early, ventilate gently, decompress the stomach (an NG or OG tube), and avoid nitrous oxide. It is a PHYSIOLOGIC (pulmonary hypertension and hypoplasia), not a surgical, emergency — stabilize first (gentle ventilation, treating the PPHN, sometimes ECMO), then repair.
Use permissive hypercapnia and gentle ventilation to spare the hypoplastic lungs. Manage the pulmonary hypertension (inhaled NO, avoiding pulmonary-resistance rises).

*Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (bowel in the chest → pulmonary hypoplasia plus pulmonary hypertension) needs early intubation with GENTLE low-pressure ventilation (avoid bag-mask and nitrous oxide, decompress the stomach) — stabilize the physiology before the surgical repair.*

cards.title: Tracheoesophageal Fistula & Esophageal Atresia
secs: The most common type is esophageal atresia with a distal tracheoesophageal fistula (a proximal pouch plus a fistula from the trachea to the distal esophagus); it presents with drooling, choking, an inability to pass an OG tube, and aspiration, with VACTERL associations. The airway goal is to position the ETT tip BELOW the fistula but above the carina (to avoid ventilating into the stomach via the fistula → distension); avoid high positive pressure before the fistula is ligated, and an awake or careful induction maintaining spontaneous ventilation is often preferred. A gastrostomy may decompress the stomach; watch for aspiration from the proximal pouch.
Gastric distension via the fistula impairs ventilation. There are associated anomalies (cardiac, vertebral, renal, limb — VACTERL).

*Esophageal atresia with a distal tracheoesophageal fistula (can’t pass an OG tube, aspiration, VACTERL) requires ETT placement below the fistula and avoiding high positive pressure (gas insufflates the stomach via the fistula) — maintain spontaneous ventilation where possible.*

cards.title: Pyloric Stenosis
secs: A hypertrophic pylorus causes projectile non-bilious vomiting, leading to a HYPOCHLOREMIC, HYPOKALEMIC METABOLIC ALKALOSIS with dehydration. It is a MEDICAL emergency, NOT a surgical one — REHYDRATE and correct the electrolytes and alkalosis FIRST (the alkalosis can cause postoperative apnea); a full stomach demands rapid-sequence induction and gastric decompression. The pyloromyotomy follows resuscitation; use awake or short-acting agents and watch for postoperative apnea and hypoglycemia.
Confirm the chloride and bicarbonate normalize before surgery. Decompress the stomach before induction (suction in multiple positions).
Pyloric stenosis: hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis; correct FIRST (medical emergency)

*Pyloric stenosis causes projectile vomiting → a hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis; it is a medical (not surgical) emergency — rehydrate and correct the electrolytes first (the alkalosis risks postoperative apnea), then do an RSI with stomach decompression.*

cards.title: Necrotizing Enterocolitis
secs: Necrotizing enterocolitis is a bowel ischemia, inflammation, and necrosis in premature infants, causing feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and pneumatosis intestinalis; it can progress to perforation, sepsis, and shock. These are critically ill neonates — manage the sepsis, fluid resuscitation, coagulopathy (DIC, thrombocytopenia), and acidosis; surgery is for perforation or necrosis. It is a high-risk anesthetic: hemodynamic instability, large fluid and blood requirements, thrombocytopenia, and ventilatory support.
Maintain perfusion and correct the coagulopathy and acidosis. The infant is often very premature and unstable — proceed with full resuscitation capability.

*Necrotizing enterocolitis (premature bowel ischemia and necrosis → distension, bloody stools, pneumatosis, perforation, sepsis) is a high-risk emergency needing aggressive resuscitation, correction of coagulopathy and acidosis, and surgery for perforation.*

cards.title: Omphalocele / Gastroschisis
secs: These are abdominal-wall defects with exposed viscera, causing heat, fluid, and evaporative losses; gastroschisis (to the right of the umbilicus, with NO sac, more bowel injury, and often isolated) versus omphalocele (central, COVERED by a sac, with more associated anomalies — cardiac, Beckwith-Wiedemann). Management: cover and protect the exposed bowel, fluid resuscitation, gastric decompression, and temperature control; staged (silo) versus primary closure. A primary or tight closure raises the intra-abdominal pressure → impaired ventilation, decreased venous return, and lower-extremity and visceral ischemia.
In omphalocele, search for associated anomalies (an echo; check the glucose for Beckwith-Wiedemann hypoglycemia). Watch the abdominal-compartment effects after closure.
Gastroschisis: no sac, isolated; omphalocele: sac + associated anomalies; closure → ↑ intra-abdominal pressure

*Gastroschisis (no sac, right of the umbilicus, isolated) and omphalocele (sac-covered, with associated anomalies) both lose heat and fluid through exposed viscera — resuscitate and protect the bowel; a tight closure raises the intra-abdominal pressure (impairing ventilation and perfusion).*

head: Feature
Omphalocele
Gastroschisis
tables.note: Both: warmth, fluids, protect bowel; staged closure if abdomen is tight.

cards.title: Myelomeningocele
secs: An open neural-tube defect with an exposed spinal cord and meninges requires early closure; positioning is a challenge (protect the sac at induction and intubation — often supine over a “doughnut” support, or lateral). The associations are Arnold-Chiari II malformation and hydrocephalus, a LATEX allergy risk (from repeated exposures), a neurogenic bladder and bowel, and lower-limb deficits. Use LATEX precautions from birth (because of the high sensitization risk).
Protect the exposed sac (with positioning) and maintain the temperature. Use a latex-free environment.

*Myelomeningocele (an open neural-tube defect) needs early closure with careful positioning to protect the exposed sac (intubate supine over a support) and strict latex precautions — it is associated with Chiari II and hydrocephalus.*

cards.title: Trisomy 21 & Chromosomal Abnormalities
secs: Down syndrome: ATLANTOAXIAL INSTABILITY (careful neck positioning, avoiding hyperextension), congenital heart disease (AV canal, VSD), subglottic stenosis (use a smaller ETT), macroglossia and OSA, hypotonia, and a tendency to bradycardia on induction. Evaluate the cardiac status and anticipate airway issues and positioning precautions. Other chromosomal syndromes have their own anomaly patterns.

Bradycardia with sevoflurane induction is common (have atropine ready). Use a downsized ETT (because of the subglottic stenosis). HYPOTHYROIDISM is the most common endocrine disorder in Down syndrome (congenital plus acquired autoimmune) — screen the thyroid function; there are also higher rates of leukemia, duodenal atresia, and OSA.
Down syndrome: atlantoaxial instability, CHD, subglottic stenosis (smaller ETT), bradycardia risk

*Trisomy 21 brings atlantoaxial instability (careful neck handling), congenital heart disease, subglottic stenosis (smaller ETT), macroglossia/OSA, induction bradycardia, and a high rate of hypothyroidism — evaluate the heart and thyroid and protect the neck.*

cards.title: Cerebral Palsy, Autism, Developmental Delay
secs: Cerebral palsy: spasticity and contractures, GERD and aspiration risk, seizures, poor nutrition, scoliosis, and communication difficulty; the patient is sensitive to positioning, with possible latex and anticonvulsant interactions; it is NOT MH-associated but may have an altered relaxant response. Autism and developmental delay: behavioral challenges — premedication, parental presence, and a calm environment ease the induction. Individualize the communication and premedication.
In cerebral palsy: temperature instability, aspiration risk, and baseline neurologic deficits. Consider oral, intramuscular, or intranasal premedication for cooperation.

*Cerebral palsy adds aspiration and GERD, seizures, contractures, and scoliosis (it is not MH-linked); autism and developmental delay need behavioral accommodation and premedication for a calm induction.*

cards.title: MH Susceptibility in Children
secs: Malignant hyperthermia (from RYR1 or CACNA1S mutations, autosomal dominant) is triggered by the volatile agents and succinylcholine, causing a hypermetabolic crisis (a rising EtCO₂, tachycardia, rigidity, hyperthermia, acidosis, hyperkalemia). Treat it with DANTROLENE (2.5 mg/kg, repeated to effect), stop the triggers, give 100% oxygen and hyperventilate, cool the patient, and treat the hyperkalemia and arrhythmia; use a trigger-free (TIVA) technique and a flushed or clean machine for susceptible patients. The associations are certain myopathies (central core disease), and masseter spasm after succinylcholine may herald MH.
An early EtCO₂ rise is the most sensitive sign. Refer susceptible families for testing (a caffeine-halothane contracture test or genetic testing).
MH: triggers = volatiles + sux; treat with dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg; earliest sign = rising EtCO2

*Malignant hyperthermia (RYR1, autosomal dominant) is triggered by the volatiles and succinylcholine → a rising EtCO₂, rigidity, hyperthermia, and hyperkalemia; treat it with dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg and supportive care, and use trigger-free TIVA in susceptible children.*

cards.title: Skeletal Abnormalities (Klippel-Feil, Achondroplasia, Marfan, Morquio)
secs: Klippel-Feil (fused cervical vertebrae) and Morquio (MPS IV — atlantoaxial instability and odontoid hypoplasia) have cervical-spine instability → difficult or dangerous airway manipulation (use careful in-line stabilization). Achondroplasia: a difficult airway (the midface), foramen-magnum stenosis (a cervicomedullary compression), restrictive lung disease, and a challenging neuraxial (canal stenosis — reduce the doses). Marfan: an aortic-root dilation and dissection risk (control the blood pressure and avoid hypertension), valve disease, lens dislocation, pneumothorax, and joint laxity.
The cervical-spine syndromes need an awake or fiberoptic approach and neutral positioning. In Marfan, avoid hemodynamic stress on the aorta.

*Klippel-Feil and Morquio carry cervical-spine instability (careful airway handling), achondroplasia adds foramen-magnum stenosis, a difficult airway, and a hard neuraxial, and Marfan risks aortic dissection (control the blood pressure) — tailor the airway and hemodynamics to each.*

cards.title: Tonsillectomy & Adenoidectomy; Peritonsillar Abscess; Airway FB
secs: Tonsillectomy is often for OSA (these patients are opioid-sensitive — reduce the doses, with caution in severe OSA; codeine is contraindicated in children); post-tonsillectomy bleeding is an emergency (hypovolemia plus a full stomach of blood → rapid-sequence induction, suction, a second IV, and blood available). Peritonsillar abscess: trismus and airway distortion → a careful airway plan. Airway foreign body: maintain SPONTANEOUS ventilation for the rigid bronchoscopy; it is a shared airway with the surgeon, with a risk of complete obstruction and ball-valve air trapping.

Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage: treat it as a full stomach with hypovolemia. For a foreign body, avoid positive pressure that can push it distally. Codeine carries an FDA boxed-warning contraindication after pediatric tonsillectomy or adenoidectomy: it is a CYP2D6 prodrug, and ultrarapid metabolizers generate high morphine levels — causing fatal respiratory depression, especially with OSA (see Pharmacogenetics).

*Tonsillectomy (often for OSA — opioid-sparing, no codeine) risks post-tonsillectomy bleeding (a full stomach of blood → RSI, suction, blood ready); an airway foreign body needs spontaneous ventilation for rigid bronchoscopy in a shared airway.*

cards.title: Anterior Mediastinal Mass
secs: Anterior mediastinal masses (lymphoma is the most common in children) can compress the airway, great vessels, heart, and pulmonary artery → cardiovascular and airway collapse under general anesthesia (losing spontaneous ventilation removes the splinting). Maintain SPONTANEOUS ventilation; consider an awake or semi-upright induction; rescue by repositioning (lateral or prone) and have rigid bronchoscopy and cardiopulmonary bypass on standby. The preoperative evaluation: symptoms (positional dyspnea, syncope), a CT scan, an echo, and flow-volume loops; consider a biopsy under local anesthesia or with steroids if the risk is high.
Avoid paralysis until the ventilation is secured. Obtain a tissue diagnosis by the least-invasive route in high-risk patients.

*A pediatric anterior mediastinal mass (often lymphoma) can collapse the airway or great vessels under general anesthesia — keep spontaneous ventilation, avoid paralysis, plan repositioning and rigid-bronchoscopy or bypass rescue, and consider a low-risk biopsy first.*

cards.title: Scoliosis Repair; Wilms Tumor; Neuroblastoma
secs: Scoliosis surgery: major blood loss, prone positioning (protect the eyes and pressure points), and NEUROMONITORING (SSEPs and MEPs) → limit the volatiles and avoid paralysis to preserve the MEPs, with the wake-up test as a backup; there is restrictive lung disease in severe curves. Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) is a renal mass with possible IVC or atrial tumor extension (assess with an echo) and hypertension; handle it carefully (to avoid rupture). Neuroblastoma may secrete catecholamines (causing hypertension) and is often extensive; manage the hemodynamics.
For scoliosis, use total intravenous or low-dose volatile anesthesia to allow MEP monitoring. For Wilms, assess for a tumor thrombus and avoid tumor rupture.

*Scoliosis repair involves major blood loss, prone positioning, and SSEP/MEP monitoring (limit the volatiles and paralysis, with a wake-up test backup); Wilms tumor may have IVC or atrial extension; and neuroblastoma can secrete catecholamines.*

cards.title: Pediatric Sedation Guidelines
secs: Sedation is a continuum (minimal → moderate → deep → general), and the practitioner must be able to RESCUE from one level deeper than intended (deep sedation can become general anesthesia). The requirements are appropriate fasting, monitoring (capnography, pulse oximetry), resuscitation equipment and personnel, and recovery to discharge criteria. Children readily progress to a deeper-than-intended sedation → so maintain vigilance and airway readiness.
Capnography detects hypoventilation early. Meet the discharge criteria (a return to baseline) before release.

*Pediatric sedation is a continuum where you must be able to rescue from one level deeper than intended — with fasting, full monitoring (capnography), resuscitation readiness, and discharge criteria; kids easily drift deeper than planned.*

cards.title: Pediatric Anesthesia Outside the OR (MRI, Radiation)
secs: Remote locations (MRI, CT, radiation oncology, the cath lab) have limited access and help, unfamiliar equipment, and environmental hazards → they require the same monitoring and standards as the OR. MRI: a ferromagnetic-projectile risk (use MRI-compatible equipment and monitors), acoustic noise, limited patient access, and long immobility → often general anesthesia or sedation with remote monitoring. Radiation therapy: the team must leave the room → use remote observation (cameras, monitors) and brief immobilization (often daily, with repeated anesthetics).
Plan for emergencies with limited help (have a plan to exit the MRI zone). Maintain OR-equivalent monitoring and equipment.

*Out-of-OR pediatric anesthesia (MRI, radiation, CT) demands OR-level monitoring despite the limited access and help; MRI needs nonferromagnetic equipment and remote monitoring, and radiation requires the team to observe from outside.*

cards.title: Pediatric URI & Perioperative Respiratory Adverse Events
secs: A recent or current upper respiratory infection (URI) leaves the pediatric airway hyperreactive for up to about 2–6 weeks, raising the risk of laryngospasm, bronchospasm, breath-holding, and desaturation (perioperative respiratory adverse events). The higher-risk features: copious secretions, fever, a wet or productive cough, lower-respiratory signs, an age under 1 year, prematurity, asthma, parental smoking, and airway surgery. Decide case-by-case whether to proceed or delay; a child with clear rhinorrhea and no systemic signs is usually fine to proceed.

Lower the risk with technique: an intravenous PROPOFOL induction (rather than inhalational sevoflurane), the least-invasive airway tolerated (a mask or LMA over an endotracheal tube), adequate depth before airway manipulation, and avoiding light anesthesia at instrumentation and extubation. Keep a laryngospasm plan ready: CPAP with 100% oxygen, deepen the anesthesia (propofol), and low-dose succinylcholine (about 0.1–0.2 mg/kg, with or without atropine) if it persists. Bronchodilators, humidification, and anticholinergics for secretions are useful adjuncts.
URI → airway hyperreactivity up to ~2–6 wk Propofol induction + LMA/mask (not sevoflurane + ETT) lowers respiratory adverse events

*A recent pediatric URI makes the airway hyperreactive for weeks — lower the risk of laryngospasm and bronchospasm with an IV propofol induction, the least-invasive airway (a mask or LMA), and adequate depth at instrumentation, while keeping a laryngospasm plan ready.*

cards.title: Iron-Deficiency Anemia — Pediatric Risk Factors
secs: Risk factors for iron-deficiency anemia (IDA) in children: low birth weight, prematurity, cow's milk before 12 months, non-iron-fortified formula, and low socioeconomic status. Cow's milk is low in bioavailable iron, displaces iron-rich foods, and its proteins can cause occult GI blood loss; excess intake also impairs absorption. Term infants deplete fetal iron stores by ~4-6 months; preterm/LBW infants deplete them sooner because most iron is transferred in the third trimester.

IDA is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide and, in children, is linked to impaired neurodevelopment and cognition. Labs show a MICROCYTIC, hypochromic anemia (low MCV, low ferritin, high RDW, high TIBC). Recommended screening ~12 months. Preoperatively, significant symptomatic anemia may warrant iron therapy and deferring elective major surgery.
Screen at 12 months. Microcytic if MCV <70-75 fL in young children. Ferritin <15 ng/mL supports iron deficiency (but ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and rises with inflammation).
Preterm, LBW, early cow's milk, non-fortified formula, low SES -> microcytic IDA that can impair neurodevelopment.

cards.title: Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia (BPD)
secs: BRONCHOPULMONARY DYSPLASIA is a chronic lung disease that is a sequela of neonatal RESPIRATORY DISTRESS SYNDROME (surfactant deficiency), occurring most commonly in PRETERM infants born at LESS THAN 32 WEEKS gestation. It results from lung injury (baro/volutrauma, oxygen toxicity, inflammation) superimposed on immature, saccular-stage lungs, producing arrested alveolarization, airway hyperreactivity, and pulmonary hypertension. Antenatal STEROIDS and exogenous SURFACTANT have decreased both incidence and severity. These infants often respond favorably to BRONCHODILATORS, reflecting the airway-reactivity component.

BPD is clinically defined by an ongoing oxygen requirement (typically at 36 weeks postmenstrual age). For anesthesia, these children have hyperreactive airways, higher risk of perioperative bronchospasm, laryngospasm and post-op apnea, chronic hypoxemia, and possible pulmonary hypertension. Optimize with bronchodilators, avoid airway irritation, use lung-protective ventilation, avoid hyperoxia, and plan for postoperative monitoring given apnea risk in the ex-premature infant.
BPD = chronic lung disease after RDS in preemies <32 weeks; surfactant + steroids reduce it, and bronchodilators help the reactive airways.

cards.title: Neonatal Thermoregulation & Nonshivering Thermogenesis
secs: NONSHIVERING THERMOGENESIS is the PRIMARY mechanism of heat production in NEONATES and infants because they cannot shiver effectively. It occurs in BROWN ADIPOSE TISSUE (rich in mitochondria, uncoupling protein UCP-1/thermogenin), where oxidative phosphorylation is uncoupled so energy is released as HEAT. The principal triggers are NOREPINEPHRINE (sympathetic), GLUCOCORTICOIDS, and THYROXINE. This process is INHIBITED by INHALATIONAL ANESTHETICS and by BETA-BLOCKERS. The four overall mechanisms of human heat generation are voluntary muscle activity, shivering, dietary (diet-induced) thermogenesis, and nonshivering thermogenesis.

Neonates lose heat rapidly (high surface-area-to-mass ratio, thin skin, little subcutaneous fat) and depend on brown fat, so intraoperative hypothermia is common and consequential: increased oxygen consumption, metabolic acidosis, apnea, and delayed drug metabolism. Because volatile agents blunt nonshivering thermogenesis, active warming (forced-air warmers, warmed fluids, raised OR temperature, humidified gases) is essential during neonatal anesthesia.
Neonates warm themselves by nonshivering thermogenesis in brown fat (driven by norepinephrine, thyroxine, cortisol) — a process blunted by volatile anesthetics.

cards.title: Prostaglandin E1 — Maintaining Ductal Patency
secs: PROSTAGLANDIN E1 (alprostadil) is infused to MAINTAIN PATENCY of the ductus arteriosus (PDA) in neonates with DUCTAL-DEPENDENT congenital heart lesions — either ductal-dependent PULMONARY blood flow (e.g., pulmonary atresia, critical pulmonary stenosis, tricuspid atresia) or ductal-dependent SYSTEMIC blood flow (e.g., critical coarctation, interrupted aortic arch, hypoplastic left heart, critical aortic stenosis) and in transposition of the great arteries for mixing. Common side effects are HYPOTENSION (vasodilation), APNEA, BRADYCARDIA (and fever/flushing), and it DECREASES pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR).

Because APNEA is common (especially at initiation and higher doses), be prepared to support ventilation and consider elective intubation before transport. The vasodilation can require volume and pressors. This contrasts with therapies to CLOSE a PDA in preterm infants — indomethacin or ibuprofen (COX inhibitors) — since endogenous prostaglandins keep the duct open. PGE1 is the bridge to surgery/catheterization in duct-dependent circulation.
PGE1 keeps the ductus arteriosus open in duct-dependent lesions; watch for apnea, hypotension, and bradycardia.

cards.title: Neonatal & Pediatric Estimated Blood Volume
secs: Estimated blood volume (EBV) is HIGHER per kilogram in neonates and falls with age. Typical values: PREMATURE infant 90-105 mL/kg; FULL-TERM NEWBORN 80-90 mL/kg; infant (~1-12 months) ~75-80 mL/kg; child ~70-75 mL/kg; adult male ~70 mL/kg and adult female ~65 mL/kg. Because the absolute blood volume of a neonate is tiny, even small absolute losses represent a large PERCENTAGE of blood volume.

EBV drives the calculation of MAXIMUM ALLOWABLE BLOOD LOSS: MABL = EBV x (Hct_initial - Hct_target)/Hct_initial. In a 3 kg term newborn EBV is only ~240-270 mL, so a 50 mL loss is ~20% of blood volume — meticulous measurement (sponges, suction) and early transfusion planning are essential. Higher per-kg volume in preemies must be balanced against their fragility and limited reserve.
EBV: premature 90-105 mL/kg; term newborn 80-90 mL/kg; infant ~75-80; child ~70-75; adult M ~70 / F ~65. MABL = EBV x (Hct_i - Hct_target)/Hct_i.
Blood volume is 80-90 mL/kg in a term newborn and 90-105 mL/kg in a preemie — higher per kg but tiny in absolute terms.

cards.title: Physiologic Anemia of Infancy
secs: Term infants undergo a PHYSIOLOGIC ANEMIA, reaching a hemoglobin NADIR of 10.5-11.5 g/dL at about 8-12 WEEKS of age. Cord blood Hgb at term birth is 14-20 g/dL. The decline is driven by: switching from fetal to adult hemoglobin, a sharp DROP in ERYTHROPOIETIN synthesis after birth (improved tissue oxygenation), the SHORTER lifespan of neonatal red cells, and rapid DILUTIONAL expansion of blood volume during early growth. PRETERM infants develop this anemia EARLIER — by about 6 weeks — and reach a LOWER nadir of roughly 7-10 g/dL (anemia of prematurity).

Physiologic anemia is normal and does not require treatment in healthy term infants; recognizing it avoids unnecessary workup or transfusion. It coincides with the period when maternally derived fetal hemoglobin (high O2 affinity, left-shifted curve) is being replaced by adult Hgb with 2,3-DPG, shifting the O2 dissociation curve rightward. Anemia of prematurity is more pronounced and, with symptoms, may warrant iron, erythropoietin, or transfusion; a rightward-shifting curve improves tissue O2 unloading.
Term: cord Hgb 14-20 g/dL → nadir 10.5-11.5 g/dL at 8-12 weeks. Preterm: nadir ~7-10 g/dL by ~6 weeks.
Physiologic anemia bottoms at Hgb 10.5-11.5 by 8-12 weeks in term infants; preterms nadir lower (~7-10) and earlier (~6 weeks).

cards.title: Infant vs Adult Airway: Key Anatomic Differences
secs: The pediatric airway differs from the adult in ways that directly change laryngoscopy technique. The infant LARYNX sits HIGHER and more anterior in the neck (glottis at C3-C4 versus C4-C5 in adults), making the view more superior and anterior. The EPIGLOTTIS is long, narrow, stiff, and OMEGA-shaped (U/Ω), angling posteriorly over the glottis - which is why a straight (Miller) blade to directly lift the epiglottis is often preferred in infants. The TONGUE is relatively large and the OCCIPUT is prominent, so a shoulder roll (not a head ring) best aligns the axes. The NARROWEST portion of the infant airway has classically been taught as the CRICOID cartilage - a fixed, complete, non-distensible ring - versus the glottis (vocal cords) in adults, though modern imaging suggests the narrowest dimension may actually be at the glottic/subglottic level. The VOCAL CORDS are angled (anterior attachment more caudad) rather than perpendicular to the tracheal axis, so a blindly passed tube can catch at the anterior commissure.

The complete cricoid ring means a snug ETT can compress the mucosa and cause POST-EXTUBATION CROUP and subglottic edema; historically uncuffed tubes were chosen with an audible leak at 20-30 cm H2O, though modern low-pressure cuffed tubes are now widely used with careful cuff-pressure monitoring. High larynx + large tongue + big occiput all make the infant view more challenging and favor the Miller blade with a sniffing-position modification (roll under shoulders).

Glottis position: infant C3-C4 vs adult C4-C5. Narrowest point (classic teaching): cricoid ring in child vs glottis in adult. Uncuffed ETT leak target: 20-30 cm H2O. Uncuffed tube size estimate: (age/4) + 4; cuffed: (age/4) + 3.5.

The infant larynx is HIGH and anterior with an omega epiglottis, and the CRICOID ring is the narrowest, non-distensible point - favoring a Miller blade and cuff-pressure vigilance to avoid subglottic croup.

cards.title: Neonatal GFR & Renal Drug Clearance
secs: Glomerular filtration rate (GFR) is LOW at birth (roughly 20-30 mL/min/1.73 m2 in a term neonate, and even lower in preterms) because of high renal vascular resistance and a low fraction of cardiac output reaching the kidney. Over the FIRST FEW DAYS to weeks of life GFR rises RAPIDLY as systemic arterial pressure INCREASES and renal vascular resistance DECREASES (with redistribution of intrarenal blood flow to outer cortical nephrons). GFR then climbs more gradually, reaching adult values (~120 mL/min/1.73 m2) by about 2 YEARS of age.

Because renally-cleared drugs depend on glomerular filtration, their ELIMINATION HALF-LIFE is PROLONGED in neonates. Drugs and active metabolites cleared by the kidney — aminoglycosides, vancomycin, many beta-lactams, digoxin, and the active morphine metabolite morphine-6-glucuronide — ACCUMULATE, so dosing intervals must be LENGTHENED to avoid toxicity. Neonates also have immature tubular function, reduced concentrating ability, and are prone to fluid/electrolyte imbalance. This is compounded by other neonatal pharmacology differences: larger total body water/volume of distribution for water-soluble drugs, reduced protein binding (more free drug), and immature hepatic enzyme systems.

Term neonate GFR ~20-30 mL/min/1.73 m2 at birth; adult GFR ~120 mL/min/1.73 m2 reached by ~2 years. t1/2 (elimination half-life) is inversely related to clearance: t1/2 = 0.693 x Vd / Cl — lower neonatal Cl lengthens t1/2. GFR roughly doubles in the first ~2 weeks of life.

Neonatal GFR is LOW at birth and rises fast as arterial pressure climbs and renal vascular resistance falls (adult levels by ~2 years) — so half-lives of renally-cleared drugs are LONGER, requiring extended dosing intervals.

cards.title: Transient Neonatal Myasthenia Gravis
secs: TRANSIENT NEONATAL MYASTHENIA GRAVIS is caused by PASSIVE transplacental transfer of maternal IgG ANTI-ACETYLCHOLINE-RECEPTOR ANTIBODIES that block postsynaptic nicotinic AChRs at the neuromuscular junction. It affects roughly 10-20% of neonates born to mothers with myasthenia gravis and is INDEPENDENT of the severity of maternal disease. Symptoms appear within hours to the first few days of life and resolve as maternal antibody is cleared, typically within 3-4 WEEKS. This is distinct from CONGENITAL myasthenic syndromes, which are inherited genetic defects of the NMJ and are NOT antibody-mediated or self-limited.

Presenting signs are generalized HYPOTONIA, weak cry, poor suck/feeding, ptosis, and, in severe cases, respiratory insufficiency requiring support. Treatment is largely SUPPORTIVE; an ACETYLCHOLINESTERASE INHIBITOR such as NEOSTIGMINE or pyridostigmine can be given (e.g., neostigmine ~30 minutes before feeds) to improve strength when weakness impairs feeding or ventilation, generally needed for up to ~3 weeks. For anesthesia, these infants have altered NMB responses: they are SENSITIVE to NONDEPOLARIZING agents (use reduced doses, titrate to a nerve stimulator) and may be RESISTANT to succinylcholine. Anticipate the possibility of postoperative ventilation and avoid drugs that worsen weakness (e.g., aminoglycosides, magnesium).

Transient neonatal MG is passive maternal anti-AChR antibody transfer causing self-limited hypotonia that resolves in ~3-4 weeks; treat supportively with neostigmine before feeds and expect sensitivity to nondepolarizers.

cards.title: Cerebral Palsy: Anesthetic Sensitivity & MAC
secs: Cerebral palsy (CP) is a NON-PROGRESSIVE disorder of movement and posture from injury to the developing brain. Patients are MORE SENSITIVE to volatile anesthetics, so a LOWER MAC (~20-25% reduction) is needed for surgical anesthesia. The leading risk factor is PREMATURITY, especially birth <32 weeks; other contributors include perinatal asphyxia, chorioamnionitis (intrauterine infection), and IUGR. Associated problems the anesthesiologist must anticipate: chronic anticonvulsant use, GERD with ASPIRATION risk, restrictive lung disease from scoliosis, contractures/positioning difficulty, malnutrition, communication barriers, and latex sensitivity.

Beyond reduced MAC, CP patients are prone to intraoperative HYPOTHERMIA (low muscle mass, hypothalamic dysfunction) and delayed emergence. Chronic anticonvulsant therapy (phenytoin, carbamazepine) induces hepatic enzymes and can cause RESISTANCE to nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockers. Importantly, SUCCINYLCHOLINE is NOT contraindicated in CP — there is no denervation-type upregulation of extrajunctional receptors, so an exaggerated hyperkalemic response is not expected (unlike upper-motor-neuron injury such as stroke or spinal cord injury). Continue seizure meds perioperatively and guard actively against heat loss.
MAC reduced roughly 20-25%. Highest CP risk with prematurity <32 weeks gestation.
Cerebral palsy patients need a LOWER MAC of volatile; prematurity <32 weeks is the top risk factor, and succinylcholine is not contraindicated.

cards.title: Airway Evaluation & Physical Exam
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: Predictors of difficult MASK ventilation are captured by BONES: Beard, Obese, No teeth, Elderly, and Snoring/OSA. Predictors of difficult INTUBATION include a Mallampati III–IV view, a thyromental distance under 6 cm, mouth opening under 3 cm, limited neck extension, large incisors, retrognathia, and a short, thick neck. The LEMON mnemonic organizes the assessment: Look, Evaluate (the 3-3-2 rule), Mallampati, Obstruction, Neck mobility.
A documented prior difficult airway or anesthetic record is the single strongest predictor. When several predictors are present, plan ahead — an awake technique, video laryngoscopy, and adjuncts.
3-3-2 rule: ≥ 3 fingers mouth opening, ≥ 3 chin–hyoid, ≥ 2 hyoid–thyroid notch Thyromental distance < 6 cm and Mallampati III–IV predict difficulty
*Combine predictors (Mallampati III–IV, thyromental < 6 cm, the 3-3-2 rule, limited extension, and BONES for mask ventilation); a documented prior difficult airway is the strongest warning of all.*

cards.title: ASA Preoperative Testing Guidelines
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: Routine, indiscriminate preoperative testing is NOT recommended — order tests based on the history, comorbidities, and the planned procedure. Unindicated tests rarely change management and can cause harm through false positives and delays. Targeted testing includes a pregnancy test when relevant, a type-and-screen for expected blood loss, and focused labs or an ECG for specific conditions.

An ECG may be considered for a patient with known cardiovascular disease facing higher-risk surgery, but not for low-risk or asymptomatic patients. Do not repeat a recent normal test unless the patient’s condition has changed.
*Skip routine preoperative testing and order tests selectively, driven by history, comorbidities, and surgical risk — unindicated tests rarely change management.*

cards.title: ACC/AHA Perioperative Cardiovascular Evaluation
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: The ACC/AHA approach is stepwise: assess the urgency of surgery, any active cardiac conditions (acute coronary syndrome, decompensated heart failure, significant arrhythmia, severe valvular disease), the surgical risk, and the patient’s functional capacity. A functional capacity of at least 4 METs (for example, climbing two flights of stairs) without symptoms means you can proceed without further cardiac testing. Risk is quantified with the Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI).
Order further testing only when the result would change management. Continue beta-blockers and statins, and manage stents and antiplatelet therapy together with cardiology.
Functional capacity ≥ 4 METs → proceed without further testing RCRI: high-risk surgery, ischemic heart disease, CHF, CVA/TIA, insulin-dependent DM, Cr > 2 mg/dL
*Use the ACC/AHA stepwise approach: at least 4 METs without symptoms means proceed; risk-stratify with the RCRI’s six predictors and test only when it will change management.*

tables.cap: Revised Cardiac Risk Index (1 pt each)
head: Predictor
tables.note: ≥ 3 predictors → high risk (≈ 11% major cardiac event).

cards.title: ASA Physical Status Classification
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: ASA I is a normal healthy patient; II is mild systemic disease (controlled hypertension, a smoker); III is severe systemic disease (poorly controlled diabetes, COPD, a BMI of 40 or more). ASA IV is severe systemic disease that is a constant threat to life (recent myocardial infarction, sepsis, severe valve disease); V is a moribund patient not expected to survive without the operation; VI is a brain-dead organ donor. An “E” suffix is added for an emergency procedure.
It grades the severity of preoperative illness — not airway difficulty — though it does correlate with outcomes. Assignment is somewhat subjective, and pregnancy is generally classed as ASA II.
I healthy → II mild → III severe → IV life-threatening → V moribund → VI brain-dead; “E” = emergency
*ASA I–VI grades systemic illness severity (II mild, III severe, IV a constant life threat, V moribund, VI organ donor); add “E” for emergencies — it reflects comorbidity, not airway difficulty.*

head: Class
Description
rows: Severe disease, constant threat to life
Moribund, not expected to survive without surgery
tables.note: Add “E” for emergency.

cards.title: Premedication & Chronic Drug Therapy Interactions
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: The goals of premedication are anxiolysis (midazolam), analgesia, drying secretions, aspiration prophylaxis, postoperative-nausea prophylaxis, and continuing essential medications. Account for interactions with the anesthetic agents — additive CNS and respiratory depression and hemodynamic effects. Monoamine-oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) can cause a hypertensive crisis with indirect sympathomimetics like ephedrine and serotonin syndrome with meperidine, so use direct-acting agents cautiously.
Anticipate withdrawal syndromes (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, and clonidine rebound). Tailor the premedication to the patient’s age, comorbidities, and procedure.
*Premedicate for anxiolysis, analgesia, and aspiration/nausea prophylaxis while continuing essential medications; watch for interactions (MAOIs with ephedrine or meperidine) and for withdrawal.*

cards.title: Disease-Specific Premedication & Optimization
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: Obstructive sleep apnea: be cautious with sedatives and opioids, continue CPAP, and plan for postoperative monitoring. COPD and asthma: optimize bronchodilators and steroids, avoid triggers, and consider a regional technique. Diabetes: manage perioperative glucose, hold SGLT2 inhibitors (risk of euglycemic ketoacidosis), and dose metformin per protocol; and render thyroid disease euthyroid before elective surgery.
Steroid-dependent patients may need stress-dose coverage for major surgery. Continue most cardiac, antiseizure, and pulmonary medications.

*Optimize the specific disease: CPAP and opioid caution in OSA, bronchodilators in COPD/asthma, glucose control (hold SGLT2 inhibitors), euthyroid before elective surgery, and stress-dose steroids when indicated.*

cards.title: NPO / Full Stomach & Aspiration Risk
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: The ASA fasting guidelines for an elective, healthy patient are: clear liquids 2 hours, breast milk 4 hours, infant formula or a light meal 6 hours, and a fatty/fried meal or meat 8 hours. Full-stomach situations (trauma, pregnancy, bowel obstruction, reflux, diabetic gastroparesis, recent food, or pain/opioids) raise aspiration risk and call for a rapid-sequence induction with cricoid pressure. The classic Mendelson aspiration-risk thresholds are a gastric volume above 25 mL and a pH below 2.5 (now debated).

Aspiration prophylaxis options include a non-particulate antacid (sodium citrate), an H2 blocker, and metoclopramide. Recent use of a GLP-1 agonist delays gastric emptying — consider a longer fast or treat the patient as a full stomach.
Clear 2 h / breast milk 4 h / formula-light meal 6 h / fatty meal 8 h Classic aspiration risk: gastric volume > 25 mL and pH < 2.5

*ASA fasting is 2/4/6/8 hours (clear liquids/breast milk/light meal/fatty meal); treat a full-stomach patient with rapid-sequence induction and cricoid pressure, and treat recent GLP-1 agonist use as delayed emptying.*

head: Intake
Min hours
tables.note: Aspiration risk rises with full stomach, GERD, obesity, pregnancy, ileus.

cards.title: Continuation vs Discontinuation of Chronic Medications
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: Continue beta-blockers, statins, most antihypertensives, inhalers, antiseizure drugs, thyroid medication, and most psychiatric medications. Commonly held on the morning of surgery are ACE inhibitors and ARBs (to reduce refractory intraoperative hypotension — though this is institution-dependent). Held in advance are SGLT2 inhibitors (3–4 days, for euglycemic ketoacidosis); many oral hypoglycemics are held on the day, and anticoagulants/antiplatelets are managed by bleeding risk.
Abruptly stopping a beta-blocker or clonidine causes rebound hypertension and ischemia. Individualize diuretics, GLP-1 agonists, and herbal supplements.
