*Continue beta-blockers, statins, inhalers, and antiseizure drugs; commonly hold ACE inhibitors/ARBs the morning of surgery and SGLT2 inhibitors for several days — and never stop a beta-blocker or clonidine abruptly.*

head: Continue
Hold
tables.note: Generally continue cardiac/pulmonary/neuro meds; hold per bleeding/glucose risk.

cards.title: Prophylactic Beta-Blockade & Cardiac Risk Reduction
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: CONTINUE beta-blockers in a patient already taking them, since abrupt withdrawal raises the risk of ischemia and death. Do NOT start high-dose beta-blockade for the first time on the day of surgery — the POISE trial found fewer heart attacks but MORE strokes and more total deaths. Continue statins through the perioperative period, where they are beneficial.
If you do start beta-blockade in a high-risk patient, begin well in advance and titrate to heart rate.
Continue chronic beta-blockers; do NOT start them on the day of surgery (POISE)
*Continue chronic beta-blockers (don’t stop them abruptly) but don’t START them on the day of surgery (POISE showed more strokes and deaths) — and continue statins.*

cards.title: Prophylactic Antibiotics
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: Give prophylactic antibiotics within 60 minutes before incision (within 120 minutes for vancomycin or fluoroquinolones) so tissue levels are adequate at incision. Cefazolin is the usual first choice, dosed by weight, and is redosed intraoperatively for long cases (about every two half-lives) or with major blood loss. Endocarditis prophylaxis is now reserved for high-risk cardiac conditions undergoing specific dental or respiratory procedures.
Tailor the choice to the surgical-site flora and the patient’s allergies (a true penicillin allergy calls for an alternative). Continue prophylaxis no longer than 24 hours after most procedures.
Give within 60 min before incision (120 min for vancomycin/fluoroquinolone) Redose for prolonged surgery (~2 half-lives) or major blood loss

*Give prophylactic antibiotics within 60 minutes of incision (120 for vancomycin), use weight-based cefazolin, and redose for long cases or major blood loss; reserve endocarditis prophylaxis for high-risk lesions.*

cards.title: Oral Anticoagulants, Antiplatelets & Reversal
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: Warfarin is reversed by vitamin K (slow), 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC; fast, preferred for urgent reversal), or fresh frozen plasma — check the INR. Among the direct oral anticoagulants, dabigatran is reversed by idarucizumab and the factor-Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban) by andexanet alfa or PCC; hold them based on renal function and bleeding risk. For antiplatelets, aspirin is often continued in cardiac patients, while P2Y12 inhibitors are held by bleeding-versus-thrombotic risk (clopidogrel ~5–7 days, ticagrelor ~3–5 days), and recent stents are coordinated with cardiology.
Heparin is reversed by protamine; low-molecular-weight heparin is only partially reversed. Follow the ASRA timing intervals before any neuraxial procedure.
Warfarin → vitamin K / 4-factor PCC; dabigatran → idarucizumab; Xa inhibitors → andexanet/PCC Hold clopidogrel ~5–7 d, ticagrelor ~3–5 d; heparin → protamine

*Know the reversal agents (warfarin→PCC or vitamin K, dabigatran→idarucizumab, Xa inhibitors→andexanet or PCC, heparin→protamine) and the antiplatelet hold times — and coordinate stents with cardiology and follow ASRA for neuraxial blocks.*

head: Drug
Reversal
tables.note: Follow ASRA neuraxial hold times.

cards.title: Stages & Signs of Anesthesia; Awareness
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › General Anesthesia (I.B.3)
secs: The Guedel stages (described for ether) are: I analgesia, II excitement/delirium (a risk of laryngospasm and vomiting, so pass through it quickly), III surgical anesthesia, and IV overdose (apnea and cardiovascular collapse). Intraoperative awareness with recall is more likely with TIVA, with paralysis, and with light anesthesia (trauma, cardiac, and obstetric cases). Monitor anesthetic depth with the end-tidal agent concentration (at least 0.7 MAC), a processed EEG/BIS (around 40–60), and clinical signs.

Prevent awareness by confirming agent delivery, using amnestics, avoiding unnecessary paralysis, and monitoring MAC or BIS. Awareness can cause distress and PTSD, so debrief and document it if it occurs.
Stage II = excitement (laryngospasm risk); maintain ≥ 0.7 MAC; BIS ~40–60

*Stage II (excitement) is the dangerous transition; prevent awareness (highest risk with TIVA, paralysis, and trauma/cardiac/obstetric cases) by keeping at least 0.7 MAC or a BIS of 40–60 and confirming agent delivery.*

head: Stage
Description
tables.note: Move through stage II quickly; airway manipulation there triggers laryngospasm.

cards.title: Inhalational, TIVA & Combined Techniques
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › General Anesthesia (I.B.3)
secs: Inhalational maintenance titrates a volatile agent to MAC, providing amnesia, immobility, and some analgesia, and it allows end-tidal monitoring. Total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA — propofol with or without remifentanil, often target-controlled) is best for malignant-hyperthermia-susceptible patients, severe PONV, neuromonitoring cases, and shared-airway surgery. Balanced anesthesia combines a hypnotic, an opioid, and a relaxant (with or without a volatile) to limit the side effects of each drug.
TIVA has no real-time concentration monitor, so awareness risk rises if intravenous access fails — use a processed EEG. Choose the technique by the patient, the surgery, and the recovery goals.

*Inhalational maintenance (titrated to MAC, end-tidal monitored) versus TIVA (best for malignant-hyperthermia risk, PONV, and neuromonitoring) versus balanced anesthesia; TIVA needs EEG vigilance against awareness.*

cards.title: Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC) Techniques & Risks
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › MAC & Sedation (I.B.4)
secs: Monitored anesthesia care (MAC) means a provider delivers sedation and analgesia with full monitoring while the patient breathes spontaneously, ready to convert to general anesthesia. The same monitoring standards and pre-anesthetic evaluation apply as for a general anesthetic. The main risks are over-sedation leading to airway obstruction, hypoventilation, and hypoxemia, and the depth continuum unintentionally deepening to general anesthesia.

Capnography improves detection of hypoventilation and apnea — important because supplemental oxygen masks desaturation on the pulse oximeter. Airway-remote sites (endoscopy, radiology) raise the risk, so plan for rescue.

*MAC is provider-delivered sedation with full monitoring and the ability to convert to general anesthesia; the chief danger is over-sedation and airway obstruction — and capnography helps because supplemental oxygen masks desaturation.*

head: Level
Responsiveness
Airway
tables.note: Sedation can deepen unexpectedly — be ready to rescue the next level.

cards.title: Sedation Guidelines for Non-Anesthesiologists
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › MAC & Sedation (I.B.4)
secs: Sedation is a continuum: minimal (anxiolysis) → moderate (“conscious,” with a purposeful response to voice or touch) → deep (response only to repeated or painful stimulation) → general anesthesia. Because depth is a continuum, providers must be able to RESCUE a patient from one level deeper than intended. Moderate sedation preserves spontaneous ventilation and a patent airway, whereas deep sedation may not.

Requirements include a pre-sedation assessment, NPO status, monitoring (with capnography), trained personnel, and rescue/reversal capability. Propofol can rapidly reach deep sedation or general anesthesia, so anesthesia involvement is often advised.

*Sedation is a continuum (minimal → moderate → deep → general); be able to rescue one level deeper than intended; moderate sedation preserves ventilation while deep may not — monitor with capnography.*

cards.title: Corneal Abrasion & Postoperative Visual Loss
secs: Corneal abrasion is the most common ocular injury under anesthesia (from exposure/drying or direct trauma); it causes eye pain and a foreign-body sensation, is treated with lubrication with or without an antibiotic, and heals in 24–72 hours. Postoperative visual loss (POVL) has two main causes: ischemic optic neuropathy (the most common, especially after prolonged prone spine surgery) and central retinal artery occlusion (from external globe compression). Ischemic optic neuropathy risk factors are a prolonged prone position, large blood loss, hypotension, anemia, a long operation, and a head-down position. Acute angle-closure glaucoma is the can’t-miss cause of a PAINFUL, RED eye in the PACU: a fixed mid-dilated pupil, cloudy cornea, very high intraocular pressure, and nausea — an ophthalmologic emergency needing immediate pressure-lowering treatment.

Prevent injury by taping the eyes closed, avoiding globe pressure (correct head positioning and foam), maintaining perfusion and hematocrit, and considering staging very long prone cases. Central retinal artery occlusion from external globe compression (e.g., a horseshoe/foam headrest while prone) gives painless monocular loss with a foveal CHERRY-RED SPOT — distinct from ischemic optic neuropathy, which leaves a normal-appearing fovea.

*Corneal abrasion is the most common eye injury (tape/lubricate); ischemic optic neuropathy is the leading POVL cause in prone spine surgery; a foveal cherry-red spot means CRAO (globe compression); and a painful red eye with a fixed mid-dilated pupil in the PACU is acute angle-closure glaucoma — protect perfusion and avoid globe pressure.*

cards.title: Venous Air Embolism
secs: Air enters open veins when the surgical site is above the level of the heart (sitting craniotomy, posterior fossa, cervical spine — and also cesarean delivery and laparoscopy). An air lock in the right ventricle or pulmonary outflow causes an early fall in EtCO₂, then hypotension, a “mill-wheel” murmur, hypoxemia, arrhythmia, and arrest; with a patent foramen ovale, air can cross into the systemic circulation (paradoxical embolism). The most sensitive monitors are precordial Doppler and transesophageal echocardiography, with capnography (a sudden EtCO₂ drop) practical and sensitive — the rough order is TEE > precordial Doppler > EtCO₂.

Manage it by flooding or lowering the surgical field, giving 100% oxygen, stopping nitrous oxide, aspirating air from a right-atrial multi-orifice catheter, positioning left-lateral/Trendelenburg, and supporting hemodynamics. Prevent it by avoiding nitrous oxide in at-risk cases and maintaining venous pressure.
Sudden ↓ EtCO2 + hypotension + mill-wheel murmur; Doppler/TEE most sensitive

*Venous air embolism (sitting and posterior-fossa cases) causes a sudden EtCO₂ drop, hypotension, and a mill-wheel murmur (TEE and precordial Doppler are most sensitive); flood the field, give 100% oxygen, stop nitrous oxide, and aspirate via a right-atrial catheter.*

cards.title: Peripheral Neuropathies & Positioning Injuries
secs: Ulnar neuropathy is the most common perioperative nerve injury (from compression or stretch at the elbow; more in men and in long cases) — pad the elbow and keep the forearm supinated. Brachial plexus stretch comes from arm abduction beyond 90°, lateral head rotation, or shoulder braces in steep Trendelenburg. In the lower limb, the common peroneal nerve is vulnerable to compression at the fibular head in the lithotomy position (causing foot drop), and the sciatic and femoral nerves can be stretched.

Prevent injury with neutral positioning, padding, arm abduction under 90°, and avoiding prolonged pressure or stretch. Most injuries resolve — document a baseline and involve neurology with EMG if a deficit persists.
Ulnar = most common; arm abduction < 90°; common peroneal at the fibular head (lithotomy)
*Ulnar neuropathy is the most common positioning injury; keep arm abduction under 90°, pad the elbow and the fibular head (common peroneal → foot drop), and maintain neutral positioning.*

head: Cause
Nerve at risk
tables.note: Ulnar neuropathy is the most common perioperative nerve injury; pad pressure points.

cards.title: Perioperative Hypothermia
secs: Anesthesia impairs thermoregulation, and redistribution of core heat to the periphery causes the largest temperature drop in the first hour. The consequences are coagulopathy, more surgical-site infection, prolonged drug metabolism, shivering (which raises oxygen consumption and stresses the heart), and delayed recovery. Prevent and treat it with forced-air warming (the most effective method), warmed IV fluids, a raised ambient temperature, and pre-warming.
Treat postoperative shivering with warming and low-dose meperidine (or dexmedetomidine/clonidine). Monitor core temperature in any case lasting more than about 30 minutes.
Redistribution = biggest early loss; forced-air warming = best prevention
*Anesthesia-induced redistribution drives early hypothermia, causing coagulopathy, infection, and shivering; forced-air warming is the best prevention, and meperidine treats shivering.*

head: Effect
Detail
rows: ↑ wound infection, shivering ↑ O₂ demand, delayed emergence
tables.note: Redistribution causes the first-hour drop; use forced-air warming.

cards.title: Latex Allergy
secs: Latex allergy is an IgE-mediated (Type I) hypersensitivity to natural rubber latex that causes intraoperative anaphylaxis, often DELAYED by about 20–60 minutes as mucosal and glove exposure builds. The highest-risk groups are patients with spina bifida or myelomeningocele, those with multiple prior surgeries, healthcare workers, and people with fruit cross-reactivity (banana, avocado, kiwi, chestnut). A separate Type IV contact dermatitis is a delayed, non-anaphylactic reaction.

Manage at-risk patients with a latex-free environment and by scheduling them as the first case of the day; treat anaphylaxis with epinephrine. Identify at-risk patients during the preoperative evaluation.

*Latex allergy is IgE-mediated anaphylaxis (often delayed intraoperatively), highest in spina bifida and multiple surgeries with fruit cross-reactivity — use a latex-free environment and treat with epinephrine.*

cards.title: Aspiration of Gastric Contents
secs: Aspiration risk is highest at induction and emergence in full-stomach patients (trauma, pregnancy, obstruction, reflux, gastroparesis, or recent GLP-1 agonist use). Aspiration causes a chemical pneumonitis (the Mendelson criteria are a gastric volume above 25 mL and a pH below 2.5), hypoxemia, and possibly ARDS or pneumonia. Prevent it with a rapid-sequence induction and cricoid pressure, NPO adherence, aspiration prophylaxis (sodium citrate, an H2 blocker, metoclopramide), and an awake extubation.
If aspiration occurs: position head-down and lateral, suction, give 100% oxygen, and support oxygenation with PEEP; reserve antibiotics for established infection, and do not give routine steroids.
Mendelson risk: gastric volume > 25 mL and pH < 2.5

*Aspiration (in full-stomach patients) causes a chemical pneumonitis (volume above 25 mL, pH below 2.5); prevent it with rapid-sequence induction, cricoid pressure, and prophylaxis, and manage it with suction, oxygen, and PEEP — not routine steroids.*

cards.title: Malignant Hyperthermia
secs: Malignant hyperthermia is autosomal dominant: a mutation in the RYR1 (ryanodine receptor) or CACNA1S gene causes uncontrolled skeletal-muscle calcium release on exposure to a volatile agent or succinylcholine, producing a hypermetabolic crisis. The earliest and most sensitive sign is an unexplained rising EtCO₂ with tachycardia, followed later by hyperthermia, rigidity, acidosis, hyperkalemia, rhabdomyolysis (a high CK and myoglobinuria), and arrhythmias. Masseter spasm after succinylcholine can herald malignant hyperthermia.

Associated conditions include Central Core Disease and King-Denborough syndrome; the diagnostic test is the caffeine-halothane contracture test. Use a trigger-free anesthetic (TIVA) and a clean machine for a susceptible patient.
RYR1 mutation; triggers = volatiles + succinylcholine; first sign = rising EtCO2 Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV, repeat to ~10 mg/kg; treat hyperkalemia/acidosis, cool

*Malignant hyperthermia (RYR1, autosomal dominant) is triggered by volatiles and succinylcholine, and the earliest sign is a rising EtCO₂ — stop the triggers, give dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg (up to about 10), cool the patient, and treat hyperkalemia and acidosis.*

head: Triggers
Safe agents
tables.note: Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV, repeat to 10 mg/kg; RYR1 mutation.

cards.title: Postoperative Hypertension & Hypotension
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Postoperative Care (I.B.7)
secs: Postoperative hypertension comes from pain, hypercarbia or hypoxia, a distended bladder, agitation, withdrawal (beta-blocker or clonidine rebound), or pre-existing hypertension — treat the cause first, then add antihypertensives. Postoperative hypotension comes from hypovolemia or hemorrhage (the most common), residual anesthetic or a neuraxial sympathectomy, sepsis, a cardiac cause (ischemia, arrhythmia, tamponade), or anaphylaxis. Always look for a treatable cause before reflexively reaching for a drug.
Use fluids or blood for hypovolemia and vasopressors or inotropes to match the deficit, and treat pain and bladder distension. Consider point-of-care ultrasound for undifferentiated hypotension.

*Treat the CAUSE: postoperative hypertension is often pain, hypercarbia, a full bladder, or withdrawal, while hypotension is most often hypovolemia or hemorrhage (also residual block, a cardiac cause, sepsis, or anaphylaxis).*

cards.title: PONV: Risk, Prevention & Treatment
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Postoperative Care (I.B.7)
secs: The Apfel risk factors (each adds roughly 20%) are female sex, being a nonsmoker, a history of PONV or motion sickness, and the use of postoperative opioids. Other contributors are the volatile agents, nitrous oxide, a longer operation, and certain procedures. Multimodal prophylaxis combines classes — a 5-HT3 antagonist, dexamethasone, an NK-1 antagonist, droperidol, scopolamine, and propofol TIVA — and breakthrough is treated with a DIFFERENT class.

Lower the baseline risk with TIVA, by minimizing opioids, volatiles, and nitrous oxide, with good hydration, and with regional techniques. Give dexamethasone at induction and ondansetron near the end of surgery.
Apfel score (0–4): female, nonsmoker, PONV/motion-sickness history, postoperative opioids

*Use the Apfel score (female, nonsmoker, history of PONV/motion sickness, postoperative opioids); prevent with multimodal prophylaxis (a 5-HT3 antagonist plus dexamethasone, and others) and TIVA, and treat breakthrough with a different class.*

head: Category
Factors
rows: Female, nonsmoker, PONV/motion-sickness history, postop opioids
Long duration; lap, gynecologic, ENT, strabismus

head: Class (receptor)
Drug
Dose
tables.note: Combine classes; dexamethasone at induction, 5-HT₃ at end of case.

cards.title: Residual Paralysis & Recovery of Airway Reflexes
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Postoperative Care (I.B.7)
secs: Residual neuromuscular blockade (a train-of-four ratio below 0.9) impairs pharyngeal function and airway protection, risking aspiration, upper-airway obstruction, hypoxemia, and reintubation. Confirm recovery with a quantitative train-of-four ratio of at least 0.9 before extubation — clinical tests like a 5-second head lift are insensitive. Reverse appropriately: neostigmine with at least 2–4 twitches present, or sugammadex for a deep block.
Risk factors include long-acting agents, inadequate reversal, hypothermia, and drug interactions. Extubate awake with intact reflexes in any patient at aspiration risk.
Adequate recovery = quantitative TOF ratio ≥ 0.9 (head lift is insensitive)

*Residual paralysis (a train-of-four ratio below 0.9) risks aspiration and obstruction; confirm a quantitative train-of-four ratio of at least 0.9 before extubation, reverse adequately, and extubate awake when aspiration risk is high.*

cards.title: Postoperative Delirium & Cognitive Dysfunction
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Postoperative Care (I.B.7)
secs: Postoperative delirium is an acute, fluctuating confusion and inattention (over hours to days), common in the elderly and linked to longer hospital stays and worse outcomes. Risk factors include advanced age, baseline cognitive impairment, polypharmacy, anticholinergics and benzodiazepines, sensory impairment, pain, sleep disruption, and infection. Postoperative cognitive dysfunction is a more subtle, longer-lasting decline in cognition.

Prevent and treat it with reorientation, sleep hygiene, early mobilization, treating pain (while minimizing opioids), avoiding deliriogenic drugs, and correcting metabolic causes; use low-dose antipsychotics for hyperactive delirium if needed. Depth-of-anesthesia monitoring may reduce delirium in some patients. Pediatric EMERGENCE delirium is distinct from postoperative delirium: a short-lived agitated, inconsolable state on waking, driven mainly by the volatile agents (sevoflurane, desflurane). It is reduced by propofol-based TIVA, dexmedetomidine or a propofol bolus at the end of the case, and adequate analgesia (e.g., caudal/regional); assess with the PAED scale.

*Postoperative delirium (acute, fluctuating, in the elderly) is driven by anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, pain, and sensory or sleep disruption; prevent it with reorientation, mobilization, and avoiding deliriogenic drugs.*

cards.title: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS)
secs: ERAS pathways bundle evidence-based perioperative measures to speed recovery and reduce complications (originally for colorectal surgery). The elements are carbohydrate loading and shortened fasting (clear liquids up to 2 hours), multimodal opioid-sparing plus regional analgesia, PONV prophylaxis, euvolemic goal-directed fluids, early mobilization and feeding, normothermia, and minimally invasive surgery. They avoid prolonged fasting, routine bowel prep, nasogastric tubes, and excess opioids and fluids.
They improve length of stay and outcomes but require multidisciplinary coordination. Regional anesthesia and opioid sparing are central to ERAS.

*ERAS bundles carbohydrate loading and short fasting, multimodal opioid-sparing plus regional analgesia, PONV prophylaxis, goal-directed euvolemia, normothermia, and early feeding and mobilization — avoiding prolonged fasting and excess opioids and fluids.*

cards.title: Controlled Hypotension
secs: Controlled hypotension is the deliberate lowering of the blood pressure (for example, a mean pressure of about 50–65) to reduce surgical blood loss and improve the operative field (in spine surgery, FESS, and major tumor resection). The agents are the volatiles, remifentanil, beta-blockers (esmolol), vasodilators (nitroprusside, nitroglycerin, nicardipine), and dexmedetomidine. The risks are organ hypoperfusion (cerebral, renal, coronary, and optic — ischemic optic neuropathy in prone spine surgery), and it is contraindicated with significant vascular, cerebral, or renal disease.
Maintain perfusion to the vital organs and respect the autoregulation limits. Use it cautiously and reverse it at any sign of ischemia.
Controlled hypotension MAP ~50–65 to reduce blood loss; risk of organ ischemia

*Controlled hypotension (a mean pressure of about 50–65) reduces blood loss and improves the field using vasodilators, beta-blockers, or remifentanil — but it risks organ ischemia (cerebral, renal, optic) and is contraindicated with significant vascular disease.*

cards.title: Targeted Temperature Management; Rewarming; Shivering
secs: Perioperative hypothermia (which is common) causes coagulopathy, increased infection, arrhythmia, a prolonged drug effect, and shivering (which raises oxygen consumption) — maintain normothermia with forced-air warming and fluid warmers. Targeted temperature management after cardiac arrest (about 32–36°C) may improve the neurologic outcome, and controlled rewarming avoids an overshoot. Shivering is treated with warming and meperidine (or dexmedetomidine or clonidine); it increases the myocardial oxygen demand.
The first intraoperative heat loss is a redistribution (core to peripheral) after induction — pre-warm to prevent it. Hypothermia impairs platelet function and the coagulation enzymes.
Maintain normothermia; post-arrest TTM ~32–36°C; shivering → meperidine

*Avoid perioperative hypothermia (coagulopathy, infection, arrhythmia, shivering) with active warming; post-arrest targeted temperature management is about 32–36°C, and shivering responds to warming and meperidine.*

cards.title: Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy
secs: Breathing 100% oxygen at an increased ambient pressure raises the dissolved oxygen — used for carbon monoxide poisoning, decompression sickness, air embolism, necrotizing infections, and problem wounds. The anesthetic implications are a fire risk (the oxygen-rich environment), pressure effects on air-filled spaces (endotracheal tube cuffs — fill them with saline; a pneumothorax expands), equipment compatibility, and limited patient access. Oxygen toxicity (CNS seizures, pulmonary) occurs with prolonged or high exposure.

For carbon monoxide poisoning, hyperbaric oxygen speeds the carboxyhemoglobin elimination and may reduce the neurologic sequelae. Manage the air-filled cuffs and spaces and use chamber-compatible equipment.

*Hyperbaric oxygen (100% oxygen at pressure) treats carbon monoxide poisoning, decompression sickness, and air embolism — but it raises the fire risk, expands air-filled spaces (saline-fill the endotracheal tube cuff), and can cause oxygen toxicity and seizures.*

cards.title: High Altitude Anesthesia
secs: A lower barometric pressure means a lower inspired PO₂, causing hypoxemia; chronic adaptation includes hyperventilation, polycythemia, and a rightward oxygen-curve shift. Vaporizer output (the partial pressure) and gas behavior change with the ambient pressure — but an agent-specific vaporizer still delivers the correct partial pressure even though the volume percent differs. Provide supplemental oxygen and watch for altitude illness (acute mountain sickness, HAPE, HACE).
Nitrous oxide has a reduced partial-pressure effect at altitude. Account for equipment calibration and patient hypoxemia.

*At high altitude, the lower barometric pressure causes hypoxemia (with chronic polycythemia and a right shift); agent-specific vaporizers still deliver the correct partial pressure though the volume percent differs — give supplemental oxygen and watch for altitude illness.*

cards.title: Extracorporeal Support
secs: ECMO and extracorporeal life support: venoarterial (supporting the heart and lung) versus venovenous (supporting the lung only); they require anticoagulation, large cannulae, and a circuit (a pump plus an oxygenator). They are used for refractory cardiac or respiratory failure and as a bridge; there is also extracorporeal CO₂ removal and the cardiopulmonary-bypass derivatives. The anesthetic implications are drug sequestration in the circuit (altered dosing — lipophilic drugs adsorb), anticoagulation and bleeding, and hemodynamic management.
Circuit sequestration changes the pharmacokinetics (so higher sedative and analgesic doses may be needed). Coordinate the anticoagulation and monitoring.

*Extracorporeal support (venoarterial supports the heart and lung, venovenous the lung) needs anticoagulation and alters pharmacokinetics (circuit sequestration of lipophilic drugs → higher doses) — manage bleeding and hemodynamics.*

cards.title: Airway Endoscopy; Laser Surgery; Airway Fires; Laser-Safe Tubes
secs: It is a shared airway with the surgeon; the techniques include jet ventilation, apneic oxygenation, and small or laser-safe tubes. The AIRWAY FIRE triad: an oxidizer (oxygen or nitrous oxide) plus a fuel (the ETT or drapes) plus an ignition source (the laser or cautery) → so minimize the FiO₂ (under 30% if possible, and avoid nitrous oxide) and use a laser-resistant tube with a saline-filled cuff. Fire management: STOP the gas flow, remove the tube, pour saline, then ventilate or reintubate and assess the airway injury.
Prevention: a low FiO₂, a laser-safe tube, a saline cuff, and vigilance. Have saline and a fire plan ready for airway laser cases.
Fire triad: oxidizer + fuel + ignition; airway fire → stop gas, remove tube, saline, reintubate

*Airway laser surgery risks fire (an oxidizer plus a fuel plus ignition) — minimize the FiO₂ and avoid nitrous oxide, and use a laser-safe tube with a saline-filled cuff; if a fire occurs, stop the gas flow, remove the tube, douse with saline, then reintubate and assess the injury.*

head: Element
Source
tables.note: Airway fire: stop gases, remove ETT, saline, then ventilate; keep FiO₂ low near cautery.

cards.title: Intraoperative Nerve Injury Monitoring
secs: Nerve monitoring (for example, the recurrent laryngeal nerve in thyroid surgery, or the facial nerve in parotid or ear surgery) detects and preserves nerves intraoperatively. It requires AVOIDING (or carefully dosing) neuromuscular blockade so that the evoked EMG responses are present — coordinate the relaxation with the surgeon (often only for intubation, then allow recovery or reverse with sugammadex). A specialized EMG tube (for the recurrent laryngeal nerve) detects the vocal-cord muscle activity.
Plan the muscle-relaxant timing around the monitoring needs. Communication with the surgical and neuromonitoring team is key.

*Intraoperative nerve monitoring (the recurrent laryngeal and facial nerves) preserves nerves but requires limiting neuromuscular blockade so the evoked responses persist — coordinate the relaxant timing (often paralyze only for intubation) with the surgeon.*

cards.title: Free Flaps & Microvascular Surgery; Liposuction
secs: Free-flap surgery: maintain the flap perfusion → an adequate cardiac output and volume, avoid vasoconstrictors, hypothermia, and hypovolemia, and balance the hematocrit; these are long cases (with positioning, temperature, and fluid considerations). Liposuction: the tumescent technique uses large volumes of dilute LIDOCAINE with epinephrine → a risk of local-anesthetic toxicity (with a delayed peak), fluid shifts, and hypothermia; respect the lidocaine limits (the tumescent limit is about 35–55 mg/kg). Watch the fluid balance and the cumulative lidocaine dose.
Free flaps: keep the patient warm, well-perfused, and normovolemic (some avoid pressors). Tumescent lidocaine peaks late (a delayed toxicity).

*Free-flap surgery requires maintaining the flap perfusion (normovolemia, warmth, cardiac output, and caution with vasopressors); tumescent liposuction uses large dilute lidocaine doses (with a delayed-peak toxicity risk) and fluid shifts — respect the dose limits.*

cards.title: Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgery; Pneumoperitoneum Effects
secs: A CO₂ pneumoperitoneum raises the intra-abdominal pressure → decreased venous return and preload, increased SVR and afterload, decreased FRC and compliance (with higher airway pressures), and hypercarbia (from the CO₂ absorption) requiring increased ventilation. Steep Trendelenburg (in robotic pelvic surgery) worsens these and causes facial, airway, and cerebral edema and tube migration; reverse Trendelenburg reduces the venous return. The risks are a CO₂ embolism (a sudden EtCO₂ drop and hypotension), subcutaneous emphysema, pneumothorax, and a vagal bradycardia on insufflation.
Limited access → secure the airway and lines before positioning. Watch for ETT migration (endobronchial) with Trendelenburg and pneumoperitoneum.
Pneumoperitoneum: ↓ preload, ↑ afterload, ↓ FRC, ↑ PaCO2; watch CO2 embolism

*A CO₂ pneumoperitoneum lowers the preload and FRC while raising the afterload and PaCO₂ (increase the ventilation); steep Trendelenburg adds edema and tube migration — watch for a CO₂ embolism (a sudden EtCO₂ drop), pneumothorax, and a vagal bradycardia.*

head: System
Effect
tables.note: Absorbed CO₂ → ↑ minute ventilation; watch for gas embolism.

cards.title: Retrobulbar/Peribulbar Blocks; Open Eye Injury; IOP
secs: The intraocular pressure rises with coughing or bucking, hypercarbia, hypoxia, a head-down position, external pressure, and SUCCINYLCHOLINE (a transient rise) — so avoid IOP spikes with an open globe (which can extrude the contents). The open-globe plus full-stomach dilemma: balance the aspiration risk against the IOP — use a controlled rapid-sequence induction with a non-depolarizer (rocuronium) and deep anesthesia to blunt the response (avoid succinylcholine where possible). The oculocardiac reflex (eye-muscle traction → bradycardia) → release the traction and give atropine.
Most volatiles and IV agents lower the IOP (ketamine is debated). Use a smooth induction and emergence to avoid IOP spikes.
Open globe: avoid IOP spikes (cough/sux); RSI with rocuronium; oculocardiac reflex → atropine

*For an open globe, avoid IOP spikes (coughing, succinylcholine, hypercarbia) — use a controlled rapid-sequence induction with rocuronium, balancing the aspiration against the extrusion risk; the oculocardiac reflex (eye traction → bradycardia) is treated by releasing the traction and giving atropine.*

head: ↑ IOP
↓ IOP
tables.note: Open globe: blunt ↑ IOP responses; succinylcholine controversial — prefer nondepol RSI.

cards.title: Tourniquet Management; Regional vs General
secs: Pneumatic tourniquets provide a bloodless field but cause tourniquet PAIN (a dull pain that develops over time, even under regional anesthesia), hypertension, and distal ischemia. Tourniquet RELEASE → a washout of acid, potassium, and CO₂ → a transient hypotension, hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, and a rise in EtCO₂; limit the inflation time (to under about 2 hours) to avoid nerve and muscle injury. Regional anesthesia (neuraxial or peripheral blocks) benefits joint arthroplasty (with analgesia, less DVT, and less blood loss); fat or cement (bone-cement implantation syndrome) and VTE are orthopedic risks.
Bone-cement implantation syndrome: hypotension, hypoxia, and arrhythmia on cementing. Time the tourniquet and anticipate the release effects.
Tourniquet release → ↑ K, acidosis, ↑ EtCO2, hypotension; limit inflation ~< 2 h

*Tourniquets give a bloodless field but cause tourniquet pain and hypertension and, on release, a transient hyperkalemia, acidosis, EtCO₂ rise, and hypotension (limit to under about 2 hours); regional anesthesia benefits arthroplasty — watch for bone-cement implantation syndrome and VTE.*

cards.title: Massive Trauma; Resuscitation from Hemorrhagic Shock
secs: The priorities (ATLS): the airway with C-spine control, breathing, circulation (hemorrhage control), disability, and exposure; assume a full stomach and a possible cervical injury (use in-line stabilization and a rapid-sequence induction). Damage-control resuscitation: permissive hypotension until control, balanced 1:1:1 transfusion, early tranexamic acid, calcium, and warming; avoid the lethal triad (hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy). Identify and treat the immediate threats (a tension pneumothorax, tamponade, massive hemorrhage); activate the massive transfusion protocol.
Treat hemorrhage with blood products, not just crystalloid. Anticipate a difficult airway and hemodynamic collapse on induction (reduce the induction dose).
Trauma: damage-control resuscitation (permissive hypotension, 1:1:1, TXA, calcium, warm)

*Massive trauma follows ATLS (the airway with C-spine control, then hemorrhage control) with damage-control resuscitation — permissive hypotension until control, balanced 1:1:1 transfusion, early tranexamic acid, calcium, and warming to avoid the lethal triad.*

cards.title: Traumatic Brain Injury Management
secs: The goal is to prevent SECONDARY injury by maintaining the cerebral perfusion and oxygenation — avoid hypotension and hypoxia (each worsens the outcome); maintain the CPP (often about 60–70) = MAP − ICP. Control the ICP: head elevation, normocapnia (a PaCO₂ of about 35; brief hyperventilation only for herniation), osmotherapy (mannitol or hypertonic saline), sedation, and avoiding hyperthermia and hyperglycemia. Secure the airway (assume a C-spine injury and a full stomach) and avoid coughing and pressure surges.

Hypotension and hypoxia are the key preventable harms. Avoid prolonged or aggressive hyperventilation (it causes ischemia). Corticosteroids are CONTRAINDICATED in TBI (they increase mortality — the CRASH trial); routine hyperoxia (a PaO₂ over 150) and prophylactic hypothermia have not improved outcomes — maintaining the CPP over 60 is the evidence-based target.
TBI: avoid hypotension/hypoxia; CPP = MAP − ICP (~60–70); PaCO2 ~35 (hyperventilate only for herniation)

*TBI management prevents secondary injury — avoid hypotension and hypoxia, maintain the CPP (about 60–70 = MAP − ICP), keep the PaCO₂ at about 35 (hyperventilate only for herniation), and use osmotherapy, head elevation, and sedation for the ICP.*

cards.title: Burn Management
secs: Early: the airway (an inhalation injury → early intubation before the edema; carbon monoxide and cyanide), fluid resuscitation (Parkland — about 4 mL/kg per %TBSA, half in the first 8 hours), and hypothermia prevention. Pharmacology: AVOID succinylcholine after about 24–48 hours (extrajunctional-receptor upregulation → a hyperkalemic arrest; the risk persists about 1–2 years); there is a RESISTANCE to non-depolarizers; and there are increased metabolic and analgesic needs (the patient is hypermetabolic, with opioid tolerance). There are repeated procedures (debridement and grafting), a difficult airway and access, and temperature and fluid management.
Carbon monoxide: the SpO₂ reads falsely normal — use co-oximetry; consider cyanide (from smoke). The succinylcholine danger window starts about 24–48 hours post-burn.
Parkland: 4 mL/kg/%TBSA (½ in first 8 h); avoid succinylcholine after ~24–48 h (hyperkalemia)

*Burns need early airway control (an inhalation injury, carbon monoxide, cyanide), Parkland fluid resuscitation (4 mL/kg per %TBSA, half in 8 hours), and warmth; avoid succinylcholine after about 24–48 hours (a hyperkalemic arrest) and expect non-depolarizer resistance and high analgesic needs.*

head: Region
% TBSA (adult)
tables.note: Parkland: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA LR; give half in first 8 h; titrate to UO.

cards.title: Mass Casualty & Disaster Preparedness; Chemical/Biological Warfare
secs: Mass casualty: TRIAGE to do the most good for the most people (a shift from an individual to a population focus); surge capacity and resource allocation. Chemical agents: the nerve agents (organophosphates → a cholinergic crisis — treat with atropine plus pralidoxime; avoid succinylcholine, which is prolonged by the cholinesterase inhibition); cyanide; and the vesicants. Biological and radiation agents: decontamination, PPE, and isolation — protect the staff.
Nerve agents inhibit cholinesterase (so succinylcholine is markedly prolonged). Decontaminate and protect the providers before treating.

*Mass-casualty care shifts to triage (the greatest good for the most); nerve agents cause a cholinergic crisis (atropine plus pralidoxime; succinylcholine is markedly prolonged) — and decontamination and PPE protect the providers from chemical, biological, and radiation agents.*

cards.title: Ambulatory: Patient Selection; Discharge Criteria; Office-Based
secs: Selection favors stable patients and procedures; OSA, poorly controlled comorbidity, and certain ex-premature infants warrant scrutiny; emphasize short-acting agents, PONV prevention, and multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia. The discharge criteria (for example, the modified Aldrete or PADSS): stable vital signs, an alert patient, controlled pain and PONV, ambulation, and minimal bleeding; with voiding and an escort as indicated. Office-based anesthesia: ensure the equipment, monitoring, emergency preparedness, and transfer plans are equal to facility standards.
Use fast-tracking with short-acting agents and regional techniques. Office-based settings need the same safety standards as the OR.

*Ambulatory anesthesia selects stable patients and procedures with short-acting agents, PONV prevention, and opioid-sparing analgesia, then discharges on objective criteria (stable vital signs, pain and PONV control, ambulation); office-based settings need OR-equivalent safety.*

head: Domain
Assessed
tables.note: Aldrete ≥ 9 for PACU; meet criteria before discharge.

cards.title: Aging: MAC Changes; CNS/CV/Renal/Hepatic Implications
secs: The MAC DECREASES with age (by about 6% per decade after 40); the elderly are more sensitive to anesthetics and opioids (so reduce the doses and titrate slowly). There is a reduced physiologic reserve: a stiffer vasculature and diastolic dysfunction (with blood-pressure lability), decreased renal and hepatic clearance (prolonging drugs), reduced lung reserve, altered thermoregulation, and a risk of postoperative delirium and cognitive dysfunction. Polypharmacy and frailty increase the risk — use careful drug selection and dosing.
Postoperative delirium is common — minimize deliriogenic drugs, treat pain, and reorient the patient. Use a “start low, go slow” approach with titration.
MAC ↓ ~6% per decade after 40; reduced reserve/clearance → lower doses

*Aging lowers the MAC (by about 6% per decade after 40) and the physiologic reserve — the elderly are sensitive to anesthetics (reduce and titrate the doses), have blood-pressure lability and slowed clearance, and are prone to postoperative delirium and cognitive dysfunction.*

head: System
Change
tables.note: Titrate doses down and expect prolonged drug effect.

cards.title: Electroconvulsive Therapy (ECT)
secs: ECT induces a therapeutic seizure (for refractory depression or catatonia) under brief general anesthesia plus muscle relaxation; the physiologic response is a PARASYMPATHETIC surge (bradycardia or asystole) then a SYMPATHETIC surge (hypertension and tachycardia). The agents: a short-acting induction agent (methohexital is classic — it minimally suppresses the seizure; propofol shortens it; etomidate prolongs it) plus succinylcholine (to limit convulsive injury); hyperventilate to lower the seizure threshold. Use an anticholinergic (glycopyrrolate) for the bradycardia and manage the hypertensive and tachycardic response (with esmolol or labetalol).
Avoid agents that excessively shorten the seizure (propofol) when the seizure duration matters. The relative cautions are a raised ICP, a recent MI, pheochromocytoma, and an aneurysm.
ECT: parasympathetic (bradycardia) then sympathetic (HTN/tachycardia) surge; methohexital + succinylcholine

*ECT induces a therapeutic seizure under brief general anesthesia plus succinylcholine, with a parasympathetic (bradycardia) then sympathetic (hypertension and tachycardia) surge — methohexital preserves the seizure (propofol shortens it); manage with glycopyrrolate and beta-blockers.*

cards.title: Organ Donor Pathophysiology; Brain Death Criteria
secs: Brain death is the irreversible loss of all brain and brainstem function: it is established by a coma with a known cause, absent brainstem reflexes, and apnea (the apnea test — no respiration despite a rising PaCO₂), with the confounders excluded (hypothermia, drugs, metabolic causes). Donor physiology after brain death: hemodynamic instability (an “autonomic storm” then vasoplegia), diabetes insipidus, hypothermia, and lung and cardiac dysfunction → so support the donor to preserve the organs (fluids, vasopressors, DDAVP, hormonal resuscitation, and lung-protective ventilation). The goal shifts to organ preservation while honoring the consent and protocols.
Spinal reflexes can persist (they don't refute brain death). Aggressive donor management improves the graft yield and function.

*Brain death is the irreversible loss of all brain and brainstem function (a coma plus absent brainstem reflexes plus a positive apnea test, confounders excluded); donor management treats the instability, diabetes insipidus, and hypothermia to preserve the organs.*

head: Requirement
Detail
tables.note: Donor management: treat DI, maintain perfusion, lung-protective ventilation.

cards.title: Anesthesia Outside the OR; CT/MRI Implications
secs: Remote sites (endoscopy, the cath lab, interventional radiology, CT or MRI, radiation) have unfamiliar layouts, limited help and equipment, and hazards → so maintain OR-standard monitoring, equipment checks, and emergency plans. MRI: a strong magnet → a ferromagnetic-projectile danger (use MRI-safe equipment), burns and heating, noise, limited access, and zone restrictions; know the quench and code procedures. CT and radiation: radiation exposure → use remote monitoring, shielding, and distance; and watch for contrast reactions.
Pre-plan how to evacuate and resuscitate in the MRI zone. Bring familiar equipment and verify the compatibility.

*Out-of-OR anesthesia (MRI, CT, interventional radiology, the cath lab) demands OR-level monitoring despite the limited access and help; MRI adds projectile, heating, and noise hazards needing MRI-safe equipment and zoning, and radiation requires shielding and remote monitoring.*

cards.title: Chemotherapy Agents — Anesthetic Implications
cards.cat: Clinical Sciences › Procedures & Techniques › Evaluation & Preoperative Preparation (I.B.1)
secs: BLEOMYCIN causes a dose-related pulmonary toxicity (pneumonitis and fibrosis); a high inspired oxygen potentiates the free-radical lung injury, so keep the FiO₂ as LOW as is safely tolerated in anyone with prior bleomycin exposure. DOXORUBICIN and the other anthracyclines cause a dose-dependent cardiomyopathy and heart failure — assess the ventricular function before anesthesia. Others: cisplatin → nephrotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, and ototoxicity; vincristine → peripheral and autonomic neuropathy; cyclophosphamide → a prolonged succinylcholine effect (from cholinesterase inhibition); methotrexate → myelosuppression and mucositis.

Take a careful chemotherapy history: limit the oxygen with prior bleomycin, check an echo with the anthracyclines, and assess the renal function and neuropathy with the platinum agents. Many agents cause myelosuppression (anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia) — relevant to the transfusion and infection risk.
Bleomycin → limit FiO₂ (O₂ potentiates lung injury) Doxorubicin → cardiomyopathy (check EF); cyclophosphamide → prolongs succinylcholine

*Know the toxicities that change the anesthetic: bleomycin (limit the FiO₂ — oxygen worsens its lung injury), doxorubicin (cardiomyopathy — check the EF), cisplatin (renal and neuropathy), and cyclophosphamide (it prolongs succinylcholine).*

cards.title: Diuretics on the Day of Surgery
secs: Diuretics are generally HELD the morning of surgery to avoid intraoperative hypovolemia, hypotension, and electrolyte disturbance (notably hypokalemia with loop/thiazide agents). Exceptions where the diuretic is CONTINUED: patients with significant volume overload — decompensated CHF, ascites, pulmonary edema. Loop diuretics prescribed specifically for HYPERTENSION may be continued for blood-pressure control.

Fits the broader medication-management framework: continue beta-blockers, statins, and (usually) antihypertensives; hold ACE-inhibitors/ARBs and diuretics case-by-case for their hypotension risk; manage anticoagulants and diabetic agents per protocol. Check and correct potassium before induction in patients on chronic loop/thiazide therapy, since hypokalemia potentiates neuromuscular blockade and predisposes to arrhythmias.
Hold diuretics on surgery day — except in volume overload (CHF, ascites) or when a loop diuretic is used for HTN.

cards.title: EVAR vs Open AAA Repair: Outcomes Trade-offs
secs: For abdominal aortic aneurysm repair, ENDOVASCULAR repair (EVAR) and OPEN surgical repair carry different risk profiles. EVAR offers LOWER perioperative (30-day) MORTALITY, less blood loss, shorter hospital and ICU stays, quicker recovery/rehabilitation, and avoids a major laparotomy and aortic cross-clamp. However, EVAR requires lifelong CT surveillance and is associated with more MINOR REINTERVENTIONS - chiefly for ENDOLEAKS (persistent flow into the aneurysm sac), graft migration, or limb occlusion. Open repair has HIGHER upfront morbidity and mortality but requires fewer, though HIGHER-RISK, reinterventions such as repeat LAPAROTOMY. The early survival advantage of EVAR seen in trials (EVAR-1, DREAM, OVER) tends to EQUALIZE with open repair by roughly 2-3 years as late aneurysm-related events and reinterventions accrue.

Anesthetic implications: open repair involves aortic CROSS-CLAMPING (abrupt afterload increase, proximal hypertension, distal ischemia, and reperfusion/declamp hypotension and acidosis on release) and large fluid/blood shifts, typically under general anesthesia with invasive monitoring and often a thoracic epidural. EVAR can be done under general, regional, or even local/MAC, has less hemodynamic derangement, but the anesthesiologist must watch for CONTRAST NEPHROPATHY, radiation, and the rare need for emergent conversion to open. For a RUPTURED AAA, EVAR (when anatomically feasible) is increasingly favored and may improve survival.

EVAR wins on early mortality and recovery but trades that for frequent MINOR reinterventions (endoleaks); open repair is more durable but higher-risk upfront - and the survival curves converge within a few years.

cards.title: Logarithms & Logarithmic Transformation
secs: pH is a logarithmic scale: pH = −log₁₀[H⁺], so a fall of one pH unit means a tenfold rise in hydrogen-ion concentration. Many biologic relationships — drug decay, dose–response — are exponential and become straight lines after a log transformation. Decibels, drug-potency ratios, and the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation all rely on logarithms.
Log scales compress data that span a wide range and linearize exponential processes (elimination kinetics, MAC fractions) so they are easier to analyze.
pH = −log10[H+]   (pH 7.4 ≈ 40 nmol/L H+) Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A−]/[HA])
*pH = −log[H⁺], so each unit is a tenfold change; log transformation straightens out exponential biologic data and underlies the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation.*

cards.title: Exponential Functions & Biologic Curves
secs: First-order processes — drug elimination, anesthetic wash-in and washout, lung emptying — all follow exponential curves. The time constant τ equals volume divided by flow (also 1/k): about 63% of the change is complete after one time constant, ~95% after three, and ~99% after five. The half-life t½ = 0.693/k = 0.693 × τ; about half is cleared each half-life, so ~97% is gone by five half-lives.
This underlies the FA/FI wash-in of inhaled agents, context-sensitive half-time, and three-compartment intravenous pharmacokinetics.
*First-order kinetics are exponential: one time constant clears ~63% (≈95% by three, ≈99% by five), and the half-life is 0.693/k.*

cards.title: Mean, Median & Mode
secs: The mean is the arithmetic average (sensitive to outliers), the median is the middle value (robust to skew), and the mode is the most frequent value. In a normal (symmetric) distribution, the mean, median, and mode all coincide. With right (positive) skew, mean > median > mode; with left (negative) skew, mean < median < mode.

Report the median for skewed data such as length of stay or cost, and the mean for symmetric data. Your choice of central-tendency measure dictates the dispersion measure: standard deviation goes with the mean, interquartile range with the median.
Normal: mean = median = mode Right skew: mean > median > mode (the tail pulls the mean)
*The median resists outliers and suits skewed data; in a skewed distribution the mean is pulled toward the tail (right skew: mean > median > mode).*

cards.title: Standard Deviation & Standard Error
secs: The standard deviation (SD) describes how spread out individual values are around the mean — it is a property of the data itself. The standard error of the mean (SEM) = SD/√n describes how precisely the mean is estimated, and it shrinks as the sample grows. In a normal distribution, about 68% of values lie within ±1 SD, ~95% within ±2 SD, and ~99.7% within ±3 SD.

Use the SD to describe variability, and the SEM (and confidence intervals) to convey the precision of an estimate. Authors sometimes report the SEM instead of the SD to make their data look tighter than it is — read carefully.
*SD describes the spread of the data, while SEM = SD/√n describes the precision of the mean; about 68/95/99.7% of values fall within ±1/±2/±3 SD.*

cards.title: T-test
secs: A t-test compares the MEANS of two groups when the data are continuous and roughly normally distributed. Use an unpaired (independent) t-test for two separate groups and a paired t-test for before/after or otherwise matched data. It assumes normality and, for the unpaired version, roughly equal variances between the groups.

For more than two groups, use analysis of variance (ANOVA) rather than many t-tests, which would inflate the false-positive (Type I) error. For non-normal data, use a non-parametric test such as the Mann–Whitney U or Wilcoxon.
Two means, continuous + normal → t-test (paired vs unpaired) > 2 groups → ANOVA;  non-normal → Mann-Whitney / Wilcoxon
*The t-test compares two means of normal, continuous data (paired vs unpaired); use ANOVA for more than two groups and a non-parametric test when the data aren’t normal.*

cards.title: Chi-square Test
secs: The chi-square test looks for an association between CATEGORICAL variables using a contingency table. It compares the observed frequencies in each cell with the frequencies you would expect if there were no association. When expected cell counts are small (under 5), Fisher’s exact test is used instead.
A typical use is comparing complication rates (yes/no) between two techniques. It does not apply to continuous data.
Chi-square: categorical data (observed vs expected counts) Small expected counts (< 5) → Fisher’s exact test
*Chi-square tests for association between categorical variables; switch to Fisher’s exact test when the expected cell counts are small.*

cards.title: Regression & Correlation
secs: Correlation (r) measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship, from −1 to +1; r-squared is the proportion of variance explained. Linear regression models a continuous outcome, logistic regression models a binary outcome (as odds), and Cox regression models time-to-event data (as a hazard). Multivariable regression adjusts for confounders.
Correlation is not causation, and an r near 0 only rules out a LINEAR relationship — a strong curved relationship could still exist. Choose the regression model according to the type of outcome.
r: −1 to +1;  r-squared = variance explained Outcome → model: continuous=linear, binary=logistic, time-to-event=Cox
*r (−1 to +1) measures linear association; choose linear, logistic, or Cox regression by the outcome type — and remember correlation does not prove causation.*

cards.title: ANOVA
secs: Analysis of variance (ANOVA) compares the means of THREE OR MORE groups of continuous, normal data using the F-statistic. A significant ANOVA tells you at least one group differs; post-hoc tests (Tukey, Bonferroni) then identify which ones. It avoids the inflated false-positive error you would get from running many separate t-tests.
There is one-way and two-way ANOVA (the latter testing interactions), plus repeated-measures ANOVA for paired or longitudinal data. The non-parametric equivalent is the Kruskal–Wallis test.
ANOVA: ≥ 3 group means (F-test) + post-hoc to localize Non-parametric: Kruskal-Wallis
*ANOVA compares three or more means with the F-test; a significant result needs post-hoc testing to localize the difference, and it avoids the Type I inflation of repeated t-tests.*

cards.title: Power Analysis
secs: Power equals 1 − β: the probability of detecting a true effect (that is, of avoiding a false-negative, Type II error); the usual target is at least 0.80. It is calculated in advance (a priori) to determine the required SAMPLE SIZE. Power increases with a larger effect size, a larger sample, lower variability, and a higher chosen alpha.
An underpowered study risks a false-negative — missing a real effect. Calculating power after the fact (post-hoc) is generally discouraged.
Power = 1 − beta (target ≥ 0.80); beta = Type II error rate ↑ power with ↑ effect size, ↑ n, ↓ variance, ↑ alpha
*Power = 1 − β (aim for ≥ 0.80) and is set in advance to size the study; an underpowered trial produces false-negative results.*

cards.title: Meta-analysis & Systematic Review
secs: A systematic review is a structured, reproducible synthesis of all eligible studies on a question. A meta-analysis goes a step further and statistically pools the results across studies into a single combined estimate, displayed as a forest plot. Heterogeneity — quantified by the I-squared statistic — measures how much the studies vary; high heterogeneity weakens the pooled result.
Done well, this is the highest level of evidence, but it is “garbage in, garbage out” — the conclusion is only as good as the included studies. Publication bias is assessed with a funnel plot.
Forest plot = effect sizes; I-squared = heterogeneity; funnel plot = publication bias
*A systematic review synthesizes the literature and a meta-analysis pools effects into a forest plot; check the I-squared for heterogeneity and a funnel plot for publication bias.*

cards.title: Confidence Intervals
secs: A 95% confidence interval (CI) is the range that would contain the true population value in 95% of repeated samples. It narrows with larger samples and lower variability. For a difference in means, a CI that crosses 0 is non-significant; for a ratio (odds ratio, relative risk, or hazard ratio), a CI that crosses 1 is non-significant.
A confidence interval conveys both the effect SIZE and its PRECISION, making it more informative than a p-value alone.
95% CI ≈ estimate ± 1.96 × SE Crosses 0 (differences) or 1 (ratios) → not significant
*A 95% CI shows both effect size and precision; it is non-significant if it crosses 0 for a difference or 1 for a ratio such as an odds ratio, relative risk, or hazard ratio.*

cards.title: Odds Ratio & Relative Risk
secs: Relative risk (RR) is the risk in the exposed group divided by the risk in the unexposed — used in cohort studies and randomized trials, where risk can be measured directly. The odds ratio (OR) is the odds in the exposed divided by the odds in the unexposed — used in case-control studies, where the underlying risk cannot be measured directly. For both, a value of 1 means no effect, greater than 1 means increased, and less than 1 means decreased.
The OR only approximates the RR when the outcome is rare; for common outcomes the OR exaggerates the size of the effect.
RR = risk(exposed)/risk(unexposed);  OR = odds(exposed)/odds(unexposed) OR ≈ RR only for rare outcomes;  = 1 means no effect

*Relative risk (risk ÷ risk) suits cohorts and trials, while the odds ratio (odds ÷ odds) suits case-control studies; both equal 1 at no effect, and the OR only approximates the RR for rare outcomes.*

head: Measure
Formula
Use
tables.note: OR ≈ RR when the outcome is rare (a = exposed with disease).

cards.title: Bland-Altman Plot
secs: A Bland–Altman plot assesses how well two measurement methods AGREE — for example, a new monitor compared with a reference standard. It plots the difference between each pair of measurements against their average. It shows the bias (the mean difference) and the limits of agreement (roughly bias ± 1.96 times the standard deviation of the differences).

It is preferred over correlation, which can look high even when two methods disagree in a systematic way. Typical uses include comparing noninvasive against arterial-line blood pressure, or one cardiac-output method against another.
Plots (method1 − method2) vs their mean Limits of agreement = bias ± 1.96 SD of differences

*A Bland–Altman plot shows the agreement between two methods (bias ± 1.96 SD limits) — the right tool when validating a new monitor against a reference, since correlation alone can hide systematic disagreement.*

cards.title: Poisson Distribution
secs: The Poisson distribution gives the probability of a certain number of independent, RARE events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space. Its defining feature is that the mean equals the variance (both called lambda). It models counts of rare events, such as adverse events per 1,000 anesthetics or cases per day.
It underlies rate-based analyses and Poisson regression for count data.
Poisson: rare independent events; mean = variance = lambda
*The Poisson distribution models counts of rare, independent events over an interval, and its mean equals its variance (lambda).*

cards.title: Number Needed to Treat & Absolute Risk Reduction
secs: Absolute risk reduction (ARR) is the control-group event rate minus the treatment-group event rate — the real, absolute benefit. Number needed to treat (NNT) = 1/ARR: the number of patients you must treat to prevent one event (a lower NNT is better). Number needed to harm (NNH) = 1/absolute risk increase: the parallel measure for adverse effects.
A relative risk reduction can sound impressive while the ARR and NNT reveal only a modest absolute benefit — always look at both.
ARR = control rate − treatment rate NNT = 1 / ARR;  NNH = 1 / absolute risk increase
*NNT = 1/ARR (lower is better) and NNH = 1/absolute risk increase; relative measures can exaggerate benefit, so judge it by the absolute risk reduction.*

head: Measure
Formula
tables.note: NNT rounds up; NNH = 1/ARI for harm.

cards.title: Hazard Ratio & Survival Analysis
secs: Survival (time-to-event) analysis is designed to handle censored data — patients who have not yet had the event when the study ends. Kaplan–Meier curves display survival over time, and the log-rank test compares two such curves. The hazard ratio (from Cox regression) compares the instantaneous event rate between groups: 1 means no difference, and above 1 means a higher hazard.
It is used for outcomes like mortality, time to extubation, and time to cancer recurrence.
Kaplan-Meier = survival curve; log-rank compares curves; Cox → hazard ratio HR = 1 no difference; > 1 higher hazard
*Survival analysis handles censored data; Kaplan–Meier curves with the log-rank test compare groups, and the Cox hazard ratio quantifies the relative event rate over time.*

cards.title: Type I / II Error, p-value & Multiple Comparisons
secs: A Type I error (alpha, a false positive) is rejecting a null hypothesis that is actually true; a Type II error (beta, a false negative) is missing a real effect. The p-value is the probability of seeing data this extreme IF the null hypothesis were true — it is NOT the probability that the null is true. Testing many comparisons inflates the false-positive rate, so correct for it (for example, with the Bonferroni method).
A p-value below 0.05 does not prove clinical importance — also weigh the effect size and confidence intervals. By convention alpha is 0.05 and power (1 − beta) should be at least 0.80.
alpha = Type I (false positive); beta = Type II (false negative); power = 1 − beta Bonferroni: adjusted alpha = 0.05 / number of comparisons
*Type I error is a false positive (alpha) and Type II a false negative (beta); the p-value is conditional on the null being true, and multiple comparisons must be corrected.*

head: H₀ true
H₀ false
tables.note: Power = 1 − β; raise it with larger n or effect size.

cards.title: Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV, Prevalence
secs: Sensitivity is the proportion of truly diseased people who test positive; a highly sensitive test, when negative, rules disease OUT (“SnNout”). It is a fixed property of the test. Specificity is the proportion of truly healthy people who test negative; a highly specific test, when positive, rules disease IN (“SpPin”). It is also a property of the test. Positive and negative predictive values (PPV, NPV) depend on PREVALENCE: as prevalence rises, PPV rises and NPV falls.

A highly sensitive test is good for screening, while a highly specific test is good for confirmation. Likelihood ratios combine sensitivity and specificity and, unlike predictive values, are independent of prevalence.
Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN);  Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) PPV/NPV depend on prevalence;  LR+ = sens / (1 − spec)
*Sensitivity and specificity are properties of the test (SnNout / SpPin); PPV and NPV shift with prevalence; sensitive tests screen and specific tests confirm.*

head: Metric
Formula
tables.note: PPV/NPV depend on prevalence; sensitivity/specificity do not. SnNout, SpPin.

cards.title: AUROC / Discrimination & Calibration
secs: A receiver-operating-characteristic (ROC) curve plots sensitivity against 1 − specificity across all thresholds, and the area under it (AUROC, or c-statistic) summarizes the test’s DISCRIMINATION. An AUROC of 0.5 is no better than chance, 0.7–0.8 is acceptable, above 0.9 is excellent, and 1.0 is perfect. Calibration — how closely predicted probabilities match observed ones — is a separate quality from discrimination.
AUROC is used to evaluate risk-prediction models and diagnostic tests. A model can discriminate well yet be poorly calibrated, or the reverse.
AUROC: 0.5 chance, 0.7–0.8 acceptable, > 0.9 excellent Discrimination ≠ calibration
*AUROC (0.5 = chance, >0.9 = excellent) measures how well a test discriminates; calibration — predicted versus observed probability — is a separate property.*

cards.title: Bias Types & Inter-rater Reliability
secs: Several biases distort study validity: selection, measurement/information, recall, lead-time, and attrition bias. Blinding and randomization reduce many of these. Inter-rater reliability — how well two observers agree — is quantified by Cohen’s kappa.

Kappa corrects for agreement that would happen by chance: below 0.2 is poor, 0.4–0.6 moderate, and above 0.8 excellent. Validity (accuracy, freedom from bias) is distinct from precision (reproducibility).
*Know the common biases (selection, recall, lead-time, attrition); Cohen’s kappa measures chance-corrected agreement between observers; validity is not the same as precision.*

cards.title: Confounding & Propensity Methods
secs: A confounder is a variable associated with BOTH the exposure and the outcome, distorting the apparent effect between them. Randomization prevents confounding; in observational data it is controlled by multivariable adjustment, stratification, matching, or propensity scores. Propensity-score weighting or matching balances measured covariates to mimic a randomized trial.
Crucially, propensity methods balance only MEASURED confounders — unmeasured confounding remains, which is why they don’t fully replace randomization.
Confounder: linked to BOTH exposure and outcome Propensity methods balance measured (not unmeasured) confounders
*A confounder is linked to both the exposure and the outcome; randomization handles it best, while propensity methods balance only the measured confounders.*

cards.title: Study Design (Cross-sectional, Case-control, Cohort)
secs: A cross-sectional study is a snapshot at a single point in time (it gives prevalence but cannot establish which came first). A case-control study starts with the outcome and looks backward at exposure (retrospective; yields odds ratios; efficient for rare diseases). A cohort study starts with the exposure and follows subjects forward for the outcome (yielding relative risk and incidence).

The evidence hierarchy runs: systematic review/meta-analysis > randomized trial > cohort > case-control > cross-sectional > case series. Observational designs can show association but cannot, on their own, prove causation.
Case-control → odds ratio;  cohort → relative risk / incidence
*Cross-sectional = snapshot, case-control = outcome→exposure (odds ratio, good for rare disease), cohort = exposure→outcome (relative risk); randomized trials outrank every observational design.*

cards.title: Randomization & Blinding
secs: Randomization balances known AND unknown confounders across groups — the central strength of a randomized controlled trial (RCT). Allocation concealment hides the upcoming group assignment from enrollers and is distinct from blinding. Blinding (single, double, or triple) reduces performance bias and assessment bias.
Intention-to-treat analysis keeps patients in their assigned groups, preserving randomization and avoiding an overestimate of efficacy. The CONSORT guidelines standardize how RCTs are reported.
Randomization balances measured AND unmeasured confounders Analyze by intention-to-treat to preserve randomization
*Randomization balances both known and unknown confounders; allocation concealment and blinding cut bias; analyze by intention-to-treat to preserve the randomization.*

cards.title: Physician Wellness: Substance Abuse, Fatigue, Aging
secs: Anesthesiologists have an elevated risk of substance use disorder (from their access to potent agents — opioids, propofol); the signs include unexplained absences, charting irregularities, and a desire to work alone; it is a treatable disease requiring intervention, not just discipline. Fatigue and sleep deprivation impair performance like alcohol intoxication → so use duty-hour limits, strategic naps, and handoffs. Burnout and aging affect performance and wellbeing → use wellness programs, peer support, and accommodations.
A suspected impairment requires a confidential intervention and reporting per policy. Fatigue management improves safety.

*Anesthesiologists face an elevated substance-use risk (from drug access — a treatable disease needing intervention); fatigue impairs performance like intoxication (so use duty limits and naps), and burnout and aging warrant wellness programs and peer support.*

cards.title: Informed Consent & Shared Decision-Making
secs: A valid informed consent requires DISCLOSURE (the risks, benefits, and alternatives), CAPACITY (understanding and reasoning), and VOLUNTARINESS (no coercion); the patient (or surrogate) decides. Shared decision-making aligns the care with the patient's values and goals; document the discussion. The exceptions: emergencies (implied consent), therapeutic privilege (rare), and waiver.
Capacity is decision-specific (not a global label). Minors and incompetent patients need a surrogate (with exceptions like emergencies).

*Informed consent requires disclosure (the risks, benefits, and alternatives), a decision-specific capacity, and voluntariness; shared decision-making aligns the care with the patient's values — and emergencies allow implied consent.*

cards.title: Advance Directives / DNR; Suspended DNR
secs: Advance directives (living wills, a healthcare proxy) guide the care when the patient lacks capacity; a DNR order limits resuscitation. A DNR is NOT automatically suspended for surgery → there is a REQUIRED RECONSIDERATION: discuss and document whether to suspend, modify (procedure-directed), or maintain the DNR perioperatively (respecting the patient's autonomy). Many intraoperative “resuscitative” actions are routine anesthesia care — so clarify the scope with the patient.
Don't assume; have the goals-of-care conversation before surgery. Restore the original DNR status postoperatively per the agreement.

*A DNR is not automatically void for surgery — a required reconsideration means discussing and documenting whether to suspend, modify, or keep it perioperatively; respect the patient's autonomy and restore the prior status afterward.*

cards.title: HIPAA & Patient Privacy
secs: HIPAA protects identifiable health information; disclose only the minimum necessary and only to those involved in the care or with authorization. Permitted without authorization: treatment, payment, operations, and certain legal and public-health requirements. Avoid casual disclosures (in elevators, on social media); secure the records and devices.
Breaches carry penalties and erode trust. A “need to know” governs the access.

*HIPAA protects identifiable health information — share only the minimum necessary with those involved in the care or as legally required; avoid casual and social-media disclosures and secure the records.*

cards.title: Jehovah’s Witness / Refusal of Transfusion
secs: A competent adult Jehovah's Witness may REFUSE blood and blood products (an exercise of autonomy) even when it is life-saving; clarify exactly what is acceptable (some accept cell salvage, albumin, factors, or erythropoietin) in advance, and document it. Plan blood-conservation strategies (iron and erythropoietin, antifibrinolytics, cell salvage if accepted, acute normovolemic hemodilution, and meticulous hemostasis). Minors and incompetent patients differ (a court may compel transfusion to save a child's life).
Respect the documented refusal and individualize the acceptable products. A preoperative discussion avoids intraoperative dilemmas.

*A competent Jehovah's Witness may refuse transfusion (autonomy) even when it is life-saving — clarify and document the acceptable products (some allow cell salvage, factors, or erythropoietin) and plan blood conservation; minors are handled differently (a court may compel it).*

cards.title: Medical Malpractice Elements
secs: Negligence (malpractice) requires four elements: DUTY (a provider-patient relationship), BREACH (a failure to meet the standard of care), CAUSATION (the breach caused the harm), and DAMAGES (an actual injury). The standard of care is what a reasonable similar practitioner would do; expert testimony usually establishes it. All four elements must be proven (by a preponderance of the evidence) for liability.
Documentation and communication reduce liability. The benchmark is the “standard of care,” not perfection.
Malpractice = Duty + Breach + Causation + Damages (all four required)

*Medical malpractice requires all four elements — duty, a breach of the standard of care, causation, and damages; the standard is what a reasonable similar practitioner would do, usually established by expert testimony.*

head: Element
Meaning
tables.note: All four are required to prove negligence.

cards.title: National Practitioner Data Bank; Closed Claims
secs: The National Practitioner Data Bank records malpractice payments and adverse licensure and privilege actions; it is queried for credentialing. The ASA Closed Claims Project analyzes settled malpractice claims to identify patterns and improve safety (for example, it highlighted respiratory and airway events, driving monitoring standards like pulse oximetry and capnography). These data inform credentialing and patient-safety initiatives.
Closed-claims analysis shaped the modern monitoring standards. Respiratory events historically dominated the severe anesthesia claims.

*The National Practitioner Data Bank tracks malpractice payments and adverse actions for credentialing; the ASA Closed Claims Project analyzes claims to improve safety (its airway and respiratory findings drove the pulse-oximetry and capnography standards).*

cards.title: Controlled Substances Law (Opioids, Cannabinoids)
secs: The DEA schedules controlled substances I–V by their abuse potential and medical use (Schedule I = no accepted medical use; II = a high abuse potential, for example fentanyl and morphine); there are strict prescribing, documentation, storage, and waste and diversion controls. Anesthesia practice requires accurate controlled-substance accounting (with witnessed waste) to prevent diversion. Cannabis is federally Schedule I but state-legal in many places (a legal conflict); chronic use affects the anesthetic requirements.
Diversion prevention: accurate documentation and witnessed waste. Chronic cannabis or opioid use alters the anesthetic and analgesic needs.

*Controlled substances are DEA-scheduled I–V by abuse potential (II = fentanyl and morphine) with strict prescribing, waste, and diversion controls; cannabis remains federally Schedule I despite state legalization, and chronic use alters the anesthetic needs.*

head: Schedule
Description
Example
tables.note: Schedule II requires strict documentation and witnessed waste.

cards.title: IRB, DSMB, ClinicalTrials.gov; Consent in Research
secs: The Institutional Review Board (IRB) reviews and approves human research to protect the subjects (informed consent, the risk-benefit balance, and vulnerable populations) per the Common Rule and the Belmont principles (respect, beneficence, justice). A Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB) monitors ongoing trials for safety and efficacy (and can stop them early); ClinicalTrials.gov provides trial registration and transparency. Research consent differs from clinical consent (it is voluntary and may include disclosure of the placebo or randomization).
The Belmont principles: respect for persons, beneficence, and justice. Vulnerable populations receive extra protection.

*The IRB protects research subjects (consent, the risk-benefit balance, the Belmont principles); a DSMB monitors trial safety (and can halt it early) and ClinicalTrials.gov ensures registration and transparency — research consent is distinct from clinical consent.*

cards.title: Publication Bias; Academic Integrity; Conflicts of Interest
secs: Publication bias: positive or significant results are published more often than negative ones → which skews the evidence base (this is mitigated by trial registration and the reporting of all results). Academic integrity: avoid fabrication, falsification, and plagiarism; authorship requires a genuine contribution. Conflicts of interest (financial or industry) must be disclosed and managed to preserve trust.
Registered trials reduce selective reporting. Disclosure manages (but doesn't eliminate) conflicts.

*Publication bias (positive results published more, which skews the evidence) is mitigated by trial registration; academic integrity forbids fabrication, falsification, and plagiarism, and financial conflicts of interest must be disclosed and managed.*

cards.title: Healthcare Funding & Cost-Conscious Practice
secs: The payment models: fee-for-service (rewarding volume) versus value-based or bundled payment and capitation (rewarding outcomes and efficiency); these involve Medicare, Medicaid, and private insurers; the shift toward value rewards quality and cost control. Cost-conscious anesthesia: choose cost-effective drugs and techniques without compromising safety (for example, low-flow anesthesia, the judicious use of expensive agents and sugammadex, and reducing waste). Balance resource stewardship against the individual patient's benefit.
Value = outcomes divided by cost; align the incentives with quality. Reduce waste (of drugs and disposables) safely.

*Healthcare is shifting from fee-for-service (volume) toward value-based and bundled payment (outcomes and efficiency); cost-conscious anesthesia chooses cost-effective drugs and techniques and reduces waste (low-flow anesthesia) without compromising safety.*

cards.title: Anesthesia Care Team & Scope of Practice
