secs: The anesthesia care team model: physician anesthesiologists medically direct or supervise CRNAs (certified registered nurse anesthetists) and anesthesiologist assistants; “medical direction” has specific TEFRA requirements (presence at the key portions, limited concurrency). The scope of practice and supervision vary by state law and facility; team-based care emphasizes communication and clear role definition. The roles, credentialing, and supervision ratios affect the billing and safety.
Medical direction requires the anesthesiologist's involvement at the key steps. State opt-outs affect the CRNA supervision requirements.

*The anesthesia care team has physician anesthesiologists directing or supervising CRNAs and anesthesiologist assistants; “medical direction” carries specific requirements (presence at the key portions, limited concurrency), with the scope and supervision varying by state law.*

cards.title: Perioperative Surgical Home; ERAS
secs: The Perioperative Surgical Home (PSH) is a patient-centered, physician-led, coordinated model spanning the entire surgical journey (pre-, intra-, post-, and post-discharge) to improve outcomes and reduce the cost and variation. ERAS protocols (evidence-based bundles) operationalize this: optimization, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia, early feeding and mobilization, and standardized pathways. Anesthesiologists are well-positioned as perioperative physicians leading these models.
The PSH reduces variation and improves coordination and value. ERAS is the protocol-level expression of the PSH principles.

*The Perioperative Surgical Home is a coordinated, physician-led model across the whole surgical journey to improve outcomes and value; ERAS bundles operationalize it (optimization, opioid-sparing analgesia, early recovery) — positioning anesthesiologists as perioperative physicians.*

cards.title: Clinical Informatics; EMR; AI & Machine Learning
secs: The anesthesia information management system (AIMS) or EMR automates the record and supports decision-making and quality reporting, but it adds a documentation burden and alert fatigue. AI and machine learning: prediction (of hypotension, a difficult airway, risk), decision support, and workflow — these require validation, bias awareness, and human oversight (they are a tool, not a replacement). Data quality, privacy and security, and interoperability are key considerations.
Beware automation bias and alert fatigue. Models need validation and monitoring for bias and drift.

*Anesthesia information systems and EMRs automate records and support quality and decision-making (with a documentation burden and alert fatigue); AI and machine learning aid prediction and decision support but need validation, bias awareness, and human oversight.*

cards.title: Documentation, Coding & Billing
secs: Anesthesia billing combines BASE UNITS (for the procedure complexity) plus TIME UNITS plus modifiers; accurate documentation (the start and stop times, the ASA status, the invasive lines, the medical direction) supports compliant billing. Document the anesthetic record completely and honestly; upcoding and unbundling are fraud. Quality reporting (MIPS, registries) and modifiers affect the reimbursement.
Time-based billing requires accurate start- and stop-time documentation. Compliance avoids fraud and abuse penalties.
Anesthesia charge ≈ (base units + time units) × conversion factor (+ modifiers)

*Anesthesia billing = base units (for the procedure) plus time units (plus modifiers), so accurate documentation (the times, the ASA status, the lines, the medical direction) drives compliant reimbursement — upcoding and unbundling are fraud.*

cards.title: Medical Error, Adverse & Sentinel Events
secs: A medical error is a failure of a planned action or the use of a wrong plan (it may or may not reach the patient); an adverse event is harm caused by the care (not by the disease); a near miss is an error that didn't reach the patient. A sentinel event is a serious, largely preventable safety event causing severe harm or death (for example, wrong-site surgery) → which triggers an immediate investigation (a root cause analysis). A systems (not just a blame) approach improves safety.
Most errors stem from system failures, not individual incompetence. Report near misses to prevent future harm.

*A medical error is a wrong or failed plan (which may not reach the patient); an adverse event is harm from the care; and a sentinel event is serious preventable harm (wrong-site surgery) triggering a root cause analysis — a systems approach beats blame.*

head: Term
Definition
tables.note: Analyze via root-cause analysis in a just (non-blame) culture.

cards.title: Human Factors & Systems Thinking
secs: Human-factors engineering designs systems around human capabilities and limitations (the equipment, workflow, and environment) to reduce error — for example, standardized connectors, forcing functions, and checklists. The Swiss-cheese model: accidents occur when the latent system holes align; so build layered defenses rather than blaming individuals. Fatigue, workload, distraction, and poor design all contribute to error.
Forcing functions and standardization prevent predictable errors. Design for the human; don't expect perfect humans.

*Human-factors engineering designs systems around human limitations (standardized connectors, forcing functions, checklists); the Swiss-cheese model shows accidents happen when the latent system flaws align — build layered defenses instead of blaming individuals.*

cards.title: Medication Error Assessment & Prevention
secs: Anesthesia is high-risk (the same person prescribes, prepares, and administers the drug without the usual pharmacy and nursing checks); the common errors are the wrong drug, the wrong dose, or syringe swaps. Prevention: standardized prefilled or ready-to-use syringes, color-coded and barcoded labels, careful labeling, double-checks for the high-alert drugs, and avoiding look-alike and sound-alike storage. Read the label or ampoule before drawing up and administering.
Syringe swaps are a classic anesthesia error. Barcoding and prefilled syringes reduce errors.

*Anesthesia medication errors are high-risk (one person prescribes, prepares, and gives the drug without pharmacy checks) — syringe swaps and the wrong dose are common; prevent them with standardized labeling and barcoding, prefilled syringes, and reading every label before administration.*

cards.title: Medication Reconciliation
secs: Medication reconciliation compares a patient's current medications against the new orders at transitions (admission, transfer, discharge) to prevent omissions, duplications, and interactions. Perioperatively, decide which drugs to continue (most cardiac and pulmonary ones), to hold (the ACE inhibitor or ARB on the morning, certain anticoagulants, SGLT2 inhibitors, and some herbals), or to adjust. An accurate medication history prevents perioperative drug errors.
Herbals and supplements (garlic, ginkgo, ginseng) affect bleeding and interactions. Reconcile at every care transition.

*Medication reconciliation compares the current medications against the new orders at every transition to prevent omissions and duplications; perioperatively it guides which to continue, hold (the ACE inhibitor or ARB, SGLT2 inhibitors, certain anticoagulants and herbals), or adjust.*

cards.title: Simulation Training; Crisis Manuals & Cognitive Aids
secs: Simulation lets teams practice rare crises (malignant hyperthermia, LAST, anaphylaxis, cardiac arrest) safely → which improves both technical and team performance (communication, roles). Crisis manuals and cognitive aids (emergency checklists or “code” handbooks) improve adherence to the key steps under stress (reducing the omission of critical actions). They are used for training, certification (for example, the MOCA simulation), and real-time crisis support.
Reading a crisis checklist during an emergency improves performance (it is not a sign of weakness). Debriefing consolidates the learning.

*Simulation safely rehearses rare crises (malignant hyperthermia, LAST, anaphylaxis) and team skills; crisis manuals and cognitive aids (emergency checklists) improve adherence to the critical steps under stress — using them during a real crisis improves performance.*

cards.title: Handoff Communication; Preprocedural Checklists
secs: Handoffs (care transitions) are error-prone → so use structured formats (for example, SBAR — Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) to transfer the information completely. The WHO Surgical Safety Checklist and the preprocedural “time-out” (sign-in, time-out, sign-out) verify the patient, site, procedure, allergies, antibiotics, and concerns → reducing wrong-site and wrong-patient errors and mortality. Standardization and a culture that empowers speaking up improve safety.
The time-out is a team pause to confirm the critical items. Structured handoffs reduce dropped information.

*Structured handoffs (SBAR) prevent information loss at transitions, and the surgical safety checklist and time-out (sign-in, time-out, sign-out) verify the patient, site, procedure, allergies, and antibiotics — reducing wrong-site errors and mortality.*

head: Content
tables.note: Structured handoffs at transitions reduce errors.

cards.title: Lean Six Sigma; Value Stream Mapping; 5S
secs: Lean reduces WASTE (the non-value-added steps); Six Sigma reduces VARIATION and defects (via DMAIC: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control); the two are combined to improve quality and efficiency. The tools: value stream mapping (to visualize and streamline a process flow) and 5S (Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain — for workplace organization). They are applied to the perioperative flow (turnover, scheduling, supply).
Lean targets waste; Six Sigma targets variation. DMAIC structures improvement projects.
Six Sigma = DMAIC (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control); Lean = remove waste

*Lean removes waste and Six Sigma reduces variation (via DMAIC); value stream mapping streamlines the process flow and 5S organizes the workplace — applied to perioperative efficiency (turnover, supply, scheduling).*

cards.title: Failure Mode & Effects Analysis (FMEA)
secs: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) is a PROACTIVE, prospective risk analysis: you map out a process, list the ways each step could fail (the failure modes), and trace their effects and causes — all BEFORE any harm occurs. Each failure mode is scored on three 1–10 scales — severity, occurrence (how likely), and detectability (how easily it would be caught) — and these are multiplied to give a Risk Priority Number (RPN = severity × occurrence × detectability). A higher RPN flags a more dangerous failure mode, so the highest-RPN items are prioritized first for redesign and mitigation. It contrasts with root cause analysis (RCA), which is REACTIVE — performed after an adverse event has already happened.

Remember the pairing: FMEA is proactive (prevent future failures), whereas RCA is reactive (explain a failure that already occurred). FMEA is the tool of choice when designing a safer process or rolling out a new workflow.
FMEA: Risk Priority Number (RPN) = severity × occurrence × detectability  — proactive scoring to rank failure modes

*FMEA is a proactive risk analysis — identify failure modes and score severity × occurrence × detectability (risk priority number) to fix them before harm (contrast with reactive root cause analysis).*

cards.title: Root Cause Analysis; M&M Conferences
secs: Root cause analysis (RCA) is a REACTIVE, structured investigation after an adverse or sentinel event to find the underlying SYSTEM causes (not just the proximate error) and prevent a recurrence (using the “5 whys” and fishbone diagrams). Morbidity and mortality (M&M) conferences review cases and complications to learn and improve, ideally in a blame-free, systems-focused culture. Both emphasize systems improvement over individual blame.
Ask “why” repeatedly to reach the system cause. A just culture balances accountability with learning.

*Root cause analysis is a reactive, systems-focused investigation after adverse events (the 5 whys, fishbone diagrams) to prevent recurrence; M&M conferences review complications to learn — both favor systems improvement over blame.*

cards.title: Benchmarking; Quality Payment Program; Anesthesia Registries
secs: Benchmarking compares performance against peers and standards to drive improvement; the quality metrics (process and outcome) are increasingly tied to payment. The Merit-based Incentive Payment System (MIPS) and the Quality Payment Program link Medicare reimbursement to quality, cost, and improvement reporting. Anesthesia registries (for example, NACOR and the AQI) aggregate data for benchmarking, research, and quality improvement.
Outcome data inform both improvement and reimbursement. Registries enable large-scale benchmarking and research.

*Benchmarking compares performance to peers and standards; the Quality Payment Program (MIPS) ties Medicare payment to quality and cost reporting, and anesthesia registries (NACOR, AQI) aggregate data for benchmarking and research.*

cards.title: Implicit Bias, Microaggression, Stereotype Threat
secs: Implicit bias: unconscious attitudes or stereotypes that affect decisions and behavior (and can drive disparities in pain treatment, for example) despite a conscious intent otherwise; awareness and structured decisions help to mitigate it. A microaggression is a subtle, often unintentional slight that communicates a demeaning message to a marginalized group. Stereotype threat is the anxiety about confirming a negative group stereotype, which impairs performance.
Implicit bias contributes to documented care disparities. Mitigation: awareness, standardized processes, and an inclusive culture.

*Implicit bias (unconscious attitudes) can drive care disparities despite good intent; microaggressions are subtle demeaning slights, and stereotype threat is the performance-impairing anxiety about confirming a stereotype — mitigated by awareness and standardized processes.*

cards.title: Systemic Racism & Discrimination Barriers
secs: Systemic (structural) racism: policies and practices embedded in institutions that produce inequitable outcomes regardless of individual intent (in access, quality, and workforce diversity). It contributes to documented health disparities (in maternal mortality, pain management, and outcomes). Addressing it requires structural and policy change, not only individual attitude change.
Distinguish the structural causes from individual bias. Workforce diversity and equitable policy are part of the solution.

*Systemic (structural) racism is inequity embedded in institutional policies and practices regardless of individual intent — it drives documented disparities (in maternal mortality and pain care) and requires structural and policy change, not just individual attitude change.*

cards.title: Allyship vs Performative Action; Upstander vs Bystander
secs: Allyship: consistent, substantive support for marginalized groups (using one's position to create change); performative action is a superficial gesture without real effort or impact. An upstander is someone who intervenes when witnessing bias or harm; a bystander is one who witnesses but does not act. Effective allyship listens, amplifies, sponsors, and acts (not just symbolically).
Sponsorship and active intervention beat symbolic gestures. Speaking up (as an upstander) interrupts harm.

*Allyship is consistent, substantive support (versus performative, superficial gestures); an upstander intervenes when witnessing bias while a bystander stays passive — effective allyship listens, amplifies, sponsors, and acts.*

cards.title: Social Determinants of Health
secs: The social determinants of health — economic stability, education, healthcare access, the neighborhood and environment, and the social context — shape a large share of health outcomes (often more than the clinical care itself). They drive disparities in disease burden, perioperative risk, and recovery; screening for and addressing them improves equity. Consider these factors in perioperative planning (access, follow-up, support).
Non-medical factors heavily influence the outcomes. Address the access and support to reduce disparities.

*The social determinants of health (economic stability, education, healthcare access, environment, social context) shape outcomes more than clinical care alone — they drive disparities and matter for perioperative planning (access, follow-up, support).*

cards.title: Maternal Healthcare Disparities; Maternal Mortality
secs: The U.S. has a high maternal mortality among the wealthy nations, with marked RACIAL disparities (Black women have a substantially higher pregnancy-related mortality), much of it preventable. The leading causes include hemorrhage, hypertensive disorders, cardiovascular disease, and venous thromboembolism; bias, access, and systemic factors all contribute. Standardized protocols (hemorrhage and hypertension bundles), bias mitigation, and equitable access aim to reduce the disparities. Cardiovascular conditions (including cardiomyopathy) account for over one-third of pregnancy-related deaths, the single leading cause (see figure).
Much maternal mortality is preventable with timely, standardized care. Listen to and act on the maternal warning signs.

*U.S. maternal mortality is high with stark racial disparities (Black women at substantially higher risk), and it is largely preventable — driven by hemorrhage, hypertensive disorders, and cardiac disease; safety bundles, bias mitigation, and equitable access aim to close the gap.*

cards.title: Child, Adolescent & ICU Disparities
secs: Disparities by race and ethnicity, language, and socioeconomic status appear across pediatric and ICU care — including in pain management, access, and outcomes. Children and adolescents and critically ill patients are vulnerable populations where bias, communication barriers, and access gaps worsen the outcomes. Equity efforts: standardized protocols, interpreter use, bias awareness, and addressing the access gaps.
Language access and standardized care reduce disparities. Vulnerable populations need deliberate equity safeguards.

*Disparities by race, language, and socioeconomic status pervade pediatric and ICU care (pain management, access, outcomes) — closing them requires standardized protocols, interpreter use, bias awareness, and improved access for these vulnerable populations.*

cards.title: HIPAA — Workers' Compensation Exception
secs: The HIPAA Privacy Rule permits disclosure of protected health information (PHI) for workers' compensation without the patient's authorization, to the extent authorized by and necessary to comply with workers' compensation laws. It is one of several recognized exceptions to the authorization requirement, alongside disclosures for treatment, payment, and health-care operations, public-health activities, and valid legal/court orders.

In a workers' comp claim, employers, insurers, and claims administrators may access records relevant to the work-related injury. Providers should still apply the MINIMUM NECESSARY standard — disclose only information pertinent to that specific injury, not the entire record. Note the minimum-necessary standard does not apply to disclosures for treatment between providers.
HIPAA allows PHI disclosure for workers' compensation without patient authorization — but only the minimum necessary.

cards.title: Occupational Bloodborne Exposure — HBV, HCV, HIV Risk
secs: After a percutaneous (needlestick) exposure, HEPATITIS B carries the HIGHEST transmission risk of the major bloodborne pathogens, and risk is greatest from a hepatitis B e-antigen (HBeAg) POSITIVE source (high viral replication): roughly a 22-31% risk of clinical hepatitis and up to ~37-62% risk of serologic evidence of infection. Remember the RULE OF 3s for per-exposure transmission: ~0.3% for HIV, ~3% for HCV, ~30% for HBV. Risk rises with hollow-bore needles, deep injury, visible blood on the device, and high source viral load.

Prevention first: HBV vaccination of health-care workers (check anti-HBs titer; protective >=10 mIU/mL). After exposure, wash the site, report immediately, and test the source. HBV post-exposure: hepatitis B immune globulin (HBIG) plus vaccine for unvaccinated/non-responders. HIV: start post-exposure prophylaxis (combination antiretrovirals) ideally within HOURS. HCV: NO PEP or vaccine exists — monitor and treat established infection with direct-acting antivirals. This is a core professionalism/occupational-safety topic on the ITE.

Rule of 3s: HIV ~0.3%, HCV ~3%, HBV ~30% per percutaneous exposure. HBeAg-positive source: ~22-31% clinical hepatitis, up to ~62% serologic infection. Protective anti-HBs titer >=10 mIU/mL. HIV PEP started within hours (ideally <72 h).
Rule of 3s — HIV 0.3%, HCV 3%, HBV 30%; HBeAg-positive HBV source is the highest-risk bloodborne exposure.

cards.title: Cardiac Output
cards.expl: Blood ejected per minute — the product of heart rate and stroke volume.
clinical: A paced or β-blocked patient with a fixed rate: output now rides entirely on stroke volume.
Falling CO at an unchanged rate → think SV problem: hypovolemia, pump failure, or tamponade.
Titrating inotropes/chronotropes — decide whether to push rate or address volume/contractility.

cards.title: Cardiac Index
cards.expl: Cardiac output normalized to body size so patients can be compared.
clinical: Comparing hemodynamics across differently sized patients on a PA catheter.
CI < ~2.2 defines the cardiogenic-shock range — triggers inotrope/MCS discussion.
Plotting a patient on Forrester subsets to guide fluid vs. pressor vs. inotrope.

cards.title: Stroke Volume
cards.expl: Blood ejected per beat; set by preload, afterload, and contractility.
clinical: Interpreting echo volumes: high ESV with low SV points to poor contractility or high afterload.
Hypovolemia lowers EDV → low SV despite a normal heart.
Foundation for SV-variation analysis to predict fluid responsiveness.

cards.title: Stroke Volume Index
cards.expl: Stroke volume indexed to BSA; feeds the stroke-work indices.
clinical: Localizing shock when combined with MAP/PCWP into stroke-work indices.
A low SVI with high filling pressures → pump failure.
Size-independent comparison of pump performance.

cards.title: Ejection Fraction
cards.expl: Fraction of end-diastolic volume ejected each beat; the standard systolic index.
clinical: Pre-op risk stratification: a reduced EF (HFrEF) changes monitoring, agents, and disposition.
Symptoms with a preserved EF → HFpEF / diastolic dysfunction.
Beware: EF is load-dependent — low afterload (e.g., sepsis, vasodilation) can mask poor contractility.

cards.title: Mean Arterial Pressure
cards.expl: Time-weighted average pressure driving organ perfusion (diastole weighted double).
clinical: Setting a perfusion target — MAP, not systolic, governs autoregulated organ flow.
Sepsis/shock resuscitation goal of MAP ≥ 65 (higher if chronic HTN).
Caution at fast heart rates: short diastole means the 1/3–2/3 rule overestimates true MAP.

cards.title: Pulse Pressure
cards.expl: Systolic–diastolic difference; reflects stroke volume and arterial compliance.
clinical: Wide PP → aortic regurgitation, stiff aorta, or high-output state.
Narrow PP → low SV: hypovolemia, tamponade, or severe aortic stenosis.
Respiratory PP variation on the arterial trace predicts fluid responsiveness in ventilated patients.

cards.title: Systemic Vascular Resistance
cards.expl: Systemic afterload — pressure drop across the circuit over flow.
clinical: Low SVR → distributive shock (septic, anaphylactic, neuraxial) — reach for a vasopressor.
High SVR → the compensatory pattern in cardiogenic/hypovolemic shock.
Quantifying the hemodynamic effect of vasodilators/anesthetics vs. pressors.

cards.title: Pulmonary Vascular Resistance
cards.expl: Right-heart afterload — the gradient from mean PA pressure to wedge over output.
clinical: Managing pulmonary hypertension / RV failure: avoid hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis, high airway pressure (all raise PVR).
Guiding selective pulmonary vasodilators (inhaled NO, prostacyclin).
Distinguishing pre- vs. post-capillary pulmonary hypertension at right-heart cath.

cards.title: SVR Index
cards.expl: SVR computed with cardiac index so afterload compares across body sizes.
clinical: Comparing systemic afterload between a large and a small patient.
Tracking afterload response to vasoactive drugs in a size-normalized way.

cards.title: Hemodynamic Ohm’s Law
cards.expl: Circulatory analog of Ohm’s law: perfusion pressure = flow × resistance.
clinical: Reasoning that vasoconstriction raises pressure but can cut flow.
Underlies MAP ≈ CO × SVR — the mental model for every pressor/inotrope decision.
Explaining why a fixed stenosis limits flow unless the gradient rises.

cards.title: Coronary Perfusion Pressure
cards.expl: Pressure perfusing the LV subendocardium, which fills in DIASTOLE.
clinical: Aortic stenosis / LVH: protect the heart by keeping diastolic pressure up and rate down.
Tachycardia shortens diastole and starves the subendocardium — avoid in CAD.
Hypotension + high LVEDP (failing heart) is a dangerous ischemic combination.

cards.title: Venous Return
cards.expl: Flow back to the heart, driven by mean systemic filling pressure minus RAP.
clinical: High PEEP, tamponade, or RV failure raise RAP → venous return and CO fall.
Volume loading / venoconstriction raise MSFP → augment return.
Explaining why excessive intrathoracic pressure during ventilation drops output.

cards.title: Arterial O₂ Content
cards.expl: O₂ carried per dL — dominated by hemoglobin; dissolved O₂ is negligible.
clinical: Deciding to transfuse: anemia cuts delivery far more than a modest low PaO₂.
Once Hb is saturated, cranking FiO₂ adds almost no content.
Building block for DO₂ and shunt calculations in the critically ill.

cards.title: Mixed Venous O₂ Content
cards.expl: O₂ content of mixed venous (PA) blood; the a–v difference reflects extraction.
clinical: Sampled from the PA catheter distal port to assess global O₂ balance.
Feeds VO₂ (Fick), O₂ER, and shunt fraction.
Widening a–v difference signals rising extraction / falling delivery.

cards.title: Oxygen Delivery
cards.expl: Total O₂ delivered to tissues per minute — output × content.
clinical: The three levers of shock resuscitation: output, hemoglobin, saturation.
Delivery-dependent VO₂ in early sepsis — push DO₂ until consumption plateaus.
Deciding whether the next move is fluids/inotrope (CO), transfusion (Hb), or O₂ (SaO₂).

cards.title: Oxygen Consumption (Fick)
cards.expl: Whole-body O₂ use — output × the a–v content difference.
clinical: Rises with fever, shivering, sepsis, pain; falls with hypothermia and anesthesia.
When delivery can’t meet consumption → anaerobic metabolism, lactic acidosis.
Postop shivering: explains the surge in O₂ demand that stresses a CAD patient.

cards.title: Cardiac Output (Fick)
cards.expl: Fick principle rearranged to compute output — the cardiac-output gold standard.
clinical: Cross-checking thermodilution CO when a value looks implausible.
Cath-lab shunt runs and CO in valvular/structural disease.
Main error: VO₂ is usually estimated, not measured.

cards.title: Oxygen Extraction Ratio
cards.expl: Fraction of delivered O₂ that tissues extract.
clinical: A rising ratio (or falling SvO₂) warns that delivery isn’t keeping up with demand.
Extraction > ~50–60% predicts a switch to anaerobic metabolism.
Trending oxygen debt during major hemorrhage or low-output states.

cards.title: Mixed Venous Saturation
cards.expl: Global balance of O₂ delivery and consumption in one number.
clinical: Low SvO₂ → low output, anemia, hypoxemia, or high VO₂ — a sensitive shock monitor.
High SvO₂ → sepsis (impaired extraction), L→R shunt, or cyanide toxicity.
Continuous SvO₂/ScvO₂ to titrate resuscitation in real time.

cards.title: Rate–Pressure Product
cards.expl: An index of myocardial O₂ demand.
clinical: Intraoperative ceiling in CAD: tachycardia is doubly harmful (more demand, less diastolic supply).
Guiding β-blockade / depth to blunt the stress response at laryngoscopy and incision.
Spotting the demand spike that precedes intraop ischemia.

cards.title: LV Stroke Work Index
cards.expl: LV work per beat, integrating stroke volume with the pressure it’s generated against.
clinical: A low LVSWI with high filling pressure → LV failure.
Localizing shock on a hemodynamic profile.
Assessing response to inotropes.

cards.title: RV Stroke Work Index
cards.expl: Right-ventricular analog of stroke-work index.
clinical: Falls in RV failure or infarction — a clue in inferior MI with RV involvement.
Rises with pulmonary hypertension until the RV decompensates.
Monitoring the RV through pulmonary endarterectomy or transplant.

cards.title: Laplace — Wall Stress
cards.expl: Wall stress rises with pressure and radius, falls with wall thickness.
clinical: Afterload reduction and avoiding distension protect the failing/ischemic ventricle.
Explains why hypertrophy offsets demand while dilation raises it.
Why aneurysms enlarge progressively (larger radius → more wall stress).

cards.title: Laplace — Wall Tension
cards.expl: For a thin vessel, wall tension equals pressure times radius.
clinical: A dilated aorta/aneurysm bears more tension at the same pressure — rupture risk.
Rationale for tight BP control in aneurysm and dissection.
Larger vessels tolerate less pressure before failing.

cards.title: Vascular Compliance
cards.expl: Volume change per unit pressure change.
clinical: Veins are the capacitance reservoir — venoconstriction recruits blood volume.
Reduced arterial compliance with aging widens pulse pressure.
Why a small volume change in a stiff (non-compliant) ventricle spikes pressure.

cards.title: Intracardiac Shunt Ratio
cards.expl: Ratio of pulmonary to systemic flow, from oxygen saturations.
clinical: Quantifying an ASD/VSD to decide on closure (Qp/Qs > ~1.5–2).
A ratio < 1 indicates a net right-to-left shunt.
Pre-op planning for congenital heart disease.

cards.title: Corrected QT (Bazett)
cards.expl: Heart-rate–corrected QT (RR in seconds); prolongation → torsades risk.
clinical: Screening before QT-prolonging drugs (droperidol, ondansetron, methadone, haloperidol).
Correcting low K/Mg/Ca pre-op to shrink the QTc.
Bazett overcorrects at fast rates — interpret a tachycardic QTc cautiously.

cards.title: Poiseuille’s Law
cards.expl: Laminar flow varies with the FOURTH power of radius — radius dominates.
clinical: Choosing IV access: a short, wide catheter delivers vastly more flow for resuscitation.
Why a small airway/vascular stenosis sharply limits flow.
ETT size and IV gauge selection for rapid transfusion.

cards.title: Reynolds Number
cards.expl: Predicts the laminar-to-turbulent transition.
clinical: Rationale for low-density heliox in upper-airway obstruction (lowers Re → less turbulence).
High velocity / large diameter promote turbulence and raise resistance.
Explaining stridor and increased work of breathing in a narrowed airway.

cards.title: Body Surface Area (Mosteller)
cards.expl: Estimates BSA from height and weight.
clinical: Indexing cardiac output to cardiac index.
BSA-based dosing of chemotherapy and some other drugs.
Normalizing any hemodynamic index across body sizes.

cards.title: Alveolar Gas Equation
cards.expl: Ideal alveolar PO₂ — the value needed to compute the A–a gradient.
clinical: Working up hypoxemia: pair with PaO₂ to get the A–a gradient.
Hypoventilation (high PaCO₂) lowers alveolar PO₂.
Altitude / low FiO₂ lowers P<sub>A</sub>O₂ with a normal gradient.

cards.title: Alveolar–Arterial Gradient
cards.expl: Alveolar minus arterial PO₂; classifies hypoxemia.
clinical: Normal gradient + hypoxemia → hypoventilation or low inspired O₂ (altitude).
Widened gradient → V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion defect.
Shunt is confirmed by failure to correct on 100% O₂.

cards.title: P/F Ratio
cards.expl: Bedside grade of oxygenation impairment; defines ARDS severity (Berlin).
clinical: Diagnosing and staging ARDS (mild/moderate/severe).
Tracking response to PEEP, recruitment, and proning.
A falling P/F intraoperatively flags worsening shunt/atelectasis.

cards.title: Minute Ventilation
cards.expl: Total gas moved per minute (rate × tidal volume); sets CO₂ elimination.
clinical: Adjusting the ventilator to correct a high PaCO₂.
Recognizing that not all of V̇<sub>E</sub> is effective (dead space wastes part).
Matching ventilation to the high CO₂ load of sepsis or malignant hyperthermia.

cards.title: Alveolar Ventilation
cards.expl: The portion of ventilation that actually reaches gas-exchanging alveoli.
clinical: PaCO₂ is inversely proportional to V̇<sub>A</sub> — the lever for CO₂ control.
Rapid shallow breathing wastes ventilation on dead space and raises CO₂.
Favoring larger tidal volumes (within limits) over fast rates to clear CO₂.

cards.title: Bohr Dead Space
cards.expl: Fraction of each breath that doesn’t exchange gas.
clinical: Rises with PE, low cardiac output, high airway pressure → widened PaCO₂–EtCO₂ gap.
Explains why more total ventilation is needed to stay normocapnic.
A sudden EtCO₂ drop with stable ventilation suggests acute dead space (PE/air embolism).

cards.title: Shunt Fraction
cards.expl: Fraction of output bypassing ventilated alveoli (Cc = end-capillary).
clinical: Hypoxemia that won’t correct on 100% O₂ — the hallmark of true shunt.
Atelectasis, pneumonia, ARDS, intracardiac shunt as causes.
PEEP and recruitment reduce shunt; guides the decision to recruit.

cards.title: Static Compliance
cards.expl: Lung/chest-wall distensibility measured at zero flow (plateau).
clinical: Low values → stiff lungs: ARDS, edema, pneumothorax, raised abdominal pressure.
Use plateau (not peak) pressure to judge alveolar distension.
Setting lung-protective limits and detecting worsening compliance intraop.

cards.title: Dynamic Compliance
cards.expl: Compliance from peak pressure, so it also reflects airway resistance.
clinical: A peak–plateau gap (low C<sub>dyn</sub>, normal C<sub>stat</sub>) → resistance: bronchospasm, secretions, kinked tube.
Both falling together → a compliance problem (edema, pneumothorax, mainstem).
Rapid bedside triage of a high-airway-pressure alarm.

cards.title: Lung Capacities
cards.expl: Capacities are sums of volumes.
clinical: FRC is the apneic O₂ reservoir — it falls with anesthesia, supine, obesity, pregnancy → faster desaturation.
Pre-oxygenation and apneic-oxygenation strategy hinge on FRC.
RV and FRC need plethysmography — spirometry can’t measure them.

cards.title: Laplace — Alveolus
cards.expl: Surface tension creates a collapsing pressure that’s greater in small alveoli.
clinical: Surfactant lowers tension more in small alveoli, preventing collapse into large ones.
Deficiency → neonatal respiratory distress syndrome and atelectasis.
Rationale for PEEP and recruitment to keep small alveoli open.

cards.title: Henderson–Hasselbalch
cards.expl: pH tracks the ratio of bicarbonate to dissolved CO₂.
clinical: The conceptual basis of every blood-gas interpretation.
Shows respiratory (PaCO₂) and metabolic (HCO₃) components set pH independently.
Predicting pH shift from a change in ventilation or bicarbonate.

cards.title: Henderson (H⁺ form)
cards.expl: Linear bedside form of the buffer equation for quick acid–base math.
clinical: Fast mental check of internal consistency of a blood gas.
Near normal, each 0.01 pH change ≈ 1 nmol/L H⁺.
Spotting a lab error when pH, PaCO₂, and HCO₃ don’t agree.

cards.title: Winter’s Formula
cards.expl: Predicts the appropriate respiratory compensation for a metabolic acidosis.
clinical: Measured PaCO₂ higher than predicted → a concurrent respiratory acidosis.
Measured PaCO₂ lower than predicted → a concurrent respiratory alkalosis.
Confirming whether a sick patient is compensating adequately (e.g., DKA, sepsis).

cards.title: Anion Gap
cards.expl: Unmeasured-anion gap; a high value signals added acid.
clinical: A high gap → MUDPILES (lactate, ketones, toxins) — drives the differential.
Correct up ~2.5 mEq/L per 1 g/dL albumin below normal, or hypoalbuminemia hides a gap.
Normal-gap (hyperchloremic) acidosis → GI or renal bicarbonate loss.

cards.title: Loading Dose
cards.expl: Set by volume of distribution and target concentration; independent of clearance.
clinical: Reaching therapeutic levels fast when waiting 4–5 half-lives is too slow.
A large Vd (lipophilic drug) demands a larger loading dose.
Why loading dose, not maintenance, is unchanged by organ failure.

cards.title: Maintenance / Infusion Rate
cards.expl: Determined by clearance and target level.
clinical: Reduce the maintenance rate in hepatic/renal failure to avoid accumulation.
Loading vs. maintenance depend on different parameters (Vd vs. clearance).
Setting a steady-state infusion (e.g., a remifentanil or pressor drip).

cards.title: Clearance
cards.expl: Volume of blood cleared of drug per unit time.
clinical: The main determinant of steady-state concentration and maintenance dosing.
Hepatic/renal disease lowers clearance and prolongs drug effect.
Explains drug accumulation in organ failure.

cards.title: Elimination Half-life
cards.expl: Time for concentration to halve; a derived (not independent) parameter.
clinical: 4–5 half-lives reach steady state or near-complete elimination.
Lengthens with a larger Vd or lower clearance.
For infusions, context-sensitive half-time is more relevant than t½.

cards.title: Volume of Distribution
cards.expl: Relates total drug in the body to plasma concentration.
clinical: Values far above body water → extensive tissue binding / lipophilicity.
Vd drives the loading dose.
Can increase in disease states (e.g., edema, ascites, burns) — alters dosing.

cards.title: Ionization (H–H)
cards.expl: Ionized fraction depends on pH relative to pKa.
clinical: Ion trapping: local anesthetic trapped in an acidotic fetus; weak bases in gastric acid.
Infected/inflamed (acidotic) tissue raises ionized fraction → poor local-anesthetic block.
Predicting placental and CNS drug transfer.

cards.title: Therapeutic Index
cards.expl: Ratio of the toxic dose to the effective dose.
clinical: Low-TI drugs (digoxin, warfarin, lithium, theophylline) require level monitoring.
Comparing the relative safety margins of agents.
Explains why narrow-TI drugs are dosed and titrated so cautiously.

cards.title: Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1)
cards.expl: Hourly maintenance fluid by weight band.
clinical: Writing pediatric maintenance fluid orders.
Estimating the hourly deficit for NPO calculation.
Baseline rate before adding deficit and third-space losses.

cards.title: Parkland (Burns)
cards.expl: 24-hour crystalloid estimate for burns; half in the first 8 h from injury.
clinical: Initiating fluid resuscitation in a major burn.
Titrate to urine output (~0.5 mL/kg/h adults) — it’s a starting estimate only.
Only 2nd- and 3rd-degree burns count toward %TBSA.

cards.title: Estimated Blood Volume
cards.expl: Blood volume from a weight factor (k by age/sex).
clinical: Computing maximum allowable blood loss and transfusion thresholds.
k: preterm 90–100, term 85–90, infant 80, child 75, adult M 70, F 65.
Planning blood availability for a high-loss case.

cards.title: Max Allowable Blood Loss
cards.expl: Blood loss tolerated before reaching a chosen transfusion trigger.
clinical: Deciding when to start transfusing intraoperatively.
Use the starting/average hematocrit and a defined target Hct.
Pre-op planning of cell-saver and crossmatch needs.

cards.title: Creatinine Clearance (C–G)
cards.expl: Estimates renal function from age, weight, serum creatinine (×0.85 for women).
clinical: Renal dose-adjustment of drugs (e.g., aminoglycosides, LMWH, gabapentin).
Overestimates clearance in obese/edematous patients (uses total body weight).
Pre-op assessment of renal reserve.

cards.title: Serum Osmolality
cards.expl: Calculated osmolality; sodium dominates.
clinical: An osmolar gap > ~10 → unmeasured osmoles (methanol, ethylene glycol, mannitol, ethanol).
Evaluating altered mental status and toxic ingestions.
Monitoring during mannitol or hypertonic-saline therapy.

cards.title: Free Water Deficit
cards.expl: Water needed to correct hypernatremia.
clinical: Calculating replacement in a hypernatremic patient.
Correct slowly (≤10–12 mEq/L per day) to avoid cerebral edema.
TBW is lower (~0.5–0.45) in women and the elderly.

cards.title: Cerebral Perfusion Pressure
cards.expl: MAP minus ICP (or CVP if CVP is higher).
clinical: TBI / raised ICP: both hypotension and intracranial hypertension cause secondary injury.
Maintaining CPP > 60 is the evidence-based target after TBI.
Level the arterial transducer at the head (tragus), not the heart, when targeting CPP.

cards.title: Monro–Kellie Doctrine
cards.expl: A rise in one intracranial compartment must be offset by a fall in another.
clinical: Once compensation is exhausted, small added volumes cause steep ICP rises.
Underlies ICP-lowering maneuvers: drain CSF, lower CBV via PaCO₂, reduce brain water.
Explains rapid decompensation in an expanding mass or hematoma.

cards.title: Nernst Equation
cards.expl: Equilibrium membrane potential for an ion from its concentration gradient.
clinical: Explains the resting (K⁺-dominated) potential.
Why hyper-/hypokalemia alter cardiac and neuronal excitability.
Conceptual basis for the ECG/arrhythmia effects of electrolyte shifts.

cards.title: Ideal Gas Law
cards.expl: Relates pressure, volume, temperature, and amount of gas.
clinical: A full O₂ E-cylinder (~2000 psi) has content proportional to pressure — calculable.
N₂O cylinders read constant pressure until the liquid is gone (weigh, don’t read).
Estimating remaining O₂ before transport.

cards.title: Graham’s Law (Diffusion)
cards.expl: Diffusion rate is inversely proportional to the square root of molecular weight.
clinical: Lighter gases diffuse faster across membranes.
Contrasts with Fick’s law (driven by gradient and solubility).
Conceptual background for gas exchange and the second gas effect.

cards.title: Fick’s Law of Diffusion
cards.expl: Gas transfer rises with area, diffusion coefficient, and gradient; falls with thickness.
clinical: Impaired diffusion in fibrosis/edema (thicker membrane) and emphysema (less area).
Why CO₂ (high solubility) crosses far faster than O₂.
Explains why oxygenation fails before CO₂ clearance in diffusion disease.

cards.title: Beer–Lambert Law
cards.expl: Absorbance is proportional to concentration and path length.
clinical: Underlies pulse oximetry (660/940 nm on pulsatile blood) and capnography.
Dyshemoglobins (COHb, MetHb) and dyes violate its assumptions → errors.
Why co-oximetry (more wavelengths) is needed to see COHb/MetHb.

cards.title: Saturated Vapor Concentration
cards.expl: Maximum vapor fraction = agent vapor pressure / ambient pressure.
clinical: Why desflurane (very high VP) needs a heated, pressurized vaporizer.
Variable-bypass vaporizers deliver a constant partial pressure across altitude.
Vapor pressure depends on agent and temperature, not ambient pressure.

cards.title: Doppler Shift
cards.expl: Frequency shift of reflected ultrasound ∝ velocity × cos of insonation angle.
clinical: Underlies esophageal-Doppler cardiac output and vascular flow measurement.
Keep the beam near-parallel; a 90° angle reads zero flow.
Precordial Doppler is the most sensitive monitor for venous air embolism.

head: Drug
Intubating
Infusion
Dur
tables.note: mg/kg; duration in min.

cards.title: TRICC (1999)
secs: Hébert's Canadian multicenter RCT randomized 838 euvolemic critically ill adults with hemoglobin below 9 g/dL within 72 hours of ICU admission to a RESTRICTIVE strategy (transfuse when Hb fell below 7, maintain 7 to 9) versus a LIBERAL strategy (transfuse below 10, maintain 10 to 12). Primary outcome was 30-day all-cause mortality.

Overall 30-day mortality did not differ (18.7 versus 23.3 percent), but among younger patients (under 55) and the less ill (APACHE II 20 or below) the restrictive strategy significantly LOWERED mortality. In-hospital mortality was lower with restrictive care, which also halved red-cell use and reduced cardiac events and pulmonary edema.
Overturned the reflexive 10/30 transfusion trigger and established a restrictive Hb 7 threshold as safe, and probably better, for most critically ill adults.

Active hemorrhage and acute coronary syndromes were excluded, so a higher trigger may still apply there. The survival benefit clustered in younger and less-sick patients, and the trial predates universal leukoreduction.

Anchor two numbers: transfuse below Hb 7 (target 7 to 9) for stable ICU patients, and use roughly 8 for active cardiac ischemia. Every later restrictive trial (TRISS, TRICS III, FOCUS) is built on TRICC.
Transfuse most ICU patients only when Hb falls below 7 g/dL.

cards.title: TRISS (2014)
secs: Scandinavian multicenter RCT of 998 patients with SEPTIC shock and hemoglobin at or below 9 g/dL randomized to a transfusion threshold of Hb 7 versus 9 g/dL, using leukoreduced blood. Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.

90-day mortality was essentially identical (43 versus 45 percent), as were ischemic events and use of life support, while the restrictive arm received about HALF as many units. Results held across severity of illness and across prespecified subgroups.
Confirmed that a restrictive Hb 7 threshold is safe specifically in septic shock, extending TRICC to a sicker population.
Applies to STABILIZED septic shock, not to active bleeding or acute myocardial infarction; do not extrapolate the 7 threshold to those settings. It was not powered for small subgroup differences.

In septic shock, hold transfusion until Hb is below 7. Combined with TRICC and TRICS III, the recurring board answer is that restrictive thresholds are non-inferior across most critically ill and surgical populations.
A Hb 7 threshold is safe in septic shock.

cards.title: TRICS III (2017)
secs: International noninferiority RCT of 5243 adults undergoing cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass at moderate-to-high risk (EuroSCORE I of 6 or more), randomized to restrictive (transfuse below Hb 7.5) versus liberal (below 9.5 in the OR or ICU, 8.5 on the ward) transfusion.

The restrictive strategy was NONINFERIOR for the composite of death, myocardial infarction, stroke, or new dialysis (11.4 versus 12.5 percent), with markedly fewer transfusions. Noninferiority persisted at 6 months, and results held even in patients 75 and older.
Restrictive transfusion is safe in cardiac surgery, refuting the old belief that bypass patients need liberal transfusion.

This was noninferiority, not superiority; a Hb 7.5 trigger is acceptable but individualize for ongoing ischemia, active bleeding, or hemodynamic instability where clinical judgment overrides the number.
Cardiac-surgery threshold to remember is Hb 7.5. TRICS III is the cardiac-surgery counterpart to TRICC and rounds out the restrictive-transfusion story across medical, surgical, and cardiac patients.
Restrictive transfusion is non-inferior in cardiac surgery.

cards.title: FOCUS (2011)
secs: RCT of 2016 patients 50 or older with cardiovascular disease or risk factors, with hemoglobin below 10 after hip-fracture surgery, randomized to a liberal threshold (Hb 10) versus a symptom-based restrictive threshold (transfuse for symptoms of anemia or Hb below 8).

There was no difference in the primary outcome of death or inability to walk across a room at 60 days (about 35 percent each), nor in in-hospital mortality or myocardial infarction, while the restrictive group received far less blood.
Even elderly patients with cardiovascular disease tolerate a Hb 8 threshold after major orthopedic surgery.

The restrictive arm was symptom-driven, so it still permitted transfusion for angina, heart failure, or hypotension despite the low numeric trigger. It does not address patients with acute coronary syndromes.
For the elderly postoperative patient, transfuse at Hb 8 or for symptoms. FOCUS extends restrictive practice to the orthopedic and cardiac-risk population most feared to need liberal transfusion.
Transfuse hip-fracture elders at Hb 8 or for symptoms.

cards.title: PROPPR (2015)
secs: Pragmatic RCT of 680 severely injured trauma patients predicted to require massive transfusion, randomized to plasma:platelets:red-cell ratios of 1:1:1 versus 1:1:2. Co-primary outcomes were 24-hour and 30-day mortality.

Neither primary endpoint was significant (24-hour 12.7 versus 17 percent; 30-day 22 versus 26 percent), but the 1:1:1 group achieved hemostasis more often and had fewer deaths from EXSANGUINATION at 24 hours, with no increase in transfusion-related complications or ARDS.
Supports a balanced 1:1:1 resuscitation as the default in major hemorrhage, favoring early plasma and platelets.
The trial was formally negative on mortality, so 1:1:1 rests on secondary hemostasis outcomes; many centers now individualize with viscoelastic (TEG/ROTEM) guidance rather than fixed ratios.
Give plasma, platelets, and red cells near 1:1:1 while activating massive transfusion, then transition to goal-directed component therapy as labs and TEG return. Add calcium and tranexamic acid.
Give plasma, platelets, and red cells near 1:1:1 in massive trauma.

cards.title: CRASH-2 (2010)
secs: Enormous international RCT of 20,211 adult trauma patients with, or at risk of, significant bleeding, randomized to tranexamic acid (1 g over 10 minutes then 1 g over 8 hours) versus placebo within 8 hours of injury.

TXA reduced all-cause mortality (14.5 versus 16 percent) and death due to bleeding, with NO increase in vascular occlusive events. A prespecified analysis showed benefit only when given within 3 hours; treatment after 3 hours increased bleeding death.
Early tranexamic acid saves lives after traumatic hemorrhage and is now standard in trauma protocols and the military.
The benefit is strictly time-critical and reverses after 3 hours, so late dosing is harmful. It was largely conducted in lower-resource settings, and the absolute effect is modest.
Memorize the dosing (1 g bolus, then 1 g over 8 hours) and the 3-hour window. CRASH-2 spawned WOMAN (PPH), CRASH-3 (TBI), and POISE-3 and ATACAS (surgery) as the TXA family.
Give tranexamic acid within 3 hours of trauma hemorrhage.

cards.title: WOMAN (2017)
secs: International RCT of 20,060 women with a clinical diagnosis of postpartum hemorrhage after vaginal or cesarean birth, randomized to tranexamic acid (1 g, repeated if bleeding continued) versus placebo.

TXA reduced death due to bleeding (1.5 versus 1.9 percent), with the greatest effect when given within 3 hours of delivery and no increase in thromboembolism or in maternal mortality overall. Laparotomy for bleeding was slightly reduced.
Tranexamic acid is a cheap, early adjunct for postpartum hemorrhage and is recommended by the WHO.
Like CRASH-2, only early administration helped, and hysterectomy decisions were often already made. Uterotonics, uterine massage, and surgical control remain the primary PPH interventions.

Give TXA as soon as PPH is diagnosed, ideally within 3 hours, alongside oxytocin and mechanical measures. The 3-hour window is the shared teaching point across all the trauma and obstetric TXA trials.
Give tranexamic acid early for postpartum hemorrhage.

cards.title: HALT-IT (2020)
secs: RCT of 12,009 adults with acute upper or lower gastrointestinal bleeding randomized to high-dose tranexamic acid (1 g then a 3 g infusion over 24 hours) versus placebo.
TXA did NOT reduce death from bleeding (about 4 percent in both arms) and modestly INCREASED venous thromboembolism and seizures. Rebleeding and need for intervention were unchanged.
Tranexamic acid should not be used routinely for gastrointestinal bleeding, a striking contrast to trauma and obstetric hemorrhage.
The higher, prolonged dosing and delayed presentation likely differ from trauma; the lesson is that antifibrinolytic benefit depends on the bleeding context, not the drug alone.
Do not give TXA for GI bleeds; prioritize endoscopic hemostasis, proton-pump inhibitors, and resuscitation. HALT-IT is the key negative counterpoint when comparing the TXA trials.
Tranexamic acid does not help gastrointestinal bleeding.

cards.title: ATACAS (2017)
secs: Factorial RCT in patients undergoing coronary artery surgery testing tranexamic acid versus placebo (and separately aspirin versus placebo), enrolling over 4600 patients.

TXA reduced major bleeding, reoperation for hemorrhage, and transfusion with NO increase in death or thrombotic events, but it modestly increased postoperative SEIZURES (0.7 versus 0.1 percent). Aspirin conferred neither benefit nor excess major bleeding.
Tranexamic acid is effective hemostatic support in cardiac surgery; perioperative aspirin can be managed by indication rather than reflexively stopped or started.
TXA seizures are dose-related and more common with renal impairment and open-chamber procedures, so use the lowest effective dose. The aspirin arm parallels POISE-2.
Use a lower TXA dose in cardiac surgery and watch for seizures, especially with renal dysfunction. Pair this with POISE-3 and CRASH-2 as the surgical antifibrinolytic evidence.
Tranexamic acid reduces cardiac-surgery bleeding, but watch for seizures.

cards.title: PROTECT (2011)
secs: RCT of 3746 medical-surgical ICU patients randomized to the low-molecular-weight heparin dalteparin versus twice-daily unfractionated heparin for venous thromboembolism prophylaxis.
The primary outcome of proximal DVT did not differ, but dalteparin significantly reduced PULMONARY EMBOLISM, with similar rates of major bleeding and heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
Low-molecular-weight heparin is an effective, possibly PE-sparing, ICU thromboprophylaxis option.
The primary endpoint (proximal DVT) was neutral, so the PE reduction is a secondary finding. Dosing must be adjusted in severe renal failure, where unfractionated heparin is often preferred.

Both agents are acceptable ICU prophylaxis; LMWH may reduce PE but accumulates in renal failure. Combine chemical prophylaxis with early mobilization and mechanical devices when bleeding risk is high.
LMWH reduced pulmonary embolism versus heparin in the ICU.

cards.title: SAFE (2004)
secs: Double-blind RCT of 6997 heterogeneous ICU patients randomized to 4 percent albumin versus 0.9 percent saline for fluid resuscitation. Primary outcome was 28-day all-cause mortality.

Mortality was equivalent overall (about 20 percent each). A prespecified subgroup showed HARM from albumin in traumatic brain injury (higher mortality, later linked to raised intracranial pressure), while a nonsignificant trend favored albumin in severe sepsis.
Albumin is safe but not superior to saline for general resuscitation and should be AVOIDED in traumatic brain injury.
Colloid theory (staying intravascular) did not translate into outcome benefit, and albumin is far costlier. The TBI harm signal was the most influential result.
Default to crystalloid; avoid albumin in TBI. Albumin retains niche roles (large-volume paracentesis, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, hepatorenal syndrome) tested in other trials, not here.
Albumin equals saline overall, but avoid it in traumatic brain injury.

cards.title: CHEST (2012)
secs: RCT of 7000 ICU patients randomized to 6 percent hydroxyethyl starch 130/0.4 versus 0.9 percent saline for all resuscitation fluid. Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.
Mortality did not differ, but the starch group required significantly MORE renal replacement therapy and had more adverse events, despite needing only slightly less fluid volume.
Hydroxyethyl starch causes kidney injury without a survival benefit and has largely been removed from ICU practice.
The renal harm applies broadly across ICU populations; regulatory agencies subsequently restricted starch use. The modest volume-sparing effect does not justify the risk.
Do not use hydroxyethyl starch for resuscitation. CHEST plus 6S form the paired evidence that synthetic colloids cause acute kidney injury, especially in sepsis.

cards.title: 6S (2012)
secs: Scandinavian RCT of 804 patients with SEVERE SEPSIS randomized to hydroxyethyl starch 130/0.42 versus Ringer acetate for resuscitation. Primary outcome was death or dialysis dependence at 90 days.
Starch INCREASED the composite of 90-day death or dialysis and increased renal replacement therapy and bleeding compared with balanced crystalloid.
Hydroxyethyl starch is harmful specifically in sepsis and is contraindicated in septic patients.
6S reinforced CHEST in the highest-risk (septic) population, where the kidneys are already threatened. Together they ended routine starch use.
Resuscitate sepsis with balanced crystalloid, never starch. Recall 6S and CHEST as the two starch trials, and SAFE for albumin, when asked about resuscitation fluids.
Do not use starch in sepsis.

cards.title: SMART (2018)
secs: Large single-center pragmatic cluster-crossover trial of 15,802 ICU patients assigned to balanced crystalloids (lactated Ringer or Plasma-Lyte) versus 0.9 percent saline. Primary outcome was major adverse kidney events at 30 days (death, new dialysis, or persistent renal dysfunction).

Balanced crystalloids modestly reduced major adverse kidney events (14.3 versus 15.4 percent), with the benefit most apparent in sepsis and in those receiving larger volumes; hyperchloremic acidosis was less frequent.
Balanced crystalloids are a reasonable, low-cost default over saline, especially in sepsis and large-volume resuscitation.
The absolute effect was small and the design was single-center cluster-crossover, not patient-level blinding. High-chloride saline appears to drive the difference.

Prefer balanced crystalloids for most resuscitation; reserve saline for specific needs (hyponatremia, hypochloremic alkalosis, or brain injury where a slightly hypotonic balanced fluid is undesirable). Pair with PLUS.

cards.title: PLUS (2022)
secs: Multicenter blinded RCT of 5037 ICU patients in Australia and New Zealand randomized to Plasma-Lyte 148 versus 0.9 percent saline. Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.
There was no difference in 90-day mortality (about 22 percent each) or in acute kidney injury and receipt of renal replacement therapy, tempering the earlier signal from SMART.
In a broad ICU population, balanced fluid and saline yield similar hard outcomes, so the choice is less decisive than sepsis-specific data suggest.
PLUS was rigorously blinded but enrolled a less septic, less sick population and gave modest fluid volumes, which may explain the neutral result compared with SMART.
Balanced fluids remain a sensible default, but do not overstate the benefit in a general ICU. When trials conflict (SMART positive, PLUS neutral), weigh the population and volume of fluid given.
Balanced fluids and saline gave similar ICU mortality (PLUS).

cards.title: RELIEF (2018)
secs: International RCT of 3000 patients at increased risk during major abdominal surgery randomized to a RESTRICTIVE versus a LIBERAL intraoperative and early postoperative fluid regimen. Primary outcome was disability-free survival at 1 year.
Disability-free survival did not differ, but the restrictive group had MORE acute kidney injury (8.6 versus 5 percent) and more surgical-site infection, without any reduction in complications.
A truly dry strategy is harmful; aim for a moderate, near zero-balance approach rather than deliberate hypovolemia.

RELIEF redefined restrictive as potentially dangerous, contrasting with the ICU sepsis literature; the optimal perioperative target is modest positive balance guided by perfusion, not maximal restriction.

Target a near-neutral fluid balance in major surgery, avoiding both overload and the kidney injury caused by excessive restriction. Combine with goal-directed monitoring (OPTIMISE) in high-risk cases.
Overly restrictive perioperative fluids cause kidney injury.

cards.title: CLASSIC (2022)
secs: Multicenter RCT of 1554 adults with SEPTIC shock who had already received initial resuscitation, randomized to restrictive IV fluid (fluid only for severe hypoperfusion) versus standard fluid therapy. Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.
There was no difference in 90-day mortality (about 42 versus 42 percent), serious adverse events, or ischemic events, though the restrictive arm received substantially less fluid.
Restricting fluid after the initial resuscitation phase is safe in septic shock, supporting a de-escalation mindset.
It applies only AFTER early resuscitation; fluids should not be withheld during the initial phase. The trial could not exclude modest differences and had some protocol crossover.
Give early fluids to resuscitate, then de-escalate and avoid ongoing liberal fluids once perfusion is restored. Pair CLASSIC with the CLOVERS trial for the fluids-versus-vasopressors debate.
Restrictive fluids are safe once septic shock is resuscitated.

cards.title: ARMA / ARDSNet (2000)
secs: Landmark NIH ARDS Network RCT of 861 patients with ARDS randomized to LOW tidal volume (6 mL/kg predicted body weight, plateau pressure 30 or less) versus TRADITIONAL tidal volume (12 mL/kg, plateau 50 or less). Stopped early for benefit.

Low tidal volume ventilation reduced mortality (31 versus 40 percent) and increased ventilator-free days, despite higher PaCO2 and lower pH (permissive hypercapnia). Plasma inflammatory markers also fell.
Lung-protective ventilation with 6 mL/kg predicted body weight is the foundation of modern ARDS management.
Tidal volume is set by PREDICTED (not actual) body weight, driven by height and sex. Permissive hypercapnia is accepted, and the trial used a specific PEEP/FiO2 table.
Set tidal volume 6 mL/kg predicted body weight, keep plateau under 30, and tolerate hypercapnia. This is the single most tested critical-care ventilation trial; know the numbers cold.
Use 6 mL/kg predicted body weight in ARDS.

cards.title: PROSEVA (2013)
secs: French/Spanish RCT of 466 patients with SEVERE ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 below 150 on FiO2 0.6 and PEEP 5 or more) randomized to early PRONE positioning at least 16 hours daily versus supine, at experienced centers. Primary outcome was 28-day mortality.
Prone positioning nearly HALVED 28-day mortality (16 versus 33 percent) and 90-day mortality, with no increase in serious complications at these expert centers.
Early, prolonged prone positioning saves lives in severe ARDS and is a core intervention alongside low tidal volume.
Benefit required early application, long sessions, and expertise; risks include endotracheal tube and line dislodgement, pressure injury, and facial edema. It applies to severe, not mild, ARDS.
Prone patients with P/F under 150 for at least 16 hours per day, early. Proning was also central to COVID-19 ARDS management, extending PROSEVA's reach.
Prone early for at least 16 hours daily in severe ARDS.

cards.title: ACURASYS (2010)
secs: French RCT of 340 patients with early severe ARDS (P/F below 150) randomized to a 48-hour infusion of the neuromuscular blocker cisatracurium versus placebo, both with deep sedation.
Cisatracurium improved the adjusted 90-day survival and increased ventilator-free days and days without organ failure, with LESS barotrauma and no increase in ICU-acquired weakness.
Early, brief neuromuscular blockade appeared to benefit severe ARDS, presumably by improving patient-ventilator synchrony and reducing injurious effort.
The survival benefit was in an adjusted analysis, and the later ROSE trial (with lighter sedation) found no benefit, so routine paralysis is no longer standard.
If used, reserve neuromuscular blockade for early, severe ARDS with refractory dyssynchrony, for about 48 hours. Contrast ACURASYS (positive) with ROSE (negative) and explain the sedation difference.

cards.title: ROSE (2019)
secs: US PETAL Network RCT of 1006 patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS (P/F below 150) randomized to early continuous cisatracurium with DEEP sedation versus a usual-care strategy with LIGHTER sedation and no routine paralysis.
There was no difference in 90-day mortality (about 43 percent each) or ventilator-free days; the paralysis group had more cardiovascular events and, at follow-up, no functional advantage.
Routine early neuromuscular blockade is not needed in ARDS when a lighter-sedation strategy is used.
The control group differed importantly from ACURASYS by using light sedation rather than deep sedation, which likely explains the divergent results; higher PEEP was also used.
Do not routinely paralyze ARDS patients; reserve neuromuscular blockade for refractory dyssynchrony or severe hypoxemia. The ACURASYS-versus-ROSE contrast is a favorite exam discussion point.
Routine neuromuscular blockade gave no benefit in ARDS (ROSE).

cards.title: FACTT (2006)
secs: ARDS Network RCT of 1000 patients with acute lung injury/ARDS randomized to a CONSERVATIVE versus a LIBERAL fluid-management protocol over 7 days, using explicit CVP/PAOP-guided algorithms (also compared PA catheter versus central line).

The conservative strategy did not change 60-day mortality but significantly increased VENTILATOR-FREE and ICU-free days and improved oxygenation and lung injury scores, without increasing shock or dialysis. The PA catheter added no benefit and more complications.
Keep ARDS patients as dry as perfusion allows to shorten mechanical ventilation, and do not routinely use PA catheters.
Conservative fluids helped only once patients were out of shock; the strategy is not for the actively hypotensive. Benefit was on lung and ICU endpoints, not survival.
After resuscitation, run ARDS patients net-even to net-negative to liberate them from the ventilator sooner. FACTT also buried the routine PA catheter alongside other negative catheter trials.
Conservative fluids improve lung outcomes in ARDS.

cards.title: OSCILLATE / OSCAR (2013)
secs: Two simultaneous RCTs testing HIGH-FREQUENCY OSCILLATORY VENTILATION against conventional lung-protective ventilation in moderate-to-severe ARDS: OSCILLATE (Canada, 548 patients, protocolized comparator) and OSCAR (UK, 795 patients).
OSCILLATE was STOPPED EARLY for HARM, with higher in-hospital mortality (47 versus 35 percent) and more vasopressor and sedative use; OSCAR showed no benefit and no harm.
High-frequency oscillation should not be used routinely in ARDS and may be harmful.

A classic example of sound physiologic rationale (small tidal volumes, open lung) failing in rigorous trials, possibly due to high mean airway pressures impairing hemodynamics. HFOV is now at most a rescue therapy.
Do not select HFOV as a primary ARDS mode; stick with conventional low-tidal-volume ventilation and consider prone or ECMO for refractory cases. Recall OSCILLATE as the harm trial.
High-frequency oscillation is harmful or useless in ARDS.

cards.title: EOLIA (2018)
secs: International RCT of 249 patients with VERY SEVERE ARDS randomized to early veno-venous ECMO versus conventional lung-protective ventilation with ECMO permitted as rescue. Primary outcome was 60-day mortality.

Mortality was lower with early ECMO (35 versus 46 percent) but did NOT reach statistical significance, and 28 percent of the control group crossed over to rescue ECMO for refractory hypoxemia (most of whom died).
ECMO is a reasonable rescue for refractory hypoxemia; early referral to an ECMO center is prudent even if universal early cannulation is not mandated.
The trial was stopped early and underpowered with heavy crossover; a subsequent Bayesian reanalysis found a high probability of benefit, so many interpret EOLIA as supportive rather than negative.
For P/F persistently very low despite optimization (proning, paralysis), refer early for veno-venous ECMO. Know that EOLIA was statistically negative but clinically influential.
ECMO is a rescue option in refractory ARDS.

cards.title: ART (2017)
secs: Brazilian-led international RCT of 1010 patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS randomized to an aggressive lung-RECRUITMENT maneuver with decremental PEEP titration versus a low-PEEP strategy.
The recruitment strategy INCREASED 28-day mortality (55 versus 49 percent) and 6-month mortality, with more barotrauma and pneumothorax and more hemodynamic instability during maneuvers.
Aggressive recruitment maneuvers with high PEEP are harmful in ARDS and should be avoided.
The maneuvers used very high airway pressures (up to 60 cm H2O), which likely caused the harm; gentler, individualized PEEP is preferred. It tempered enthusiasm for the open-lung approach.
Titrate PEEP modestly and individually; do not perform high-pressure staircase recruitment. Pair ART with OSCILLATE as trials where high mean airway pressure strategies backfired.
Do not perform aggressive lung recruitment in ARDS.

cards.title: PROVHILO (2014)
secs: International RCT of 900 patients at risk during open abdominal surgery randomized to HIGH PEEP (12 cm H2O) with recruitment maneuvers versus LOW PEEP (0 to 2) with no recruitment, all with 8 mL/kg tidal volume. Primary outcome was postoperative pulmonary complications.

High PEEP did NOT reduce pulmonary complications (about 40 percent in both arms) and caused more intraoperative HYPOTENSION and vasopressor use, with no oxygenation benefit that persisted postoperatively.
Routine high intraoperative PEEP with recruitment is not beneficial and worsens hemodynamics.

The trial isolated PEEP level (both arms used the same modest tidal volume), so it argues against a fixed high PEEP rather than against protective ventilation overall. Titration to the individual may still help.
Do not apply blanket high PEEP intraoperatively; titrate PEEP to the patient and expect hypotension at higher levels. Contrast with Futier, where a full protective bundle did help.
Routine high intraoperative PEEP does not help.

cards.title: IMPROVE / Futier (2013)
secs: French RCT of 400 patients at intermediate-to-high risk during major abdominal surgery randomized to a LUNG-PROTECTIVE bundle (6 to 8 mL/kg tidal volume, PEEP 6 to 8, and recruitment maneuvers) versus NON-PROTECTIVE ventilation (10 to 12 mL/kg, no PEEP or recruitment).
The protective bundle reduced major pulmonary and extrapulmonary complications by day 7 (10.5 versus 27.5 percent), reduced need for noninvasive or invasive ventilation, and shortened hospital stay.
Intraoperative lung-protective ventilation reduces postoperative complications in major surgery.

Because the intervention BUNDLED low tidal volume, PEEP, and recruitment, the specific active ingredient is unclear; PROVHILO later suggested high PEEP alone is not the driver, pointing to tidal volume.

Use protective settings for major surgery: about 6 to 8 mL/kg predicted body weight with modest PEEP. Reconcile Futier (bundle helps) with PROVHILO (PEEP alone does not) by emphasizing low tidal volume.

cards.title: PReVENT (2018)
secs: Dutch RCT of 961 ICU patients WITHOUT ARDS expected to need ventilation beyond 24 hours, randomized to LOW tidal volume (starting 4 to 6 mL/kg) versus INTERMEDIATE tidal volume (starting around 10 mL/kg). Primary outcome was ventilator-free days.
There was no difference in ventilator-free days or in mortality, ICU stay, ARDS development, or other outcomes between low and intermediate tidal volumes.
In patients without ARDS, low and intermediate tidal volumes are equivalent, though frankly high volumes should still be avoided.
This addresses the non-ARDS ventilated population specifically; it does not license large tidal volumes, and plateau/driving-pressure limits still apply. Many kept plateau pressures low in both arms.
You need not push to very low tidal volumes in non-ARDS patients, but keep them out of the injurious high range and watch driving pressure. Contrast with ARDSNet, which mandates 6 mL/kg in ARDS.
Low and intermediate tidal volumes are equal without ARDS.

cards.title: PROXI (2009)
secs: Danish RCT of 1400 patients undergoing laparotomy randomized to 80 percent versus 30 percent inspired oxygen intraoperatively and for 2 hours postoperatively. Primary outcome was surgical-site infection.
High FiO2 did NOT reduce surgical-site infection (about 19 to 20 percent in both), and long-term follow-up raised concerns about worse survival, particularly in cancer patients.
Routine perioperative hyperoxia does not prevent infection and may carry long-term harm.
The result conflicts with some earlier smaller trials and WHO guidance, and the long-term mortality signal is debated. Hyperoxia also promotes atelectasis.
Do not use high FiO2 solely to prevent surgical infection; target normoxia and reserve high oxygen for genuine hypoxemia or preoxygenation. This complicates the WHO 80 percent recommendation.
High intraoperative oxygen does not cut infection (PROXI).

cards.title: Rivers EGDT (2001)
secs: Single-center RCT of 263 emergency-department patients with severe sepsis or septic shock randomized to 6 hours of EARLY GOAL-DIRECTED THERAPY (protocolized fluids, vasopressors, transfusion, and inotropes targeting CVP 8 to 12, MAP 65 or more, and central venous O2 saturation 70 percent or more) versus standard care.
In-hospital mortality fell sharply (30.5 versus 46.5 percent), with less organ dysfunction, driven by more fluid, dobutamine, and transfusion in the first 6 hours.
Launched the modern era of early, aggressive, protocolized sepsis resuscitation and the Surviving Sepsis Campaign bundles.
It was single-center and later NOT reproduced by ProCESS, ARISE, and ProMISe; specific targets (ScvO2, mandatory transfusion, dobutamine) fell away, but early recognition endured.
The enduring lessons are early identification, prompt antibiotics, and early fluids/vasopressors, not the exact ScvO2 catheter protocol. Rivers sets up the three confirmatory trials that followed.

cards.title: ProCESS / ARISE / ProMISe (2014-15)
secs: Three large multicenter RCTs (US ProCESS 1341, Australasian ARISE 1600, UK ProMISe 1260) re-tested protocolized early goal-directed therapy versus usual care in early septic shock, using central venous oxygen monitoring in the EGDT arms.

None showed a mortality benefit for strict EGDT over usual care; outcomes were similar and generally better than in the Rivers era, and mandatory ScvO2 catheters and transfusion targets added cost without benefit.
Rigid EGDT protocols and routine central-venous-oxygen catheters are no longer required; usual care with early antibiotics and fluids suffices.
Usual care had improved substantially since 2001 (earlier antibiotics, more fluids), which likely explains the null result rather than EGDT being harmful.
Give early antibiotics and fluids and titrate vasopressors clinically; skip mandatory ScvO2 catheters. These three trials are the standard counterpoint to Rivers on exams.
Strict EGDT protocols are no longer required.

cards.title: SOAP II (2010)
secs: RCT of 1679 patients with shock of any type randomized to DOPAMINE versus NOREPINEPHRINE as the first-line vasopressor. Primary outcome was 28-day mortality.
Overall mortality did not differ, but dopamine caused significantly MORE arrhythmias (24 versus 12 percent), and a subgroup analysis showed HIGHER mortality with dopamine in CARDIOGENIC shock.
Norepinephrine is the preferred first-line vasopressor; dopamine causes more arrhythmias and is harmful in cardiogenic shock.
The overall primary endpoint was neutral; the practice change rests on the arrhythmia burden and the cardiogenic-shock subgroup. Dopamine retains few niche roles.
Reach for norepinephrine first in septic, cardiogenic, and hypovolemic shock. Recall the arrhythmia difference and the cardiogenic-shock harm as the reasons dopamine fell out of favor.
Choose norepinephrine over dopamine in shock.

cards.title: VASST (2008)
secs: RCT of 778 patients with septic shock already on norepinephrine randomized to add low-dose VASOPRESSIN (0.01 to 0.03 units/min) versus additional norepinephrine. Primary outcome was 28-day mortality.
There was no overall mortality difference, but a prespecified subgroup with LESS SEVERE shock (lower baseline norepinephrine) had lower mortality with vasopressin; catecholamine requirements fell.
Low-dose vasopressin is a reasonable catecholamine-sparing adjunct in septic shock, especially at lower norepinephrine doses.
The primary endpoint was negative, and the benefit was a subgroup finding; the later VANISH trial found no renal or mortality benefit from early vasopressin. Doses above 0.04 units/min risk ischemia.
Add fixed low-dose vasopressin (0.03 units/min) to spare catecholamines; do not titrate it up. Pair VASST with VANISH when discussing vasopressin evidence.
Add vasopressin to spare catecholamines in septic shock.

cards.title: SEPSISPAM (2014)
secs: French RCT of 776 patients with septic shock randomized to a mean arterial pressure target of 65 to 70 versus 80 to 85 mmHg. Primary outcome was 28-day mortality.
Mortality did not differ between targets. The higher target caused more atrial FIBRILLATION but, in patients with CHRONIC HYPERTENSION, reduced the need for renal replacement therapy.
A MAP target of 65 suffices for most patients with septic shock; individualize upward for chronic hypertension to protect the kidneys.
The higher target increases arrhythmia and vasopressor exposure, so it is not free; the renal benefit was confined to a chronically hypertensive subgroup.
Default to MAP 65, but consider 75 to 85 in long-standing hypertension. Combine with the 65 trial (permissive hypotension in the elderly) for the MAP-target discussion.
Target MAP 65 in most septic shock.

cards.title: 65 trial (2020)
secs: UK RCT of 2600 patients aged 65 or older with vasodilatory shock randomized to PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION (MAP target 60 to 65) versus usual care. Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.
There was no significant mortality difference (adjusted analysis hinted at slightly lower mortality with permissive hypotension) and the intervention reduced vasopressor exposure and duration.
A lower MAP target is safe and vasopressor-sparing in older patients with vasodilatory shock.
Applies to older adults; caution with known severe cerebrovascular or renovascular disease. The mortality benefit was borderline and seen only after adjustment.
In elderly vasodilatory shock, accept MAP 60 to 65 to limit vasopressors. Together with SEPSISPAM, the theme is that MAP 65 is adequate and higher targets add harm without clear benefit.
Permissive hypotension is safe in older shock patients.

cards.title: ANDROMEDA-SHOCK (2019)
secs: Latin American RCT of 424 patients with septic shock randomized to resuscitation guided by CAPILLARY REFILL TIME (targeting normalization every 30 minutes) versus SERUM LACTATE clearance. Primary outcome was 28-day mortality.

Mortality was not significantly lower with capillary-refill guidance (34.9 versus 43.4 percent, p 0.06), but the refill group had LESS organ dysfunction at 72 hours and received less fluid; a Bayesian reanalysis favored capillary refill.
Capillary refill time is a valid, simple, no-cost resuscitation target that performs at least as well as lactate.
The primary endpoint was statistically negative, and both are surrogate targets. Lactate can stay elevated for non-perfusion reasons (adrenergic drive, clearance), which may mislead resuscitation.
Use capillary refill (normal under about 3 seconds) as a bedside perfusion target; avoid chasing lactate with endless fluids. The Bayesian reanalysis is why many favor the refill strategy.

cards.title: CORTICUS (2008)
