secs: European RCT of 499 patients with septic shock (broad severity, not just refractory) randomized to hydrocortisone 50 mg every 6 hours versus placebo, incorporating ACTH-stimulation testing. Primary outcome was 28-day mortality in non-responders.
Steroids did NOT improve survival regardless of ACTH response, but they SPED reversal of shock; superinfection and new sepsis episodes were more common with hydrocortisone.
Hydrocortisone hastens shock reversal without a clear survival benefit, and ACTH testing does not guide therapy.
The population was less severely ill than the earlier Annane trial, likely diluting any mortality effect; it established that the cosyntropin test is not useful for selecting steroid responders.

Do not order ACTH-stimulation testing to guide steroids; consider hydrocortisone for pressor-dependent shock expecting faster reversal, not lower mortality. Sits between Annane, ADRENAL, and APROCCHSS.
Hydrocortisone reversed shock faster, no survival gain (CORTICUS).

cards.title: ADRENAL (2018)
secs: Large ANZICS RCT of 3800 patients with septic shock on mechanical ventilation randomized to a CONTINUOUS hydrocortisone infusion (200 mg/day) versus placebo. Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.
There was no 90-day mortality difference (27.9 versus 28.8 percent), but hydrocortisone produced FASTER shock resolution, faster weaning from ventilation, shorter ICU stay, and fewer transfusions.
Hydrocortisone accelerates recovery from septic shock without changing mortality; it is reasonable for pressor-dependent patients.
The large, definitive trial contrasts with APROCCHSS, which found a mortality benefit, likely because APROCCHSS enrolled a sicker population and added fludrocortisone.
Use hydrocortisone to speed shock reversal and liberation from support; do not expect a mortality benefit from ADRENAL alone. Contrast infusion (ADRENAL) with bolus plus fludrocortisone (APROCCHSS).

cards.title: APROCCHSS (2018)
secs: French RCT of 1241 patients with SEVERE septic shock (high vasopressor requirement) randomized to hydrocortisone 50 mg every 6 hours PLUS oral fludrocortisone versus placebo. Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.
The steroid combination REDUCED 90-day mortality (43 versus 49 percent) and increased vasopressor-free and organ-failure-free days, without excess serious adverse events aside from hyperglycemia.
Combined hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone may lower mortality in severe, pressor-dependent septic shock.

The positive result likely reflects a SICKER population than ADRENAL plus the added mineralocorticoid; whether fludrocortisone is essential remains debated (the COIITSS trial suggested it adds little).

In severe septic shock, hydrocortisone with fludrocortisone is a defensible mortality-directed choice. Explain the ADRENAL-versus-APROCCHSS discrepancy by population severity and the added fludrocortisone.

cards.title: VANISH (2016)
secs: Factorial RCT of 409 patients with septic shock randomized to EARLY vasopressin versus norepinephrine as first vasopressor, and separately to hydrocortisone versus placebo. Primary outcome was kidney-failure-free days.
There was no difference in kidney-failure-free days; early vasopressin did not reduce renal replacement therapy or mortality, though it reduced norepinephrine requirements.
Early vasopressin does not protect the kidneys or improve survival in septic shock.
Timing and role differ from VASST (where vasopressin was added to norepinephrine later); as a first-line agent early, vasopressin showed no renal benefit. The factorial steroid arm was also neutral.
Vasopressin remains a catecholamine-sparing ADJUNCT, not a first-line or renal-protective agent. Contrast VANISH (early, first-line, negative) with VASST (add-on, subgroup benefit).
Early vasopressin did not protect kidneys (VANISH).

cards.title: PROWESS-SHOCK (2012)
secs: Confirmatory RCT of 1697 patients with septic shock randomized to recombinant human activated protein C (drotrecogin alfa) versus placebo, mandated after the original PROWESS trial. Primary outcome was 28-day mortality.
Drotrecogin showed NO mortality benefit (26.4 versus 24.2 percent) and carried bleeding risk, prompting worldwide market WITHDRAWAL of the drug.
Activated protein C was abandoned for sepsis, a cautionary tale about premature adoption on a single positive trial.
The original PROWESS was positive, leading to approval; the mandated confirmatory trial reversed it. It illustrates regulatory reliance on confirmatory evidence.
Know drotrecogin as the sepsis drug that was approved then withdrawn after PROWESS-SHOCK. There is no role for activated protein C in sepsis.

cards.title: CITRIS-ALI / VITAMINS (2019-20)
secs: Two RCTs of high-dose vitamin C in critical illness: CITRIS-ALI (167 patients with sepsis and ARDS, IV vitamin C versus placebo) and VITAMINS (216 patients with septic shock, the vitamin C/thiamine/hydrocortisone metabolic cocktail versus hydrocortisone alone).
Neither improved the primary outcomes (organ dysfunction scores or time alive and vasopressor-free); secondary mortality signals in CITRIS-ALI were exploratory and not confirmed by later trials.
The vitamin C metabolic cocktail does not improve organ failure or survival in sepsis.
These followed a small enthusiastic before-after study; multiple subsequent RCTs (including LOVIT) reinforced no benefit and possible harm from high-dose vitamin C.
Do not use high-dose vitamin C or the metabolic cocktail for sepsis. This is a modern example of a promising observational signal failing rigorous trials.
Vitamin C does not improve sepsis outcomes.

cards.title: RECOVERY dexamethasone (2020)
secs: Pragmatic UK platform RCT randomizing 6425 hospitalized COVID-19 patients to dexamethasone 6 mg daily for up to 10 days versus usual care. Primary outcome was 28-day mortality.

Dexamethasone reduced mortality in patients requiring OXYGEN (23 versus 26 percent) or MECHANICAL VENTILATION (29 versus 41 percent), but showed NO benefit, and a trend toward harm, in those NOT needing oxygen.
Corticosteroids reduce mortality in hypoxemic COVID-19 respiratory failure, but only when oxygen is required.
The benefit depends entirely on disease severity/hypoxemia, a clean illustration of treatment-effect heterogeneity and the value of large simple platform trials.
Give dexamethasone only to hypoxemic COVID-19 patients; withhold it in mild disease. RECOVERY is the model modern platform trial and the anchor for steroid use in viral pneumonia.
Dexamethasone cut mortality in hypoxemic COVID-19.

cards.title: Van den Berghe (2001)
secs: Single-center Leuven RCT of 1548 SURGICAL ICU patients (mostly cardiac surgery) randomized to INTENSIVE insulin therapy (glucose 80 to 110 mg/dL) versus conventional control (treat above 215). Primary outcome was ICU mortality.
Intensive control reduced ICU mortality (4.6 versus 8 percent), bloodstream infections, acute kidney injury, and critical-illness polyneuropathy in this surgical population.
Generated enormous enthusiasm for tight glucose control in the ICU, later overturned by multicenter data.
Single-center, surgical, and heavily parenteral-fed; a medical-ICU follow-up was less convincing, and severe HYPOGLYCEMIA was common, foreshadowing NICE-SUGAR.
Recognize this as the trial that launched tight glucose control and the hypoglycemia concern that NICE-SUGAR later proved harmful. Do not target 80 to 110 in general ICU patients.
Tight glucose helped in one surgical trial, later refuted.

cards.title: NICE-SUGAR (2009)
secs: Large international RCT of 6104 ICU patients randomized to INTENSIVE glucose control (target 81 to 108 mg/dL) versus CONVENTIONAL control (target 180 or below). Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.
Intensive control INCREASED 90-day mortality (27.5 versus 24.9 percent) and caused far more severe HYPOGLYCEMIA (6.8 versus 0.5 percent); cardiovascular deaths drove the excess.
Avoid tight glucose control in the ICU; target a glucose below 180 and prevent hypoglycemia.
The mortality excess is attributed largely to hypoglycemia. It definitively overturned the single-center Van den Berghe result across a broad, multicenter population.
Target roughly 140 to 180 mg/dL in critically ill patients; the enemy is hypoglycemia. NICE-SUGAR versus Van den Berghe is a classic single-center-versus-multicenter teaching pair.
Target glucose under 180, not tight control.

cards.title: MENDS / SEDCOM (2007-09)
secs: Two RCTs comparing DEXMEDETOMIDINE with benzodiazepine sedation in ventilated ICU patients: MENDS (dexmedetomidine versus lorazepam, 106 patients) and SEDCOM (dexmedetomidine versus midazolam, 375 patients).

Dexmedetomidine increased DELIRIUM-FREE and coma-free days and, in SEDCOM, reduced the prevalence of delirium and shortened time to extubation, with more bradycardia but less tachycardia and hypertension.
Non-benzodiazepine sedation (dexmedetomidine) reduces delirium and ventilator time compared with benzodiazepines.
These support the PADIS guideline preference for propofol or dexmedetomidine over benzodiazepines, though the later SPICE III trial found dexmedetomidine is not a mortality intervention.
Prefer dexmedetomidine or propofol and minimize benzodiazepines to lower delirium; reserve benzodiazepines for alcohol withdrawal, seizures, and deep sedation needs. Watch for bradycardia.

cards.title: SPICE III (2019)
secs: International RCT of 4000 ventilated ICU patients randomized to EARLY dexmedetomidine as the primary sedative versus usual-care sedation (propofol or benzodiazepines). Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.

There was no mortality difference (about 29 percent each); many dexmedetomidine patients needed supplemental sedation, and the drug caused more bradycardia and hypotension. Subgroups suggested possible benefit in older patients and harm in the young.
Dexmedetomidine is a useful sedative but not a mortality-changing primary agent, and it often requires supplementation.
It tempers enthusiasm from MENDS/SEDCOM by showing no survival benefit and real hemodynamic side effects when used as the sole early sedative.
Use dexmedetomidine to reduce delirium and opioid/benzodiazepine load, not to change mortality; anticipate the need for additional agents and monitor for bradycardia.
Early dexmedetomidine gave no mortality benefit (SPICE III).

cards.title: ABC trial (2008)
secs: RCT of 336 ventilated ICU patients randomized to a paired protocol of daily spontaneous AWAKENING trials (sedation interruption) linked to spontaneous BREATHING trials versus spontaneous breathing trials alone with usual sedation.

The wake-and-breathe group had more ventilator-free days, shorter ICU and hospital stays, and lower 1-YEAR MORTALITY (44 versus 58 percent), despite more self-extubations that rarely required reintubation.
Coordinating daily sedation interruption with breathing trials improves survival and liberation from the ventilator.
Requires safety screens before each trial; the strategy underpins the ABCDEF ICU liberation bundle. Benefit must be balanced against the small self-extubation risk.
Pair a daily sedation holiday with a spontaneous breathing trial as a bundle. ABC is the foundation of the ICU liberation (ABCDEF) approach.
Paired awakening and breathing trials improve survival.

cards.title: MIND-USA (2018)
secs: RCT of 566 ventilated ICU patients with delirium randomized to HALOPERIDOL, ZIPRASIDONE, or placebo. Primary outcome was days alive without delirium or coma.
Antipsychotics did NOT shorten delirium duration, reduce coma, shorten ventilation, or improve survival compared with placebo.
Antipsychotics do not treat or prevent ICU delirium and should not be used routinely for it.
The trial addresses pharmacologic treatment of established delirium; nonpharmacologic prevention (the ABCDEF bundle, sleep, mobility, sensory aids) remains the evidence-based approach.

Manage delirium with nonpharmacologic bundles; reserve low-dose antipsychotics only for dangerous agitation or distressing hallucinations, not as routine therapy. MIND-USA is the key negative delirium-drug trial.
Antipsychotics do not fix ICU delirium.

cards.title: SUP-ICU (2018)
secs: Scandinavian RCT of 3298 ICU patients at risk for GI bleeding randomized to the proton-pump inhibitor pantoprazole versus placebo for stress-ulcer prophylaxis. Primary outcome was 90-day mortality.

There was no mortality difference; pantoprazole reduced clinically important GI BLEEDING (2.5 versus 4.2 percent) but with a nonsignificant signal toward more infections and a possible mortality increase in the sickest.
Stress-ulcer prophylaxis reduces significant GI bleeding but does not improve survival, so it should be targeted.
The benefit is modest and confined to bleeding; concerns about pneumonia and C. difficile argue against blanket use in low-risk patients. Enteral feeding itself is protective.
Reserve PPI prophylaxis for genuinely high-risk patients (coagulopathy, shock, high-dose steroids, prolonged ventilation) and stop it when risk resolves. Pair with PEPTIC (PPI versus H2 blocker).
PPI prophylaxis cuts GI bleeds, not deaths.

cards.title: ICU-ROX / LOCO2 / HOT-ICU (2020-21)
secs: Three RCTs of oxygen targets in critically ill adults: ICU-ROX (1000 ventilated patients, conservative versus usual O2), LOCO2 (201 ARDS patients, PaO2 55 to 70 versus 90 to 105), and HOT-ICU (2928 hypoxemic patients, PaO2 target 60 versus 90 mmHg).

Most outcomes were NEUTRAL for mortality and ventilator-free days; LOCO2 was stopped early for a HARM signal (more mesenteric ischemia and higher mortality) at the lowest targets, while hyperoxia offered no benefit.
Avoid both hyperoxia and dangerously low oxygen targets; a moderate SpO2 is the pragmatic goal.
The trials used different targets and populations, producing mixed results; the clear messages are that hyperoxia is not helpful and extreme conservative targets can harm.
Aim for SpO2 roughly 92 to 96 percent (or PaO2 near 60 to 90); do not chase supranormal oxygen and do not drive PaO2 dangerously low. LOCO2 is the harm-at-low-target cautionary trial.
Avoid excess oxygen, but do not target very low SpO2.

cards.title: FLORALI (2015)
secs: French RCT of 310 patients with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (mostly pneumonia, not hypercapnic, not cardiogenic) randomized to HIGH-FLOW NASAL CANNULA, standard oxygen mask, or noninvasive ventilation. Primary outcome was intubation rate.

Intubation rates did not differ overall, but high-flow nasal cannula REDUCED 90-day mortality and, in the severe subgroup (P/F 200 or below), also reduced intubation; NIV was associated with possible harm from large tidal volumes.
High-flow nasal cannula is first-line oxygen therapy for de novo hypoxemic respiratory failure.
The mortality benefit was a SECONDARY outcome, and NIV may injure the lung via high tidal volumes in this setting (distinct from COPD or cardiogenic edema where NIV excels).
Choose high-flow nasal cannula over NIV for de novo hypoxemic failure; reserve NIV for COPD exacerbation and cardiogenic pulmonary edema. Monitor the ROX index to predict HFNC success.

cards.title: PARAMEDIC-2 (2018)
secs: UK placebo-controlled RCT of 8014 out-of-hospital cardiac arrests randomized to ADRENALINE (epinephrine 1 mg every 3 to 5 minutes) versus placebo. Primary outcome was 30-day survival.

Epinephrine increased 30-day survival (3.2 versus 2.4 percent) and return of spontaneous circulation, but survivors more often had SEVERE NEUROLOGIC impairment, so survival with good neurologic outcome was similar.
Epinephrine improves overall survival after arrest but at the cost of more neurologically impaired survivors.
The neurologic trade-off fuels ongoing debate about dose and timing; epinephrine remains guideline-recommended, but the benefit is modest and partly offset by brain injury.
Continue epinephrine every 3 to 5 minutes in arrest per ACLS, but understand the survival-versus-neurology trade-off. Emphasis remains on high-quality CPR and early defibrillation.
Epinephrine raises survival but worsens neuro outcome.

cards.title: HACA (2002)
secs: European RCT of 275 comatose survivors of out-of-hospital VF/VT arrest randomized to therapeutic HYPOTHERMIA (32 to 34 degrees for 24 hours) versus standard normothermia. Primary outcome was favorable neurologic recovery.
Hypothermia improved favorable neurologic outcome (55 versus 39 percent) and 6-month survival, with no significant excess of complications.
Cooling comatose survivors of shockable arrest improves neurologic recovery, establishing targeted temperature management.
Along with the concurrent Bernard trial, it launched post-arrest cooling; later TTM and TTM2 refined the target away from deep hypothermia toward fever avoidance.
Provide targeted temperature management to comatose post-arrest patients; the modern emphasis (from TTM/TTM2) is strict fever control rather than deep cooling. HACA started the field.
Hypothermia improved neuro outcome after VF arrest (HACA).

cards.title: TTM (2013)
secs: International RCT of 950 comatose out-of-hospital arrest survivors (any rhythm) randomized to targeted temperature 33 versus 36 degrees, both with strict protocolized temperature control and fever avoidance. Primary outcome was mortality.
There was NO difference in mortality or neurologic outcome between 33 and 36 degrees.
Deep cooling to 33 is not superior to 36; controlled normothermia with fever prevention is what matters.
Crucially, BOTH arms were actively temperature-controlled, so TTM is not a trial of cooling versus no control; it argues against deep hypothermia, not against temperature management.

Target a controlled temperature (33 to 36) and rigorously prevent fever after arrest. TTM shifted practice from deep cooling toward fever avoidance; do not misread it as abandoning temperature control.

cards.title: TTM2 (2021)
secs: Large international RCT of 1900 comatose post-arrest patients randomized to hypothermia at 33 degrees versus targeted NORMOTHERMIA (aggressively treat fever above 37.8). Primary outcome was 6-month mortality.
There was no difference in mortality (50 versus 48 percent) or neurologic outcome, and the hypothermia group had more ARRHYTHMIAS causing hemodynamic compromise.
Targeted normothermia with fever control is sufficient after arrest; routine cooling to 33 adds risk without benefit.
TTM2 pushed guidelines toward normothermia with fever prevention rather than mandatory hypothermia; some argue certain subgroups may still benefit from cooling.
Prevent fever (keep below about 37.5) after arrest; deep cooling is not required and adds arrhythmia. TTM2 is the most recent, practice-defining temperature trial.

cards.title: POISE (2008)
secs: International RCT of 8351 patients with, or at risk of, atherosclerotic disease undergoing noncardiac surgery randomized to extended-release METOPROLOL (100 mg started 2 to 4 hours before surgery, up-titrated) versus placebo. Primary outcome was a cardiovascular composite.

Metoprolol REDUCED nonfatal myocardial infarction (3.6 versus 5.1 percent) but INCREASED total mortality (3.1 versus 2.3 percent), STROKE (1 versus 0.5 percent), clinically significant hypotension, and bradycardia.
Starting high fixed-dose beta-blockade acutely before surgery causes net harm through hypotension and stroke.
The harm stemmed from a large, non-titrated starting dose causing hypotension; it does not condemn beta-blockers continued in chronic users or started early and carefully titrated.

Never start high-dose beta-blockers on the day of surgery; continue chronic beta-blockers and, if indicated, begin days to weeks ahead with titration. POISE anchors the perioperative beta-blocker debate.
Acute perioperative metoprolol cut MI but raised stroke and death.

cards.title: POISE-2 (2014)
secs: Factorial RCT of 10,010 noncardiac surgery patients randomized to ASPIRIN versus placebo and, separately, CLONIDINE versus placebo perioperatively. Primary outcome was death or nonfatal myocardial infarction.

Neither helped: aspirin did not reduce the composite and INCREASED major bleeding, while clonidine did not reduce events and caused clinically important HYPOTENSION and bradycardia (and more nonfatal cardiac arrest).
Routine perioperative aspirin or clonidine does not prevent cardiac events and adds harm.
The aspirin arm included both initiation and continuation strata; chronic aspirin for secondary prevention or recent stents is managed separately by indication, not by POISE-2.
Do not add aspirin or clonidine solely for surgery; manage antiplatelets by cardiac indication and stent timing. Alpha-2 agonists are not cardioprotective perioperatively.
Perioperative aspirin and clonidine do not help.

cards.title: POISE-3 (2023)
secs: International RCT of 9535 noncardiac surgery patients at cardiovascular risk randomized to TRANEXAMIC ACID versus placebo, with a partial-factorial comparison of hypotension-avoidance versus hypertension-avoidance blood-pressure strategies.

TXA REDUCED major bleeding (9.1 versus 11.7 percent) and met noninferiority thresholds for most vascular outcomes, though the composite vascular endpoint did not formally satisfy noninferiority; the blood-pressure strategies showed little difference.
Tranexamic acid meaningfully reduces surgical bleeding with an acceptable, if not fully proven, cardiovascular safety profile.
The vascular-safety noninferiority margin was narrowly missed, leaving residual uncertainty; the BP-strategy arm was underpowered for hard outcomes.
Consider TXA to reduce bleeding in noncardiac surgery, weighing the small residual thrombotic uncertainty. POISE-3 extends the TXA story (CRASH-2, WOMAN, ATACAS) to general surgery.
Tranexamic acid reduces bleeding in noncardiac surgery.

cards.title: VISION (2017)
secs: Large international prospective COHORT measuring high-sensitivity troponin routinely on the first postoperative days in patients 45 or older undergoing noncardiac surgery.
MYOCARDIAL INJURY after noncardiac surgery (MINS) was common (about 1 in 6 at some thresholds), frequently ASYMPTOMATIC, and each troponin tier independently predicted 30-day mortality.
Postoperative troponin surveillance uncovers prognostically important, often silent, myocardial injury.
As a cohort it shows association and risk, not a treatment; whether acting on MINS improves outcomes was addressed later by MANAGE (dabigatran).
Consider surveillance troponins after major surgery in higher-risk patients; a rise predicts death even without symptoms or ECG changes. VISION defined the entity of MINS.

cards.title: METS (2018)
secs: International prospective cohort of 1401 patients before major noncardiac surgery comparing subjective clinician assessment of functional capacity (METs) with cardiopulmonary EXERCISE testing, the DASI questionnaire, and NT-proBNP for predicting complications.

Subjective assessment poorly predicted outcomes and misclassified many patients; objective measures (peak oxygen uptake, DASI score, and NT-proBNP) better predicted death, myocardial injury, and complications.
Do not rely on eyeballed exercise tolerance; use objective tools to estimate perioperative risk.
It is a cohort, so it validates prediction rather than an intervention; CPET is resource-intensive, making DASI and biomarkers practical alternatives.
Replace subjective questions about stairs with structured tools (DASI, NT-proBNP, or CPET) for risk assessment. METS is the evidence behind moving away from the old MET-estimate approach.
Subjective METs poorly predict perioperative risk (METS).

cards.title: RCRI / Lee Index (1999)
secs: Prospective derivation and validation study in 4315 patients undergoing major elective noncardiac surgery, creating the REVISED CARDIAC RISK INDEX from independent predictors of major cardiac complications.

Six predictors, high-risk surgery, ischemic heart disease, congestive heart failure, cerebrovascular disease, insulin-treated diabetes, and creatinine above 2 mg/dL, stratify cardiac risk from under 1 percent (0 factors) to over 5 percent (3 or more).
Provides the durable, simple cardiac risk score still embedded in perioperative guidelines.
It UNDERESTIMATES risk in vascular surgery and does not capture functional capacity; newer models (NSQIP MICA, Gupta calculator) complement or outperform it.
Memorize the six RCRI predictors and use them with guideline algorithms and biomarkers. It remains the most-quoted perioperative cardiac risk tool on exams.
RCRI: six predictors of perioperative cardiac risk.

cards.title: INPRESS (2017)
secs: French RCT of 298 high-risk patients having major surgery randomized to INDIVIDUALIZED blood-pressure management (keep systolic within 10 percent of the ward baseline using a norepinephrine infusion) versus standard management (treat systolic below 80 or a 40 percent drop).
The individualized-pressure group had significantly LESS postoperative organ dysfunction (a composite of renal, respiratory, coagulation, and neurologic dysfunction) by day 7.
Tightly maintaining blood pressure near the patient baseline reduces postoperative organ dysfunction.
It is a small single-center trial with a composite endpoint; it reinforces the broader observational theme that intraoperative HYPOTENSION drives organ injury.

Avoid intraoperative hypotension; individualize the pressure target to the patient baseline rather than a universal number. Fits the large body of evidence linking MAP under about 65 to renal and myocardial injury.
Keeping BP near baseline reduces organ dysfunction (INPRESS).

cards.title: MANAGE (2018)
secs: International RCT of 1754 patients who sustained myocardial injury after noncardiac surgery (MINS) randomized to the direct thrombin inhibitor DABIGATRAN 110 mg twice daily versus placebo. Primary outcome was a major vascular composite.
Dabigatran REDUCED the major vascular composite (11 versus 15 percent), driven by fewer vascular events, without a significant increase in major bleeding, but discontinuation was high.
Anticoagulation with dabigatran may reduce vascular complications after MINS, the first therapy shown to act on the entity.
High drop-out weakens the result, and bleeding risk must be individualized; MINS management remains an evolving area.
Consider dabigatran (with aspirin/statin optimization) after MINS in appropriate patients, balancing bleeding risk. MANAGE is the intervention follow-up to the VISION cohort.
Dabigatran reduced vascular events after MINS (MANAGE).

cards.title: GALA (2008)
secs: International RCT of 3526 patients undergoing carotid endarterectomy randomized to LOCAL (regional cervical block) versus GENERAL anesthesia. Primary outcome was a composite of stroke, myocardial infarction, or death at 30 days.
There was NO difference in stroke, myocardial infarction, or death between local and general anesthesia.
Either anesthetic technique is acceptable for carotid endarterectomy; the choice can follow patient and surgeon preference.

The awake patient under regional allows real-time neurologic monitoring (the gold-standard shunt indicator), whereas general anesthesia needs surrogate monitors; GALA showed neither is clearly superior for hard outcomes.

Select local or general based on the patient, surgeon, and monitoring strategy; awake testing detects cerebral ischemia directly. Pair GALA with the general principle of avoiding hypotension during clamping.
General and local anesthesia are equivalent for carotid surgery (GALA).

cards.title: MASTER (2002)
secs: RCT of 888 high-risk patients undergoing major abdominal surgery randomized to combined EPIDURAL plus general anesthesia with postoperative epidural analgesia versus general anesthesia with systemic opioids. Primary outcome was major morbidity/mortality.
There was no difference in overall major morbidity or mortality; the epidural group had significantly better analgesia and fewer episodes of RESPIRATORY FAILURE.
Epidural analgesia provides better pain control and fewer respiratory complications but does not, by itself, reduce mortality.
Benefits are analgesic and respiratory, and must be weighed against anticoagulation timing (ASRA), hypotension, and the risk of epidural hematoma; enthusiasm has since been tempered.
Offer epidural analgesia for major abdominal/thoracic surgery mainly for pain and pulmonary benefit, respecting anticoagulation guidelines. Do not promise a mortality reduction.
Epidural adds analgesic and respiratory benefit, not survival (MASTER).

cards.title: ENIGMA (2007)
secs: RCT of 2050 patients undergoing major (over 2 hour) noncardiac surgery randomized to NITROUS OXIDE-based anesthesia (70 percent N2O) versus a nitrous-free technique (80 percent oxygen).
Nitrous oxide increased severe postoperative nausea and vomiting and was associated with more WOUND infection, pneumonia, atelectasis, and longer hospital stay.
Raised early concern that routine nitrous oxide worsens surgical and pulmonary complications.
Confounded by the high-oxygen comparator, small nitrous concentrations, and long exposure; the later, larger ENIGMA-II specifically refuted the cardiovascular and major-complication concerns.
Nitrous still worsens PONV and expands closed gas spaces (avoid in bowel obstruction, pneumothorax, middle-ear and retinal gas surgery). ENIGMA overstated harms that ENIGMA-II corrected.

cards.title: ENIGMA-II (2014)
secs: Large international RCT of 7112 patients with cardiovascular risk factors undergoing noncardiac surgery randomized to nitrous-based versus nitrous-free anesthesia. Primary outcome was death and cardiovascular complications at 30 days.
There was NO increase in death, myocardial infarction, stroke, or wound infection with nitrous oxide; only postoperative nausea and vomiting were more frequent (manageable with prophylaxis).
Nitrous oxide is cardiovascularly and surgically safe, reversing the alarms from the first ENIGMA.
It reassures on hard outcomes but does not negate the physical gas-expansion hazards or the PONV effect; homocysteine elevation did not translate into cardiac events.
Nitrous oxide is safe for the heart; just give antiemetic prophylaxis and avoid it where gas expansion is dangerous. ENIGMA versus ENIGMA-II is a classic small-versus-large reversal.
Nitrous oxide is safe for the heart (ENIGMA-II).

cards.title: B-Aware (2004)
secs: Australian RCT of 2463 adults at HIGH RISK for awareness (e.g., cardiac surgery, cesarean, trauma, high opioid use) randomized to BIS-guided anesthesia (target 40 to 60) versus routine clinical care. Primary outcome was confirmed awareness.
BIS guidance reduced confirmed awareness (2 cases versus 11; 0.17 versus 0.91 percent), a roughly 82 percent relative reduction.
Processed-EEG (BIS) monitoring reduces explicit awareness in high-risk patients.

The benefit was in a high-risk population; later trials (B-Unaware, BAG-RECALL) found BIS was NOT superior to monitoring end-tidal anesthetic concentration, so routine BIS for all is not clearly justified.
Consider depth monitoring (BIS or end-tidal agent) when awareness risk is high (TIVA, paralysis, hemodynamic instability). B-Aware is positive, but weigh it against the later end-tidal-agent trials.
BIS reduces awareness in high-risk patients (B-Aware).

cards.title: B-Unaware / BAG-RECALL (2008-11)
secs: Two RCTs comparing BIS-guided anesthesia with a protocol targeting END-TIDAL ANESTHETIC concentration (about 0.7 to 1.3 MAC) with a low-agent alarm, in patients at high risk of awareness (over 6000 combined).
Neither showed BIS superior; BAG-RECALL actually found MORE awareness with BIS than with the end-tidal agent protocol, and B-Unaware found equivalence.
A simple end-tidal anesthetic-agent alarm is at least as effective as BIS for preventing awareness, at no extra cost.
These temper B-Aware and argue against mandatory BIS; they apply to volatile-based anesthesia, where an agent alarm is available (BIS retains a role in TIVA).
Use an end-tidal agent alarm (around 0.7 MAC) as a cheap awareness safeguard during volatile anesthesia; reserve processed EEG especially for total intravenous anesthesia. Contrast with B-Aware.
End-tidal agent monitoring rivals BIS for awareness.

cards.title: GAS (2016)
secs: International RCT of 722 infants undergoing inguinal hernia repair randomized to awake REGIONAL (spinal) versus sevoflurane-based GENERAL anesthesia, with blinded neurodevelopmental follow-up.
There was NO difference in neurodevelopmental outcome (IQ and composite scores) at either 2 or 5 years after a single anesthetic averaging under 1 hour.
A single, brief general anesthetic in infancy does not measurably harm neurodevelopment.
It addresses SINGLE, SHORT exposure only and cannot speak to prolonged or repeated anesthesia; the equivalence is reassuring within those limits.
Reassure families that one short anesthetic is safe; remaining caution (FDA labeling) concerns repeated or long exposures under age 3. GAS is the strongest randomized neurodevelopmental evidence.
Brief infant anesthesia does not harm neurodevelopment (GAS).

cards.title: PANDA (2016)
secs: Sibling-matched observational cohort comparing children who had a SINGLE anesthesia exposure (for hernia repair) before 36 months with their unexposed siblings, using detailed neuropsychological testing.
There was no significant difference in IQ or in a broad battery of neurocognitive domains between exposed and unexposed siblings.
Single early anesthesia exposure is not associated with detectable neurocognitive deficits.
Observational design limits causal inference, and it studied single exposures; it aligns with GAS in being reassuring for brief, one-time anesthesia.
Use PANDA and GAS together to counsel that a single early anesthetic appears safe, while multiple/prolonged exposures remain under study. Both underlie the FDA under-3 caution.
Single early anesthesia exposure appears safe (PANDA).

cards.title: MASK (2018)
secs: Population-based cohort from Olmsted County comparing children with NO, SINGLE, or MULTIPLE anesthesia exposures before age 3, using a battery of neuropsychological and processing tests.
Single exposure showed no deficit; MULTIPLE exposures were associated with modest decreases in processing speed and fine-motor coordination, without differences in general intelligence.
Repeated early anesthesia carries a small, selective signal, while single exposure appears safe.
Observational, so unmeasured confounding (why children need multiple procedures) may contribute; the deficits were subtle and of uncertain clinical significance.
Counsel that single exposures look safe but repeated ones warrant thoughtful scheduling; MASK, GAS, and PANDA together inform the FDA warning on prolonged/repeated anesthesia under 3.

cards.title: REGAIN (2021)
secs: US/Canadian pragmatic RCT of 1600 patients (mean age 78) undergoing hip-fracture surgery randomized to SPINAL versus GENERAL anesthesia. Primary outcome was death or new inability to walk at 60 days.
There was NO difference in the primary composite (about 18 percent each), nor in delirium or mortality alone, between spinal and general anesthesia.
Spinal and general anesthesia yield equivalent outcomes for hip-fracture surgery.
It contradicts long-held assumptions that neuraxial is safer for the elderly hip-fracture patient; either technique is defensible, letting comorbidity and anticoagulation guide the choice.

Choose spinal or general based on anticoagulation status, comorbidity, and patient preference, not on a presumed neuraxial advantage. REGAIN and RAGA jointly settle the hip-fracture anesthesia debate.
Spinal and general are equivalent for hip fracture (REGAIN).

cards.title: RAGA (2021)
secs: Chinese RCT of 950 elderly patients (over 65) undergoing hip-fracture surgery randomized to REGIONAL (spinal/epidural) versus GENERAL anesthesia. Primary outcome was postoperative DELIRIUM during the first 7 days.
There was NO significant difference in incidence of delirium (about 6 to 7 percent) or its severity/duration between regional and general anesthesia.
Anesthetic technique does not meaningfully change postoperative delirium after hip-fracture surgery.
Delirium is multifactorial (pain, sleep, medications, baseline cognition), so anesthetic type alone is a weak lever; RAGA aligns with REGAIN in finding equivalence.

Prevent delirium with multicomponent bundles (orientation, sleep, early mobilization, minimizing deliriogenic drugs) rather than by choosing regional anesthesia. RAGA and REGAIN together settle this question.
Regional does not reduce hip-fracture delirium (RAGA).

cards.title: NAP4 (2011)
secs: UK Royal College 4th National Audit Project, a prospective nationwide registry of MAJOR airway complications (death, brain injury, emergency surgical airway, unanticipated ICU admission) during anesthesia, ICU, and emergency care over one year.

It found underuse of CAPNOGRAPHY (especially in ICU, where absence contributed to many deaths), failures to plan for failed intubation, aspiration as a leading cause, and dangers of supraglottic airways in inappropriate patients; ICU/ED events had high mortality.
Continuous waveform capnography and a pre-formulated failed-airway plan are mandatory in every location that manages airways.
As an audit it describes practice and harm rather than testing an intervention, but its recommendations reshaped airway safety standards worldwide.
Use waveform capnography for EVERY intubation (a flat trace after intubation means esophageal placement) and always have a Plan A/B/C. NAP4 is the key airway-safety citation, especially for the ICU.
Capnography and failed-airway planning save lives (NAP4).

cards.title: MACMAN (2017)
secs: French RCT of 371 ICU intubations randomized to VIDEO (Macintosh-style video blade) versus DIRECT laryngoscopy, performed by residents and attendings. Primary outcome was first-attempt intubation success.

First-attempt success did NOT differ (about 68 percent each), and the video group had a signal of MORE severe life-threatening complications, partly from difficulty advancing the tube despite a good view.
Video laryngoscopy did not improve first-pass success in the ICU in this trial and may have a complication trade-off.
Results are operator- and device-dependent and conflict with the later, larger DEVICE trial; a good glottic VIEW does not guarantee easy tube DELIVERY.
A good view is not the same as successful intubation; train for tube delivery with video devices. Contrast MACMAN (ICU, neutral) with DEVICE (ED/ICU, video superior).
Video did not beat direct laryngoscopy in the ICU (MACMAN).

cards.title: DEVICE (2023)
secs: US multicenter RCT of 1417 critically ill adults undergoing emergency intubation in the ED or ICU randomized to VIDEO versus DIRECT laryngoscopy. Primary outcome was first-attempt success.

Video laryngoscopy significantly improved first-attempt success (85.1 versus 70.8 percent), with similar rates of esophageal intubation and complications; benefit was consistent across operator experience.
Video laryngoscopy improves first-attempt intubation success in emergency settings and is a reasonable default.
It enrolled mostly less-experienced operators, which may amplify the benefit; it contrasts with the older, smaller MACMAN, reflecting improved video devices and training.
Favor video laryngoscopy for emergency and difficult intubation, especially with less-experienced operators. DEVICE is the modern, larger trial that shifted practice toward video.

cards.title: PreVent (2019)
secs: US PETAL Network RCT of 401 critically ill adults randomized to BAG-MASK ventilation versus NO ventilation during the interval between induction/paralysis and laryngoscopy (the apneic period of rapid-sequence intubation).
Bag-mask ventilation reduced severe HYPOXEMIA (SpO2 below 80: 10.9 versus 22.8 percent) and raised the lowest oxygen saturation, WITHOUT increasing aspiration.
Gentle bag-mask ventilation during induction is safe and reduces hypoxemia in the critically ill.
It challenges the traditional no-ventilation dogma of classic RSI; it excluded patients at highest aspiration risk, so judgment is still required.
Provide gentle bag-mask breaths during RSI in hypoxemia-prone critically ill patients; this differs from the classic full-stomach teaching. Combine with ramped positioning and apneic oxygenation.

cards.title: PREOXI (2024)
secs: US PENprep/PREOXI RCT of critically ill adults undergoing tracheal intubation randomized to preoxygenation with NONINVASIVE VENTILATION versus an oxygen MASK (or high-flow) before induction.
Noninvasive-ventilation preoxygenation substantially reduced the incidence of HYPOXEMIA during intubation and reduced peri-intubation cardiac arrest compared with oxygen-mask preoxygenation.
Noninvasive ventilation is the preferred preoxygenation method for high-risk critically ill patients.
It applies to the critically ill with shunt physiology and high aspiration-adjusted risk; NIV preoxygenation requires a cooperative patient and carries a small aspiration concern.
Preoxygenate hypoxemia-prone patients with NIV (or high-flow) rather than a simple mask; combine with apneic oxygenation and ramped positioning. PREOXI is the newest preoxygenation evidence.

cards.title: CRASH (2004)
secs: Huge international RCT (the Corticosteroid Randomisation After Significant Head injury trial) of 10,008 adults with traumatic brain injury and reduced consciousness randomized to a 48-hour infusion of high-dose METHYLPREDNISOLONE versus placebo.
The trial was STOPPED early because steroids INCREASED death (21.1 versus 17.9 percent at 2 weeks), with the excess persisting at 6 months.
Corticosteroids are contraindicated in traumatic brain injury; they increase mortality.
A landmark NEGATIVE trial that ended a common practice; the mechanism of harm (infection, hyperglycemia) is not fully defined but the signal is unequivocal.
Never give steroids for TBI. CRASH is the definitive trial and a favorite example of a plausible therapy proven harmful only by a large RCT.
Steroids increase death in head injury (CRASH).

cards.title: CRASH-3 (2019)
secs: International RCT of 12,737 patients with traumatic brain injury (GCS 12 or below or intracranial bleeding on CT) randomized to TRANEXAMIC ACID versus placebo within 3 hours of injury.

TXA reduced head-injury-related death in MILD-to-MODERATE injury (those with reactive pupils), with benefit greatest when given early; there was NO benefit in severe injury with fixed, unreactive pupils, and no increase in thrombotic events or seizures.
Early tranexamic acid reduces death in mild-to-moderate traumatic brain injury.
Like other TXA trials the effect is time-critical, and the overall primary endpoint was neutral, with benefit confined to the less-severe subgroup.
Give TXA within 3 hours for mild-to-moderate TBI; it will not help devastating injury with fixed pupils. CRASH-3 extends the TXA family (CRASH-2, WOMAN) to head injury.
Early TXA helps mild-to-moderate head injury (CRASH-3).

cards.title: DECRA (2011)
secs: Australasian RCT of 155 patients with severe DIFFUSE traumatic brain injury and intracranial pressure refractory to first-tier measures randomized to early bifrontotemporoparietal DECOMPRESSIVE CRANIECTOMY versus continued medical management.
Craniectomy lowered intracranial pressure and ICU stay but produced WORSE functional outcomes (more unfavorable Glasgow Outcome Scale) at 6 months.
Lowering intracranial pressure by early craniectomy did not translate into better function and may worsen it.
Baseline pupil imbalance differed between groups, and the ICP threshold for surgery was relatively low; it addresses EARLY craniectomy for diffuse injury, not last-tier rescue.
Do not perform early craniectomy simply to control pressure; controlling ICP is not the same as improving outcome. Contrast DECRA (early, worse function) with RESCUEicp (last-tier, more survival).
Early craniectomy lowered ICP but worsened outcome (DECRA).

cards.title: RESCUEicp (2016)
secs: International RCT of 408 patients with traumatic brain injury and intracranial hypertension REFRACTORY to maximal medical therapy randomized to LAST-TIER decompressive craniectomy versus continued medical management (including barbiturates).

Craniectomy REDUCED mortality (26.9 versus 48.9 percent) but increased the proportion of survivors in a vegetative state or with severe disability; rates of moderate disability and good recovery were similar.
Last-tier craniectomy is a life-saving but disability-increasing option for refractory intracranial hypertension.
The survival benefit comes with a clear trade-off toward severe disability, so decisions require goals-of-care discussion; it differs from DECRA by targeting truly refractory pressure.
Offer craniectomy as a last resort for refractory ICP, counseling families about the survival-versus-severe-disability trade-off. DECRA and RESCUEicp frame the craniectomy debate.
Craniectomy cut death but increased disability (RESCUEicp).

cards.title: Eurotherm (2015)
secs: European RCT of patients with traumatic brain injury and intracranial pressure above 20 mmHg randomized to therapeutic HYPOTHERMIA (32 to 35 degrees) plus standard care versus standard care alone for pressure control. Stopped early.
Hypothermia controlled intracranial pressure but produced WORSE outcomes (more death and severe disability) than standard stepwise care.
Therapeutic hypothermia should not be used to treat intracranial hypertension in traumatic brain injury.
Another example of an ICP-lowering strategy that harms outcome; cooling has cardiovascular, coagulation, and infection costs that outweigh the pressure benefit.
Do not cool TBI patients to control ICP; use stepwise medical measures and reserve surgery per RESCUEicp. Eurotherm joins DECRA and Eurotherm as negative ICP-directed trials.

cards.title: BEST TRIP (2012)
secs: RCT in Bolivia/Ecuador of 324 patients with severe traumatic brain injury randomized to management guided by intracranial-pressure MONITORING (maintaining ICP below 20) versus management guided by serial imaging and clinical examination.
There was NO difference in survival or functional outcome between the monitor-guided and clinical-examination-guided strategies.
Structured care guided by examination and imaging can match ICP-monitor-guided care; monitoring alone is not automatically superior.
It does not prove monitors are useless, but that a disciplined protocol without invasive monitoring performed comparably; generalizability from the study setting is debated.
ICP monitoring is a tool, not a therapy; what matters is a consistent protocol to treat raised pressure. BEST TRIP is the key trial questioning routine ICP monitors.
ICP-monitor-guided care was no better than clinical (BEST TRIP).

cards.title: Magpie (2002)
secs: Enormous international RCT of 10,141 women with PREECLAMPSIA randomized to MAGNESIUM SULFATE versus placebo. Primary outcomes were eclampsia (seizures) and, for the baby, death.

Magnesium more than HALVED the risk of eclampsia (0.8 versus 1.9 percent) and reduced maternal death (nonsignificant trend), with no substantive harm to babies; side effects were flushing and injection-site reactions.
Magnesium sulfate is the agent for eclampsia seizure prophylaxis in preeclampsia.
Magnesium prevents SEIZURES rather than lowering blood pressure; toxicity (loss of reflexes, respiratory depression, cardiac arrest) requires monitoring, with calcium gluconate as the antidote.
Give magnesium for seizure prophylaxis in severe preeclampsia and treat established eclampsia; monitor reflexes, respiration, and urine output. It also potentiates neuromuscular blockers.

cards.title: Term Breech Trial (2000)
secs: International RCT of 2088 women with a singleton term breech fetus randomized to planned CESAREAN versus planned VAGINAL birth. Primary outcome was perinatal/neonatal mortality or serious morbidity.
Planned cesarean reduced perinatal mortality and serious neonatal morbidity (1.6 versus 5 percent), with a small increase in maternal morbidity.
Planned cesarean became standard practice for the term breech presentation.
Long-term follow-up found no difference in childhood outcomes, and methodology was later criticized; some centers offer selective vaginal breech delivery with strict criteria.
For a term breech, planned cesarean remains the common recommendation, though experienced centers may offer vaginal breech birth. Anesthetic readiness for urgent cesarean is essential.
Planned cesarean is safer for term breech.

cards.title: CORONARY (2012)
secs: International RCT of 4752 patients undergoing coronary artery bypass randomized to OFF-PUMP (beating-heart) versus ON-PUMP (cardiopulmonary bypass) surgery. Primary outcome was a composite of death, MI, stroke, or new dialysis at 30 days.

There was NO difference in the 30-day or 1-year composite; off-pump reduced transfusion, acute kidney injury, and respiratory complications but led to MORE repeat revascularization and a trend to fewer completed grafts.
Off-pump and on-pump CABG produce similar major clinical outcomes.
Off-pump results depend heavily on surgeon experience, and long-term graft patency concerns persist, so technique is individualized.

Expect equivalent hard outcomes; off-pump trades fewer transfusions and less AKI for possible incomplete revascularization. Anesthetic implications include hemodynamic swings during heart positioning off-pump.

cards.title: SIRS / DECS steroids (2015)
secs: Two large RCTs of prophylactic corticosteroids in cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass: SIRS (7507 patients, methylprednisolone) and DECS (4494 patients, dexamethasone), each versus placebo.

Prophylactic steroids did NOT reduce mortality or major morbidity; steroids modestly increased myocardial injury (SIRS) and hyperglycemia while reducing some infections and shortening ventilation slightly.
Routine corticosteroids are not indicated for cardiopulmonary bypass despite the inflammatory rationale.

The strong anti-inflammatory rationale of bypass did not yield outcome benefit, echoing other negative steroid trials; any small benefits are outweighed by hyperglycemia and myocardial injury signals.
Do not give prophylactic steroids for the systemic inflammatory response to bypass. SIRS and DECS are the definitive negative trials on this question.
Steroids do not improve cardiac-surgery outcomes (SIRS/DECS).

cards.title: IABP-SHOCK II (2012)
secs: German RCT of 600 patients with acute myocardial infarction complicated by CARDIOGENIC SHOCK undergoing early revascularization, randomized to INTRA-AORTIC BALLOON PUMP versus no balloon pump. Primary outcome was 30-day mortality.
The balloon pump did NOT reduce 30-day mortality (39.7 versus 41.3 percent), and there was no benefit at 1 year or 6 years, nor improvements in shock resolution or organ perfusion.
The intra-aortic balloon pump does not improve survival in MI-related cardiogenic shock and is no longer routine.
It shifted guideline support away from routine IABP; interest moved to other mechanical support devices (Impella, ECMO), which have their own uncertain evidence.
Do not place an IABP reflexively for cardiogenic shock; prioritize early revascularization. IABP-SHOCK II is why the balloon pump lost its class-I recommendation.
IABP does not improve cardiogenic-shock survival.

cards.title: POPULAR (2018)
secs: Prospective European observational study of over 17,000 patients receiving general anesthesia with neuromuscular blockers, examining the association between reversal strategy/monitoring and postoperative PULMONARY complications.

Use of neuromuscular blockers was associated with MORE pulmonary complications; neither routine neostigmine nor sugammadex eliminated the risk, and quantitative (objective) neuromuscular MONITORING was underused.
Residual neuromuscular blockade is a real hazard, and objective train-of-four monitoring, not just choice of reversal agent, is what matters.
As an observational study it shows association, not causation, and cannot prove sugammadex is no better; it strongly supports quantitative monitoring and confirmed recovery before extubation.
Use QUANTITATIVE train-of-four monitoring and confirm a ratio of 0.9 or greater before extubation, regardless of reversal agent. POPULAR is the key residual-paralysis safety study.
Quantitative NMB monitoring reduces pulmonary risk (POPULAR).

cards.title: PADDI (2021)
secs: Australian RCT of 8880 adults undergoing noncardiac surgery randomized to a single dose of DEXAMETHASONE 8 mg versus placebo, primarily to test whether the antiemetic dose increases infection.
Dexamethasone did NOT increase surgical-site infection (8.1 versus 9.1 percent, meeting noninferiority) and reduced nausea, vomiting, and fever, with only transient, minor hyperglycemia.
A single antiemetic dose of dexamethasone is safe and does not raise infection risk, including in diabetics.
It settles a long-standing worry; the transient glucose rise is modest and clinically unimportant for a single dose.
Use dexamethasone (4 to 8 mg) freely for PONV prophylaxis and its analgesic benefit without fear of infection. PADDI is the reassurance trial for perioperative dexamethasone.
Single-dose dexamethasone is safe for PONV (PADDI).

cards.title: Apfel factorial (2004)
secs: RCT with a factorial design in 5199 high-risk patients testing six PONV interventions: ondansetron, dexamethasone, droperidol, propofol (versus volatile), nitrogen (versus nitrous oxide), and remifentanil (versus fentanyl).

Each antiemetic (ondansetron, dexamethasone, droperidol) independently reduced PONV by about 26 percent, and their effects were ADDITIVE; using propofol and avoiding nitrous oxide also helped. Combining interventions gave the greatest reduction.
PONV prophylaxis should be multimodal, with the number of interventions matched to baseline risk.
The independent, additive effect underlies the modern risk-stratified approach (Apfel score: female, nonsmoker, PONV/motion-sickness history, postoperative opioids).
Count Apfel risk factors and add one antiemetic (from a different class) per risk factor, favoring agents that act on different receptors; consider TIVA. This is the basis of PONV guidelines.
Combine independent antiemetics by PONV risk (Apfel).

cards.title: Perioperative gabapentinoids (2020 meta-analysis)
secs: Large systematic review and meta-analysis of gabapentin and pregabalin given perioperatively for acute and chronic postsurgical pain, pooling many randomized trials.
There was NO clinically meaningful reduction in acute pain scores or in chronic postsurgical pain, and no opioid-sparing benefit of importance, but MORE sedation, dizziness, and visual disturbance.
Routine perioperative gabapentinoids provide little analgesic benefit and increase side effects.
Earlier enthusiasm rested on smaller trials with heterogeneity; the harms are amplified in the elderly and when combined with opioids or in obstructive sleep apnea.
Do not add gabapentinoids routinely to multimodal analgesia; reserve them for selective neuropathic pain, and avoid combining with opioids in patients with OSA. Guidelines now discourage routine use.
Perioperative gabapentinoids: little benefit, more sedation.

cards.title: OPTIMISE (2014)
secs: UK RCT of 734 high-risk patients undergoing major gastrointestinal surgery randomized to a GOAL-DIRECTED hemodynamic therapy algorithm (fluid boluses and low-dose dopexamine guided by stroke volume) versus usual care.
The primary composite of complications and 30-day mortality was lower but did NOT reach statistical significance; an updated meta-analysis including OPTIMISE did show a significant benefit.
Goal-directed hemodynamic therapy offers a modest, probably real benefit concentrated in the highest-risk surgical patients.
Individually underpowered and neutral; its influence comes from the pooled meta-analysis. The specific inotrope and targets vary across GDT protocols.

Consider stroke-volume-guided goal-directed therapy for high-risk major surgery to reduce complications; benefit is modest and greatest in the sickest. Pair with RELIEF on perioperative fluid balance.

cards.title: LAS VEGAS (2017)
secs: International prospective OBSERVATIONAL study of intraoperative ventilation in over 9800 patients undergoing general anesthesia for various surgeries, relating ventilator settings to postoperative pulmonary complications.

Postoperative pulmonary complications were COMMON (about 1 in 5) and were associated with higher tidal volumes, low or absent PEEP, and higher driving pressures; complications independently predicted worse outcomes.
Intraoperative ventilator settings are linked to pulmonary complications, supporting protective ventilation for all.
Observational design means association, not proof of causation; nonetheless it reinforces the protective-ventilation message from Futier and the ARDS literature.

Apply protective ventilation to essentially every anesthetic (about 6 to 8 mL/kg predicted body weight, appropriate PEEP, low driving pressure). LAS VEGAS documents how common and consequential pulmonary complications are.
Intraoperative ventilation is linked to pulmonary complications (LAS VEGAS).

cards.title: NASCET (1991)
secs: North American RCT of patients with SYMPTOMATIC carotid stenosis (recent TIA or nondisabling stroke) randomized to carotid ENDARTERECTOMY plus medical therapy versus medical therapy alone.
Surgery markedly reduced ipsilateral stroke for 70 to 99 percent stenosis (about 9 versus 26 percent at 2 years); benefit was smaller for 50 to 69 percent stenosis and absent below 50 percent.
Carotid endarterectomy benefits symptomatic severe carotid stenosis, a foundational vascular-surgery trial.
Benefit depends on a LOW perioperative complication rate and on the degree of stenosis and symptoms; asymptomatic disease (ACAS/ACST) has a much smaller absolute benefit.

Operate on symptomatic high-grade carotid stenosis promptly; anesthetic priorities are blood-pressure control and cerebral protection during clamping (see GALA for technique). Know the stenosis thresholds.
CEA benefits symptomatic severe carotid stenosis (NASCET).

cards.title: MR CLEAN (2015)
secs: Dutch RCT of 500 patients with acute ischemic stroke from a proximal ANTERIOR-circulation large-vessel occlusion, randomized to endovascular THROMBECTOMY (mostly stent retrievers) plus usual care versus usual care alone, within 6 hours.
Thrombectomy substantially improved functional independence at 90 days (33 versus 19 percent achieving modified Rankin 0 to 2), launching a wave of confirmatory trials.
Endovascular thrombectomy is standard care for large-vessel-occlusion ischemic stroke, highly relevant to neuroanesthesia.
It requires confirmed large-vessel occlusion and timely access; later trials extended the window to 24 hours with perfusion imaging (DAWN, DEFUSE-3).
For thrombectomy, anesthetic goals are avoiding HYPOTENSION and hyperglycemia, rapid workflow, and careful choice of sedation versus general anesthesia. MR CLEAN opened the modern thrombectomy era.

cards.title: DOSE VF (2022)
secs: Canadian cluster-randomized crossover trial of 405 patients with out-of-hospital REFRACTORY ventricular fibrillation (persisting after three standard shocks) randomized among continued standard defibrillation, VECTOR-CHANGE defibrillation, and DOUBLE SEQUENTIAL external defibrillation.
Double sequential external defibrillation improved survival to hospital discharge (30.4 versus 13.3 percent standard) and vector-change also improved survival; neurologic outcomes trended better.
Alternative defibrillation strategies improve survival in refractory ventricular fibrillation.
It is a relatively small pilot-scale trial, so results need confirmation, and double sequential defibrillation risks device damage if performed incorrectly.
For refractory VF after three shocks, consider changing pad vector or double sequential defibrillation while continuing high-quality CPR. DOSE VF is the practice-changing recent arrest trial.
Double or vector-change defibrillation aids refractory VF (DOSE VF).
